1.summary: Notification No. 02/2026-Central Tax, issued on May 7, 2026, introduces an important change regarding the appeals process under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Specifically, it empowers the Principal Bench of the Appellate Tribunal located in New Delhi to hear appeals made under section 101B of the Act. This change is effective from April 1, 2026, and is based on recommendations from the GST Council. Additionally, this notification corrects a previous reference number from 18/2024 to 02/2026, ensuring clarity in the legal framework. A corrigendum regarding this reference number change was published shortly after, on May 8, 2026. This update is significant for stakeholders involved in GST appeals, as it clarifies the authority responsible for hearing such cases, thereby streamlining the appeals process for taxpayers and businesses affected by GST regulations.
key_points: The notification is titled Notification No. 02/2026-Central Tax.
It was issued on May 7, 2026.
The notification empowers the Principal Bench of the Appellate Tribunal in New Delhi to hear appeals under section 101B of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
This change is effective from April 1, 2026.
The notification is based on the recommendations of the GST Council.
It amends the earlier notification by correcting the reference number from 18/2024 to 02/2026.
The corrigendum for the reference number change was published on May 8, 2026.

2.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 01/2025-Compensation Cess (Rate) on January 1, 2025, which provides an exemption from compensation cess for the intra-state and inter-state supply of taxable goods intended for export. This exemption is limited to a rate of 0.1% and comes with specific conditions that must be met by registered suppliers and recipients. For instance, the registered supplier must issue a tax invoice, and the registered recipient must export the goods within 90 days of the invoice date. Additionally, the recipient is required to include the supplier's GSTIN and invoice number in the shipping bill and must be registered with an Export Promotion Council or a Commodity Board. The goods must be transported directly to designated export points or a registered warehouse. If the recipient aggregates supplies from multiple suppliers, the goods must first be stored in a registered warehouse. Importantly, if the recipient fails to export the goods within the stipulated 90 days, the exemption will not apply. This notification is effective immediately and impacts registered suppliers and recipients involved in the export of taxable goods.
key_points: Notification No. 01/2025-Compensation Cess (Rate) issued on 2025-01-01.
Exempts intra-state and inter-state supply of taxable goods for export from compensation cess exceeding 0.1%.
Conditions for exemption include supplying goods on a tax invoice and exporting within 90 days.
Registered recipient must indicate the supplier's GSTIN and tax invoice number in the shipping bill.
Recipient must be registered with an Export Promotion Council or Commodity Board.
Goods must move directly to export points or a registered warehouse before export.
If aggregating supplies from multiple suppliers, goods must first go to a registered warehouse.
Exemption is void if goods are not exported within 90 days from the tax invoice date.

3.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 02/2025-Compensation Cess (Rate) on September 17, 2025, which amends the previous notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) from June 28, 2017. The key change is that the entries in column (4) for various items in the SCHEDULE have been updated to 'Nil'. This affects a total of 18 specific entries, including S. No. 2, 3, 4, 4A, 4B, and others up to S. No. 55. The new rates will take effect from September 22, 2025. Businesses and stakeholders involved in the sectors related to these entries should take note of this change, as it signifies that no compensation cess will be applicable for these items moving forward. This amendment is part of the ongoing adjustments to the GST framework to streamline tax rates and compliance for various goods and services.
key_points: Notification No. 02/2025-Compensation Cess (Rate) issued on 2025-09-17.
This notification amends the earlier notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendments involve substituting 'Nil' for the entries in column (4) for several items in the SCHEDULE.
Specifically, entries for S. No. 2, 3, 4, 4A, 4B, 39, 40, 41, 42, 47, 48, 50, 51, 52, 52A, 52B, 53, 54, and 55 are affected.
This notification will come into force on 22nd September 2025.

4.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 03/2025-Compensation Cess (Rate) on 31st December 2025, which amends the earlier notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This amendment specifically alters the entries in column (4) of the Schedule for S. No. 1 through S. No. 38, changing all of them to 'Nil'. This means that there will be no compensation cess applicable for these items starting from 1st February 2026. Businesses and individuals who deal with the goods or services listed under these serial numbers will no longer be required to pay compensation cess on them. This change is significant as it may affect pricing and compliance for various sectors that were previously subject to this cess. The notification aims to simplify the tax structure and provide relief to taxpayers by eliminating the cess for a wide range of goods and services.
key_points: Notification No. 03/2025-Compensation Cess (Rate) issued on 31st December 2025.
This notification amends the principal notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
All entries in column (4) for S. No. 1 to S. No. 38 in the Schedule are changed to 'Nil'.
The changes will come into effect on 1st February 2026.

5.summary: On January 10, 2025, the Government of India issued Notification No. 01/2025-Central Tax, which amends the earlier Notification No. 83/2020-Central Tax from November 10, 2020. This amendment introduces a new proviso that extends the deadlines for filing FORM GSTR-1. Specifically, registered persons must now submit their GSTR-1 for the tax period of December 2024 by January 13, 2025. Additionally, those required to furnish returns for the tax periods from October to December 2024 have until January 15, 2025, to complete their filings. This extension is significant for businesses as it provides additional time to comply with their GST filing obligations for the specified periods. The changes are aimed at easing the compliance burden on taxpayers during this period.
key_points: The notification is dated January 10, 2025.
It amends Notification No. 83/2020-Central Tax, originally issued on November 10, 2020.
A new proviso is added after the fifth proviso in Notification No. 83/2020.
The deadline for filing FORM GSTR-1 for the tax period December 2024 is extended to January 13, 2025.
The deadline for registered persons required to furnish returns for the tax periods October to December 2024 is extended to January 15, 2025.

6.summary: The recent Notification No. 02/2025-Central Tax, issued on January 10, 2025, brings important changes to the due dates for filing GSTR-3B returns. For the month of December 2024, the deadline has been extended from January 20, 2025, to January 22, 2025. Additionally, for the quarter covering October to December 2024, registered persons in specific states will have their due dates adjusted. Those whose principal place of business is in states like Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, and others will now need to file by January 24, 2025. Meanwhile, registered persons in states such as Himachal Pradesh, Punjab, and others will have until January 26, 2025, to submit their returns. This extension is aimed at providing relief to taxpayers in these regions, allowing them more time to comply with their filing obligations.
key_points: Extends GSTR-3B due date for December 2024 from January 20, 2025, to January 22, 2025.
Extends GSTR-3B due date for the quarter October to December 2024 for certain states until January 24, 2025, or January 26, 2025, depending on the state.

Registered persons in Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Goa, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, Daman and Diu, Dadra and Nagar Haveli, Puducherry, Andaman and Nicobar Islands, and Lakshadweep have a new due date of January 24, 2025.

Registered persons in Himachal Pradesh, Punjab, Uttarakhand, Haryana, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Sikkim, Arunachal Pradesh, Nagaland, Manipur, Mizoram, Tripura, Meghalaya, Assam, West Bengal, Jharkhand, Odisha, Jammu and Kashmir, Ladakh, Chandigarh, and Delhi have a new due date of January 26, 2025.

7.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 03/2025-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for non-resident taxable persons to file their GSTR-5 return for December 2024. The new due date is now set for January 15, 2025. This change is based on the recommendations made by the GST Council and is in accordance with the powers granted under section 39(6) and section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The relevant rule governing this return is rule 63 of the CGST Rules, 2017. This extension specifically affects non-resident taxable persons who are required to submit their returns for the specified month. It provides additional time for these entities to comply with their filing obligations without facing immediate repercussions. This notification aims to facilitate smoother compliance for non-residents engaged in taxable activities in India.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-5 for December 2024.
New due date is now January 15, 2025.
Applicable to non-resident taxable persons.
This extension is based on the recommendations of the GST Council.
The notification is issued under section 39(6) and section 168 of the CGST Act.
The relevant rule for this return is rule 63 of the CGST Rules, 2017.

8.summary: Notification No. 04/2025-Central Tax, issued on January 10, 2025, extends the deadline for Input Service Distributors to file their GSTR-6 return for December 2024. The new due date is now set for January 15, 2025. This change is significant for businesses that qualify as Input Service Distributors, as it provides them with additional time to prepare and submit their returns. The extension is granted under the authority of section 39(6) and section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, following recommendations from the GST Council. Previously, the due date was earlier than January 15, 2025, but this notification allows for a more manageable timeline for compliance. Businesses should take note of this new deadline to ensure they meet the revised requirements without any issues.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-6 for December 2024.
New due date for GSTR-6 is now January 15, 2025.
This extension applies specifically to Input Service Distributors.
The notification is issued under section 39(6) and section 168 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The extension is based on recommendations from the GST Council.
The original due date was prior to January 15, 2025.

9.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 05/2025-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for filing GSTR-7 for the month of December 2024. Originally, the due date was set for January 10, 2025, but it has now been pushed to January 12, 2025. GSTR-7 is specifically for registered persons who are required to deduct tax at source as per section 51 of the Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) Act. This extension is made possible through the powers granted under sub-section (6) of section 39 and section 168 of the CGST Act. The notification was officially issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs on January 10, 2025. This change will benefit those registered persons who need additional time to prepare and submit their returns for the specified month, ensuring they can comply with the requirements without rushing.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-7 for December 2024 from January 10, 2025, to January 12, 2025.
GSTR-7 is the return for registered persons required to deduct tax at source under section 51 of the CGST Act.
The extension is granted under sub-section (6) of section 39 and section 168 of the CGST Act.
This notification was issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs on January 10, 2025.

10.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 06/2025-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for e-commerce operators to file their GSTR-8 for December 2024. Originally, the due date was set for earlier in January, but it has now been pushed to January 12, 2025. This change is significant for e-commerce operators who are required to report details of outward supplies made through their platforms. The extension is granted under the first proviso to sub-section (4) of section 52 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and is based on recommendations from the GST Council. The notification was officially issued on January 10, 2025, and it references Rule 67 of the CGST Rules, 2017. This adjustment provides additional time for compliance, allowing businesses to ensure accurate reporting of their transactions for the specified month.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing FORM GSTR-8 for December 2024.
New deadline for FORM GSTR-8 is now January 12, 2025.
This extension is applicable to e-commerce operators.
The extension is based on the first proviso to sub-section (4) of section 52 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The notification was issued on January 10, 2025.
The change is made under the recommendations of the GST Council.
Rule 67 of the CGST Rules, 2017 is referenced in this notification.

11.summary: The recent Notification No. 07/2025-Central Tax, issued on January 23, 2025, introduces significant amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. A new rule, 16A, has been added, allowing the proper officer to grant a temporary identification number to individuals who are not required to register but need to make payments under the GST Act. Additionally, rule 19(1) has been amended to include intimation from composition taxpayers using FORM GST CMP-02. Rule 87(4) has also been updated to reference the new rule 16A concerning the common portal. Furthermore, FORM REG-12 has been replaced with a new format that outlines the process for granting temporary registration and identification numbers. These changes will take effect from a date that will be notified later, impacting individuals and businesses that fall under these provisions, particularly those who may need temporary identification for compliance purposes.
key_points: Notification No. 07/2025-Central Tax issued on 23rd January 2025.
Introduces a new rule 16A for granting temporary identification numbers to persons not liable for registration but required to make payments.
Inserts a provision in rule 19(1) allowing intimation from composition taxpayers in FORM GST CMP-02.
Amends rule 87(4) to reference the new rule 16A regarding the common portal.
Substitutes FORM REG-12 with a new format for temporary registration and identification numbers.
The changes will come into effect from a date to be notified later.

12.summary: Notification No. 08/2025-Central Tax, issued on January 23, 2025, introduces a waiver for late fees under section 47 of the CGST Act for specific financial years, namely 2017-18 to 2022-23. This waiver is targeted at registered persons who were required to submit a reconciliation statement in FORM GSTR-9C along with their annual return in FORM GSTR-9 but failed to do so on time. To benefit from this waiver, these individuals must file FORM GSTR-9C by March 31, 2025. However, it is important to note that any late fees already paid for the delayed submission of FORM GSTR-9C will not be refunded. This change aims to provide relief to those who missed the deadline for filing their reconciliation statements, allowing them to comply without incurring additional late fees. The notification is issued under the authority of section 128 of the CGST Act, reflecting the government's ongoing efforts to ease compliance burdens for taxpayers.
key_points: Waives late fee under section 47 of the CGST Act for certain financial years.
Applies to returns for financial years 2017-18 to 2022-23.
Specifically for registered persons required to file FORM GSTR-9C along with FORM GSTR-9.
Late fee waiver is applicable only if FORM GSTR-9C is filed by 31st March 2025.
No refund of late fees already paid for delayed FORM GSTR-9C submissions.
This notification is issued under section 128 of the CGST Act.

13.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 09/2025-Central Tax on 11th February 2025, which brings into effect several rules under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. Specifically, Rules 2, 24, 27, and 32 will be effective immediately from the date of the notification. Additionally, Rules 8, 37, and clause (ii) of rule 38 will come into force starting 1st April 2025. This notification is part of the amendments made in the Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Rules, 2024, as outlined in the earlier Notification No. 12/2024-Central Tax. Businesses and taxpayers should be aware of these changes as they will impact compliance and reporting requirements under the GST framework. The provisions aim to streamline processes and enhance clarity in tax administration. It is essential for affected parties to review these rules to understand their implications fully.
key_points: Notification No. 09/2025-Central Tax issued on 11th February 2025.
Rules 2, 24, 27, and 32 come into force on 11th February 2025.
Rules 8, 37, and clause (ii) of rule 38 will take effect from 1st April 2025.
This notification is issued under section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It references the Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Rules, 2024, from notification No. 12/2024-Central Tax.

14.summary: The recent Notification No. 10/2025-Central Tax, issued on March 13, 2025, brings significant amendments to the earlier Notification No. 02/2017-Central Tax. This update specifically alters the entries in the table of districts for several regions. For instance, serial number 7 now includes various districts in Alwar, Rajasthan. Similarly, serial number 23 has been revised to reflect the Chennai Outer Districts in Tamil Nadu, which now includes several new areas. The Jaipur Districts listed under serial number 49 have also been updated to include Ajmer and Beawer. Additionally, the Jodhpur Districts under serial number 53 have been expanded to cover more areas in Rajasthan. The Madurai Districts at serial number 63 now specify exclusions for Dindigul. Furthermore, serial number 100 has been updated to include additional districts in Tiruchirapalli, Tamil Nadu, while serial number 102 now lists various districts in Udaipur, Rajasthan. These changes will affect businesses operating in these specified districts, as they may have implications for GST compliance and reporting.
key_points: Notification No. 10/2025-Central Tax was issued on 2025-03-13.
This notification amends Notification No. 02/2017-Central Tax, originally published on 2017-06-19.
Serial number 7 is updated to include Alwar Districts in Rajasthan: Alwar, Khairthal-Tijara, Kotputli-Behror, Bharatpur, Deeg, Dholpur, Dausa, Karauli, Sawaimadhopur, Sikar, and Jhunjhunu.

Serial number 23 is updated to include Chennai Outer Districts in Tamil Nadu: Viluppuram, Kallakurichi, Thiruvannamalai, Vellore, Tirupathur, Ranipet, Tiruvallur, Kanchipuram, Chengalpattu, and areas under Pallavaram and St. Thomas Mount Cantonment Boards.
Serial number 49 is updated to include Jaipur Districts in Rajasthan: Jaipur, Ajmer, Beawer, and Tonk.

Serial number 53 is updated to include Jodhpur Districts in Rajasthan: Jodhpur, Phalodi, Nagaur, Didwana-Kuchaman, Pali, Sirohi, Jalore, Barmer, Balotra, Jaisalmer, Bikaner, Churu, Ganganagar, and Hanumangarh.

Serial number 63 is updated to include Madurai Districts in Tamil Nadu: Madurai, Ramanathapuram, Sivagangai, Virudhunagar, Tuticorin, Tirunelveli, Tenkasi, Kanyakumari, Theni, Dindigul (excluding D. Gudalur Village).

Serial number 100 is updated to include Tiruchirapalli Districts in Tamil Nadu: Tiruchirappalli, Perambalur, Ariyalur, Karur, Pudukottai, Thanjavur, Thiruvarur, Nagapattinam, Mayiladuthurai, Cuddalore, and D. Gudalur village of Dindigul District.
Serial number 102 is updated to include Udaipur Districts in Rajasthan: Udaipur, Salumbar, Rajsamand, Bhilwara, Chittorgarh, Pratapgarh, Dungarpur, Banswara, Bundi, Baran, Kota, and Jhalawar.

15.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 11/2025-Central Tax on 27th March 2025, amending the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. A significant change in rule 164, sub-rule (4) now includes the phrase 'related to period mentioned in the said sub-section and' after the requirement for full tax payment. Additionally, a new Explanation clarifies that no refunds will be available for any tax, y already paid before these rules came into effect, specifically in cases where a demand includes periods both covered and not covered by the sub-section. Furthermore, a new proviso in sub-rule (7) allows applicants to notify the appellate authority if they choose not to pursue an appeal for certain periods, effectively deeming the appeal withdrawn for the period from 1st July 2017 to 31st March 2020. These amendments are effective immediately upon publication in the Official Gazette, impacting taxpayers involved in appeals related to these specified periods.
key_points: Notification No. 11/2025-Central Tax was issued on 27th March 2025.
This notification amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
An amendment to rule 164, sub-rule (4) adds the phrase 'related to period mentioned in the said sub-section and' after 'after payment of the full amount of tax'.
In rule 164, sub-rule (7), a new proviso allows applicants to inform the appellate authority if they do not wish to pursue an appeal for a specific period.
The appeal application will be deemed withdrawn for the period from 1st July 2017 to 31st March 2020 to the extent of the intimation provided.
The rules come into force on the date of their publication in the Official Gazette.

16.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 12/2025-Central Tax, which extends the due date for filing the GSTR-3B return for the month of July 2025. The new deadline is now set for August 27, 2025. This extension specifically applies to registered persons whose principal place of business is located in the districts of Mumbai (City), Mumbai (sub-urban), Thane, Raigad, and Palghar in Maharashtra. The decision to extend the due date is made under the authority of sub-section (6) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This change aims to provide relief to businesses in these districts, allowing them additional time to prepare and submit their returns. The notification was officially issued on August 20, 2025, and is part of ongoing efforts to support taxpayers in compliance with GST regulations.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-3B for July 2025.
New due date is August 27, 2025.
Applies to registered persons in Mumbai (City), Mumbai (sub-urban), Thane, Raigad, and Palghar districts.
This extension is based on the powers conferred by sub-section (6) of section 39 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The notification is issued on August 20, 2025.

17.summary: The Central Government has issued the 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Third Amendment) Rules, 2025', which will take effect on 22nd September 2025. A significant change is in rule 31A, where the figure '128' is now '140', impacting how certain calculations are made. Additionally, rule 39 has been updated to incorporate provisions from the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, effective from 1st April 2025. Starting 1st October 2025, rule 91 will alter the refund order process, requiring the proper officer to issue an order in FORM GST RFD-04 within seven days. A new rule, 110A, has been introduced to streamline appeals to a single Member Bench, allowing for quicker resolutions. Furthermore, rules 110 and 111 have been revised to update the procedures related to FORM GST APL-02A. Lastly, FORM GSTR-9 has undergone several amendments, including new entries for Input Tax Credit (ITC) and tax details, which will affect how businesses report their GST information for the financial year.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Third Amendment) Rules, 2025'.
These rules will come into effect on 22nd September 2025.
In rule 31A, the figure '128' is replaced with '140'.
Rule 39 is amended to include provisions from the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, effective from 1st April 2025.
Rule 91 is amended to change the order process for refunds, effective from 1st October 2025.
New rule 110A introduces procedures for appeals to be heard by a single Member Bench.
Changes in rules 110 and 111 include updates to FORM GST APL-02A.
FORM GSTR-9 has multiple amendments, including new entries for ITC and tax details.

18.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 14/2025-Central Tax, which will come into effect on 1st October 2025. This notification specifies that certain registered persons will not be allowed to claim refunds on a provisional basis under the GST Act. Specifically, individuals who have not completed Aadhaar authentication as required by rule 10B will be ineligible for refunds. Additionally, this restriction applies to those engaged in the supply of specific goods, including Areca nuts, Pan masala, Tobacco and manufactured tobacco substitutes, and Essential oils. The notification clarifies that terms such as 'tariff item', 'heading', 'sub-heading', and 'Chapter' will be interpreted according to the First Schedule of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975. This change primarily affects businesses dealing in the specified goods and those who have not completed the Aadhaar authentication process, impacting their ability to receive refunds under the GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 14/2025-Central Tax issued on 17th September 2025.
Effective date for the notification is 1st October 2025.
Registered persons not allowed refund on provisional basis if they haven't undergone Aadhaar authentication as per rule 10B.
Refunds are also restricted for those supplying specific goods listed in the notification.
Goods affected include Areca nuts (0802 80), Pan masala (2106 90 20), Tobacco and manufactured tobacco substitutes (24), and Essential oils (3301).
The interpretation of terms follows the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975.

19.summary: The recent Notification No. 15/2025-Central Tax, issued on 17th September 2025, introduces an important change regarding the filing of annual returns under the Goods and Services Tax (GST) framework. Specifically, it exempts registered persons whose aggregate turnover does not exceed two crore rupees from the requirement to file an annual return for the financial year 2024-25 and onwards. This exemption is granted under the first proviso to sub-section (1) of section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. As a result, small businesses and individuals with lower turnover will benefit from reduced compliance burdens, allowing them to focus more on their operations rather than paperwork. This change is significant as it simplifies the GST process for a large segment of taxpayers, making it easier for them to manage their tax obligations without the annual return filing requirement.
key_points: Exemption from filing annual return for financial year 2024-25 onwards.
Applicable to registered persons with aggregate turnover up to two crore rupees.
Notification issued on 17th September 2025.
Based on the first proviso to sub-section (1) of section 44 of the CGST Act, 2017.

20.summary: Notification No. 16/2025-Central Tax, issued on 17th September 2025, announces that several provisions of the Finance Act, 2025 will come into effect on 1st October 2025. Specifically, clauses (ii) and (iii) of section 121, as well as sections 122 to 124 and sections 126 to 134, will be applicable from this date. This means that businesses and individuals affected by these sections should prepare for the changes that will be implemented. The notification is part of the government's ongoing efforts to update and clarify tax regulations under the GST framework. The Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs is responsible for this notification, indicating the importance of these provisions in the broader context of GST compliance and administration. Stakeholders should review these sections to understand their implications and ensure they are ready for the upcoming changes.
key_points: The notification is issued under Notification No. 16/2025-Central Tax.
It is dated 17th September 2025.
The provisions of clauses (ii) and (iii) of section 121 will come into force on 1st October 2025.
Sections 122 to 124 of the Finance Act, 2025 will also be effective from 1st October 2025.
Sections 126 to 134 of the Finance Act, 2025 will be applicable starting from 1st October 2025.
This notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.

21.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 17/2025-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for filing the GSTR-3B return for September 2025 and the quarterly return for July to September 2025. Both deadlines have been moved to October 25, 2025. This change affects all registered persons who are required to submit these returns electronically through the common portal. The extension is granted under the authority of section 39(6) and section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This move aims to provide additional time for businesses to prepare and file their returns, ensuring compliance with GST regulations. It is important for businesses to take note of this new deadline and plan their filing accordingly.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-3B for September 2025 from the original date to October 25, 2025.
Extends the due date for filing GSTR-3B for the quarter of July 2025 to September 2025 to October 25, 2025.
This extension applies to all registered persons under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The notification is issued under the powers of section 39(6) and section 168 of the CGST Act.

22.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 18/2025-Central Tax, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, effective from 1st November 2025. A significant change is the introduction of rule 9A, which allows for electronic registration within three working days for applicants identified based on data analysis. Additionally, rule 14A provides an option for taxpayers whose monthly output tax liability does not exceed ₹2,50,000 to register electronically, streamlining the process for smaller businesses. To facilitate withdrawals from this option, Form GST REG-32 has been introduced, detailing the requirements for application and conditions for withdrawal. The amendment also updates several existing forms, including GST REG-01 and REG-05, to reflect these new rules and processes. Importantly, Aadhaar authentication is now mandatory for those opting for registration under rule 14A, ensuring a more secure and efficient registration process. These changes aim to simplify compliance for smaller taxpayers and enhance the overall efficiency of the GST registration system.
key_points: The notification is titled Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourth Amendment) Rules, 2025.
It comes into effect on 1st November 2025.
A new rule 9A is added, allowing electronic registration within three working days for applicants identified through data analysis.
Rule 14A is introduced, providing an option for taxpayers with monthly output tax liability below ₹2,50,000 to register electronically.
Form GST REG-32 is introduced for registered persons to apply for withdrawal from the option under rule 14A.
Changes are made to Forms GST REG-01, REG-02, REG-03, REG-04, REG-05, and REG-33 to incorporate new rules and processes.
The notification specifies that Aadhaar authentication is mandatory for registration under rule 14A.
The provisions for withdrawal applications under rule 14A include conditions for filing and processing timelines.

23.summary: The recent Notification No. 01/2025-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 16th January 2025, introduces several important changes to the GST framework. Notably, it adds a new entry for 'Fortified Rice Kernel (FRK)' under Schedule I, which will be taxed at a rate of 2.5%. Additionally, this notification amends Schedule III to include 'Fortified Rice Kernel (FRK)' alongside 'Murki', which will be taxed at a rate of 9%. Furthermore, the definition of 'pre-packaged and labelled' commodities has been updated to specify that these are items intended for retail sale that do not exceed 25 kg or 25 litres. This change aims to clarify the requirements for packaging and labeling under the Legal Metrology Act. The notification takes effect immediately, impacting businesses involved in the sale of these products. This amendment is part of ongoing updates to the GST framework, following the last change made by notification No. 5/2024 on 8th October 2024, which had previously amended the original notification from 28th June 2017.
key_points: Notification No. 01/2025-Central Tax (Rate) issued on 16th January 2025.
Inserts new entry 98B for 'Fortified Rice Kernel (FRK)' under Schedule I at 2.5%.

24.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 01/2025-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 16th January 2025, which introduces several amendments to the existing tax rates. A new entry for 'Fortified Rice Kernel (FRK)' has been added as S. No. 98B under Schedule I, which will be taxed at a rate of 5%. Additionally, 'Fortified Rice Kernel (FRK)' is now included in Schedule III, listed after 'commonly known as Murki', and will be taxed at 18%. The notification also revises the definition of 'pre-packaged and labelled' commodities, clarifying that it refers to items intended for retail sale that do not exceed 25 kg or 25 liters. This change aims to with the Legal Metrology Act, 2009. Importantly, this notification takes effect immediately, impacting businesses involved in the sale of these products. The principal notification was previously amended by Notification No. 5/2024 on 8th October 2024, and these changes reflect ongoing adjustments in the GST framework.

29.summary: On January 16, 2025, the Government of India issued Notification No. 04/2025-Central Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 8/2018-Central Tax (Rate) from January 25, 2018. This change means that businesses and individuals who fall under this category will now be required to pay a higher tax rate of 9% instead of the previous 6%. 31.summary: On 31st January 2025, a corrigenda was issued to amend Notification No. 05/2025-Integrated Tax (Rate) which was originally published on 16th January 2025. 32.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 05/2025-Central Tax (Rate) on 16th January 2025, which amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This amendment omits clause (xxxv) and substitutes clause (xxxvi) to redefine 'Specified premises' for hotel accommodation services. Under the new definition, a specified premises must either provide accommodation valued above ₹7,500 per unit per day or have a declaration filed by a registered person between 1st January and 31st March of the preceding financial year. Additionally, three new annexures have been introduced to facilitate the declaration process for both registered persons and those applying for registration. These declarations must be filed separately for each premises and within the specified timeframes. The changes will come into effect on 1st April 2025, impacting hotel accommodation service providers who need to comply with the new declaration requirements.

33.summary: The recent Notification No. 05/2025-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on January 16, 2025, amends the earlier Notification No. 08/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate). 34.summary: The recent Notification No. 06/2025-Central Tax (Rate), issued on January 16, 2025, amends the earlier notification 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate). One significant change is the modification of the phrase in column (3) against serial number 25A, changing 'transmission and distribution' to 'transmission or distribution'. Additionally, a new entry, serial number 36B, has been added to include services of insurance provided by the Motor Vehicle Accident Fund, which will be taxed at Nil. Furthermore, under serial number 69, a new item (f) has been introduced to recognize training partners approved by the National Skill Development Corporation. Notably, item (w) will be omitted from paragraph 2 effective April 1, 2025, while a new definition for 'insurer' has been added as item (zja), aligning it with the Insurance Act, 1938. These changes will impact businesses involved in insurance services and training partnerships, clarifying tax obligations and definitions in the GST framework.

42.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 09/2025-Central Tax (Rate) on January 1, 2025, which introduces new central tax rates for various goods. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 01/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated June 28, 2017. The new tax rates are set at 2.5% for goods listed in Schedule I, 9% for Schedule II, 20% for Schedule III, 1.5% for Schedule IV, 0.125% for Schedule V, 0.75% for Schedule VI, and 14% for Schedule VII. These rates will apply to intra-State supplies of goods, and the notification provides detailed descriptions of the goods covered under each tax rate in the respective Schedules. Businesses involved in the supply of these goods will need to refer to the new Schedules to determine the applicable tax rates for their products. This change aims to streamline the tax structure and ensure clarity in the taxation of goods under the GST framework.

The new rates apply to intra-State supplies of goods as specified in the corresponding entries of the Schedules.
The notification includes detailed descriptions of goods under each tax rate in the respective Schedules.

These amendments affect the integrated tax rates applicable to specific goods listed in the original notification.

46.summary: The recent Notification No. 10/2025-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 17th September 2025, introduces significant changes by exempting certain intra-State supplies of goods from the entire central tax under section 9 of the CGST Act. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 02/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The exemption covers a wide range of goods, including live animals like poultry and bovine, fresh or chilled meat, fish, dairy products, various fruits and vegetables, and specific agricultural products. Notably, the exemption applies to goods that are not pre-packaged and labelled, which is a crucial detail for suppliers. This change is particularly beneficial for businesses dealing in these goods, as they will no longer need to charge central tax on these items, easing their operational costs. Overall, this notification aims to support the agricultural and food sectors by reducing tax burdens on essential goods.

The exemption is applicable to goods that are not pre-packaged and labelled, as specified in the entries.
This change affects suppliers of the listed goods, allowing them to operate without the burden of central tax on these items.

47.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 10/2025-Integrated Tax (Rate) on September 17, 2025, which supersedes the earlier Notification No. 02/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from June 28, 2017. This new notification exempts inter-State supplies of a wide range of goods from the entire integrated tax as per section 5 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act. The goods listed in the Schedule include live animals such as poultry and bovine, fresh or chilled meat, various fish, dairy products like fresh milk and curd, and a variety of fruits and vegetables. Notably, the exemption applies to goods that are not pre-packaged and labelled, which means that many agricultural and food products can be supplied without incurring integrated tax. This change is aimed at supporting the agricultural sector and is based on recommendations from the GST Council, reflecting the government's commitment to public interest. Businesses involved in the supply of these goods will benefit from this exemption, potentially reducing their tax burden significantly.

48.summary: The recently issued Notification No. 11/2025-Central Tax (Rate) on 17th September 2025 introduces a significant change to the GST rates. No. 1 in the table of the original notification, changing the rate to 9%. This new rate will be applicable starting from 22nd September 2025. Businesses and taxpayers who fall under this category should prepare for this adjustment in their tax calculations. The change reflects the government's ongoing efforts to align tax rates with current economic conditions and public interest. It is crucial for affected parties to review their pricing and compliance strategies in light of this new rate to ensure they are aligned with the updated regulations.

49.summary: On 17th September 2025, the Government of India issued Notification No. 11/2025-Integrated Tax (Rate), which amends the previous Notification No. 3/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Specifically, this amendment changes the entry for S. No. 1 in the table, updating the tax rate to 18%. 50.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 12/2025-Central Tax (Rate) on 17th September 2025, which amends the earlier Notification No. 8/2018-Central Tax (Rate) from 25th January 2018. The key change involves replacing the reference to 'Schedule IV of Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate)' with 'Schedule II or Schedule III of Notification No. 9/2025-Central Tax (Rate)'. This amendment is made in the interest of public welfare and follows recommendations from the GST Council. The new provisions will take effect from 22nd September 2025. Businesses and taxpayers who refer to these schedules for their tax calculations or compliance will need to update their records and processes accordingly to align with the new schedules. This change is significant for those involved in the sectors impacted by the specified schedules, ensuring they are aware of the updated tax rates and classifications.

52.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 13/2025-Central Tax (Rate) on 17th September 2025, which amends the earlier Notification No. 21/2018-Central Tax (Rate) from 26th July 2018. This amendment introduces a new table listing various goods along with their applicable GST rates, primarily set at 2.5%. Notable items included in this list are handcrafted candles, handbags, carved wood products, and handmade carpets. Additionally, certain products like handmade imitation jewellery and silver filigree work will now be taxed at a lower rate of 1.5%. These changes are significant for manufacturers and sellers of these goods, as they will need to adjust their pricing and tax calculations accordingly. The new rates will take effect from 22nd September 2025. This notification follows the previous amendment made on 28th December 2021, ensuring that the tax structure remains updated to reflect current market conditions.

53.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 13/2025-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 17th September 2025, which amends the earlier Notification No. 22/2018-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 26th July 2018. Notable items included in this list are handcrafted candles, handbags, carved wood products, and handmade carpets. Additionally, some items like handmade imitation jewellery and silver filigree work have a lower tax rate of 3%. This change aims to streamline the tax structure for these goods and is set to take effect on 22nd September 2025. The previous amendment to the original notification was made through Notification No. 20/2021 on 28th December 2021, indicating ongoing adjustments to the GST framework for better clarity and compliance.

54.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 14/2025-Central Tax (Rate) on 17th September 2025, which introduces a new central tax rate of 6% on certain goods. This rate applies to intra-State supplies of specific items listed in the accompanying schedule. The goods affected by this notification include fly ash bricks, bricks made from fossil meals, building bricks, and earthen or roofing tiles. Each of these items is identified by its respective tariff item and description in the schedule. This change is significant for manufacturers and suppliers of these goods, as they will now need to apply the new tax rate in their transactions. The notification will take effect from 22nd September 2025, giving businesses a short window to adjust their pricing and tax calculations accordingly. This update is part of the government's ongoing efforts to streamline the GST framework and ensure clarity in tax rates for various goods.

55.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 14/2025-Integrated Tax (Rate) on September 17, 2025, which introduces a new integrated tax rate of 12% for certain goods. This rate will apply to inter-State supplies of goods such as fly ash bricks, bricks made from fossil meals, building bricks, and earthen or roofing tiles, as detailed in the accompanying Schedule. The notification specifies the tariff items and headings under which these goods fall, in accordance with the Customs Tariff Act, 1975. Importantly, this new tax rate will take effect from September 22, 2025. Businesses dealing with these specific goods should be aware of this change, as it directly impacts the tax they will need to charge on inter-State transactions. The notification also clarifies that terms used are defined in the relevant GST Acts, ensuring consistency in interpretation. This update is significant for manufacturers and suppliers of the listed goods, as it alters their tax obligations starting later this month.

56.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 15/2025-Central Tax (Rate) on 17th September 2025, which introduces several amendments to the existing tax rates under Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate). These changes will take effect from 22nd September 2025. Notably, items under serial number 3, specifically (vii), (viii), and (x), will now be taxed at a rate of 9%. Additionally, for serial number 7, item (i) will have a tax rate of 2.5%, accompanied by a new proviso that restricts input tax credit claims. Serial number 8 sees changes as well, with item (v) now taxed at 9% and item (vi) at 6%. Furthermore, serial number 9 introduces a new tax rate of 2.5% for item (iv), along with conditions regarding input tax credit. Postal and courier services under serial number 12 will now also be taxed at 9%. Lastly, job work services related to diamonds under serial number 26 will have a reduced tax rate of 0.75%. These changes will impact various sectors, particularly those involved in transportation, job work, and delivery services.

57.summary: The recent Notification No. 15/2025-Integrated Tax (Rate), effective from September 22, 2025, introduces several key changes to the GST rates outlined in the earlier Notification No. 8/2017. Specifically, the GST rates for items (vii), (viii), and (x) under serial number 3 have been increased to 18%. Additionally, for serial number 7, item (i) now has a GST rate of 5%, accompanied by a new proviso that restricts input tax credit under certain conditions. Similarly, items (v) and (vi) under serial number 8 will also see their rates rise to 18%. For serial number 9, item (iv) is now taxed at 5%, with a new proviso regarding input tax credit. Furthermore, the rate for item (i) under serial number 10 has been set to 18%, with a new proviso as well. The notification also standardizes the GST rate for postal and courier services under serial number 12 to 18%. Lastly, it clarifies definitions related to 'goods transport agency' and other transportation terms, ensuring better understanding and compliance for businesses involved in these sectors.

58.summary: Notification No. 16/2025-Central Tax (Rate), issued on September 17, 2025, amends the earlier Notification No. 06/2025-Central Tax (Rate). This new notification introduces three entries—36C, 36D, and 36E—related to life insurance, health insurance, and reinsurance services, which are exempt from tax when provided to individuals or families, rather than groups. Additionally, an explanation has been added to serial number 18, clarifying that local delivery services provided by Electronic Commerce Operators are excluded from this entry. The definition of 'goods transport agency' has also been revised to exclude electronic commerce operators involved in local delivery services. Furthermore, a new clause defines 'group' for insurance purposes, differentiating between employer-employee and non-employer-employee groups. These changes will take effect from September 22, 2025, impacting insurers and electronic commerce operators involved in these services.

60.summary: On 17th September 2025, the Government of India issued Notification No. 17/2025-Central Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 17/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The key change is the introduction of a new clause (v) that specifies services related to local delivery will be exempt from GST, except in cases where the service provider is required to register under section 22(1) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. This means that if a person providing local delivery services is not liable for registration, they will not have to charge GST on those services. However, if they are liable for registration, they must comply with GST regulations. This amendment will come into effect on 22nd September 2025, impacting businesses involved in local delivery services and their GST obligations.

61.summary: The recently issued Notification No. 17/2025-Integrated Tax (Rate) introduces an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 14/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate). Specifically, it adds a new clause (v) that includes services related to local delivery. This inclusion is significant as it clarifies that these services will be subject to the same tax treatment unless the service provider is required to register under specific provisions of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax (IGST) Act. This means that electronic commerce operators will be responsible for the tax on these local delivery services, similar to how they handle other specified services. The amendment will come into effect on September 22, 2025, impacting businesses involved in local delivery services. Companies providing such services should be aware of this change to with the updated tax obligations. Overall, this amendment aims to streamline the tax process for local delivery services within the framework of the IGST Act.

The changes will take effect from 2025-09-22.

64.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 01/2025-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 16th January 2025, which introduces several amendments to the existing tax framework. A new entry for 'Fortified Rice Kernel (FRK)' has been added under Schedule I at S. No. 98B, which will be taxed at a rate of 2.5%. Additionally, Schedule III has been amended to include 'Fortified Rice Kernel (FRK)' after 'commonly known as Murki' at S. No. 15, with a tax rate of 9%. Furthermore, the definition of 'pre-packaged and labelled' commodities has been updated to clarify that such items must not exceed 25 kg or 25 litres and must adhere to the requirements set forth in the Legal Metrology Act, 2009. These changes are effective immediately, impacting businesses dealing with these specific goods and requiring them to adjust their tax calculations accordingly.

65.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 02/2025-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 16th January 2025, which amends the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Importantly, this notification takes effect immediately, impacting businesses involved in the sale of gene therapy products and those dealing with pre-packaged goods. Companies should review these changes to with the updated definitions and tax entries.

66.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 03/2025-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 16th January 2025, which amends the earlier Notification No. 39/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 18th October 2017. 68.summary: The recent Notification No. 05/2025, issued on January 16, 2025, amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax rates. This amendment will take effect from April 1, 2025. Notably, clause (xxxv) has been removed, while clause (xxxvi) has been revised to clarify the definition of 'Specified premises' for hotel accommodation services. Now, a 'Specified premises' is defined as one that provided hotel accommodation services valued over ₹7,500 per unit per day in the previous financial year. Additionally, registered persons can declare their premises as 'specified' between January 1 and March 31 of the preceding financial year. The amendment also introduces three new annexures: Annexure VII for registered persons to opt-in, Annexure VIII for those applying for registration, and Annexure IX for opting out of the 'specified premises' designation. Each declaration must be filed separately for each premises, ensuring clarity in compliance for hotel service providers.

69.summary: The recent Notification No. 06/2025-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on January 16, 2025, brings several amendments to the earlier Notification No. 12/2017. Additionally, a new entry under serial number 36B introduces services of insurance provided by the Motor Vehicle Accident Fund, which will be exempt from tax. Furthermore, the entry under serial number 69 now includes training partners approved by the National Skill Development Corporation. Importantly, item (w) in paragraph 2 of the parent notification is removed, effective April 1, 2025, while a new definition for 'insurer' is added as item (zja), aligning it with the Insurance Act, 1938. These changes primarily affect service providers in the insurance sector and those involved in training partnerships, ensuring clarity and updated definitions in the Union Territory tax framework.
