185.summary: Notification No. 26/2023-Central Tax, issued on 17th July 2023, brings an important change to the earlier Notification No. 08/2023-Central Tax, which was published on 31st March 2023. Specifically, this new notification extends the due date for a certain compliance requirement from 30th June 2023 to 31st August 2023. This change is significant for businesses and taxpayers who were originally expected to meet their obligations by the end of June. The amendment is retroactively effective from 30th June 2023, meaning that the new deadline applies as if it had always been in place since that date. This extension provides additional time for compliance, which can be beneficial for businesses managing their GST filings. It is essential for affected parties to take note of this new deadline to ensure they are aligned with the updated requirements.
key_points: Notification No. 26/2023-Central Tax was issued on 17th July 2023.
This notification amends Notification No. 08/2023-Central Tax dated 31st March 2023.
The amendment changes the due date from 30th June 2023 to 31st August 2023.
The amendment is effective from 30th June 2023.

186.summary: Notification No. 27/2023-Central Tax, issued on 31st July 2023, announces that section 123 of the Finance Act, 2021 will come into effect on 1st October 2023. This section pertains to specific provisions that are likely to impact various stakeholders in the GST framework. The notification is issued under the authority of the Central Government, specifically the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs. Business owners and GST practitioners should be aware that this change will take place soon, and they may need to adjust their compliance and operational processes accordingly. The exact implications of section 123 will depend on its content, which may introduce new requirements or modify existing ones. Therefore, it is essential for those affected to review the provisions of the Finance Act, 2021 to understand how this change will impact their GST obligations and operations.
key_points: The notification is issued on 31st July 2023.
It appoints 1st October 2023 as the date when section 123 of the Finance Act, 2021 will come into force.
The notification is issued under the powers conferred by clause (b) of sub-section (2) of section 1 of the Finance Act, 2021.
The notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.

187.summary: Notification No. 28/2023-Central Tax, issued on 31st July 2023, outlines the effective dates for certain provisions of the Finance Act, 2023. Specifically, sections 149 to 154 will take effect from 1st August 2023, while sections 137 to 162, excluding sections 149 to 154, will come into force on 1st October 2023. This means that businesses and individuals should be aware that the provisions under sections 149 to 154 will be applicable starting next month, while the broader set of sections from 137 to 162 will be relevant from October. This staggered implementation allows for a phased approach to the new regulations introduced in the Finance Act, which may impact compliance and operational processes for various stakeholders. It is essential for affected parties to review these sections to understand their implications and prepare accordingly.
key_points: Sections 137 to 162 of the Finance Act, 2023 will come into force on 1st October 2023.
Sections 149 to 154 of the Finance Act, 2023 will come into force on 1st August 2023.
The notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.
The notification is dated 31st July 2023.

188.summary: Notification No. 29/2023-Central Tax, issued on 31st July 2023, establishes a new procedure for registered persons or officers wishing to appeal orders under sections 73 or 74 of the CGST Act. This notification allows appeals to be filed in duplicate using the specified form in ANNEXURE-1, which must be submitted manually to the Appellate Authority. The time frame for filing these appeals will be calculated from either the date of this notification or the date of the order being appealed, whichever is later. Importantly, appellants are not required to make any pre-deposit as a condition for filing their appeal, as stated in subsection (6) of section 107. Once the appeal is submitted and meets all requirements, an acknowledgment will be issued in FORM GST APL-02. Additionally, the Appellate Authority is mandated to provide a summary of the order in the format outlined in ANNEXURE-2. This notification is particularly relevant for those involved in disputes regarding transitional credit claims, following the Supreme Court's directions in the case of Union of India v/s Filco Trade Centre Pvt. Ltd.
key_points: Notification No. 29/2023-Central Tax issued on 31st July 2023.
Introduces a special procedure for filing appeals against orders under sections 73 or 74 of the CGST Act.
Appeals must be filed in duplicate using the form in ANNEXURE-1 and presented manually to the Appellate Authority.
Time for filing appeals is computed from the date of this notification or the date of the order, whichever is later.
No pre-deposit is required for filing an appeal as per subsection (6) of section 107.
Acknowledgement of the appeal will be issued in FORM GST APL-02 upon fulfilling all requirements.
The Appellate Authority will provide a summary of the order in the form in ANNEXURE-2.

189.summary: Notification No. 30/2023-Central Tax, issued on July 31, 2023, introduces a special procedure for registered persons engaged in manufacturing specific goods. These manufacturers are required to submit details of their packing machines using FORM SRM-I within 30 days of the notification. For those who register after this date, the deadline is shortened to 15 days. Additionally, any new packing machines installed must be reported within 24 hours using FORM SRM-IIA, while existing machines that are removed must also be reported within 24 hours via FORM SRM-IIB. Manufacturers are now mandated to keep daily records of inputs and waste in FORM SRM-IIIA, along with machine-wise production details in FORM SRM-IIIB. Furthermore, a special monthly statement must be submitted in FORM SRM-IV by the 10th of the following month. This notification aims to enhance transparency and tracking in the manufacturing sector, affecting all registered manufacturers of the specified goods.
key_points: Registered persons manufacturing specified goods must submit details of packing machines in FORM SRM-I within 30 days of this notification.
New manufacturers registered after this notification must submit packing machine details in FORM SRM-I within 15 days of registration.
Details of additional packing machines installed must be submitted within 24 hours in FORM SRM-IIA.
Existing machines removed must be reported within 24 hours in FORM SRM-IIB.
Manufacturers must maintain daily records of inputs and waste in FORM SRM-IIIA and machine-wise production in FORM SRM-IIIB.
A special monthly statement in FORM SRM-IV must be submitted by the 10th of the following month.
The notification is effective from the date of issuance, July 31, 2023.

190.summary: Notification No. 31/2023-Central Tax, issued on 31st July 2023, introduces an amendment to Notification No. 27/2022-Central Tax, which was originally published on 26th December 2022. The key change in this amendment is the addition of the words 'and the State of Puducherry' following 'State of Gujarat'. This change expands the applicability of the provisions in the earlier notification to include Puducherry alongside Gujarat. The amendment is made under the authority of sub-rule (4B) of rule 8 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This notification follows the previous amendment made by Notification No. 05/2023-Central Tax on 31st March 2023, which had already modified aspects of the original notification. Businesses operating in both Gujarat and Puducherry should take note of this change as it may affect their compliance and reporting obligations under GST.
key_points: Notification No. 31/2023-Central Tax was issued on 31st July 2023.
This notification amends Notification No. 27/2022-Central Tax dated 26th December 2022.
The amendment adds the words 'and the State of Puducherry' after 'State of Gujarat' in the earlier notification.
The amendment is made under the powers conferred by sub-rule (4B) of rule 8 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The principal Notification No. 27/2022 was last amended by Notification No. 05/2023-Central Tax on 31st March 2023.

191.summary: Notification No. 32/2023-Central Tax, issued on July 31, 2023, provides an important update for registered persons under the GST regime. It exempts those whose aggregate turnover for the financial year 2022-23 is up to two crore rupees from the requirement to file their annual return for that year. This change is made under the first proviso to section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. As a result, small businesses and taxpayers with lower turnover will benefit from reduced compliance burdens, allowing them to focus more on their operations rather than paperwork. This exemption is particularly significant for those who may find the annual return process cumbersome or challenging. Overall, this notification aims to simplify the GST filing process for smaller entities, reflecting the government's ongoing efforts to ease the compliance framework for businesses with lower turnover.
key_points: Exempts registered persons with an aggregate turnover up to two crore rupees from filing the annual return for the financial year 2022-23.
This exemption is granted under the first proviso to section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The notification was issued on 31st July 2023.
This applies specifically to the financial year 2022-23.

192.summary: Notification No. 33/2023-Central Tax, issued on 31st July 2023, introduces a significant change regarding information sharing under the Goods and Services Tax (GST) framework. Starting from 1st October 2023, the term 'Account Aggregator' is officially recognized as a system through which information can be shared via the common portal, based on user consent. This is in accordance with Section 158A of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. An 'Account Aggregator' is defined as a non-financial banking company that operates under the guidelines set by the Reserve Bank of India, specifically those outlined in the Non-Banking Financial Company - Account Aggregator (Reserve Bank) Directions, 2016. This change is aimed at enhancing the efficiency of information sharing in the GST ecosystem, allowing for better compliance and data management. The notification is issued under the authority of both the CGST and IGST Acts, marking a step forward in the integration of financial services with GST processes.
key_points: Notification No. 33/2023-Central Tax issued on 31st July 2023.
Effective date of the notification is 1st October 2023.
Introduces 'Account Aggregator' as a system for information sharing under Section 158A of the CGST Act.
Defines 'Account Aggregator' as a non-financial banking company following RBI guidelines.
The definition of 'Account Aggregator' is based on the Non-Banking Financial Company - Account Aggregator (Reserve Bank) Directions, 2016.
This notification is issued under the powers of Section 158A of the CGST Act and Section 20 of the IGST Act.

193.summary: Notification No. 34/2023-Central Tax, issued on 31st July 2023, introduces new provisions effective from 1st October 2023, exempting certain suppliers from the requirement to obtain GST registration. This exemption applies to suppliers making goods available through an electronic commerce operator, provided their aggregate turnover does not exceed the threshold for registration. However, these suppliers must adhere to specific conditions: they cannot engage in inter-State supplies, and they are limited to operating in only one State or Union territory. Additionally, they must possess a Permanent Account Number (PAN) from the Income Tax Act, which they need to declare on the common portal along with their business address before making any supplies. Upon successful validation of their PAN, they will receive an enrolment number, which is mandatory for making supplies. If they later obtain formal GST registration, this enrolment number will become invalid from the date of registration.
key_points: Notification No. 34/2023-Central Tax issued on 31st July 2023.
Effective date of the notification is 1st October 2023.
Exempts certain suppliers from obtaining GST registration if their aggregate turnover does not exceed the registration threshold.
Suppliers must not make inter-State supplies of goods.
Suppliers can only make supplies through an electronic commerce operator in one State or Union territory.
Suppliers must have a Permanent Account Number (PAN) issued under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
Suppliers must declare their PAN and business address on the common portal before making supplies.
Suppliers will receive an enrolment number on the common portal after successful validation of their PAN.

194.summary: Notification No. 35/2023-Central Tax, issued on 31st July 2023, designates specific officers to handle the adjudication of notices related to BSH Household Appliances Manufacturing Pvt. Ltd. This company has multiple locations, including Mumbai, Chennai, and Bengaluru. The notification specifies that the Superintendent of Division-X, CGST and Central Excise Mumbai East, along with the Additional Commissioner of CGST and Central Excise Chennai North, will oversee the adjudication process. The relevant notices include 03/CGST/ME/Div-X/Supdt/BSH/2022-23 dated 16.03.2023, 02/2023-GST CH.N (ADC) dated 27.03.2023, and 58/2022-23 dated 03.03.2023. The authority to adjudicate these notices has now been transferred to the Joint or Additional Commissioner of Central Tax, Bengaluru South Central Excise and GST Commissionerate. This change is significant for BSH Household Appliances Manufacturing Pvt. Ltd. as it affects how their notices will be processed and adjudicated moving forward.
key_points: Notification No. 35/2023-Central Tax was issued on 31st July 2023.
The notification appoints specific officers to adjudicate notices related to BSH Household Appliances Manufacturing Pvt. Ltd.
The noticees include BSH Household Appliances Manufacturing Pvt. Ltd. with addresses in Mumbai, Chennai, and Bengaluru.
The adjudicating authorities include the Superintendent of Division-X, CGST and Central Excise Mumbai East, and the Additional Commissioner of CGST and Central Excise Chennai North.
The notice numbers and dates for adjudication include 03/CGST/ME/Div-X/Supdt/BSH/2022-23 dated 16.03.2023, 02/2023-GST CH.N (ADC) dated 27.03.2023, and 58/2022-23 dated 03.03.2023.
The authority to adjudicate these notices has been transferred to the Joint or Additional Commissioner of Central Tax, Bengaluru South Central Excise and GST Commissionerate.

195.summary: Notification No. 36/2023-Central Tax, issued on 4th August 2023, introduces new requirements for electronic commerce operators effective from 1st October 2023. These operators will not be permitted to facilitate inter-State supply of goods for persons who are paying tax under section 10 of the GST Act. Additionally, they are mandated to collect tax at source as specified under section 52 and ensure that this tax is paid to the Government according to the provisions outlined in section 52(3). Furthermore, electronic commerce operators must report the details of these transactions in FORM GSTR-8 through the common portal. This notification aims to streamline the tax collection process for e-commerce transactions and among operators, thereby impacting how they manage inter-State transactions and tax reporting moving forward.
key_points: Notification No. 36/2023-Central Tax issued on 4th August 2023.
Effective from 1st October 2023.
Electronic commerce operators must not allow inter-State supply of goods by persons paying tax under section 10.
Operators are required to collect tax at source under section 52 for these supplies.
Tax collected must be paid to the Government as per section 52(3).
Operators must furnish details of these supplies in FORM GSTR-8 electronically.

196.summary: Notification No. 37/2023-Central Tax, issued on August 4, 2023, amends the earlier Notification No. 34/2023-Central Tax from July 31, 2023. This new notification outlines specific procedures for electronic commerce operators regarding the supply of goods by persons exempt from obtaining GST registration. Starting October 1, 2023, electronic commerce operators can only allow such supplies if the supplier has been assigned an enrolment number on the common portal. Additionally, these operators are prohibited from facilitating any inter-State supplies by these exempted persons. They are also not required to collect tax at source for these transactions. Furthermore, electronic commerce operators must report the details of these supplies in FORM GSTR-8. This change primarily affects electronic commerce operators and suppliers who fall under the exemption criteria, ensuring compliance with the new procedural requirements.
key_points: Notification No. 37/2023-Central Tax issued on 2023-08-04.
This notification amends Notification No. 34/2023-Central Tax dated 2023-07-31.
It specifies that electronic commerce operators must allow supply of goods only if the supplier has an enrolment number.
Inter-State supply of goods by exempted persons through electronic commerce operators is prohibited.
Electronic commerce operators are not required to collect tax at source for supplies made by exempted persons.
Details of supplies made by exempted persons must be reported in FORM GSTR-8.
The changes will take effect from 2023-10-01.

197.summary: The Central Government has issued the 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Second Amendment) Rules, 2023', effective from 4th August 2023. A significant change is the removal of the requirement for the presence of the registered person during certain processes in rule 9. Additionally, rule 10A mandates that registered persons must submit their bank account details within 30 days of registration or before filing GSTR-1. The amendment to rule 21A allows for the suspension of registration if there are significant discrepancies in returns, with a 30-day notice period for the registered person to explain. Furthermore, rule 23 extends the time to appeal against registration cancellation from 30 to 90 days, with a possible extension of up to 180 days. Rule 25 now requires physical verification of business premises, with reports to be submitted within 15 working days. The value of exempt supplies has been clarified to include goods from Duty Free Shops at international airports under rule 43. Lastly, a new rule 88D has been introduced to manage discrepancies in input tax credit between GSTR-3B and GSTR-2B, ensuring registered persons are notified of any differences and required to respond accordingly.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Second Amendment) Rules, 2023' and comes into force on 4th August 2023.
In rule 9(1), the phrase 'in the presence of the said person' has been removed.
Rule 10A now requires registered persons to provide bank account details within 30 days of registration or before filing GSTR-1, whichever is earlier.
Rule 21A(2A) has been amended to allow registration suspension for significant discrepancies in returns or non-compliance with rule 10A, with a 30-day notice for explanation.
Rule 23(1) changes the timeline for filing appeals against cancellation of registration from 30 days to 90 days, with possible extensions up to 180 days.
Rule 25 introduces a requirement for physical verification of business premises, with reports to be uploaded in FORM GST REG-30 within 15 working days.
Rule 43 now specifies that the value of exempt supplies includes goods from Duty Free Shops at international airports.
New rule 88D outlines procedures for addressing discrepancies in input tax credit between GSTR-3B and GSTR-2B.

198.summary: Notification No. 39/2023-Central Tax, issued on 17th August 2023, brings amendments to the earlier Notification No. 02/2017-Central Tax, which was originally published on 19th June 2017. These amendments are effective from 4th April 2022 and specifically alter entries in Table II of the earlier notification. Serial number 39 has been updated to include various mandals in Guntur District, such as Kovvur and Nidadavole, among others. Similarly, serial number 101 now lists specific mandals in Tirupati District, including Srikalahasti and Tirupati Urban. Lastly, serial number 107 has been revised to reflect mandals in Visakhapatnam District, including Rajamahendravaram Urban. These changes are significant for businesses operating in these districts, as they affect the GST applicability in these areas of Andhra Pradesh.
key_points: Notification No. 39/2023-Central Tax was issued on 17th August 2023.
This notification amends Notification No. 02/2017-Central Tax dated 19th June 2017.
The amendments are effective from 4th April 2022.
Serial number 39 in Table II is replaced to include specific mandals in Guntur District, Andhra Pradesh.
Serial number 101 in Table II is replaced to include specific mandals in Tirupati District, Andhra Pradesh.
Serial number 107 in Table II is replaced to include specific mandals in Visakhapatnam District, Andhra Pradesh.
The changes affect the GST applicability in the specified districts and mandals of Andhra Pradesh.

199.summary: On August 17, 2023, the Government of India issued Notification No. 40/2023-Central Tax, which specifically appoints officers to handle the adjudication process for M/s United Spirits Ltd. This notification is significant as it designates the Joint or Additional Commissioner of Central Tax from the Kolkata North Central Excise and GST Commissionerate to oversee the case. The notice in question, numbered 37/ADC/CGST/MC/Audit-II/2022, was originally issued on August 24, 2022. The appointed authority will now be responsible for exercising the powers and duties related to this notice. This change is crucial for M/s United Spirits Ltd. as it clarifies who will be managing their adjudication process, ensuring that the appropriate authority is in place to address any issues that arise from the audit. This notification streamlines the adjudication process by clearly defining the roles of the involved authorities.
key_points: Notification No. 40/2023-Central Tax was issued on 17th August 2023.
The notification appoints officers to adjudicate matters related to M/s United Spirits Ltd.
The notice number for M/s United Spirits Ltd. is 37/ADC/CGST/MC/Audit-II/2022 dated 24.08.2022.
The adjudicating authority for this case is the Joint or Additional Commissioner, CGST and Central Excise, Mumbai Central Commissionerate.
The authority exercising powers is the Joint or Additional Commissioner of Central Tax, Kolkata North Central Excise and GST Commissionerate.

200.summary: The recent Notification No. 41/2023-Central Tax, issued on August 25, 2023, brings important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 83/2020-Central Tax. Specifically, it expands the tax periods covered to include July 2023, in addition to the previously specified April, May, and June 2023. This means that businesses will now need to consider transactions from July 2023 when filing their returns. Furthermore, the due date for these tax periods has been extended from July 31, 2023, to August 25, 2023. This change is significant for taxpayers as it provides additional time to prepare and submit their returns for the specified months. The amendments are effective retroactively from July 31, 2023, which means that businesses should align their filing processes accordingly. Overall, this notification aims to ease compliance for taxpayers by allowing them to include an additional month in their filings and extending the deadline for submission.
key_points: Amends notification No. 83/2020-Central Tax dated 10th November 2020.
Substitutes 'tax periods April 2023, May 2023 and June 2023' with 'tax periods April 2023, May 2023, June 2023 and July 2023'.
Changes the due date from 'thirty-first day of July, 2023' to 'twenty-fifth day of August, 2023'.
Notification is effective from 31st July 2023.

201.summary: Notification No. 42/2023-Central Tax, issued on 25th August 2023, brings important changes to the earlier notification No. 12/2023-Central Tax from 24th May 2023. Specifically, it extends the tax periods to now include July 2023, which was not part of the previous notification that only covered April, May, and June 2023. Additionally, the due date for filing has been moved from 31st July 2023 to 25th August 2023. This means that businesses that were required to file returns for these months now have an additional month to include July in their filings. The changes are effective retroactively from 31st July 2023, allowing taxpayers to adjust their submissions accordingly. This amendment is particularly relevant for those who may have been impacted by the earlier deadlines and provides them with a bit more time to comply with their GST obligations.
key_points: This notification is No. 42/2023-Central Tax, issued on 25th August 2023.
It amends notification No. 12/2023-Central Tax, dated 24th May 2023.
The tax periods are extended to include July 2023, replacing the previous periods of April, May, and June 2023.
The due date for filing has been changed from 31st July 2023 to 25th August 2023.
The amendment is effective from 31st July 2023.

202.summary: Notification No. 43/2023-Central Tax, issued on 25th August 2023, brings an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 20/2023-Central Tax dated 17th July 2023. Specifically, it changes the due date for a certain compliance requirement from 31st July 2023 to 25th August 2023. This amendment is made under the authority of sub-section (6) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Importantly, while the new due date is now set for 25th August 2023, the amendment itself is deemed to have come into effect from 31st July 2023. This means that businesses and taxpayers who were expecting to meet the earlier deadline now have an extended timeline to comply with the requirements set forth in the original notification. It is crucial for affected parties to take note of this change to ensure they meet the new deadline effectively.
key_points: Notification No. 43/2023-Central Tax was issued on 25th August 2023.
This notification amends Notification No. 20/2023-Central Tax dated 17th July 2023.
The amendment changes the due date from '31st July 2023' to '25th August 2023'.
The amendment is made under sub-section (6) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The amendment is effective from 31st July 2023.

203.summary: Notification No. 44/2023-Central Tax, issued on August 25, 2023, amends the earlier Notification No. 26/2019-Central Tax. Specifically, it extends the tax period covered from April, May, and June 2023 to now include July 2023. Additionally, the deadline for compliance has been changed from July 31, 2023, to August 25, 2023. This means that businesses that were required to file returns for these months now have an extended timeframe to do so, allowing them to include July in their filings. The amendment is effective retroactively from July 31, 2023. This change is particularly relevant for businesses that may have faced challenges in meeting the original deadline, as it provides them with additional time to ensure their filings are accurate and complete. Overall, this notification aims to ease compliance burdens for taxpayers during this period.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 26/2019-Central Tax dated 28th June 2019.
Substitutes 'months of April 2023, May 2023 and June 2023' with 'months of April 2023, May 2023, June 2023 and July 2023'.
Changes the deadline from 'thirty-first day of July, 2023' to 'twenty-fifth day of August, 2023'.
This amendment is effective from 31st July 2023.

204.summary: Notification No. 45/2023-Central Tax, issued on September 6, 2023, introduces significant amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, specifically by inserting new rules 31B and 31C. Rule 31B establishes how to determine the value of supply for online gaming, which includes all amounts paid or deposited by players, but explicitly states that any refunds are not to be deducted from this value. Similarly, Rule 31C outlines the value of supply for actionable claims in casinos, which encompasses the total amounts paid for tokens or participation in events, again excluding any refunds. Additionally, the notification clarifies that winnings used for further gaming do not count as amounts paid to the supplier. These changes are particularly relevant for businesses involved in online gaming and casino operations, as they redefine how taxable values are calculated in these sectors. This amendment follows the previous updates made in notification No. 38/2023 on August 4, 2023, ensuring that the rules governing these activities are current and clear.
key_points: Notification No. 45/2023-Central Tax was issued on 2023-09-06.
This notification amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
New rules 31B and 31C are inserted after rule 31A.
Rule 31B defines the value of supply for online gaming, stating it includes total amounts paid or deposited, excluding refunds.
Rule 31C defines the value of supply for actionable claims in casinos, including amounts paid for tokens or participation, also excluding refunds.
The explanation clarifies that winnings used for further play are not considered as amounts paid to the supplier.
The principal rules were first published on 19th June 2017 and last amended by notification No. 38/2023 on 4th August 2023.

205.summary: Notification No. 46/2023-Central Tax, issued on 18th September 2023, designates specific officers to handle adjudication for certain GST matters. This notification specifically mentions M/s Inkuat Infrasol Pvt. Ltd., located in Thane, as the noticee, with the notice number 39/PK/Inkuat/2021-22 dated 25.03.2022. The Joint or Additional Commissioner from the CGST and Central Excise Bhiwandi Commissionerate has been appointed as the adjudicating authority for this case. Additionally, the notification indicates that the Joint or Additional Commissioner from the CGST and Central Excise Thane Commissionerate will oversee the adjudication of cases related to the Directorate General of GST Intelligence (DGGI). This change is significant for M/s Inkuat Infrasol Pvt. Ltd. as it clarifies who will be handling their adjudication process, ensuring that the appropriate authorities are in place to address their GST-related matters.
key_points: Notification No. 46/2023-Central Tax was issued on 18th September 2023.
The notification appoints officers to act as the Authority for adjudication purposes.
M/s Inkuat Infrasol Pvt. Ltd. is the noticee mentioned in the notification.
The notice number for M/s Inkuat Infrasol Pvt. Ltd. is 39/PK/Inkuat/2021-22, dated 25.03.2022.
The adjudicating authority is the Joint or Additional Commissioner, CGST and Central Excise Bhiwandi Commissionerate.
The authority responsible for adjudication of DGGI cases is the Joint or Additional Commissioner, CGST and Central Excise Thane Commissionerate.

206.summary: Notification No. 47/2023-Central Tax, issued on 25th September 2023, brings an important amendment to Notification No. 30/2023-Central Tax, which was originally published on 31st July 2023. The key change is the insertion of the phrase 'with effect from 1st day of January 2024' into the earlier notification. This means that the special procedure outlined in Notification No. 30/2023 will officially start applying from January 1, 2024. However, this amendment is considered to have been effective from the date of the original notification, July 31, 2023. This change is significant for businesses that need to prepare for the new procedures as they will be in effect from the beginning of 2024, while the legal acknowledgment of this change goes back to the end of July. Companies and stakeholders should take note of this timeline to ensure they are ready for the upcoming changes in GST compliance.
key_points: Notification No. 47/2023-Central Tax was issued on 25th September 2023.
This notification amends Notification No. 30/2023-Central Tax dated 31st July 2023.
The amendment inserts the phrase 'with effect from 1st day of January 2024' into Notification No. 30/2023.
The inserted phrase is deemed to have been effective from 31st July 2023.

207.summary: Notification No. 48/2023-Central Tax, issued on 29th September 2023, announces that the provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Act, 2023, will take effect from 1st October 2023. This amendment is enacted under the authority of sub-section (2) of section 1 of the Amendment Act, which is designated as Act No. 30 of 2023. The notification is officially signed by Raghavendra Pal Singh, who is a Director at the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs. This change means that any business or individual subject to GST will need to be aware of the new provisions that will be applicable starting from the specified date. It is essential for all stakeholders in the GST framework to understand these changes as they may impact compliance and operational processes. The notification serves as a formal announcement of the commencement of the amended provisions, ensuring that all parties are informed of the upcoming changes in the GST landscape.
key_points: The notification is issued as Notification No. 48/2023-Central Tax.
It was issued on 29th September 2023.
The provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Act, 2023, will come into force on 1st October 2023.
This notification is based on the powers conferred by sub-section (2) of section 1 of the Amendment Act.
The Amendment Act is referred to as Act No. 30 of 2023.
The notification is signed by Raghavendra Pal Singh, Director at the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.

208.summary: Notification No. 49/2023-Central Tax, issued on 29th September 2023, introduces specific supplies that fall under the purview of section 15(5) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Effective from 1st October 2023, this notification identifies three categories of supplies: the supply of online money gaming, the supply of online gaming that is not classified as online money gaming, and the supply of actionable claims in casinos. This means that businesses involved in these activities will now have to consider the implications of GST on their operations. The inclusion of these supplies under the GST framework signifies a regulatory step towards formalizing the online gaming and casino sectors, which have been growing rapidly. Businesses engaged in these activities should prepare for compliance with the new GST requirements as they come into effect shortly.
key_points: Notification No. 49/2023-Central Tax issued on 29th September 2023.
The notification comes into effect on 1st October 2023.
It specifies supplies under section 15(5) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The supplies notified include: (i) supply of online money gaming, (ii) supply of online gaming (other than online money gaming), and (iii) supply of actionable claims in casinos.

209.summary: On 29th September 2023, the Central Government issued Notification No. 50/2023-Central Tax, which amends the earlier Notification No. 66/2017-Central Tax from 15th November 2017. This amendment will come into effect on 1st October 2023. The key change is the addition of a clause that excludes registered persons who supply specified actionable claims from being eligible for the composition levy under section 10 of the GST Act. This means that businesses making these specific types of supplies will not be able to opt for the simplified tax scheme provided under the composition levy. The term 'specified actionable claims' is defined in clause (102A) of section 2 of the GST Act, which outlines what qualifies as such claims. This change primarily affects registered persons involved in supplying these actionable claims, as they will need to adhere to the regular GST compliance requirements instead of the simplified composition scheme.
key_points: The notification is dated 29th September 2023.
It amends Notification No. 66/2017-Central Tax, originally issued on 15th November 2017.
The amendment takes effect from 1st October 2023.
The amendment adds a clause excluding registered persons making supplies of specified actionable claims from the composition levy under section 10.
The specific clause being amended is the one regarding the composition levy under section 10 of the GST Act.
The new wording specifies that the composition levy does not apply to certain registered persons.
The definition of 'specified actionable claims' is referenced from clause (102A) of section 2 of the GST Act.

210.summary: The Central Government has issued the 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Third Amendment) Rules, 2023', effective from October 1, 2023. This amendment introduces significant changes to the GST registration and reporting processes, particularly for businesses involved in online gaming and information services. Rule 8 now mandates that applicants for GST registration must declare their Permanent Account Number and state, with specific exceptions for non-resident taxable persons and those involved in tax deduction or collection at source. Additionally, Rule 14 has been updated to encompass online money gaming services supplied from outside India. New rules 31B and 31C have been established to clarify how to determine the value of supplies related to online gaming and casino activities. Furthermore, Rule 64 has been revised to require the filing of FORM GSTR-5A for online gaming and information services, which has also been newly formatted to include detailed reporting requirements. Lastly, FORM GST REG-10 has been amended to accommodate new entries related to online money gaming and information services, ensuring that businesses comply with the latest regulations.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Third Amendment) Rules, 2023' and comes into effect on October 1, 2023.
Rule 8 has been amended to require applicants for GST registration to declare their Permanent Account Number and state before applying, with exceptions for certain categories.
Rule 14 has been updated to include provisions for online money gaming services supplied from outside India to recipients in India.
New rules 31B and 31C have been introduced to define the value of supply for online gaming and casino actionable claims, respectively.
Rule 64 has been substituted to require specific returns (FORM GSTR-5A) for online money gaming and online information services provided from outside India.
FORM GST REG-10 has been amended to include new entries for online money gaming and online information services.
The existing FORM GSTR-5A has been replaced with a new format that includes detailed reporting requirements for online gaming and information services.

211.summary: The recent Notification No. 52/2023-Central Tax, issued on 26th October 2023, introduces several amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. Notably, Rule 28 now includes a new sub-rule that establishes the value of corporate guarantees at 1% of the guarantee amount or the actual consideration, whichever is higher. Additionally, Rule 142 has been modified to change the language from 'order' to 'intimation' regarding actions taken by the proper officer. Another significant change is in Rule 159(2), which now states that the Commissioner's order will expire after one year or upon the issuance of a new order. The registration form, FORM GST REG-01, has been updated to include 'One Person Company', while FORM GST REG-08 has been entirely replaced with a new format for cancellation requests. Furthermore, FORM GSTR-8 has seen revisions, including the removal of serial number 5 and updates to serial numbers 7 and 9 to clarify interest and late fee details. Lastly, FORM GST PCT-01 has been amended to broaden the qualifications for enrolment, adding new categories for eligibility. These changes will affect businesses and individuals involved in GST compliance and registration processes.
key_points: Notification No. 52/2023-Central Tax was issued on 26th October 2023.

Rule 28 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 is amended to include a new sub-rule (2) regarding the value of corporate guarantees, set at 1% of the guarantee amount or actual consideration, whichever is higher.
In Rule 142, the phrase 'proper officer shall issue an order' is replaced with 'proper officer shall issue an intimation'.
Rule 159(2) is amended to include a provision that the Commissioner’s order will cease after one year from issuance or upon a new order, whichever comes first.
FORM GST REG-01 is updated to include 'One Person Company' in the registration details.
FORM GST REG-08 is replaced with a new format for cancellation of registration as Tax Deductor or Collector at source.
In FORM GSTR-8, serial number 5 is omitted, and serial number 7 and 9 are revised to include details on interest, late fees, and debit entries.
FORM GST PCT-01 is updated to revise the qualifications for enrolment, adding new categories for eligibility.

212.summary: Notification No. 53/2023-Central Tax, issued on November 2, 2023, introduces a special procedure for taxable persons who failed to file an appeal by the March 31, 2023 deadline under sections 73 or 74 of the GST Act. These individuals can now submit their appeals using FORM GST APL-01 by January 31, 2024. To proceed with the appeal, they must pay the admitted tax amount and an additional 12.5% of the remaining disputed tax, with a maximum limit of 25 crore rupees. Importantly, at least 20% of this disputed tax must be paid from the Electronic Cash Ledger. The notification also states that no refunds will be allowed for any excess payments made prior to this notification until the appeal is resolved. Additionally, appeals that do not involve tax demands are not permitted under this new provision. The existing rules in Chapter XIII of the GST Rules will still apply to these appeals, ensuring a structured process for those affected.
key_points: Taxable persons who missed the appeal deadline of March 31, 2023, under sections 73 or 74 can now file an appeal.
The new appeal must be submitted in FORM GST APL-01 by January 31, 2024.
To file an appeal, the appellant must pay the admitted tax and 12.5% of the remaining disputed tax, capped at 25 crore rupees.
At least 20% of the disputed tax must be paid from the Electronic Cash Ledger.
No refunds will be granted for amounts paid in excess of the specified limits until the appeal is resolved.
Appeals related to demands not involving tax are not admissible under this notification.
The provisions of Chapter XIII of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, will apply to appeals filed under this notification.

213.summary: Notification No. 54/2023-Central Tax, issued on 17th November 2023, brings an important amendment to Notification No. 27/2022-Central Tax, originally published on 26th December 2022. The key change is the substitution of the phrase 'State of Gujarat and the State of Puducherry' with 'States of Andhra Pradesh, Gujarat and Puducherry'. This means that Andhra Pradesh is now included alongside Gujarat and Puducherry in the context of the provisions outlined in the earlier notification. This amendment is made under sub-rule (4B) of rule 8 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, and follows a previous amendment made by Notification No. 31/2023-Central Tax on 31st July 2023. The changes are officially published in the Gazette of India, ensuring that stakeholders are informed of the updated jurisdiction. This notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs, and it is important for businesses operating in these states to be aware of this inclusion as it may affect their compliance and reporting obligations.
key_points: Notification No. 54/2023-Central Tax was issued on 17th November 2023.
This notification amends Notification No. 27/2022-Central Tax dated 26th December 2022.
The amendment replaces 'State of Gujarat and the State of Puducherry' with 'States of Andhra Pradesh, Gujarat and Puducherry'.
The amendment is made under the authority of sub-rule (4B) of rule 8 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The previous amendment to Notification No. 27/2022 was made by Notification No. 31/2023-Central Tax on 31st July 2023.
The changes are published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i).
The notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.
The reference number for this notification is F. No. CBIC-20006/23/2023-GST.

214.summary: Notification No. 55/2023-Central Tax, issued on December 20, 2023, extends the deadline for filing the GSTR-3B return for November 2023. The new due date is now December 27, 2023. This extension specifically applies to registered persons whose principal place of business is located in the districts of Chennai, Tiruvallur, Chengalpattu, and Kancheepuram in Tamil Nadu. The decision was made under the authority of section 39(6) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, following recommendations from the GST Council. This change is significant for businesses in the specified districts, allowing them additional time to prepare and submit their returns for the month of November. It is important for affected businesses to take note of this new deadline to with the updated timeline.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-3B for November 2023.
New due date is December 27, 2023.
Applies to registered persons in Chennai, Tiruvallur, Chengalpattu, and Kancheepuram districts.
This extension is granted under section 39(6) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

215.summary: Notification No. 56/2023-Central Tax, issued on December 28, 2023, extends the time limits for issuing orders related to tax recovery under section 73 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. Specifically, for the financial year 2018-19, the deadline is now April 30, 2024, while for the financial year 2019-20, it is extended to August 31, 2024. This change affects cases where tax has not been paid, has been short paid, or where input tax credit has been wrongly availed or utilized. The notification modifies earlier notifications, including 35/2020, 14/2021, 13/2022, and 09/2023, which previously set different deadlines. This extension provides additional time for taxpayers to address these issues without the immediate pressure of recovery actions. The notification is part of the government's ongoing efforts to facilitate compliance and ease the burden on taxpayers during these financial years.
key_points: Extends the time limit for issuing orders under section 73(9) for tax recovery.
For the financial year 2018-19, the new deadline is April 30, 2024.
For the financial year 2019-20, the new deadline is August 31, 2024.
This notification modifies earlier notifications: 35/2020, 14/2021, 13/2022, and 09/2023.
The extension applies to cases of tax not paid, short paid, or input tax credit wrongly availed.
The notification is issued under section 168A of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It was published in the Gazette of India on December 28, 2023.

216.summary: On 28th February 2023, the Central Government issued Notification No. 01/2023-Central Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The key change is the addition of a new clause (iva) in the Explanation of paragraph 3, which clarifies that any authority, board, or body established by the Central or State Government, including the National Testing Agency, is considered an educational institution for the limited purpose of conducting entrance examinations for admissions. This means that these bodies will be treated as educational institutions when providing services related to entrance exams. This amendment is effective from 1st March 2023. The last amendment to the principal notification was made on 30th December 2022, under Notification No. 15/2022. This change is significant for educational authorities involved in entrance examinations, as it formalizes their status under GST regulations.
key_points: This notification is issued on 28th February 2023.
It amends Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment adds a new clause (iva) in the Explanation of paragraph 3.
The new clause clarifies that authorities set up by the Central or State Government for entrance examinations are considered educational institutions.
This clarification is specifically for the purpose of providing services related to entrance examinations.
The amendment comes into effect from 1st March 2023.
The last amendment to the principal notification was made by Notification No. 15/2022 on 30th December 2022.

217.summary: On 28th February 2023, the Government of India issued Notification No. 01/2023, which amends the earlier notification No. 9/2017 - Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The key change is the addition of a new clause (iva) in the Explanation of paragraph 3. This clause specifies that any authority, board, or body established by the Central or State Government, including the National Testing Agency, will be recognized as an educational institution solely for the purpose of conducting entrance examinations for admissions to educational institutions. This clarification is important for entities involved in such examinations, as it defines their status under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act. The amendment takes effect from 1st March 2023, following the last amendment made on 30th December 2022. This change aims to provide clarity and ensure proper categorization of these bodies in relation to GST regulations.

218.summary: On 28th February 2023, the Government of India issued Notification No. 02/2023-Central Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The specific change made is in the Explanation, clause (h), where the phrase 'and State Legislatures' has been updated to include 'State Legislatures, Courts and Tribunals'. This means that the scope of entities referenced in this clause has been broadened to include not just State Legislatures but also Courts and Tribunals. This amendment will take effect from 1st March 2023. The last amendment to the principal notification was made through Notification No. 05/2022-Central Tax (Rate) on 13th July 2022. This change is relevant for stakeholders involved in GST compliance and legal interpretations, as it clarifies the entities considered under this provision.
key_points: The notification is dated 28th February 2023.
It amends notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax (Rate) issued on 28th June 2017.
The change is in the Explanation, clause (h) of the earlier notification.
The words 'and State Legislatures' are replaced with ', State Legislatures, Courts and Tribunals'.
This amendment comes into effect from 1st March 2023.
The last amendment to the principal notification was made by notification No. 05/2022-Central Tax (Rate) on 13th July 2022.

219.summary: The recent Notification No. 02/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 27th February 2023, introduces an important amendment to the existing Integrated Tax regulations. Specifically, it modifies clause (h) of the Explanation in the earlier notification No. 10/2017, which was originally published on 28th June 2017. The key change involves the addition of 'Courts and Tribunals' to the existing phrase 'and State Legislatures', thereby broadening the scope of entities referenced in this clause. This amendment will take effect from 1st March 2023. It is crucial for businesses and stakeholders involved in the Integrated Goods and Services Tax framework to note this change, as it may impact how they interpret and apply the regulations concerning legislative and judicial authorities. The principal notification No. 10/2017 was previously amended by notification No. 05/2022 on 13th July 2022, highlighting the ongoing updates to the GST framework.

220.summary: The recent Notification No. 03/2023-Central Tax (Rate) issued on 28th February 2023 brings several amendments to the existing GST rates. Specifically, it updates the entry for S. No. 91A in Schedule I, changing the description to include various types of jaggery and khandsari sugar, now subject to a 2.5% tax rate. Additionally, a new entry for 'Pencil sharpeners' has been added as S. No. 186A in Schedule II, which will be taxed at 6%. Furthermore, the existing entry for S. No. 302A in Schedule III has been modified to clarify that it excludes pencil sharpeners, maintaining a 9% tax rate for other items. These changes will take effect from 1st March 2023, impacting businesses dealing with these specific goods by adjusting their tax obligations accordingly.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

Changes the entry for S. No. 91A in Schedule I – 2.5% to include 'Jaggery of all types including Cane Jaggery (gur), Palmyra Jaggery, pre-packaged and labelled; Khandsari Sugar, pre-packaged and labelled; Rab, pre-packaged and labelled'.
Inserts a new entry S. No. 186A in Schedule II – 6% for 'Pencil sharpeners'.
Modifies Schedule III – 9% by adding '[other than pencil sharpeners]' to the end of the entry for S. No. 302A.
The changes are effective from 1st March 2023.

221.summary: The recent Notification No. 03/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate) issued on 28th February 2023 amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Effective from 1st March 2023, the entry for S. No. 91A in Schedule I has been updated to include various types of jaggery and khandsari sugar, which will now be taxed at 5%. Additionally, a new entry for 'Pencil sharpeners' has been added as S. No. 186A in Schedule II, attracting a 12% tax rate. Furthermore, Schedule III has been modified to clarify that the 18% tax rate for S. No. 302A does not apply to pencil sharpeners. These changes aim to provide clearer tax classifications and ensure that specific products are accurately categorized under the GST framework. Businesses dealing with these products should take note of these updates to ensure proper compliance with the revised tax rates.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

222.summary: Notification No. 04/2023-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 28th February 2023, introduces an important amendment to the earlier notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Specifically, it adds a new item (iii) under S. No. 94 in the Schedule, which is 'Rab, other than pre-packaged and labelled'. This means that Rab, which is a type of traditional Indian beverage, will now have specific tax implications under GST. The change is effective from 1st March 2023. This amendment is significant for manufacturers and sellers of Rab, as they will need to be aware of the new tax classification and with the updated GST provisions. The government has made this change based on recommendations from the GST Council, indicating its focus on refining tax categories to better reflect market realities.
key_points: Notification No. 04/2023-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 28th February 2023.
This notification amends the earlier notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new item (iii) 'Rab, other than pre-packaged and labelled' has been inserted in the Schedule against S. No. 94.
The amendment will come into force on 1st March 2023.

223.summary: Notification No. 04/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 28th February 2023, introduces an amendment to the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Specifically, it adds a new item under S. No. 94 in the Schedule, which states that 'Rab, other than pre-packaged and labelled' will now be included. This means that Rab, a traditional Indian product, will be recognized under the integrated tax framework, allowing for clearer tax treatment. The amendment is effective from 1st March 2023, meaning businesses dealing with Rab should be aware of this change and prepare accordingly. This update is significant for manufacturers and sellers of Rab, as it clarifies the tax implications for this product. The change reflects the government's ongoing efforts to streamline the GST framework and ensure that traditional products are adequately categorized.

This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new entry is added to the Schedule against S. No. 94 in Column (3).
The new entry states: '(iii) Rab, other than pre-packaged and labelled'.
This amendment will come into force on 1st March 2023.

224.summary: The recent Notification No. 05/2023-Central Tax (Rate), issued on May 9, 2023, introduces important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 03/2022-Central Tax (Rate). Specifically, it alters the conditions for Goods Transport Agencies (GTAs) under serial number 9, item (iii). A significant change is the introduction of a new deadline; GTAs must now exercise their option for the Financial Year 2023-2024 by May 31, 2023. Furthermore, the notification provides flexibility for GTAs that either commence new business or exceed the registration threshold during the financial year. These agencies can opt to pay GST on their services by submitting a declaration within 45 days of applying for GST registration or within one month of obtaining their registration, whichever is later. This amendment aims to streamline the GST compliance process for GTAs and ensure they have adequate time to make necessary declarations.
key_points: Notification No. 05/2023-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 2023-05-09.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 03/2022-Central Tax (Rate) dated 2022-07-13.
It specifically modifies the conditions under serial number 9, item (iii), sub-item (b) in the table.
A new proviso has been added stating that the option for the Financial Year 2023-2024 must be exercised by 31st May, 2023.

Additionally, a new provision allows a Goods Transport Agency (GTA) that starts a new business or exceeds the registration threshold to opt for GST payment within 45 days of applying for registration or one month from obtaining registration, whichever is later.

225.summary: The recent Notification No. 05/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on May 9, 2023, introduces important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 08/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate). Specifically, it adds two new provisos to the conditions for Goods Transport Agencies (GTAs) under serial number 9, item (iii), sub-item (b). The first proviso requires that the option for the Financial Year 2023-2024 be exercised by May 31, 2023. The second proviso provides flexibility for GTAs that either commence a new business or exceed the registration threshold during the financial year. These GTAs can opt to pay GST on their services by submitting a declaration in Annexure V within 45 days of applying for GST registration or one month from the date of obtaining registration, whichever is later. This amendment is significant for GTAs as it allows them to manage their GST obligations more effectively during the financial year.
key_points: Notification No. 05/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate) was issued on 2023-05-09.
This notification amends the parent notification No. 08/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 2017-06-28.
The amendment specifically adds new provisos under condition (2) of column (5) against serial number 9, item (iii), sub-item (b) in the Table.
The first new proviso states that the option for the Financial Year 2023-2024 must be exercised by 31st May, 2023.
The second new proviso allows a Goods Transport Agency (GTA) that starts a new business or exceeds the registration threshold during the Financial Year to opt to pay GST on its services.
This option must be declared in Annexure V within 45 days of applying for GST registration or one month from obtaining registration, whichever is later.

227.summary: The recently issued Notification No. 06/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 26th July 2023 amends the earlier Notification No. 08/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. A key change is the addition of an explanation to serial number 3, clarifying that it pertains to certain sub-items that were previously omitted. For serial number 9, the deadline for Goods Transport Agencies (GTAs) to exercise their option to pay GST has been changed to a more flexible timeframe, allowing them to do so from 1st January to 31st March of the preceding financial year. Additionally, a new proviso ensures that once GTAs opt to pay GST, this choice will automatically carry over to future financial years unless they file a declaration to revert to the reverse charge mechanism. Furthermore, clause (h) has been removed from the explanation at serial number 24. The amendments also include changes to Annexure V, which now specifies a new deadline for reverting under the reverse charge mechanism, and introduces a new Annexure VI for GTAs to formally declare their intention to revert. These changes are effective from 27th July 2023.
key_points: Notification No. 06/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate) was issued on 26th July 2023.
This notification amends Notification No. 08/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
An explanation has been added to serial number 3, column (3), clarifying that it refers to sub-items of items (iv), (v), and (vi) as they existed before their omission in Notification No. 03/2022.

For serial number 9, column (3), item (iii), condition (2) has been modified to change the deadline for exercising options from 'on or before the 15th March' to 'on or after the 1st January but not later than 31st March' of the preceding financial year.

A new proviso has been added stating that the option exercised by Goods Transport Agencies (GTAs) to pay GST will remain valid for future financial years unless a declaration is filed to revert to the reverse charge mechanism.
Clause (h) has been omitted from the explanation at serial number 24, column (3), item (i).
Annexure V has been amended to change the deadline for exercising options to revert under the reverse charge mechanism.
A new Annexure VI has been introduced for GTAs to declare their intention to revert to the reverse charge mechanism.

228.summary: On 26th July 2023, the Government of India issued Notification No. 07/2023-Central Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The key change is in the Table of the earlier notification, specifically at serial number 19C, where the entry has been updated to 'Satellite launch services.' This means that satellite launch services will now be recognized under this specific entry for GST purposes. The amendment is effective from 27th July 2023, which means businesses involved in satellite launch services should take note of this change immediately. This update follows the last amendment made to the principal notification on 28th February 2023 through Notification No. 01/2023-Central Tax (Rate). The changes reflect the government's ongoing efforts to refine the GST framework in response to industry needs and public interest.
key_points: Notification No. 07/2023-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 26th July 2023.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

This amendment comes into effect from 27th July 2023.
The principal notification No. 12/2017 was last amended by Notification No. 01/2023-Central Tax (Rate) on 28th February 2023.

229.summary: On 26th July 2023, the Government of India issued Notification No. 07/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 9/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This means that satellite launch services will now be categorized under this specific entry for tax purposes. The amendment is effective from 27th July 2023, which means businesses involved in satellite launch services should take note of this change immediately. This amendment follows the last update made to the principal notification on 28th February 2023, indicating ongoing adjustments in the tax framework for integrated goods and services. Overall, this change is significant for companies in the aerospace and satellite industry, as it clarifies the tax treatment of their services.

230.summary: On 26th July 2023, the Government of India issued Notification No. 08/2023-Central Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The key change is in Annexure III, where the wording has been updated to clarify that the forward charge mechanism applies only if it has not reverted to the reverse charge mechanism. This means that businesses using the forward charge must ensure they remain under that mechanism and do not switch back to the reverse charge. This amendment is effective from 27th July 2023, impacting businesses that deal with goods and services under the GST framework. The principal notification had previously been amended by Notification No. 02/2023-Central Tax (Rate) on 28th February 2023, indicating ongoing adjustments to the GST regulations. This change aims to provide clearer guidance on the application of the forward charge mechanism in GST compliance.
key_points: Notification No. 08/2023-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 26th July 2023.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment changes the wording in Annexure III regarding the forward charge mechanism.
The new wording specifies that the forward charge must not have reverted to the reverse charge mechanism.
The changes come into effect from 27th July 2023.
The principal notification was last amended by Notification No. 02/2023-Central Tax (Rate) on 28th February 2023.

231.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 08/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 26th July 2023, which amends the earlier Notification No. 10/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The key change is in Annexure III, where the phrase 'during the Financial Year ____ under forward charge' has been replaced with 'from the Financial Year ____ under forward charge and have not reverted to reverse charge mechanism'. This amendment aims to clarify that the forward charge mechanism applies only if there has been no reversion to the reverse charge mechanism during the specified financial year. This change is significant for businesses that deal with integrated goods and services tax, as it defines the conditions under which they can apply the forward charge. The new provisions will take effect from 27th July 2023, impacting how businesses report and manage their tax obligations under the GST framework.

This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 10/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

The amendment changes the wording in Annexure III from 'during the Financial Year ____ under forward charge' to 'from the Financial Year ____ under forward charge and have not reverted to reverse charge mechanism'.
The new wording clarifies the conditions under which forward charge applies.
This amendment will come into effect from 27th July 2023.

232.summary: Notification No. 09/2023-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 26th July 2023, introduces several amendments to the existing GST rates outlined in Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. New entries have been added to Schedule I, which now includes un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets, fish soluble paste, Linz-Donawitz (LD) slag, and imitation zari thread or yarn, all taxed at 2.5%. Additionally, Schedule II has a revised entry for metallised yarn at 6%, clarifying what is excluded from this category. In Schedule III, the entry for toasted bread now also includes un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets, while the slag entry has been updated to specify types of slag, including a distinction for Linz-Donawitz (LD) slag. These changes will take effect from 27th July 2023, impacting manufacturers and suppliers of these goods by adjusting their tax obligations accordingly.
key_points: Notification No. 09/2023-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 26th July 2023.
This notification amends the earlier notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

New entries added to Schedule I at 2.5%: S. No. 99B for un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets, S. No. 108A for fish soluble paste, S. No. 156B for Linz-Donawitz (LD) slag, and S. No. 218AA for imitation zari thread or yarn.
In Schedule II at 6%, the entry against S. No. 137 is substituted to clarify the definition of metallised yarn.
In Schedule III at 9%, the entry against S. No. 16 is updated to include un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets, and the entry against S. No. 28 is substituted to specify slag types.
The changes in tax rates and definitions will come into effect on 27th July 2023.

233.summary: The recent Notification No. 09/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 26th July 2023, introduces several amendments to the Integrated Tax rates. Effective from 27th July 2023, new entries have been added to Schedule I at a 5% tax rate, including S. No. 99B for un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets made through extrusion, S. No. 108A for fish soluble paste, S. No. 156B for Linz-Donawitz (LD) slag, and S. No. 218AA for imitation zari thread or yarn. Additionally, Schedule II has been amended at a 12% rate, where the entry against S. No. 137 regarding metallised yarn has been substituted to clarify its definition. Furthermore, Schedule III at an 18% rate has also seen changes; the entry against S. No. 16 now includes un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets, while the entry against S. No. 28 has been updated to specify slag and dross, excluding Linz-Donawitz (LD) slag. These changes will affect manufacturers and suppliers of the specified goods, ensuring they are aware of the new classifications and tax implications.
key_points: Notification No. 09/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate) issued on 26th July 2023.
New entries added to Schedule I at 5%: S. No. 99B for un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets manufactured through extrusion.
New entries added to Schedule I at 5%: S. No. 108A for fish soluble paste.
New entries added to Schedule I at 5%: S. No. 156B for Linz-Donawitz (LD) slag.
New entries added to Schedule I at 5%: S. No. 218AA for imitation zari thread or yarn.

Amendment in Schedule III at 18%: Substitution of entry against S. No. 16 to include un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets.

234.summary: Notification No. 10/2023-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 26th July 2023, brings amendments to the earlier Notification No. 26/2018-Central Tax (Rate) from 31st December 2018. The opening paragraph now substitutes 'paragraph 4.41' with 'paragraph 4.40', indicating a specific change in reference. Additionally, the Explanation has been updated: clause (a) now defines 'Foreign Trade Policy' as the one notified on 31st March 2023, while clause (b) defines 'Handbook of Procedures' as the one notified on 1st April 2023. These changes clarify the references to the latest policies and procedures relevant to foreign trade. The amendments are effective from 27th July 2023, impacting businesses that rely on these definitions for compliance and operational purposes. This ensures that stakeholders are aligned with the most current regulations and guidelines set forth by the Government of India.
key_points: This notification is issued on 26th July 2023.
It amends Notification No. 26/2018-Central Tax (Rate) dated 31st December 2018.
The phrase 'paragraph 4.41' is replaced with 'paragraph 4.40' in the opening paragraph.
Clause (a) in the Explanation is updated to refer to the 'Foreign Trade Policy, 2023' notified on 31st March 2023.
Clause (b) in the Explanation is updated to refer to the 'Handbook of Procedures' notified on 1st April 2023.
This notification comes into effect on 27th July 2023.

235.summary: Notification No. 10/2023-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 26th July 2023, brings important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 27/2018-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 31st December 2018. The opening paragraph now references 'paragraph 4.40' instead of 'paragraph 4.41', indicating a correction in the document. Additionally, the definition of 'Foreign Trade Policy' has been updated to reflect the latest policy notified on 31st March 2023, ensuring that businesses are aligned with current regulations. Similarly, the definition of 'Handbook of Procedures' has been revised to include the latest procedures notified on 1st April 2023. These changes are significant for businesses engaged in foreign trade as they need to refer to the most recent guidelines and policies. The amendments take effect from 27th July 2023, meaning that businesses should start applying these updated definitions immediately to with the latest regulations.

236.summary: The recent Notification No. 11/2023-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 29th September 2023, introduces significant amendments to the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. A new entry, S. No. 227A, has been added to include 'specified actionable claims' which cover activities such as betting, casinos, gambling, horse racing, lottery, and online money gaming. This means that these activities will now be recognized under the GST framework. Additionally, the previous entries related to S. No. 228 and 229 have been removed, indicating a shift in how certain transactions are categorized. Furthermore, an important clarification has been made in the Explanation section, stating that any terms not defined in this notification will carry the meanings assigned to them in the CGST, IGST, and UTGST Acts. These changes will take effect from 1st October 2023, impacting businesses involved in the specified activities and requiring them to align with the new definitions and categorizations.

238.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 12/2023-Central Tax (Rate) on 19th October 2023, which amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This new notification introduces specific conditions for claiming input tax credit under serial numbers 8 and 10. If a supplier charges a central tax rate higher than 2.5%, businesses can only claim input tax credit up to the 2.5% rate, effectively limiting the credit to the lower rate. An example provided clarifies that if a service is charged at a higher rate, the credit is restricted to 2.5% of the service cost. Additionally, changes have been made to serial number 34, replacing certain terms and omitting item (v). The notification also removes entries related to serial numbers 696 and 698 from the classification of services. These amendments will take effect from 20th October 2023, impacting businesses that deal with input services in the specified categories.

These changes clarify the tax treatment of certain services and expand the scope of entities recognized under the provisions. The amendments will take effect from October 20, 2023, impacting businesses and authorities involved in these services.

244.summary: On 19th October 2023, the Government of India issued Notification No. 15/2023-Central Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 15/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The key change involves the wording in the opening paragraph related to the construction of complexes and buildings. The new text clarifies that the service charge includes the value of land or an undivided share of land, but this does not apply if the full payment is received after a completion certificate is issued by the competent authority or after the first occupation of the property. This amendment is significant for builders and developers, as it affects how they account for GST on construction services. The changes will take effect from 20th October 2023, impacting transactions related to property sales and construction services moving forward.

246.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 16/2023-Central Tax (Rate) on 19th October 2023, which amends the earlier Notification No. 17/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The key change in this amendment is the modification of clause (i), which now specifies that the term 'motor vehicle' excludes 'omnibus'. Additionally, a new clause (ia) has been added, which addresses the transportation of passengers by an omnibus, stating that this applies unless the service is provided by a company through an electronic commerce operator. Furthermore, the term 'Company' is now defined in the Explanation section, aligning it with the definition in the Companies Act, 2013. These changes will come into effect on 20th October 2023, impacting businesses involved in passenger transportation services, particularly those using omnibuses and electronic platforms for service delivery.

248.summary: The recent Notification No. 17/2023-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 19th October 2023, introduces several amendments to the GST rates. 259.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 01/2023 on 28th February 2023, which amends the earlier Notification No. 12/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax rates. This amendment introduces a new clause (iva) in paragraph 3 of the Explanation, clarifying that any authority, board, or body established by the Central or State Government, including the National Testing Agency, will be treated as an educational institution. This classification is specifically for the purpose of providing services related to conducting entrance examinations for admission to educational institutions. This change is significant as it formalizes the status of these bodies in the context of GST, ensuring that their services are recognized under the educational institution category. The amendment will take effect from 1st March 2023, impacting how these authorities are treated under the Union Territory GST framework.
