Specifically, it updates the language in the Explanation, clause (h), by replacing 'and State Legislatures' with ', State Legislatures, Courts and Tribunals'. This change broadens the scope of entities referenced in the clause, now explicitly including Courts and Tribunals alongside State Legislatures. This amendment is significant as it clarifies the legal framework under which the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax operates. The new wording will take effect from 1st March 2023, impacting how the provisions are interpreted and applied in relation to these additional entities. Stakeholders in the Union Territories should take note of this change to ensure they understand the implications for compliance and governance under the GST framework.
key_points: This notification is No. 02/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 28th February 2023.
It amends notification No. 13/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment substitutes the words 'and State Legislatures' with ', State Legislatures, Courts and Tribunals' in the Explanation, clause (h).
The changes will come into effect from 1st March 2023.

261.summary: The recent Notification No. 03/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 28th February 2023 introduces several amendments to the existing tax rates under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act. Effective from 1st March 2023, the notification updates the entry for S. No. 91A in Schedule I to include various types of jaggery and khandsari sugar, ensuring they are recognized as pre-packaged and labelled products. Additionally, a new entry for 'Pencil sharpeners' has been added under Schedule II at S. No. 186A, which will now be taxed at a rate of 6%. Furthermore, in Schedule III, the existing entry for S. No. 302A has been modified to clarify that it excludes pencil sharpeners. These changes are significant for manufacturers and sellers of these products, as they will affect the applicable tax rates and compliance requirements moving forward.
key_points: The notification is dated 28th February 2023 and comes into effect on 1st March 2023.
It amends Notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

In Schedule I, the entry for S. No. 91A is updated to include 'Jaggery of all types including Cane Jaggery (gur), Palmyra Jaggery, pre-packaged and labelled; Khandsari Sugar, pre-packaged and labelled; Rab, pre-packaged and labelled'.
A new entry is added in Schedule II, S. No. 186A for 'Pencil sharpeners' at a tax rate of 6%.
In Schedule III, for S. No. 302A, the phrase '[other than pencil sharpeners]' is added at the end of the entry.

262.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 04/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 28th February 2023, which amends the earlier notification No. 2/2017-Union Territory (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This amendment introduces a new item under S. No. 94 in the Schedule, specifically adding 'Rab, other than pre-packaged and labelled'. This means that from 1st March 2023, Rab that is not pre-packaged and labelled will be subject to Union Territory tax. This change is significant for manufacturers and sellers of Rab, as they will need to account for this item in their tax calculations and compliance. The amendment reflects the government's ongoing efforts to update and refine the tax structure in response to market needs and public interest. Businesses involved in the production or sale of Rab should take note of this change to ensure they are aligned with the new tax regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 04/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 28th February 2023.
This notification amends the earlier notification No. 2/2017-Union Territory (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new item (iii) 'Rab, other than pre-packaged and labelled' is added to S. No. 94 in the Schedule.
The amendment will come into effect on 1st March 2023.

263.summary: The recent Notification No. 05/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on May 9, 2023, introduces important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 11/2017. Specifically, it modifies the provisions related to Goods Transport Agencies (GTAs) under serial number 9, item (iii) of the parent notification. Two new provisos have been added to clarify the process for GTAs to opt for GST payment. The first proviso requires that GTAs must exercise this option for the Financial Year 2023-2024 by May 31, 2023. The second proviso provides flexibility for new GTAs or those crossing the registration threshold, allowing them to opt for GST payment within 45 days of applying for GST registration or one month from the date of obtaining registration, whichever is later. This change is significant for GTAs as it provides clearer guidelines on their GST obligations and options, ensuring they can comply effectively as they establish or expand their businesses.
key_points: Notification No. 05/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 2023-05-09.
This notification amends the parent notification No. 11/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 2017-06-28.
The amendment specifically affects serial number 9, item (iii), sub-item (b) in column (5) of the parent notification.
It adds two new provisos regarding the option for Goods Transport Agency (GTA) to pay GST.
The first new proviso states that the option for the Financial Year 2023-2024 must be exercised by 31st May 2023.

The second new proviso allows a GTA that starts a new business or exceeds the registration threshold to opt for GST payment within 45 days of applying for registration or one month from obtaining registration, whichever is later.

264.summary: The recent Notification No. 06/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 26th July 2023, brings several amendments to the earlier Notification No. 11/2017. One significant change is the addition of an explanation at serial number 3, which clarifies references to sub-items from previous notifications. For Goods Transport Agencies (GTA), the deadline for exercising options has shifted from 'on or before the 15th March' to 'on or after the 1st January but not later than 31st March' of the preceding financial year. Additionally, a new proviso states that once a GTA opts to pay GST, this choice will remain in effect for future financial years unless a declaration is filed to revert to the reverse charge mechanism. Furthermore, clause (h) under serial number 24 has been removed, and Annexure V has been updated to reflect the new deadlines for exercising options. Lastly, a new Annexure VI has been introduced, allowing GTAs to formally declare their intention to revert to the reverse charge mechanism before the start of any financial year. These changes will take effect from 27th July 2023.
key_points: Notification No. 06/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 26th July 2023.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
An explanation has been added to serial number 3 in the Table, clarifying references to sub-items from previous notifications.
For serial number 9, the deadline for exercising options has changed from 'on or before the 15th March' to 'on or after the 1st January but not later than 31st March' of the preceding financial year.
A new proviso has been added stating that the option for Goods Transport Agencies (GTA) to pay GST will continue unless a declaration is filed to revert to reverse charge mechanism.
Clause (h) under serial number 24 has been omitted from the explanation in column (3).
In Annexure V, the wording regarding the deadline for exercising options has been updated to reflect the new timeline.
A new Annexure VI has been introduced for GTAs to declare their intention to revert to the reverse charge mechanism.

265.summary: On 26th July 2023, the Government of India issued Notification No. 07/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 12/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The specific change made is in the table under serial number 19C, where the entry in column (3) has been updated to 'Satellite launch services.' This means that satellite launch services are now recognized under this specific entry for Union Territory tax purposes. The amendment will take effect from 27th July 2023. This change is significant for businesses involved in satellite launch services, as it clarifies their tax obligations under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax framework. The principal notification had previously been amended by Notification No. 01/2023 on 28th February 2023, indicating ongoing updates to the tax structure in this area.
key_points: This notification is issued as Notification No. 07/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 26th July 2023.
It amends Notification No. 12/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment specifically changes the entry in column (3) against serial number 19C.
The new entry for serial number 19C is 'Satellite launch services.'
This amendment comes into effect from 27th July 2023.
The principal notification was last amended by Notification No. 01/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 28th February 2023.

266.summary: On 26th July 2023, the Government of India issued Notification No. 08/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 13/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The key change is in Annexure III, where the phrase 'during the Financial Year ____ under forward charge' has been replaced with 'from the Financial Year ____ under forward charge and have not reverted to reverse charge mechanism'. This clarification specifies that the forward charge mechanism applies only if the taxpayer has not switched back to the reverse charge mechanism during the specified financial year. This amendment is effective from 27th July 2023. Businesses operating in Union Territories should take note of this change, as it may affect their tax reporting and compliance under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act. The amendment aims to provide clearer guidance on the application of the forward charge mechanism in specific scenarios.
key_points: Notification No. 08/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 26th July 2023.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 13/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

The amendment changes the wording in Annexure III from 'during the Financial Year ____ under forward charge' to 'from the Financial Year ____ under forward charge and have not reverted to reverse charge mechanism'.
The changes in the notification will take effect from 27th July 2023.

267.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 09/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 26th July 2023, which introduces several amendments to the existing Union Territory tax rates. Effective from 27th July 2023, new entries have been added to Schedule I at a tax rate of 2.5%. These include S. No. 99B for un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets, S. No. 108A for fish soluble paste, S. No. 156B for Linz-Donawitz (LD) slag, and S. No. 218AA for imitation zari thread or yarn. Additionally, Schedule II at 6% has been amended to clarify the entry for metallised yarn under S. No. 137. Furthermore, Schedule III at 9% has been updated to include un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets in the entry for S. No. 16 and to specify types of slag in the entry for S. No. 28. This notification amends the principal notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), which was last updated on 28th February 2023. Businesses dealing with these products should take note of these changes to with the new tax rates.
key_points: Notification No. 09/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 26th July 2023.

Inserts new entries in Schedule I at 2.5%: S. No. 99B for un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets, S. No. 108A for fish soluble paste, S. No. 156B for Linz-Donawitz (LD) slag, and S. No. 218AA for imitation zari thread or yarn.
Amends Schedule II at 6% by substituting the entry against S. No. 137 regarding metallised yarn.
Amends Schedule III at 9% by substituting the entry against S. No. 16 to include un-fried or un-cooked snack pellets and against S. No. 28 to specify slag types.
This notification comes into force on 27th July 2023.
The principal notification being amended is No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The last amendment to the principal notification was made by notification No. 03/2023 on 28th February 2023.

268.summary: Notification No. 10/2023-Central Tax, issued on January 1, 2023, serves to amend an earlier notification dated July 26, 2023. The primary change involves a correction in the Hindi version of the notification. Specifically, on page 13, entry 20, the phrase 'हैंडबुकऑफ़प्रोसीजसथ' has been updated to ensure accurate representation. This amendment is important for those who rely on the Hindi text for understanding the procedures outlined in the notification. It ensures that the terminology used is consistent and clear for Hindi-speaking stakeholders. This change does not affect any tax periods or compliance requirements but is crucial for maintaining clarity in official communications. Stakeholders who refer to the Hindi version of the notification should take note of this correction to avoid any confusion in interpretation.
key_points: Notification No. 10/2023-Central Tax was issued on January 1, 2023.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 10/2023-Central Tax dated July 26, 2023.
The amendment specifically alters the Hindi version of the notification.
On page 13, in entry 20, the phrase 'हैंडबुकऑफ़प्रोसीजसथ' is corrected to 'हैंडबुकऑफ़प्रोसीजसथ' in the Hindi text.

269.summary: On 26th July 2023, the Government of India issued Notification No. 10/2023, which amends the earlier Notification No. 26/2018 regarding Union Territory Tax rates. The amendment specifically changes the reference in the opening paragraph from 'paragraph 4.41' to 'paragraph 4.40'. Additionally, the definitions in the Explanation have been updated: clause (a) now refers to the 'Foreign Trade Policy, 2023', which was notified on 31st March 2023, and clause (b) now refers to the 'Handbook of Procedures', notified on 1st April 2023. These updates clarify the relevant policies and procedures that businesses must follow. The changes take effect from 27th July 2023, meaning that businesses operating in Union Territories should be aware of these updates to with the latest regulations. This notification is part of the government's ongoing efforts to streamline tax regulations and align them with current trade policies.
key_points: Notification No. 10/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 26th July 2023.
This notification amends Notification No. 26/2018-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 31st December 2018.
In the opening paragraph, 'paragraph 4.41' is replaced with 'paragraph 4.40'.
Clause (a) in the Explanation is updated to refer to the 'Foreign Trade Policy, 2023' notified on 31st March 2023.
Clause (b) in the Explanation is updated to refer to the 'Handbook of Procedures' notified on 1st April 2023.
The changes in this notification come into effect on 27th July 2023.

270.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 11/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 29th September 2023, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. A significant change is the introduction of a new entry, S. No. 227A, in Schedule IV, which pertains to 'specified actionable claims.' This includes activities such as betting, casinos, gambling, horse racing, lotteries, and online money gaming. Additionally, the previous entries related to S. No. 228 and 229 have been removed from the schedule. Furthermore, a new clause has been added to clarify that any terms not defined in this notification will carry the meanings assigned to them in the CGST, IGST, and UTGST Acts. These amendments will take effect from 1st October 2023, impacting businesses involved in the specified activities and ensuring clarity in the definitions used in the context of Union Territory tax.
key_points: Notification No. 11/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 29th September 2023.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new entry, S. No. 227A, is added to Schedule IV for 'specified actionable claim' including betting, casinos, gambling, horse racing, lottery, and online money gaming.
Entries related to S. No. 228 and 229 are omitted from Schedule IV.
An additional clause (v) is inserted in the Explanation, clarifying that terms not defined in this notification will have meanings as per the CGST, IGST, and UTGST Acts.
The changes will come into effect on 1st October 2023.

271.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 12/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 19th October 2023, which amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017. This new notification introduces specific conditions for claiming input tax credit under serial numbers 8 and 10. If a supplier of input services charges a Union Territory tax rate higher than 2.5%, businesses can only claim input tax credit up to the amount corresponding to the 2.5% rate, effectively disallowing any credit for the excess amount. An illustrative example is provided to clarify this change. Additionally, there are amendments to serial number 34, where the phrase 'totalisator or a license to' is replaced with 'licensing a', and item (v) is removed. Furthermore, serial numbers 696 and 698 have been omitted from the Annexure concerning the classification of services. These changes will take effect from 20th October 2023, impacting businesses that engage in services subject to these tax rates and classifications.
key_points: Notification No. 12/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 19th October 2023.
Amends Notification No. 11/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
Inserts a new condition for serial number 8 and 10 regarding input tax credit when the supplier charges a higher rate than 2.5%.
The new condition states that credit of input tax charged in excess of 2.5% shall not be taken.
Illustration provided for clarity on how the input tax credit works under the new condition.
Changes in serial number 34 include substitution of 'totalisator or a license to' with 'licensing a'.
Omission of item (v) under serial number 34.
Omission of serial numbers 696 and 698 from the Annexure: Scheme of Classification of Services.
The amendments come into effect from 20th October 2023.

272.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 13/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 19th October 2023, which amends the previous Notification No. 12/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. A significant addition is made to the Table in the notification, where a new entry (3B) is introduced for Chapter 99. This entry specifies that services provided to a Governmental Authority in areas such as water supply, public health, sanitation conservancy, solid waste management, and slum improvement will be exempt from tax, marked as 'Nil'. Furthermore, the Ministry of Railways is now explicitly included in several entries (serial numbers 6, 7, 8, and 9) alongside the Department of Posts, indicating that services related to both entities will be treated similarly under the tax framework. These amendments will take effect from 20th October 2023, impacting businesses and authorities involved in these services by clarifying their tax obligations.
key_points: Notification No. 13/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 19th October 2023.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 12/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

A new entry is added in the Table after serial number 3A for Chapter 99, covering services to a Governmental Authority related to water supply, public health, sanitation conservancy, solid waste management, and slum improvement, with a tax rate of 'Nil'.
The words 'and the Ministry of Railways (Indian Railways)' are added to serial number 6, 7, 8, and 9 in the respective columns of the Table.
The changes come into effect from 20th October 2023.

273.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 14/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 19th October 2023, which amends the earlier Notification No. 13/2017. This amendment specifically affects the classification of services related to the Department of Posts and the Ministry of Railways. In the updated notification, the phrase 'and the Ministry of Railways (Indian Railways)' has been added to the description of services under serial number 5, indicating that these services are now included. Additionally, for services supplied by the Central Government listed under serial number 5A, the Ministry of Railways is explicitly excluded. These changes will come into effect on 20th October 2023, impacting how services are categorized under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax. Businesses and entities dealing with postal and railway services should take note of these adjustments for compliance and reporting purposes.
key_points: Notification No. 14/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 19th October 2023.
Amends Notification No. 13/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
Inserts 'and the Ministry of Railways (Indian Railways)' after 'Department of Posts' in serial number 5, column (2), item (2), sub-item (i).
Excludes 'the Ministry of Railways (Indian Railways)' from services supplied by the Central Government in serial number 5A, column (2).
The changes take effect from 20th October 2023.

274.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 15/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 19th October 2023, which amends the earlier Notification No. 15/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This amendment specifically alters the language in the opening paragraph concerning the construction of complexes that are intended for sale. The new text clarifies that the service charge includes the value of land or an undivided share of land, but this does not apply if the entire payment has been received after the issuance of a completion certificate or after the first occupation of the property, whichever occurs first. This change is significant for businesses involved in real estate and construction, as it affects how they account for the value of land in their transactions. The amendment will come into effect on 20th October 2023, meaning businesses should be aware of this change immediately to .
key_points: Notification No. 15/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 19th October 2023.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 15/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment changes the wording in the opening paragraph regarding the construction of complexes intended for sale.
The new wording specifies that the service includes the value of land or undivided share of land, except when the entire consideration is received after the completion certificate is issued.
The amendment will take effect from 20th October 2023.

275.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 16/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 19th October 2023, which amends the earlier Notification No. 17/2017. The key change in this amendment is the modification of clause (i), where the phrase 'omnibus or any other motor vehicle' is replaced with 'or any other motor vehicle except omnibus'. This means that the tax treatment for motor vehicles now excludes omnibuses. Additionally, a new clause (ia) has been added, which specifies that services provided by an omnibus for passenger transportation are exempt from tax unless the service is supplied through an electronic commerce operator by a company. Furthermore, the term 'Company' is now defined in the Explanation section, aligning it with the definition in the Companies Act, 2013. These changes will come into effect on 20th October 2023, impacting businesses involved in passenger transportation services, particularly those using omnibuses.
key_points: Notification No. 16/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 19th October 2023.
Amends Notification No. 17/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
Changes clause (i) to substitute 'omnibus or any other motor vehicle' with 'or any other motor vehicle except omnibus'.
Inserts a new clause (ia) for transportation services by an omnibus, excluding companies supplying such services through electronic commerce operators.
Defines 'Company' in the Explanation as per clause (20) of section 2 of the Companies Act, 2013.
The amendments take effect from 20th October 2023.

276.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 17/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 19th October 2023, which introduces several amendments to the existing tax rates. Effective from 20th October 2023, new entries have been added to Schedule I at a 2.5% tax rate, specifically for 'Molasses' and 'Food preparation of millet flour, in powder form, containing at least 70% millets by weight, pre-packaged and labelled'. Additionally, Schedule III at a 9% tax rate has been updated to include the same food preparation under S. No. 13 and introduces a new entry for 'Spirits for industrial use' under S. No. 25A. Furthermore, S. No. 1 and its entries have been removed from Schedule IV at a 14% tax rate. These changes are part of ongoing adjustments to the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax framework, following the last amendment made by notification No. 11/2023. Businesses dealing with these specific goods will need to take note of these updates to with the new tax structure.
key_points: Notification No. 17/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 19th October 2023.

Amends Schedule III at 9% by substituting the description for S. No. 13 to include 'food preparation of millet flour, in powder form, containing at least 70% millets by weight, pre-packaged and labelled'.
Inserts new entry in Schedule III at 9%: S. No. 25A for 'Spirits for industrial use'.
Omitted S. No. 1 and its entries from Schedule IV at 14%.
The changes come into effect from 20th October 2023.
This notification amends the principal notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) last amended by notification No. 11/2023.

277.summary: On 19th October 2023, the Government of India issued Notification No. 18/2023, which amends the earlier notification No. 2/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax rates. This amendment introduces a new entry, S. No. 94A, to the Schedule of the previous notification. The new entry specifically covers 'Food preparation of millet flour, in powder form, containing at least 70% millets by weight, other than pre-packaged and labelled'. This means that from 20th October 2023, this specific food preparation will be recognized under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax framework. Businesses involved in the production or sale of this type of millet flour should take note of this change, as it may affect their tax obligations and compliance requirements. The amendment aims to support the millet industry and promote the use of millets in food preparations, aligning with public interest and health initiatives.
key_points: Notification No. 18/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 19th October 2023.
This notification amends the earlier notification No. 2/2017-Union Territory (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

S. No. 94A pertains to 'Food preparation of millet flour, in powder form, containing at least 70% millets by weight, other than pre-packaged and labelled'.

278.summary: Notification No. 19/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 19th October 2023, introduces an amendment to the earlier notification No. 4/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The amendment specifically alters the entry in column 4 of the Table against S. No. 6. The new entry clarifies that it applies to the 'Central Government [excluding Ministry of Railways (Indian Railways)], State Government, Union territory or a local authority.' This change is significant as it delineates the entities that fall under this category, excluding the Ministry of Railways. The amendment will take effect from 20th October 2023, ensuring that the updated classification is applied moving forward. This notification is part of ongoing efforts to refine and clarify the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax framework, with the last amendment occurring on 30th December 2022. Stakeholders should take note of this change to understand its implications on their operations and compliance.
key_points: This notification is No. 19/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 19th October 2023.
It amends the earlier notification No. 4/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), dated 28th June 2017.
The specific change is in the Table against S. No. 6, where the entry in column 4 is substituted.
The new entry states: 'Central Government [excluding Ministry of Railways (Indian Railways)], State Government, Union territory or a local authority.'
This amendment will come into effect on 20th October 2023.
The last amendment to the principal notification was made by notification No. 14/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate), dated 30th December 2022.

279.summary: The recent Notification No. 20/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 19th October 2023, introduces an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 5/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Specifically, it adds a new entry, S. No. 6AA, to the existing table, which addresses 'Imitation zari thread or yarn made out of Metallised polyester film/plastic film'. This new entry clarifies that the refund of input tax credit is applicable only for polyester film or plastic film used in the production of these imitation zari threads or yarns. This change is significant for manufacturers and suppliers dealing with these materials, as it directly affects their eligibility for input tax credit refunds. The amendment takes effect from 20th October 2023, meaning businesses should be aware of this update to ensure they can benefit from the revised provisions regarding input tax credits related to these specific products.
key_points: Notification No. 20/2023-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 19th October 2023.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 5/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new entry, S. No. 6AA, is added to the table, which pertains to 'Imitation zari thread or yarn made out of Metallised polyester film/plastic film'.
The new entry specifies that the refund of input tax credit applies only to polyester film/plastic film.
The amendment comes into effect on 20th October 2023.

280.summary: The notification titled 'Goods and Services Tax (Period of Levy and Collection of Cess) Rules, 2022' was issued on June 24, 2022, and will come into effect from July 1, 2022. This notification extends the period for the levy and collection of compensation cess under the Goods and Services Tax (Compensation to States) Act, 2017, until March 31, 2026. This change is significant as it allows for continued collection of cess, which is crucial for compensating states for revenue losses due to the implementation of GST. The rules are established under specific sections of the GST Act, namely section 12(2) and section 8, and are based on recommendations from the GST Council. This means that businesses and stakeholders involved in the collection and payment of this cess will need to be aware of the extended timeline, ensuring they comply with the updated regulations. Overall, this notification provides clarity on the duration of the cess collection, which is essential for financial planning and compliance for affected entities.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Goods and Services Tax (Period of Levy and Collection of Cess) Rules, 2022'.
It was issued on June 24, 2022.
The rules will come into effect from July 1, 2022.
The period for levy and collection of cess is extended until March 31, 2026.
This notification is issued under the Goods and Services Tax (Compensation to States) Act, 2017.
It is based on the recommendations of the GST Council.
The rules are established under section 12(2) and section 8 of the GST (Compensation to States) Act.

281.summary: On 24th February 2022, the Government of India issued Notification No. 01/2022-Central Tax, which amends the earlier Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax from 21st March 2020. The key change in this amendment is the reduction of the threshold for certain compliance requirements from 'fifty crore rupees' to 'twenty crore rupees'. This new threshold will take effect from 1st April 2022. The amendment is made under sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, which pertains to the filing of returns. This change will impact businesses whose aggregate turnover is between twenty crore rupees and fifty crore rupees, as they will now be subject to different compliance obligations. The principal notification had previously been amended by Notification No. 23/2021-Central Tax on 1st June 2021, but this latest amendment further adjusts the compliance landscape for businesses in this turnover bracket.
key_points: This notification is issued on 24th February 2022.
It amends Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax dated 21st March 2020.
The amendment changes the threshold from 'fifty crore rupees' to 'twenty crore rupees'.
The new threshold will be effective from 1st April 2022.
This change is made under sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The principal notification was last amended by Notification No. 23/2021-Central Tax dated 1st June 2021.

282.summary: The recent Notification No. 02/2022-Central Tax, issued on 11th March 2022, introduces significant amendments to the earlier Notification No. 02/2017-Central Tax from 19th June 2017. It adds a new paragraph, 3A, which empowers Additional Commissioners and Joint Commissioners of Central Tax to make decisions regarding notices issued by the Directorate General of Goods and Services Tax Intelligence. Additionally, a new Table V has been included, outlining the specific powers these officials hold across various regions in India. This includes the ability to pass orders under several sections of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, such as sections 67, 73, 74, 76, 122, 125, 127, 129, and 130. The notification specifically lists the Principal Commissioners and Commissioners who are granted these powers, thereby enhancing the operational authority of tax officials in their respective jurisdictions. This change is crucial for ensuring that tax enforcement actions are handled efficiently and effectively across the country.
key_points: Notification No. 02/2022-Central Tax was issued on 11th March 2022.
This notification amends Notification No. 02/2017-Central Tax dated 19th June 2017.
A new paragraph 3A is inserted after paragraph 3, granting powers to Additional Commissioners or Joint Commissioners of Central Tax.
Table V is added, detailing the powers of Additional Commissioners or Joint Commissioners regarding notices from the Directorate General of Goods and Services Tax Intelligence.
The powers specified in Table V apply to various Principal Commissioners and Commissioners across India.
The powers include passing orders or decisions under specific sections of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The sections mentioned include 67, 73, 74, 76, 122, 125, 127, 129, and 130.
The notification affects the operational authority of tax officials in specified regions.

283.summary: Notification No. 03/2022-Central Tax, issued on 31st March 2022, introduces amendments to Notification No. 10/2019-Central Tax from 7th March 2019. Specifically, it adds new entries to the existing Table in the earlier notification. These entries include fly ash bricks or aggregates with at least 90% fly ash content, bricks made from fossil meals or similar siliceous earths, building bricks, and earthen or roofing tiles. This change is significant as it expands the list of goods covered under the specified tax provisions. The new entries will be effective from 1st April 2022, meaning that businesses dealing with these products need to be aware of the updated classifications and any related tax implications. This amendment aims to clarify the GST treatment of these materials, which may benefit manufacturers and suppliers in the construction and building materials sector.
key_points: This notification is No. 03/2022-Central Tax, issued on 31st March 2022.
It amends Notification No. 10/2019-Central Tax, dated 7th March 2019.
New entries are added to the Table after serial number 3.

The new entries include: 4. Fly ash bricks or fly ash aggregate with 90% or more fly ash content; 5. Bricks of fossil meals or similar siliceous earths; 6. Building bricks; 7. Earthen or roofing tiles.
The notification comes into force on 1st April 2022.

284.summary: Notification No. 04/2022-Central Tax, issued on 31st March 2022, introduces amendments to Notification No. 14/2019-Central Tax from 7th March 2019. Specifically, it adds new entries to the existing Table in the earlier notification, which now includes four new types of products. These products are: fly ash bricks or fly ash aggregate with 90% or more fly ash content, bricks made from fossil meals or similar siliceous earths, building bricks, and earthen or roofing tiles. This change is significant for manufacturers and suppliers of these materials, as it clarifies their classification under GST. The amendments will take effect from 1st April 2022, meaning that businesses dealing with these products should be aware of the new classifications and ensure they are compliant with the updated regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 04/2022-Central Tax was issued on 31st March 2022.
This notification amends Notification No. 14/2019-Central Tax dated 7th March 2019.
New entries are added to the Table in Notification No. 14/2019 after serial number 3.

The new entries include: 4. Fly ash bricks or fly ash aggregate with 90% or more fly ash content; 5. Bricks of fossil meals or similar siliceous earths; 6. Building bricks; 7. Earthen or roofing tiles.
The notification comes into force on 1st April 2022.

285.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 05/2022-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for filing the GSTR-3B return for the month of April 2022. Originally, the due date was set for an earlier date, but it has now been moved to May 24, 2022. This change is made following the recommendations of the GST Council, reflecting the government's responsiveness to the needs of taxpayers. The extension applies specifically to the GSTR-3B form, which is a key return that businesses must file to report their GST liabilities. This notification is grounded in the provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, ensuring that it is legally supported. Businesses that are required to file this return should take note of the new date to with the updated timeline.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-3B for April 2022.
New due date for GSTR-3B is now May 24, 2022.
This extension is based on the recommendations of the GST Council.
The notification is issued under sub-section (6) of section 39 of the CGST Act, 2017.

286.summary: Notification No. 06/2022-Central Tax, issued on May 17, 2022, extends the deadline for businesses to deposit their tax dues for April 2022. Specifically, the due date for filing FORM GST PMT-06 has been moved to May 27, 2022. This change is made under the authority of the first proviso to sub-rule (3) of rule 61 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, following recommendations from the GST Council. This extension is significant for businesses that need more time to manage their tax obligations for the specified month. It allows them to without the immediate pressure of the original deadline. This notification is particularly relevant for all registered taxpayers who are required to file this form for the month of April 2022, providing them with additional time to prepare and submit their tax payments.
key_points: Extends the due date for depositing tax due under section 39(7) of the CGST Act.
New due date for FORM GST PMT-06 for April 2022 is now May 27, 2022.
This extension is based on the recommendations of the GST Council.
The notification is issued under the powers of the first proviso to sub-rule (3) of rule 61 of the CGST Rules, 2017.

287.summary: Notification No. 07/2022-Central Tax, issued on May 26, 2022, introduces an important amendment to Notification No. 73/2017-Central Tax, originally published on December 29, 2017. Specifically, it adds a new proviso after the fifth proviso, which waives the late fee for registered persons who delay filing FORM GSTR-4 for the Financial Year 2021-22. This waiver is applicable for the period from May 1, 2022, to June 30, 2022. As a result, businesses that are required to submit FORM GSTR-4 during this timeframe will not incur late fees, easing the compliance burden for those affected. This change is particularly beneficial for small businesses or those with minimal tax liabilities, as it provides relief from penalties during this specified period. Overall, this amendment aims to support taxpayers by reducing financial penalties associated with late submissions of their GST returns.
key_points: Notification No. 07/2022-Central Tax was issued on 2022-05-26.
This notification amends Notification No. 73/2017-Central Tax dated 29th December 2017.
A new proviso is added after the fifth proviso in Notification No. 73/2017.
The new proviso waives the late fee for FORM GSTR-4 for the Financial Year 2021-22.
The waiver applies for delays from 1st May 2022 to 30th June 2022.
This amendment affects registered persons who are required to file FORM GSTR-4.

288.summary: Notification No. 08/2022-Central Tax, issued on June 7, 2022, provides a significant update for certain electronic commerce operators. It states that the interest rate for these operators will be 'Nil' if they failed to file FORM GSTR-8 for December 2020 due to a technical glitch on the GST portal. This applies specifically to those who had already deposited the tax collected for that month in their electronic cash ledger. The interest relief is effective from the date they deposited the tax until they eventually filed the required statement. Additionally, the notification extends this relief to other electronic commerce operators who encountered similar filing issues from September 2020 to January 2021, provided they also deposited the necessary tax. The notification lists the GST Identification Numbers of all affected operators, ensuring clarity on who qualifies for this interest waiver. This measure aims to alleviate the burden on operators who faced technical difficulties during the filing process.
key_points: Notification No. 08/2022-Central Tax issued on 07th June 2022.
The interest rate for certain electronic commerce operators is set to 'Nil'.
This applies to operators who failed to file FORM GSTR-8 for December 2020 due to a technical glitch.
The affected operators had deposited the tax collected for December 2020 in the electronic cash ledger.
The interest relief is applicable from the date of tax deposit until the date of filing the statement.
Additional electronic commerce operators are included for the months from September 2020 to January 2021.
These operators also faced issues filing due to technical glitches but had deposited the required tax.
The notification specifies the GST Identification Numbers of the affected operators.

289.summary: Notification No. 09/2022-Central Tax, issued on July 5, 2022, establishes that certain provisions of the Finance Act, 2022, will come into effect on the same date. Specifically, it enforces clause (c) of section 110 and section 111 of the Finance Act, 2022. This means that the rules or changes outlined in these sections are now applicable from July 5, 2022. The notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs, which oversees GST regulations in India. The notification is part of the government's efforts to implement the provisions of the Finance Act, ensuring that businesses and stakeholders are aware of the new rules. The reference number for this notification is F.No.CBIC-20001/2/2022-GST, which can be used for further inquiries or clarifications regarding its contents. This change may affect various businesses and taxpayers who need to align their operations with the newly enforced provisions.
key_points: The notification is titled Notification No. 09/2022-Central Tax.
It was issued on July 5, 2022.
The notification appoints July 5, 2022, as the effective date for certain provisions.
Specifically, it enforces clause (c) of section 110 and section 111 of the Finance Act, 2022.
This notification is issued under the powers of clause (b) of sub-section (2) of section 1 of the Finance Act, 2022.
The notification is published by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.
The reference number for this notification is F.No.CBIC-20001/2/2022-GST.

290.summary: Notification No. 10/2022-Central Tax, issued on July 5, 2022, provides an important update for small businesses. It exempts registered persons whose aggregate turnover in the financial year 2021-22 is up to two crore rupees from the requirement to file their annual return for that year. This change is significant as it reduces the compliance burden for smaller taxpayers, allowing them to focus more on their business operations rather than paperwork. The exemption is granted under the first proviso to section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, which outlines the conditions under which such exemptions can be made. This notification is particularly beneficial for small businesses that may find the annual return filing process cumbersome and resource-intensive. By not having to file this return, these businesses can save time and resources, which can be redirected towards growth and development.
key_points: Exemption from filing the annual return for the financial year 2021-22.
Applicable to registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to two crore rupees.
Issued under Notification No. 10/2022-Central Tax.
Notification date is July 5, 2022.
Exemption is based on the first proviso to section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

291.summary: Notification No. 11/2022-Central Tax, issued on 5th July 2022, introduces an important amendment to Notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax from 23rd April 2019. Specifically, it adds a new proviso that mandates certain individuals to submit a statement in FORM GST CMP-08. This statement must detail the payment of self-assessed tax for the quarter ending on 30th June 2022. The deadline for this submission is set for 31st July 2022. This change primarily affects those who fall under the category of taxpayers required to report their self-assessed tax payments. It is crucial for these individuals to be aware of this new requirement to with the updated regulations. The amendment aims to streamline the reporting process and ensure timely submission of tax details for the specified quarter.
key_points: Notification No. 11/2022-Central Tax was issued on 5th July 2022.
This notification amends Notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax dated 23rd April 2019.
A new proviso is added after the fourth proviso in the second paragraph of Notification No. 21/2019.
The new proviso requires certain persons to furnish a statement in FORM GST CMP-08.
The statement must include details of payment of self-assessed tax for the quarter ending 30th June 2022.
The deadline for submitting this statement is 31st July 2022.

292.summary: Notification No. 12/2022-Central Tax, issued on 5th July 2022, brings a significant change to the sixth proviso of Notification No. 73/2017-Central Tax. Specifically, it extends the deadline for a certain compliance requirement from 30th June 2022 to 28th July 2022. This change is relevant for businesses that are required to comply with the provisions outlined in Notification No. 73/2017. The amendment is made under section 128 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and follows a previous amendment made by Notification No. 07/2022 on 26th May 2022. The principal notification, which was originally published on 29th December 2017, has undergone several amendments, and this latest change aims to provide additional time for compliance. Businesses should take note of this new deadline to ensure they meet their obligations under the GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 12/2022-Central Tax was issued on 5th July 2022.
This notification amends the sixth proviso of Notification No. 73/2017-Central Tax.
The date '30th June 2022' in the sixth proviso is replaced with '28th July 2022'.
The amendment is made under the powers conferred by section 128 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The principal notification No. 73/2017 was published on 29th December 2017.
This amendment follows the earlier amendment made by Notification No. 07/2022-Central Tax on 26th May 2022.

293.summary: Notification No. 13/2022-Central Tax, issued on July 5, 2022, brings significant changes regarding the recovery of taxes and filing of refund applications. It extends the deadline for issuing orders related to tax recovery for the financial year 2017-18 until September 30, 2023. This means that businesses that have not paid or have short-paid their taxes during this period now have additional time for compliance. Furthermore, the notification excludes the period from March 1, 2020, to February 28, 2022, when calculating the limitation period for both issuing orders for erroneous refunds and for filing refund applications under sections 54 and 55. This exclusion allows businesses to have a more extended timeframe to address any issues related to refunds without the pressure of the usual limitation period. The changes are a partial modification of previous notifications, specifically No. 35/2020 and No. 14/2021, and are effective retroactively from March 1, 2020.
key_points: Extends the time limit for issuing orders under section 73(9) for recovery of tax not paid or short paid for the financial year 2017-18 to September 30, 2023.
Excludes the period from March 1, 2020, to February 28, 2022, from the limitation period for issuing orders under section 73(9) for recovery of erroneous refunds.
Excludes the same period (March 1, 2020, to February 28, 2022) for filing refund applications under sections 54 and 55.
This notification is a partial modification of earlier notifications No. 35/2020-Central Tax and No. 14/2021-Central Tax.

294.summary: Notification No. 14/2022-Central Tax, issued on 5th July 2022, introduces several amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. A significant change in Rule 21A(4) allows for the automatic revocation of registration suspension if all pending returns are filed, benefiting those whose registrations were suspended for specific contraventions. Additionally, Rule 43 now includes the value of Duty Credit Scrips from Notification No. 35/2017-Central Tax (Rate) for clarity on input tax credit. In Rule 46, a new declaration requirement is added for taxpayers with aggregate turnover exceeding a specified limit, ensuring compliance in invoice issuance. Rule 86(4B) establishes a mechanism for re-crediting erroneous refunds, enhancing the process for registered persons. Furthermore, Rule 87 expands payment options to include UPI and IMPS, making transactions more accessible. Rule 88B outlines how interest on delayed tax payments will be calculated, providing transparency. Lastly, Rule 89 clarifies the process for refund applications, particularly for electricity exports, ensuring that exporters have clear guidelines to follow. These changes aim to streamline processes and enhance compliance for businesses under GST.
key_points: Notification No. 14/2022-Central Tax was issued on 5th July 2022.
Rule 21A(4) is amended to state that if registration is suspended for certain contraventions, it will be deemed revoked upon furnishing all pending returns.
Rule 43 is amended to include the value of Duty Credit Scrips as specified in Notification No. 35/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 13th October 2017.
Rule 46 now requires a declaration for taxpayers with aggregate turnover exceeding a specified limit regarding invoice issuance.
Rule 86(4B) introduces a process for re-crediting erroneous refunds deposited by registered persons.
Rule 87 is amended to allow payments via Unified Payment Interface (UPI) and Immediate Payment Services (IMPS).
Rule 88B is introduced to detail the calculation of interest on delayed tax payments.
Rule 89 is amended to clarify refund applications and includes specific provisions for electricity exports.

295.summary: Notification No. 15/2022-Central Tax, issued on 13th July 2022, brings an amendment to Notification No. 10/2019-Central Tax, which was originally published on 7th March 2019. The specific change occurs in the Table of the earlier notification, where the entry against serial number 4 is updated. However, the new entry remains the same as the previous one, stating 'Fly ash bricks; Fly ash aggregates; Fly ash blocks.' This means that while the wording is substituted, the actual terms listed do not change. The effective date for this amendment is set for 18th July 2022. This notification primarily affects businesses dealing with fly ash products, ensuring they are aware of the formal amendment process, even though the terms themselves have not changed.
key_points: Notification No. 15/2022-Central Tax was issued on 13th July 2022.
This notification amends Notification No. 10/2019-Central Tax dated 7th March 2019.
The amendment specifically changes the entry in column (3) against serial number 4 of the Table.
The new entry replaces 'Fly ash bricks; Fly ash aggregates; Fly ash blocks' with the same terms.
This amendment will come into force on 18th July 2022.

296.summary: Notification No. 16/2022-Central Tax, issued on 13th July 2022, introduces an amendment to Notification No. 14/2019-Central Tax, which was originally published on 7th March 2019. The specific change occurs in the TABLE under serial number 4, where the previous entry has been replaced with 'Fly ash bricks; Fly ash aggregates; Fly ash blocks'. This amendment is significant as it clarifies the classification of these materials under GST. The new entry will take effect from 18th July 2022, ensuring that businesses dealing with these products are aware of the updated terminology. This change follows the last amendment made to the principal notification by Notification No. 04/2022-Central Tax on 31st March 2022. Overall, this notification aims to streamline the GST framework concerning fly ash products, which are essential in construction and other industries.
key_points: Notification No. 16/2022-Central Tax was issued on 13th July 2022.
This notification amends Notification No. 14/2019-Central Tax dated 7th March 2019.
The amendment specifically changes the entry in the TABLE against serial number 4.
The previous entry has been replaced with 'Fly ash bricks; Fly ash aggregates; Fly ash blocks'.
This change will come into effect on 18th July 2022.
The principal notification No. 14/2019 was last amended by notification No. 04/2022-Central Tax on 31st March 2022.

297.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 17/2022-Central Tax on 1st August 2022, which amends the earlier Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax from 21st March 2020. The key change in this amendment is the reduction of the threshold limit for certain compliance requirements from 'twenty crore rupees' to 'ten crore rupees'. This new limit will be applicable starting from 1st October 2022. The amendment is made under sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, which governs the filing of returns and related compliance. This change will primarily affect businesses whose aggregate turnover is between ten crore and twenty crore rupees, as they will now need to adhere to the compliance requirements previously applicable only to those with a turnover above twenty crore. This adjustment aims to streamline compliance for smaller businesses and ensure they meet the necessary GST obligations.
key_points: Notification No. 17/2022-Central Tax was issued on 1st August 2022.
This notification amends Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax dated 21st March 2020.
The amendment changes the threshold limit from 'twenty crore rupees' to 'ten crore rupees'.
This change will take effect from 1st October 2022.
The amendment is made under sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The principal notification No. 13/2020 was last amended by notification No. 01/2022-Central Tax on 24th February 2022.

298.summary: Notification No. 18/2022-Central Tax, issued on 28th September 2022, establishes that the provisions of sections 100 to 114 of the Finance Act, 2022 will take effect from 1st October 2022. However, it specifically excludes clause (c) of section 110 and section 111 from this implementation. This means that business owners and stakeholders in the GST framework should be aware that these sections will now be applicable, impacting various compliance and procedural aspects under GST. The Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs has facilitated this change, which is part of the government's ongoing efforts to streamline tax regulations. It is crucial for those affected to review these sections to understand how they may influence their operations and compliance requirements moving forward.
key_points: The notification is dated 28th September 2022.
It appoints 1st October 2022 as the effective date for certain provisions.
Sections 100 to 114 of the Finance Act, 2022 will come into force, excluding clause (c) of section 110 and section 111.
This notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.

299.summary: The Central Goods and Services Tax (Second Amendment) Rules, 2022, effective from October 1, 2022, introduce several significant changes to the GST framework. New clauses (h) and (i) in rule 21 specify that registered persons who do not file their returns for six months or two consecutive tax periods may face consequences. Rule 36 has been amended to eliminate the requirement for information to be provided in FORM GSTR-2, focusing instead on input tax credit details. Rule 37 now mandates that registered persons must pay their suppliers within 180 days to retain their input tax credit, with specific provisions for deemed payments included. Additionally, multiple sub-rules in rule 38 have been revised to remove references to FORM GSTR-2 and clarify how input tax credit should be reversed in FORM GSTR-3B. Notably, rules 69 to 79 have been completely omitted, streamlining the regulations. Changes in rule 89 also introduce new provisions for claiming refunds from the electronic cash ledger. Lastly, FORM GSTR-1A, FORM GSTR-2, and FORM GSTR-3 have been entirely removed, simplifying the filing process for registered persons.
key_points: The notification is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Second Amendment) Rules, 2022, effective from October 1, 2022.
New clauses (h) and (i) are added to rule 21, stating that registered persons who fail to file returns for six months or two tax periods may face consequences.
Rule 36 is amended to remove the requirement for information to be furnished in FORM GSTR-2 and to specify input tax credit details.
Rule 37 is revised to clarify that registered persons must pay suppliers within 180 days to retain input tax credit, with specific provisions for deemed payments.
Several sub-rules in rule 38 are amended to remove references to FORM GSTR-2 and clarify the reversal of input tax credit in FORM GSTR-3B.
Rules 69 to 79 are omitted entirely.
Changes in rule 89 include new provisions for claiming refunds from the electronic cash ledger.
FORM GSTR-1A, FORM GSTR-2, and FORM GSTR-3 are completely omitted.

300.summary: This corrigendum, issued on 29th September 2022, corrects an earlier notification, No. 20/2022-Central Tax, published on 28th September 2022. The amendment specifically alters a line in the document, changing the reference from '2018' to '2018, with effect from the 1st day of October, 2022'. This change clarifies that the provisions related to the year 2018 will be applicable starting from October 1, 2022. This correction is important for businesses and tax professionals who rely on accurate dates for compliance and reporting purposes. It ensures that the effective date is clearly stated, preventing any confusion regarding the applicability of the rules from that year. All stakeholders should take note of this amendment to ensure they are aligned with the updated timeline.
key_points: The notification is a corrigendum to Notification No. 20/2022-Central Tax.
The corrigendum was issued on 29th September 2022.
It amends the text published in the Gazette of India on 28th September 2022.
The specific change is in line 33, where '2018' is replaced with '2018, with effect from the 1st day of October, 2022'.

301.summary: On 28th September 2022, the Central Government issued Notification No. 20/2022-Central Tax, which rescinds the earlier Notification No. 20/2018-Central Tax that was published on 28th March 2018. This means that the provisions and rules established in the 2018 notification are no longer in effect, effective immediately. However, any actions taken under the 2018 notification before this rescission will still be considered valid. The decision to rescind was made under the authority of section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, indicating that the government believes this change is in the public interest. Businesses and stakeholders who were following the guidelines set out in the 2018 notification should take note of this change, as they will need to adjust their practices accordingly. This move reflects the government's ongoing efforts to streamline GST regulations and ensure they align with current needs.
key_points: Notification No. 20/2022-Central Tax was issued on 28th September 2022.
This notification rescinds Notification No. 20/2018-Central Tax dated 28th March 2018.
The rescission is effective immediately, except for actions taken before this date.
The rescission is based on the powers conferred by section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

302.summary: Notification No. 21/2022-Central Tax, issued on October 21, 2022, extends the deadline for filing the GSTR-3B return for the month of September 2022. The new due date for this return is now set for October 21, 2022. This extension applies specifically to registered persons who are required to submit their returns under section 39(1) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The notification is issued under the authority of section 39(6) of the same Act, reflecting the government's response to the needs of taxpayers. This change provides additional time for businesses to prepare and submit their returns, ensuring they can comply with the requirements without rushing. It is important for affected businesses to note this new deadline and plan accordingly to meet their filing obligations.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-3B for September 2022.
New due date is now October 21, 2022.
Applicable to registered persons required to file under section 39(1) of the CGST Act.
This notification is issued under the powers of section 39(6) of the CGST Act.

303.summary: Notification No. 22/2022-Central Tax, issued on 15th November 2022, amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, specifically affecting FORM GSTR-9. The key change is the extension of the filing period for GSTR-9 from 'between April, 2022 to September, 2022' to 'of April, 2022 to October, 2022 filed up to 30th November, 2022'. This adjustment is reflected in the instructions and table entries for serial numbers 10, 11, 12, and 13 of FORM GSTR-9, which now all indicate the new filing period. This amendment was made following the recommendations of the GST Council and will take effect immediately upon publication in the Official Gazette. The last amendment to these rules was made by notification No. 19/2022-Central Tax on 28th September 2022. This change primarily benefits businesses that need to file their GSTR-9 for the extended period, providing them with additional time to comply with the filing requirements.
key_points: Notification No. 22/2022-Central Tax was issued on 15th November 2022.
This notification amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The amendment changes the filing period for FORM GSTR-9 from 'between April, 2022 to September, 2022' to 'of April, 2022 to October, 2022 filed up to 30th November, 2022'.
In the Table of FORM GSTR-9, entries for serial numbers 10, 11, 12, and 13 are updated to reflect the new filing period of 'April, 2022 to October, 2022 filed up to 30th November, 2022'.
The changes apply specifically to the instructions and table entries in FORM GSTR-9.
This amendment follows the recommendations of the GST Council.
The rules come into force on the date of their publication in the Official Gazette.
The last amendment to the principal rules was made by notification No. 19/2022-Central Tax on 28th September 2022.

304.summary: Notification No. 23/2022-Central Tax, issued on November 23, 2022, empowers the Competition Commission of India to investigate whether the input tax credits claimed by registered persons or any reductions in tax rates have resulted in a corresponding decrease in the prices of goods or services provided. This action is based on the provisions of section 171 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and follows recommendations from the Goods and Services Tax Council. The aim is to ensure that the benefits of tax reductions are passed on to consumers. This notification will take effect from December 1, 2022. Businesses that claim input tax credits or benefit from tax rate reductions should be aware that their pricing practices may be scrutinized to with this new directive. This change is significant as it enhances the oversight of pricing in relation to tax benefits, potentially impacting how businesses price their goods and services.
key_points: Empowers the Competition Commission of India to examine input tax credits and tax rate reductions.
The examination will determine if these reductions have led to a corresponding decrease in the prices of goods or services.
This empowerment is based on sub-section (2) of section 171 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The notification is issued under the recommendations of the Goods and Services Tax Council.
The notification comes into effect on 1st December 2022.

305.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 24/2022-Central Tax, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, effective from 1st December 2022. This amendment includes the omission of several rules: specifically, rules 122, 124, 125, and 134, as well as rule 137. Additionally, there is a significant change in rule 127, where the term 'Duties' has been replaced with 'Functions' in the heading, and the wording regarding the responsibilities of the authority has been updated for clarity. Furthermore, the definition of 'Authority' in the Explanation following rule 137 has been revised to align with section 171 of the GST Act. These changes streamline the rules and clarify the functions of the authority, impacting businesses and stakeholders involved in GST compliance and administration.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourth Amendment) Rules, 2022'.
It comes into effect on 1st December 2022.
Rule 122 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 is omitted.
Rules 124 and 125 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 are also omitted.
In rule 127, the term 'Duties' is replaced with 'Functions' in the marginal heading.
The phrase 'It shall be the duty of the Authority,' is replaced with 'The authority shall discharge the following functions, namely:' in rule 127.
Rule 134 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 is omitted.
Rule 137 is omitted, and clause (a) in the Explanation after rule 137 is substituted to define 'Authority' as per section 171 of the Act.

306.summary: On December 13, 2022, the Government of India issued Notification No. 25/2022-Central Tax, which amends the earlier Notification No. 83/2020-Central Tax from November 10, 2020. This amendment introduces a new proviso that extends the deadline for filing GSTR-1 for the tax period of November 2022. Specifically, registered persons whose principal place of business is in certain districts of Tamil Nadu, including Chennai, Tiruvallur, Chengalpattu, and others, will now have until the 13th day of the following month to submit their GSTR-1 returns. This change is significant for businesses operating in these districts, as it provides additional time to comply with filing requirements under section 39(1) of the CGST Act. The extension aims to ease the compliance burden for taxpayers in these regions during the specified tax period.
key_points: The notification is dated December 13, 2022.
It amends Notification No. 83/2020-Central Tax issued on November 10, 2020.
A new proviso is added after the second proviso in Notification No. 83/2020.
The new proviso extends the GSTR-1 filing deadline for November 2022.
The extended deadline applies to registered persons in specific districts of Tamil Nadu.
The affected districts include Chennai, Tiruvallur, Chengalpattu, Kancheepuram, Tiruvannamalai, Ranipet, Vellore, Villupuram, Cuddalore, Thiruvarur, Nagapattinam, Mayiladuthurai, and Thanjavur.
The new deadline for filing GSTR-1 is the 13th day of the month following the tax period.
This extension is applicable to those required to furnish returns under section 39(1) of the CGST Act.

307.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 26/2022-Central Tax on December 26, 2022, amending several provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. Key changes include the removal of mobile number and email address requirements in rule 8, while introducing mandatory Aadhaar authentication for certain applicants. Rule 37A has been added to specify the reversal of input tax credit if a supplier fails to file their GSTR-3B by September 30 of the following financial year. Additionally, rule 46 now mandates that tax invoices for services provided to unregistered recipients must include their name, address, and PIN code. Rule 59 prohibits registered persons from filing GSTR-1 if they have outstanding amounts as per rule 88C, which has also been updated to address discrepancies between reported outward supplies and tax returns. Lastly, rule 89 has been revised to require additional documentation for refund claims by unregistered persons, particularly in cases of service contract cancellations. These changes will affect various stakeholders, including registered persons and suppliers, by altering compliance requirements and processes.
key_points: Notification No. 26/2022-Central Tax was issued on December 26, 2022.
The rules amend the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, specifically rule 8, rule 9, rule 12, rule 37, and others.
In rule 8, mobile number and email address requirements are removed, and Aadhaar authentication is now mandatory for certain applicants.
Rule 37A introduces a process for reversing input tax credit if the supplier does not file their GSTR-3B by September 30 of the following financial year.
Rule 46 now requires tax invoices for services supplied to unregistered recipients to include the recipient's name, address, and PIN code.
Rule 59 prohibits registered persons from filing GSTR-1 if they have an outstanding amount as per rule 88C.
Rule 88C outlines a new process for addressing discrepancies between reported outward supplies and tax returns.
Rule 89 updates refund claim requirements for unregistered persons, including additional documentation for service contract cancellations.

308.summary: Notification No. 27/2022-Central Tax, issued on December 26, 2022, introduces a significant change regarding the application of certain GST rules. Specifically, it states that sub-rule (4A) of rule 8 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 will not be applicable across all States and Union Territories, with the sole exception being the State of Gujarat. This means that businesses and taxpayers in most regions will not have to adhere to the provisions outlined in sub-rule (4A), which could simplify compliance for them. However, those operating in Gujarat will still need to follow these provisions. The notification is based on the recommendations of the GST Council and is issued under the authority of sub-rule (4B) of rule 8. This change is important for businesses to note, as it alters the compliance landscape for GST in India, particularly affecting operations outside Gujarat.
key_points: Notification No. 27/2022-Central Tax was issued on 26th December 2022.
The notification specifies that sub-rule (4A) of rule 8 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 will not apply in all States and Union Territories.
The only exception to this rule is the State of Gujarat.
This change is made under the powers conferred by sub-rule (4B) of rule 8.

309.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 01/2022-Central Tax (Rate) on 31st March 2022, which amends the previous notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Notably, it removes the entries for serial numbers 225B, 226, 227, and 228 from Schedule I, which were taxed at 2.5%. This change means that these specific items will no longer be subject to this tax rate. Additionally, the notification introduces new entries in Schedule II at a 6% tax rate, specifically for fly ash bricks and related products. The new entries include serial number 176B for fly ash bricks, 176C for bricks made from fossil meals, 176D for building bricks, and 176E for earthen or roofing tiles. These changes will take effect from 1st April 2022, impacting manufacturers and suppliers of these materials by altering their tax obligations.
key_points: Notification No. 01/2022-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 31st March 2022.
This notification amends the earlier notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In Schedule I, the entries for serial numbers 225B, 226, 227, and 228 at the 2.5% tax rate are omitted.

This notification comes into effect on 1st April 2022.

310.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 01/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 31st March 2022, which amends the previous Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Effective from 1st April 2022, this new notification removes certain entries from Schedule I that were previously taxed at 5%, specifically entries 225B, 226, 227, and 228. Additionally, it introduces new entries in Schedule II under the 12% tax rate, adding four new items: fly ash bricks or aggregates, bricks of fossil meals, building bricks, and earthen or roofing tiles. These changes will affect manufacturers and suppliers of these specific products, as they will now have different tax implications under the GST framework. The adjustments aim to streamline the tax structure for these materials, reflecting the government's ongoing efforts to refine GST regulations.

In Schedule II, under the 12% tax rate, new entries are added after serial number 176A.

This notification comes into effect on 1st April 2022.

311.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 02/2022-Central Tax (Rate) on 31st March 2022, which comes into effect on 1st April 2022. This notification provides an exemption from central tax for certain intra-state supplies of goods, specifically fly ash bricks, bricks of fossil meals, building bricks, and earthen tiles, where the tax rate is capped at 3%. To qualify for this exemption, suppliers must adhere to specific conditions, notably that they cannot claim input tax credit on goods used solely for these exempt supplies. Additionally, if goods are used partially for exempt supplies and partially for other taxable supplies, the input tax credit must be reversed as per the rules outlined in the GST Act. This change is significant for businesses dealing in the specified goods, as it reduces their tax burden and encourages the use of eco-friendly materials like fly ash bricks. The exemption is applicable under the relevant tariff items and chapters as defined in the Customs Tariff Act, 1975.
key_points: Notification No. 02/2022-Central Tax (Rate) issued on 31st March 2022.
Effective from 1st April 2022.
Exempts intra-state supplies of specific goods from central tax above 3%.

Conditions for exemption include not taking input tax credit on goods used exclusively for these supplies.
The exemption applies to goods under specified tariff items and chapters as per the Customs Tariff Act, 1975.
Condition No. 1 outlines the rules for input tax credit reversal for partially used goods.

312.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 02/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 31st March 2022, which introduces an exemption for inter-state supplies of certain goods from integrated tax. This exemption applies to goods such as fly ash bricks, bricks of fossil meals, building bricks, and earthen tiles, all of which will be taxed at a reduced rate of 6%. The exemption is effective from 1st April 2022. However, businesses must adhere to specific conditions to qualify for this exemption. Notably, they cannot claim input tax credit on goods used exclusively for supplying these exempted goods. This means that if a business uses materials solely for these supplies, they must not take any input tax credit related to those materials. The notification provides a detailed table listing the tariff items and their corresponding descriptions, along with the conditions that must be met to benefit from the exemption. This change is significant for manufacturers and suppliers of the specified goods, as it reduces their tax burden on inter-state transactions.

Exempts inter-state supplies of specific goods from integrated tax exceeding 6%.
