The exemption applies from 1st April 2022.
Conditions for exemption include not taking input tax credit on goods used exclusively for these supplies.
The relevant tariff items and descriptions are listed in a table with specific conditions.

313.summary: Notification No. 03/2022-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. Key changes include the omission and substitution of various items in the tax rate table, particularly affecting transport services. For instance, the entry for item (vii) under serial number 8 now encompasses passenger transport services not previously specified, with a tax rate of 9%. Additionally, a new entry for 'Transport of passengers by ropeways' has been introduced, attracting a tax rate of 2.5%. Importantly, Goods Transport Agencies (GTAs) must declare their option to pay GST by 16th August 2022 for the financial year 2022-2023. The notification also introduces definitions for 'clinical establishment' and 'health care services', clarifying the scope of these terms. These amendments will take effect from 18th July 2022, impacting businesses involved in transport and healthcare services.

The notification comes into effect from 18th July 2022.

314.summary: Notification No. 03/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, amends the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Key changes include the omission of several items related to transport services, specifically items (iii), (iv), (v), (va), (vi), and (ix) under serial number 3. The tax rates for items (vii) and (x) have been updated to 12%. New provisions have been introduced for transport services by ropeways and for renting goods carriages that include fuel costs. Additionally, Goods Transport Agencies (GTA) must declare their option to pay GST on services by 16th August 2022 for the financial year 2022-2023. The notification also adds definitions for 'print media', 'clinical establishment', and 'health care services' to clarify these terms. These amendments will take effect from 18th July 2022, impacting businesses involved in transport and healthcare services.

315.summary: Notification No. 04/2022-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, brings significant amendments to the earlier Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate). Key changes include the removal of several services from the tax exemption list, specifically those related to speed post, express parcel post, life insurance, and agency services. Additionally, a new provision has been introduced for tour operator services provided to foreign tourists, which outlines how to calculate exemptions based on the duration of the tour outside India. A new entry has also been added for postal services by the Department of Posts, specifically for post cards and inland letters. Furthermore, the notification clarifies that residential properties rented to registered persons will not qualify for certain exemptions. These changes will take effect from 18th July 2022, impacting businesses involved in these services and requiring them to adjust their tax calculations accordingly.

316.summary: Notification No. 04/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, brings significant amendments to the earlier Notification No. 9/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Key changes include the removal of tax exemptions for services such as speed post, express parcel post, life insurance, and agency services provided to individuals other than government entities. Additionally, new provisions have been introduced for tour operator services offered to foreign tourists, specifying how to determine the exempt value based on the duration of the tour outside India. A new entry has also been added for certain postal services provided by the Department of Posts. Furthermore, several existing entries related to clinical services and recreational activities have been omitted. These amendments will come into effect on 18th July 2022, impacting businesses involved in these services and requiring them to adjust their tax calculations accordingly.

317.summary: Notification No. 05/2022-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, brings significant amendments to the earlier Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. One of the key changes is the removal of the stipulation that suppliers must not have paid central tax at the rate of 6% for certain services. Additionally, a new proviso clarifies that this entry does not apply if the supplier is registered under the CGST Act and has opted to pay tax on Goods Transport Agency (GTA) services under forward charge. The notification also omits specific services from the list of taxable services under serial number 5, including speed post and life insurance. Furthermore, a new entry (5AA) has been added to cover the renting of residential dwellings to registered persons. To , a new Annexure III has been introduced, requiring a declaration from suppliers who are registered and have chosen to pay tax on GTA services. These amendments will come into effect from 18th July 2022, impacting suppliers and service providers in the relevant sectors.

318.summary: Notification No. 05/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, brings several amendments to the earlier Notification No. 10/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Notably, it removes the stipulation that suppliers must have paid integrated tax at 12% for certain services. A new proviso clarifies that this exemption does not apply if the supplier is registered under the CGST Act and has chosen to pay tax on Goods Transport Agency (GTA) services under forward charge. Additionally, the notification omits specific services from serial number 6, including speed post and life insurance, which were previously included. A new entry, serial number 6AA, is added to cover services related to renting residential dwellings to registered persons. Furthermore, Annexure III is introduced, requiring suppliers to declare their registration status and tax payment option for GTA services. These amendments will come into effect on 18th July 2022, impacting suppliers and service providers in the specified categories.

319.summary: The recent Notification No. 6/2022-Central Tax (Rate), issued on July 13, 2022, amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from June 28, 2017. A significant change is the introduction of a new tax rate of 0.75% for goods listed in Schedule VII. Additionally, several entries in Schedules I, II, and III have been modified to replace 'put up in' with 'pre-packaged and labelled,' affecting various food and non-food items. New entries have been added for products such as curd, lassi, and jaggery, while some entries, including S. Nos. 163, 70, and 120, have been omitted. The notification also includes new entries for orthopaedic appliances and solar water heaters, expanding the scope of taxable goods. Furthermore, the descriptions of goods in Schedule VI have been updated, particularly for rough diamonds and synthetic stones, ensuring clarity in tax applicability. These changes will impact manufacturers and sellers of the specified goods, requiring them to adjust their tax calculations and compliance accordingly.

320.summary: The recent Notification No. 6/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, brings several amendments to the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. A significant change is the introduction of a new tax rate of 1.50% for goods listed in the newly created Schedule VII. Additionally, various entries in Schedules I and II have been updated to specify that certain goods must be 'pre-packaged and labelled', which affects items like curd, lassi, and jaggery. New entries have been added, such as curd and lassi under S. No. 9A and jaggery under S. No. 91A. The notification also omits several entries from Schedules I, II, and III, including S. Nos. 163, 70, and 120, which may impact the tax treatment of those goods. Furthermore, Schedule VI has been revised to change the descriptions for rough diamonds and synthetic stones. Lastly, new entries in Schedule III include machinery for cleaning agricultural produce and parts for bicycle pumps, which may affect manufacturers and suppliers in those sectors.

321.summary: Notification No. 7/2022-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, brings several amendments to the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. Key changes include the replacement of the phrase 'other than those put up in' with ', other than pre-packaged and labelled' for various entries, affecting S. Nos. 9, 22, 26, 27, 29, 30B, 45, 46A, 46B, 65-75, 77, and 78. Additionally, S. No. 94 now specifies that Jaggery of all types, including Cane Jaggery and Palmyra Jaggery, is exempt from the pre-packaged and labelled condition. The definition of 'pre-packaged and labelled' has also been updated to comply with the Legal Metrology Act, 2009. Furthermore, S. Nos. 118 and 141 have been omitted from the schedule. These changes will take effect from 18th July 2022, impacting businesses dealing with the specified goods and their packaging requirements.

323.summary: On 13th July 2022, the Government of India issued Notification No. 08/2022-Central Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 3/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. Specifically, this amendment affects the entry in column (4) of the table against S. No. 1, changing the tax rate to 6%. This new rate will be applicable starting from 18th July 2022. Businesses that fall under this category will need to adjust their tax calculations accordingly. The change reflects the government's ongoing efforts to streamline tax rates and with the GST framework. It is important for affected businesses to be aware of this adjustment to avoid any discrepancies in their tax filings. This amendment follows previous changes made by Notification No. 16/2019-Central Tax (Rate) dated 30th September 2019, which had also modified the earlier tax structure. Overall, this notification signifies a shift in the tax landscape that businesses must adapt to promptly.

324.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 08/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 13th July 2022, which amends the earlier Notification No. 3/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. 329.summary: Notification No. 11/2022-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, rescinds the earlier Notification No. 45/2017-Central Tax (Rate) which was published on 14th November 2017. 331.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 12/2022-Central Tax (Rate) on 30th December 2022, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) effective from 1st January 2023. Key changes include updates to the GST rates and descriptions for various goods. For instance, in Schedule I, the entry for S. No. 102A now specifically mentions 'Ethyl alcohol supplied to Oil Marketing Companies or Petroleum refineries for blending with motor spirit (petrol)', while S. No. 103A clarifies the definition of 'Bran, sharps and other residues' by excluding certain animal feeds. Schedule II sees the entry for S. No. 48 revised to specify 'Fruit pulp or fruit juice based drinks', excluding carbonated options, and S. No. 180 now includes 'Mathematical boxes, geometry boxes and colour boxes'. Additionally, Schedule III's S. No. 25 is modified to clarify that it refers to 'Ethyl alcohol and other spirits, denatured', excluding the ethyl alcohol meant for blending with petrol. These changes will impact businesses dealing with these specific goods and their GST compliance.

332.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 12/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 30th December 2022, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Effective from 1st January 2023, several entries in the GST rate schedules have been updated. In Schedule I, the entry for S. No. 102A now specifies that ethyl alcohol supplied to Oil Marketing Companies or petroleum refineries for blending with petrol is included. The entry for S. No. 103A has been revised to provide a detailed description of bran and residues from cereals. Schedule II sees changes as well, with S. No. 48 now referring to fruit pulp or juice-based drinks, excluding carbonated varieties, and S. No. 180 updated to include mathematical and geometry boxes. Lastly, Schedule III's S. No. 25 now clarifies that it pertains to denatured ethyl alcohol and spirits, excluding those supplied for blending with petrol. These changes are significant for businesses dealing with these products, ensuring they are aware of the updated classifications and descriptions.
key_points: Notification No. 12/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate) was issued on 30th December 2022.

In Schedule II, the entry for S. No. 48 is updated to 'Fruit pulp or fruit juice based drinks [other than Carbonated Beverages of Fruit Drink or Carbonated Beverages with Fruit Juice]'.

In Schedule III, the entry for S. No. 25 is amended to specify 'Ethyl alcohol and other spirits, denatured, of any strength [other than ethyl alcohol supplied to Oil Marketing Companies or Petroleum refineries for blending with motor spirit (petrol)]'.
These amendments will take effect from 1st January 2023.

333.summary: Notification No. 13/2022-Central Tax (Rate), issued on December 30, 2022, introduces important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from June 28, 2017. Specifically, it updates the entry for S. No. 102 to include a broader range of animal feeds, such as aquatic feed for shrimp and prawn, as well as poultry and cattle feed, along with various supplements and additives. Additionally, a new entry, S. No. 102C, has been added to cover husks of pulses and certain concentrates. These changes are significant for businesses involved in the production or sale of these feeds, as they may affect the applicable GST rates. The amendments will take effect from January 1, 2023, meaning that businesses should prepare for these changes in their GST calculations and compliance processes.

A new entry is added as S. No. 102C, which includes 'Husk of pulses including Chilka, Concentrates including chuni or churi, Khanda'.
This amendment will come into effect from January 1, 2023.

334.summary: Notification No. 13/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 30th December 2022, brings important amendments to the existing Notification No. 2/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. No. 102 to now include a broader range of animal feeds, specifically mentioning aquatic feed for shrimp and prawn, as well as poultry and cattle feed, along with various supplements and additives. These changes are set to take effect from 1st January 2023, impacting businesses involved in the production and sale of these specific feed products. This amendment aims to clarify and expand the scope of taxable items under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax, reflecting the evolving needs of the agricultural and aquaculture sectors.

335.summary: On 30th December 2022, the Government of India issued Notification No. 14/2022-Central Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 4/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. The key change involves the substitution of entries for S. No. 3A in the Table of the earlier notification. The new entries specifically relate to essential oils other than citrus fruits, detailing various types of mint oils, including peppermint oil and other mints such as spearmint and water mint oils. This amendment is significant for businesses dealing with these essential oils, as it clarifies the GST treatment applicable to them. The changes will take effect from 1st January 2023, providing businesses with a clear timeline for compliance. This amendment follows the last update made to Notification No. 4/2017 by Notification No. 10/2021 on 30th September 2021, ensuring that the GST framework remains current and relevant for stakeholders in this sector.

337.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 15/2022-Central Tax (Rate) on 30th December 2022, which amends the earlier Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This new notification introduces an explanation under S. No. 12, clarifying that the exemption for renting residential dwellings applies when a registered person, who is a proprietor of a proprietorship concern, rents a dwelling for personal use. This means that if the proprietor rents a residential property for their own residence, it qualifies for exemption, provided it is not for the business of the proprietorship. Additionally, S. No. 23A and its related entries have been completely removed from the notification. These amendments will come into effect from 1st January 2023, impacting registered persons who are proprietors and involved in renting residential properties.
key_points: Notification No. 15/2022-Central Tax (Rate) issued on 30th December 2022.
Amends Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

Omission of S. No. 23A and its related entries.
The changes take effect from 1st January 2023.

338.summary: Notification No. 15/2022-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 30th December 2022, introduces important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 9/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate). No. 13, which clarifies that the exemption for renting residential dwellings now includes situations where a registered person, who is a proprietor, rents a dwelling for personal use. This means that if a proprietor rents a residential property in their personal capacity, it qualifies for the exemption, provided it is not done on behalf of their business. Additionally, S. No. 24A and its related entries have been completely removed from the notification. These amendments will take effect from 1st January 2023, impacting registered proprietors who rent residential properties and ensuring clarity on the conditions under which these rentals are exempt from integrated tax.

339.summary: Notification No. 01/2022-Union Territory Tax, issued on 31st March 2022, introduces amendments to the earlier Notification No. 02/2019-Union Territory Tax from 7th March 2019. Specifically, it adds new entries to the Table of the previous notification, which now includes four new categories of products. These categories are: fly ash bricks or aggregates with at least 90% fly ash content, bricks made from fossil meals or similar siliceous earths, building bricks, and earthen or roofing tiles. This change is significant for manufacturers and suppliers of these products as it updates the classification under the Union Territory Goods and Services Act. The amendments will take effect from 1st April 2022, meaning that businesses dealing with these materials should be aware of the new classifications and ensure they are compliant with the updated regulations.

The new entries include: 4. Fly ash bricks or fly ash aggregate with 90% or more fly ash content; 5. Bricks of fossil meals or similar siliceous earths; 6. Building bricks; 7. Earthen or roofing tiles.
The notification comes into force on 1st April 2022.

340.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 01/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 31st March 2022, which amends the previous Notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Effective from 1st April 2022, this new notification removes certain entries from Schedule I, specifically the 2.5% tax rate for serial numbers 225B, 226, 227, and 228. Additionally, it introduces new entries in Schedule II under a 6% tax rate, adding items such as fly ash bricks, bricks of fossil meals, building bricks, and earthen or roofing tiles. These changes will impact businesses dealing with these specific goods, as they will need to adjust their tax calculations and compliance accordingly. The amendments reflect the government's ongoing efforts to refine the tax structure for goods in the Union Territories.

This notification comes into effect on 1st April 2022.

341.summary: On 31st March 2022, the Government of India issued Notification No. 02/2022-Union Territory Tax, which amends the earlier Notification No. 02/2017-Union Territory Tax from 27th June 2017. This amendment introduces new entries to the existing Table in the notification, specifically adding four new items related to construction materials. These include fly ash bricks or aggregates with at least 90% fly ash content, bricks made from fossil meals or similar siliceous earths, standard building bricks, and earthen or roofing tiles. This change is significant for manufacturers and suppliers of these materials, as it updates the classification under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax. The new entries will be effective from 1st April 2022, allowing businesses to adjust their operations accordingly. The principal notification had previously been amended by Notification No. 01/2018-Union Territory Tax on 1st January 2018, making this the latest update in the series.

342.summary: Notification No. 02/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 31st March 2022, introduces a tax exemption for specific intra-state supplies of goods starting from 1st April 2022. The goods affected by this exemption include fly ash bricks, bricks of fossil meals, building bricks, and earthen or roofing tiles, all of which will now be taxed at a reduced rate of 3%. However, businesses must adhere to Condition No. 1, which stipulates that input tax credits cannot be claimed on goods used exclusively for these supplies, and if used partially for other supplies, the credit must be reversed as if these goods are exempt. This change is significant for manufacturers and suppliers of the specified goods, as it directly impacts their tax liabilities and input tax credit eligibility. Overall, this notification aims to support the construction sector by reducing the tax burden on essential building materials.
key_points: This notification is issued under Notification No. 02/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 31st March 2022.
It exempts certain intra-state supplies of goods from union territory tax as specified in the table.

The applicable tax rate for these goods is set at 3%.
Condition No. 1 applies, which restricts input tax credit for these goods under specific circumstances.
This notification comes into effect on 1st April 2022.

343.summary: Notification No. 03/2022-Union Territory Tax, issued on 13th July 2022, introduces an amendment to the earlier Notification No. 02/2019-Union Territory Tax from 7th March 2019. Specifically, it alters the entry listed under serial number 4 in the notification's table. The previous entry has been replaced with 'Fly ash bricks; Fly ash aggregates; Fly ash blocks'. This change is significant for businesses dealing with these materials, as it clarifies the items covered under the Union Territory Tax. The new entry will take effect from 18th July 2022, meaning that any transactions involving these products will need to adhere to the updated classification from that date. This amendment reflects the government's ongoing efforts to refine and clarify tax regulations in the Union Territories, ensuring that businesses have clear guidelines on what is taxable.

344.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 03/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 13th July 2022, which amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017. This amendment removes several items from the tax rate table, specifically items (iii), (iv), (v), (va), (vi), and (ix) under serial number 3. Additionally, the tax rate for items (vii) and (x) has been updated to '6'. A new entry for 'Transport of passengers by ropeways' has been introduced under serial number 8. For Goods Transport Agencies (GTA), the deadline to declare their option to pay GST for the financial year 2022-2023 is set for 16th August 2022. This notification will take effect from 18th July 2022, impacting businesses involved in passenger and goods transport services, particularly those utilizing ropeways and GTAs. The changes aim to streamline tax rates and clarify obligations for service providers in these sectors.

346.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 04/2022 on 13th July 2022, which amends the earlier Notification No. 12/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax rates. This new notification removes several entries related to services such as speed post, express parcel post, life insurance, and agency services, which were previously taxable. Additionally, it introduces a new exemption for tour operator services provided to foreign tourists, where the service is performed both in India and abroad. A new entry has also been added for services provided by the Department of Posts for lightweight postal items. These changes aim to streamline the tax structure and provide clarity on specific services. The amendments will take effect from 18th July 2022, impacting businesses involved in these services and requiring them to adjust their tax calculations accordingly.
key_points: Notification No. 04/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 13th July 2022.

347.summary: Notification No. 05/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, amends the earlier Notification No. 13/2017. One significant change is the removal of the phrase 'who has not paid union territory tax at the rate of 6%' from the first entry in the Table. Additionally, a new proviso has been added, clarifying that this entry does not apply if the supplier is registered under the CGST Act and has chosen to pay tax on Goods Transport Agency (GTA) services under forward charge. Furthermore, the notification eliminates certain services from the fifth entry, specifically those provided to non-government entities. A new entry, 5AA, has been introduced for renting residential dwellings to registered persons. To support these changes, Annexure III has been added, requiring suppliers to declare their registration status and tax payment options. These amendments will take effect from 18th July 2022, impacting suppliers and service providers in the Union Territories.

The changes come into effect on 18th July 2022.

348.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 6/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 13th July 2022, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017. A significant change is the introduction of a new tax rate of 0.75% for goods listed in Schedule VII. Additionally, several entries in Schedules I, II, and III have been modified to include the phrase 'pre-packaged and labelled' for various products, ensuring clarity on the packaging requirements. New entries have been added for items like Curd, Lassi, Butter milk, and Jaggery in Schedule I, while some entries, such as S. Nos. 163, 70, and 197A-E, have been removed. The descriptions for various goods have also been updated, including changes related to leather products and e-waste. Furthermore, the tax rates for rough diamonds and synthetic stones in Schedule VI have been revised. These changes will affect businesses dealing with the specified goods, requiring them to adjust their tax calculations and compliance accordingly.

349.summary: Notification No. 07/2022, issued on 13th July 2022, amends the earlier Notification No. 2/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax rates, with changes effective from 18th July 2022. Nos. 9, 22, 26, and others, where the wording 'other than those put up in' is substituted with ', other than pre-packaged and labelled'. This change clarifies the tax treatment of various food items, including curd and lassi, which are now explicitly categorized as 'other than pre-packaged and labelled'. Additionally, S. No. 94 has been updated to specify that all types of jaggery, including cane and palmyra, are also classified under this new definition. The notification further refines the definition of 'pre-packaged and labelled' to align with the Legal Metrology Act, 2009, ensuring clarity on what constitutes a pre-packaged commodity. These changes primarily affect businesses dealing with food products and packaging, as they will need to adjust their tax calculations and compliance accordingly.
key_points: This notification is issued on 13th July 2022.
It amends Notification No. 2/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The changes will take effect from 18th July 2022.
For S. Nos. 9 and 22, the phrase 'other than those put up in' is replaced with ', other than pre-packaged and labelled'.
For S. No. 26, the entry is changed to 'Curd, Lassi, Butter milk, other than pre-packaged and labelled'.
S. Nos. 27, 29, 30B, 45, 46A, and 46B also have similar changes, replacing 'other than those put up in' with ', other than pre-packaged and labelled'.
S. No. 94 now includes 'Jaggery of all types including Cane Jaggery, Palmyra Jaggery, other than pre-packaged and labelled'.
The definition of 'pre-packaged and labelled' is updated to align with the Legal Metrology Act, 2009.

350.summary: On 13th July 2022, the Government of India issued Notification No. 08/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 3/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. The key change made is in the TABLE under S. No. 1, where the tax rate in column (4) has been updated to '6%'. This adjustment reflects a new tax rate applicable under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax framework. The amendment is significant for businesses operating in Union Territories, as it directly affects the tax they need to charge on their goods and services. The new rate will be effective from 18th July 2022, meaning that any transactions occurring on or after this date will need to adhere to the updated tax rate. This change is part of the government's ongoing efforts to streamline tax regulations and ensure clarity in tax rates for businesses.
key_points: The notification is dated 13th July 2022.
It amends the earlier notification No. 3/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The specific change is in the TABLE, S. No. 1, where the entry in column (4) is changed to '6%'.
This amendment will come into effect on 18th July 2022.

351.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 09/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 13th July 2022, which amends the earlier Notification No. 5/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The amendment replaces the reference to 'serial numbers 1' with 'serial numbers 1AA' in the opening paragraph. Additionally, a new table of entries has been introduced, which includes various oils and their fractions, such as soya-bean oil, ground-nut oil, and olive oil, among others. These new entries are numbered from 1A to 1O and cover a range of vegetable oils and solid fuels. This change is significant for businesses dealing with these products, as it updates the classification and potentially the tax implications for these oils. The new provisions will take effect from 18th July 2022, ensuring that affected businesses are aware of the updated tax structure for these goods.
key_points: Notification No. 09/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 13th July 2022.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 5/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In the opening paragraph, 'serial numbers 1' is replaced with 'serial numbers 1AA'.
A new table entry is added before S. No. 1AA, which includes multiple oils and their fractions, such as soya-bean oil, ground-nut oil, and olive oil.
The new entries range from S. No. 1A to S. No. 1O, covering various vegetable oils and solid fuels.
This notification comes into effect on 18th July 2022.

352.summary: Notification No. 10/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, brings an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 02/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 31st March 2022. This change is made under the authority of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Specifically, it alters the entry in column (3) of the Table against Sl. No. 1. The previous entry has been replaced with 'Fly ash bricks; Fly ash aggregates; Fly ash blocks', which indicates a focus on these specific materials. This amendment is significant for businesses dealing with construction materials, particularly those using fly ash, as it clarifies the tax treatment of these products. The new provisions will take effect from 18th July 2022, meaning that businesses should prepare for this change in their tax calculations and compliance from that date onward.
key_points: This notification is issued as Notification No. 10/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 13th July 2022.
It amends Notification No. 02/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 31st March 2022.
The amendment is made under sub-section (1) of section 8 and clause (v) of section 21 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
In the Table of the earlier notification, against Sl. No. 1, the entry in column (3) is changed.
The new entry in column (3) is 'Fly ash bricks; Fly ash aggregates; Fly ash blocks'.
This amendment will come into force on 18th July 2022.

353.summary: Notification No. 11/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 13th July 2022, brings significant changes by rescinding the earlier Notification No. 45/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 14th November 2017. This means that the provisions and rates specified in the 2017 notification are no longer valid, except for any actions that were already taken under it before this new notification was issued. The change is effective from 18th July 2022, which means that any business or individual operating under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (UTGST) framework will need to be aware of the implications of this rescission. It is crucial for those affected to review the new guidelines and with the updated regulations. This notification reflects the government's ongoing efforts to streamline tax regulations in Union Territories and may lead to changes in tax rates or compliance requirements for businesses operating in these regions.
key_points: Notification No. 11/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 13th July 2022.
This notification rescinds Notification No. 45/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 14th November 2017.
The rescission applies except for actions taken before this notification.
The new notification comes into force on 18th July 2022.

354.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 12/2022 on 30th December 2022, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax rates. Effective from 1st January 2023, several entries in the tax schedules have been updated. In Schedule I, the entry for S. No. 102A now specifies that ethyl alcohol supplied to Oil Marketing Companies or petroleum refineries for blending with petrol is included. The entry for S. No. 103A has been clarified to define bran and residues while excluding certain animal feeds. In Schedule II, the entry for S. No. 48 now refers specifically to fruit pulp or juice-based drinks, excluding carbonated options. Additionally, S. No. 180 in Schedule II has been updated to include mathematical and geometry boxes. Lastly, Schedule III's S. No. 25 has been revised to specify denatured ethyl alcohol and spirits, excluding those supplied for blending with petrol. These changes will affect businesses dealing with these specific goods and services in the Union Territories.
key_points: Notification No. 12/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 30th December 2022.
Amends Notification No. 1/2017 - Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
Changes the entry for S. No. 102A in Schedule I to specify 'Ethyl alcohol supplied to Oil Marketing Companies or Petroleum refineries for blending with motor spirit (petrol)'.
Updates the entry for S. No. 103A in Schedule I to clarify the definition of 'Bran, sharps and other residues' excluding certain feeds.
Modifies the entry for S. No. 48 in Schedule II to specify 'Fruit pulp or fruit juice based drinks' excluding carbonated beverages.
Alters the entry for S. No. 180 in Schedule II to include 'Mathematical boxes, geometry boxes and colour boxes'.
Revises the entry for S. No. 25 in Schedule III to specify 'Ethyl alcohol and other spirits, denatured, of any strength' excluding ethyl alcohol for blending with petrol.
The changes take effect from 1st January 2023.

355.summary: Notification No. 13/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 30th December 2022, introduces amendments to the earlier notification No. 2/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Specifically, it updates the entry for S. No. 102 to include a broader range of animal feeds, such as aquatic feed for shrimp and prawn, poultry feed, and cattle feed, along with various supplements and additives. Additionally, a new entry has been added as S. No. 102C, which covers husk of pulses and concentrates. These changes are significant for businesses involved in the production or sale of these feeds, as they may affect the applicable tax rates. The amendments will take effect from 1st January 2023, meaning businesses should prepare for these changes in their GST calculations and reporting starting from that date.
key_points: This notification is No. 13/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 30th December 2022.
It amends the earlier notification No. 2/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

The entry against S. No. 102 in the Schedule is updated to include 'Aquatic feed including shrimp feed and prawn feed, poultry feed and cattle feed, including grass, hay and straw, supplement and additives, wheat bran and de-oiled cake [other than rice bran]'.
A new entry is added as S. No. 102C, which includes 'Husk of pulses including Chilka, Concentrates including chuni or churi, Khanda'.
This notification will come into effect from 1st January 2023.

356.summary: Notification No. 14/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 30th December 2022, introduces amendments to the earlier Notification No. 4/2017 regarding the Union Territory Tax rates. Specifically, it substitutes the entries for S. No. 3A in the Table, which now includes essential oils other than citrus fruits, such as peppermint and various types of mints. This change is significant for businesses dealing with these essential oils, as it clarifies the tax treatment applicable to them. The new entries will take effect from 1st January 2023, ensuring that businesses are aware of the updated tax implications. This amendment follows a previous update made by Notification No. 10/2021 on 30th September 2021, which also pertained to the same principal notification. Overall, this notification aims to provide clearer guidelines for the taxation of specific essential oils, impacting both registered and unregistered persons involved in their trade.
key_points: Notification No. 14/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 30th December 2022.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 4/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment specifically substitutes the entries for S. No. 3A in the Table of the earlier notification.
The new entries under S. No. 3A relate to essential oils other than citrus fruits, including peppermint and various mints.
The new provisions will come into effect from 1st January 2023.
The previous amendment to Notification No. 4/2017 was made by Notification No. 10/2021 on 30th September 2021.

357.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 15/2022 on 30th December 2022, which amends the earlier Notification No. 12/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax rates. This new notification introduces an explanation to S. No. 12, which now clarifies that services related to renting residential dwellings to a registered person include instances where the registered person is a proprietor renting the dwelling for personal use. Importantly, this renting must be done on the proprietor's own account and not on behalf of their proprietorship concern. Additionally, S. No. 23A and its related entries have been completely removed from the notification. These changes will come into effect starting 1st January 2023, impacting how certain rental services are treated under the Union Territory GST framework. Business owners and registered persons involved in renting residential properties should take note of these amendments to with the updated regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 15/2022-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 30th December 2022.
This notification amends Notification No. 12/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
An explanation is added to S. No. 12, clarifying that renting of residential dwellings to a registered person includes cases where the registered person is a proprietor renting for personal use.
The new explanation specifies that the renting must be on the proprietor's own account, not on behalf of the proprietorship concern.
S. No. 23A and its related entries are omitted from the notification.
The amendments take effect from 1st January 2023.

358.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 1/2021-Compensation Cess (Rate) on 30th September 2021, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This amendment introduces a new entry in the Schedule, specifically S. No. 4B, which imposes a 12% compensation cess on carbonated beverages that are either fruit drinks or contain fruit juice. This change is significant for manufacturers and sellers of these beverages, as they will now need to account for this additional cess in their pricing and compliance. The new rate will be applicable starting from 1st October 2021, meaning businesses should prepare for this change to ensure they are aligned with the updated tax structure. This amendment reflects the government's ongoing adjustments to the GST framework to address various sectors, including the beverage industry.
key_points: Notification No. 1/2021-Compensation Cess (Rate) was issued on 30th September 2021.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new entry, S. No. 4B, is added to the Schedule, specifying a 12% compensation cess on carbonated beverages of fruit drink or carbonated beverages with fruit juice.
The new cess rate comes into effect on 1st October 2021.

359.summary: On 28th December 2021, the Government of India issued Notification No. 02/2021-Compensation Cess (Rate), which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) from 28th June 2017. These changes will come into effect on 1st January 2022. Specifically, for S. No. 25, the entry in column (2) has been updated to '2403 91 00 or 2404 11 00'. Similarly, for S. No. 37 and S. No. 38, the entries have been modified to '2403 99 90 or 2404 11 00 or 2404 19 00'. Lastly, for S. No. 54, the entry is now '8802 or 8806'. These amendments are relevant for businesses dealing with the specified goods under the Compensation Cess framework, ensuring that they are aware of the updated classifications and codes that will apply starting in the new year.
key_points: This notification is issued on 28th December 2021.
It amends Notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendments will take effect from 1st January 2022.
For S. No. 25, the entry in column (2) is changed to '2403 91 00 or 2404 11 00'.
For S. No. 37, the entry in column (2) is changed to '2403 99 90 or 2404 11 00 or 2404 19 00'.
For S. No. 38, the entry in column (2) is changed to '2403 99 90 or 2404 11 00 or 2404 19 00'.
For S. No. 54, the entry in column (2) is changed to '8802 or 8806'.

360.summary: The Notification No. 01/2021-Central Tax, effective from 1st January 2021, introduces significant amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. Specifically, it adds a new sub-rule (6) to rule 59, which imposes restrictions on registered persons regarding the filing of GSTR-1. Under this amendment, if a registered person has not submitted their GSTR-3B returns for the preceding two months, they will not be allowed to furnish details of outward supplies in GSTR-1. Additionally, those required to file quarterly returns must also have submitted their GSTR-3B for the preceding tax period to be eligible to file GSTR-1. Furthermore, registered persons who are restricted from utilizing more than 99% of their electronic credit ledger under rule 86B will face the same requirement. This change affects all registered taxpayers who file GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B, emphasizing the importance of timely compliance with GSTR-3B submissions to maintain the ability to report outward supplies.
key_points: Notification No. 01/2021-Central Tax was issued on 1st January 2021.
This notification amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
A new sub-rule (6) is added to rule 59 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
Registered persons cannot file GSTR-1 if they haven't submitted GSTR-3B for the preceding two months.
Quarterly filers must also submit GSTR-3B for the preceding tax period to file GSTR-1.
Those restricted under rule 86B from using electronic credit ledger over 99% cannot file GSTR-1 without submitting GSTR-3B for the preceding tax period.

361.summary: The recently issued Notification No. 01/2021-Integrated Tax on May 1, 2021, amends the earlier Notification No. 6/2017-Integrated Tax from June 28, 2017. This amendment introduces new interest rate provisions for taxpayers based on their aggregate turnover for the months of March and April 2021. Specifically, taxpayers with an aggregate turnover exceeding 5 crores will incur an interest rate of 9% for the first 15 days past the due date, followed by 18% thereafter. For those with a turnover of up to 5 crores, the interest is nil for the first 15 days, 9% for the next 15 days, and then 18% after that. Additionally, the notification outlines interest rates for various categories of taxpayers required to file returns under different sections of the GST Act. Importantly, this amendment is effective retroactively from April 18, 2021, impacting how interest is calculated for late payments during this period.
key_points: Notification No. 01/2021-Integrated Tax was issued on 2021-05-01.
This notification amends Notification No. 6/2017-Integrated Tax dated 28th June 2017.
It introduces new entries in the interest rate table for taxpayers with aggregate turnover exceeding and up to 5 crores.
For taxpayers with turnover over 5 crores, the interest rate is 9% for the first 15 days from the due date and 18% thereafter for March and April 2021.
For taxpayers with turnover up to 5 crores, the interest rate is nil for the first 15 days, 9% for the next 15 days, and 18% thereafter for March and April 2021.
The notification also specifies interest rates for taxpayers required to furnish returns under different sections of the GST Act.
This amendment is deemed to have come into force from 18th April 2021.

362.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 02/2021-Central Tax on 12th January 2021, which amends the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax from 19th June 2017. This amendment updates the entries for the Commissioners of Appeals in Table I. Specifically, for Sl. No. 7, the entry now identifies the 'Commissioner (Appeals I) Delhi and Additional Commissioner (Appeals II) Delhi' as the relevant authorities. Similarly, for Sl. No. 14, it now specifies 'Commissioner (Appeals II) Mumbai and Additional Commissioner (Appeals I) Mumbai'. Additionally, Table III has been updated to include two notes that clarify the jurisdiction of these Commissioners. Note 1 states that the Commissioner (Appeals I) Delhi will oversee both Delhi I and Delhi II, while Note 2 indicates that the Commissioner (Appeals II) Mumbai will have jurisdiction over Mumbai I and Mumbai II. This change is significant for businesses and taxpayers in these regions as it clarifies the authority structure for appeals related to GST matters.
key_points: Notification No. 02/2021-Central Tax was issued on 12th January 2021.
This notification amends Notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax dated 19th June 2017.
In Table I, Sl. No. 7 now specifies 'Commissioner (Appeals I) Delhi and Additional Commissioner (Appeals II) Delhi' instead of the previous entry.
In Table I, Sl. No. 14 now specifies 'Commissioner (Appeals II) Mumbai and Additional Commissioner (Appeals I) Mumbai' instead of the previous entry.
Table III now includes two new notes regarding jurisdiction for the Commissioners of Appeals in Delhi and Mumbai.
Note 1 clarifies that the Commissioner (Appeals I) Delhi has jurisdiction over Delhi I and Delhi II.
Note 2 clarifies that the Commissioner (Appeals II) Mumbai has jurisdiction over Mumbai I and Mumbai II.

363.summary: The recently issued Notification No. 02/2021-Integrated Tax, effective from May 18, 2021, brings several amendments to the earlier Notification No. 6/2017-Integrated Tax. Notably, the language regarding taxpayers who fail to pay tax has been simplified; it now states 'liable to pay tax but fail to do so'. Additionally, the heading 'Tax period' in the interest rate table has been updated to 'Month/Quarter'. The tax periods specified for interest calculations have been expanded to include March, April, and May 2021. For taxpayers with an aggregate turnover exceeding 5 crores, the interest rate is set at 9% for the first 15 days post due date, escalating to 18% thereafter for these three months. Meanwhile, those with a turnover of up to 5 crores will see different interest rates based on the month and the duration past the due date. These changes aim to clarify the interest structure and align it with the recent financial periods.
key_points: Notification No. 02/2021-Integrated Tax was issued on 2021-06-01.
This notification amends Notification No. 6/2017-Integrated Tax dated 28th June 2017.
The phrase 'required to furnish the returns in FORM GSTR-3B, but fail to furnish the said return along with payment of tax' is replaced with 'liable to pay tax but fail to do so'.
In the Table, the heading 'Tax period' is changed to 'Month/Quarter'.
For serial numbers 4, 5, 6, and 7 in the Table, the tax periods now include March, April, and May 2021.
Taxpayers with an aggregate turnover of more than 5 crores will face 9% interest for the first 15 days from the due date and 18% thereafter for the months of March, April, and May 2021.
Taxpayers with an aggregate turnover of up to 5 crores will have varying interest rates based on the month and the time elapsed since the due date.
This notification is effective from 18th May 2021.

364.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 03/2021-Central Tax on 23rd February 2021, which supersedes the earlier Notification No. 17/2020-Central Tax from 23rd March 2020. This new notification specifically amends the provisions of sub-sections (6B) and (6C) of section 25 of the Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) Act. It states that these provisions will not apply to certain entities, including individuals who are not citizens of India, various government departments, local authorities, statutory bodies, Public Sector Undertakings, and those applying for registration under sub-section (9) of section 25. This change is significant as it clarifies the registration requirements for these specific groups, ensuring they are not subject to the previously applicable provisions. As a result, these entities can navigate the registration process under the CGST Act without the constraints imposed by sub-sections (6B) and (6C).
key_points: Notification No. 03/2021-Central Tax issued on 23rd February 2021.
Supersedes Notification No. 17/2020-Central Tax dated 23rd March 2020.
Amends provisions of sub-section (6B) and (6C) of section 25 of the CGST Act.
Exempts certain entities from the provisions of sub-section (6B) and (6C).
Entities exempted include non-citizens, Central and State Government departments, local authorities, statutory bodies, and Public Sector Undertakings.
Also includes individuals applying for registration under sub-section (9) of section 25.

365.summary: On June 2, 2021, the Government of India issued Notification No. 03/2021-Integrated Tax, which amends the earlier Notification No. 04/2019-Integrated Tax from September 30, 2019. This amendment introduces a new entry in Table A, specifically after serial number 2. The new entry, numbered 3, addresses the supply of maintenance, repair, or overhaul services related to ships and other vessels. Importantly, it specifies that the place of supply for these services will be the location of the recipient. This change is effective immediately from the date of the notification. The previous principal notification had been amended earlier by Notification No. 02/2020-Integrated Tax on March 26, 2020. This update is particularly relevant for businesses involved in maritime services, ensuring clarity on the place of supply for tax purposes.
key_points: Notification No. 03/2021-Integrated Tax was issued on 2021-06-02.
This notification amends Notification No. 04/2019-Integrated Tax dated 30th September 2019.
A new entry is added in Table A after serial number 2.
The new entry (serial number 3) pertains to the supply of maintenance, repair, or overhaul services for ships and other vessels.
The place of supply for these services is defined as the location of the recipient.
This amendment is effective from 2021-06-02.
The principal notification was last amended by Notification No. 02/2020-Integrated Tax dated 26th March 2020.

366.summary: Notification No. 04/2021-Central Tax, issued on 28th February 2021, amends the earlier Notification No. 95/2020-Central Tax from 30th December 2020. The key change is the extension of a deadline, where the date '28.02.2021' has been replaced with '31.03.2021'. This amendment is made under the authority of section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and follows the provisions of rule 80 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This change affects businesses that were required to comply with the original deadline, giving them additional time until the end of March 2021. It is important for affected businesses to note this new deadline for their compliance requirements. The extension aims to provide relief and facilitate smoother compliance for taxpayers during this period.
key_points: This notification is issued on 28th February 2021.
It amends Notification No. 95/2020-Central Tax dated 30th December 2020.
The amendment changes the date from '28.02.2021' to '31.03.2021'.
The amendment is made under section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It is also in accordance with rule 80 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

367.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 05/2021-Central Tax on 8th March 2021, which amends the earlier Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax dated 21st March 2020. The key change in this amendment is the reduction of the threshold for certain GST compliance requirements from 'one hundred crore rupees' to 'fifty crore rupees'. This new threshold will take effect from 1st April 2021. This amendment is made under sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, which governs the filing of returns and related compliance. The principal notification had previously been amended by Notification No. 88/2020-Central Tax on 10th November 2020. Businesses with a turnover exceeding fifty crore rupees will need to be aware of this change, as it may affect their GST filing obligations and compliance processes moving forward.
key_points: Notification No. 05/2021-Central Tax was issued on 8th March 2021.
This notification amends Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax dated 21st March 2020.
The amendment changes the threshold from 'one hundred crore rupees' to 'fifty crore rupees'.
The new threshold will be effective from 1st April 2021.
This change is made under sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The principal notification was last amended by Notification No. 88/2020-Central Tax dated 10th November 2020.

368.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 06/2021-Central Tax on 30th March 2021, which amends the earlier Notification No. 89/2020-Central Tax dated 29th November 2020. This amendment specifically changes two important dates related to the GST compliance timeline. Firstly, the deadline previously set for '31st day of March' has now been extended to '30th day of June'. Secondly, the start date that was '01st day of April' is now revised to '1st day of July'. These changes are made under the authority of section 128 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This notification will primarily affect businesses and taxpayers who are required to comply with GST regulations, as they will now have additional time to meet their obligations. It is crucial for affected parties to take note of these new dates to ensure they align their compliance activities accordingly.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 89/2020-Central Tax dated 29th November 2020.
Changes the date from '31st day of March' to '30th day of June'.
Changes the date from '01st day of April' to '1st day of July'.
The amendments are made under section 128 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

369.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 07/2021-Central Tax on 27th April 2021, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. Specifically, it introduces a new proviso in rule 26(1) that allows registered persons under the Companies Act, 2013 to file their GSTR-3B and GSTR-1 returns during a specified period from 27th April 2021 to 31st May 2021. This is a significant change as it provides these businesses with an extended timeframe to comply with their filing obligations. Additionally, the returns can be submitted using an electronic verification code (EVC), simplifying the process for these companies. This amendment is part of ongoing efforts to streamline GST compliance for businesses, particularly in light of the challenges posed by the pandemic. The principal GST rules were first published in June 2017 and have undergone several amendments, with the last one occurring in January 2021.
key_points: Notification No. 07/2021-Central Tax was issued on 27th April 2021.
This notification amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The amendment inserts a new proviso in rule 26(1) after the third proviso.
The new proviso allows registered persons under the Companies Act, 2013 to file GSTR-3B and GSTR-1 from 27th April 2021 to 31st May 2021.
Returns can be filed using electronic verification code (EVC).
The principal rules were first published on 19th June 2017 and last amended on 1st January 2021.

370.summary: Notification No. 08/2021-Central Tax, issued on May 1, 2021, introduces amendments to Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax, specifically updating the interest rates applicable for late filing of returns. The changes affect taxpayers based on their aggregate turnover for the preceding financial year. For those with an aggregate turnover exceeding 5 crores, the interest rate is set at 9% for the first 15 days after the due date, increasing to 18% thereafter for the tax periods of March and April 2021. Meanwhile, taxpayers with an aggregate turnover of up to 5 crores will have a nil interest rate for the first 15 days, followed by 9% for the next 15 days, and then 18% thereafter. This amendment is significant as it clarifies the financial implications for different categories of taxpayers regarding their compliance with GST return filing. The changes are effective retroactively from April 18, 2021, ensuring that all affected taxpayers are aware of their obligations and the associated interest rates for late submissions.
key_points: Notification No. 08/2021-Central Tax was issued on 2021-05-01.
This notification amends Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax, specifically the first proviso in the Table.
New entries have been added for taxpayers with an aggregate turnover of more than 5 crores and those with up to 5 crores for the tax periods of March 2021 and April 2021.
For taxpayers with more than 5 crores, the interest rate is 9% for the first 15 days from the due date and 18% thereafter.
For taxpayers with up to 5 crores, the interest rate is nil for the first 15 days, 9% for the next 15 days, and 18% thereafter.
The notification is deemed to have come into force from 2021-04-18.

371.summary: The recently issued Notification No. 09/2021-Central Tax, effective from April 20, 2021, introduces amendments to Notification No. 76/2018-Central Tax. It adds a new proviso that waives late fees under section 47 for certain registered persons who fail to file their GSTR-3B returns by the due date. Specifically, for taxpayers with an aggregate turnover exceeding 5 crores, late fees for the tax periods of March and April 2021 will be waived for 15 days from the respective due dates. For those with a turnover of up to 5 crores, the waiver extends to 30 days from the due date for the same months. Additionally, for the January-March 2021 period, these smaller taxpayers will also benefit from a 30-day waiver from the due date. This change aims to provide relief to businesses during challenging times by easing the financial burden of late fees.
key_points: Notification No. 09/2021-Central Tax was issued on 2021-05-01.
This notification amends Notification No. 76/2018-Central Tax dated 31st December 2018.
A new proviso is added after the seventh proviso in Notification No. 76/2018.
The new proviso waives late fees under section 47 for specific tax periods.
For taxpayers with turnover over 5 crores, late fees are waived for March and April 2021 for 15 days from the due date.
For taxpayers with turnover up to 5 crores, late fees are waived for March and April 2021 for 30 days from the due date.
For taxpayers with turnover up to 5 crores, late fees are waived for January-March 2021 for 30 days from the due date.
This notification is effective from 20th April 2021.

372.summary: Notification No. 10/2021-Central Tax, issued on May 1, 2021, introduces an amendment to Notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax, which was originally published on April 23, 2019. This amendment adds a new proviso that requires certain individuals to submit their GSTR-4 return for the financial year ending March 31, 2021, by May 31, 2021. This change is significant for those who fall under this category, as it provides a specific deadline for filing their returns. The amendment is effective from April 30, 2021, meaning that the new requirement is already in place. This notification also notes that the principal notification had been previously amended by Notification No. 64/2020-Central Tax on August 31, 2020. Business owners and clients who are required to file GSTR-4 should take note of this new deadline to with the updated regulations.
key_points: This notification is issued under Notification No. 10/2021-Central Tax on May 1, 2021.
It amends Notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax, originally issued on April 23, 2019.
A new proviso is added after the first proviso in the third paragraph of Notification No. 21/2019.
The new proviso states that certain persons must file their GSTR-4 return for the financial year ending March 31, 2021, by May 31, 2021.
The notification is effective from April 30, 2021.
The principal notification was last amended by Notification No. 64/2020-Central Tax on August 31, 2020.

373.summary: Notification No. 11/2021-Central Tax, issued on May 1, 2021, extends the deadline for businesses to file FORM GST ITC-04 until May 31, 2021. This form is crucial for reporting goods that were dispatched to or received from job workers during the period from January 1, 2021, to March 31, 2021. The extension is beneficial for businesses that may have faced challenges in meeting the original deadline. The notification is effective retroactively from April 25, 2021, allowing businesses to take advantage of this additional time. This change is part of the government's ongoing efforts to support businesses in complying with GST regulations, particularly during challenging periods. It is important for businesses engaged in job work to be aware of this extension to ensure they can accurately report their transactions without the pressure of an imminent deadline.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing FORM GST ITC-04 to May 31, 2021.
Covers goods dispatched to or received from job workers between January 1, 2021, and March 31, 2021.
The notification is effective from April 25, 2021.
Issued under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

374.summary: Notification No. 12/2021-Central Tax, issued on May 1, 2021, introduces an important amendment to Notification No. 83/2020-Central Tax, which was originally published on November 10, 2020. The key change is the addition of a new proviso that extends the deadline for filing GSTR-1 for the tax period of April 2021. Registered persons who are required to furnish returns under sub-section (1) of section 39 of the CGST Act will now have until the 26th day of the month following April 2021 to submit their GSTR-1. This amendment is significant as it provides additional time for compliance, which was not previously specified for this tax period in the parent notification. The change is effective immediately, allowing affected taxpayers to plan their filing accordingly. This amendment is made under the authority of sections 37 and 168 of the CGST Act, ensuring that the process remains aligned with the regulatory framework.
key_points: Notification No. 12/2021-Central Tax was issued on 2021-05-01.
This notification amends Notification No. 83/2020-Central Tax dated 2020-11-10.
A new proviso is added to extend the GSTR-1 filing deadline for April 2021.
The new deadline for GSTR-1 for April 2021 is now the 26th day of the month succeeding the tax period.
The amendment applies to registered persons required to furnish returns under sub-section (1) of section 39.
The previous deadline for GSTR-1 filing was not specified for April 2021 in the parent notification.
This change is effective immediately from the date of the new notification.
The amendment is made under the powers conferred by section 37 and section 168 of the CGST Act.

375.summary: The Central Goods and Services Tax (Third Amendment) Rules, 2021, were issued on May 1, 2021, and introduce important changes regarding input tax credit and filing procedures. A new proviso has been added to rule 36, which states that the conditions for claiming input tax credit will apply cumulatively for the months of April and May 2021. This means that businesses will need to account for input tax credit adjustments for both months when filing their GSTR-3B return for May 2021. Additionally, rule 59 has been amended to allow registered persons to submit details for April 2021 using the Invoice Furnishing Facility (IFF) from May 1 to May 28, 2021. These changes are particularly relevant for businesses that need to manage their input tax credit claims and filing timelines effectively during this period.
key_points: This notification is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Third Amendment) Rules, 2021.
The rules come into force on the date of publication in the Official Gazette, which is May 1, 2021.
A new proviso is added to sub-rule (4) of rule 36, stating that conditions for input tax credit apply cumulatively for April and May 2021.
The return in FORM GSTR-3B for May 2021 must include cumulative adjustments for input tax credit for April and May 2021.
A new proviso is added to sub-rule (2) of rule 59, allowing registered persons to furnish details for April 2021 using IFF from May 1 to May 28, 2021.

376.summary: Notification No. 14/2021-Central Tax, issued on May 1, 2021, extends various compliance deadlines due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Specifically, any compliance actions that were due between April 15, 2021, and May 30, 2021, now have an extended deadline of May 31, 2021. This includes proceedings, orders, notices, and the filing of appeals or documents. However, certain provisions are excluded from this extension, such as those related to Chapter IV and specific sections like 10(3), 25, and 39, among others. Additionally, for actions under rule 9 of the GST Rules, the deadline is further extended to June 15, 2021. The notification also addresses refund claims, allowing an extension for issuing orders to 15 days after a response is received or until May 31, 2021, whichever is later. This notification is effective from April 15, 2021, and aims to provide relief to taxpayers during the ongoing pandemic.
key_points: Extends time limits for compliance actions from April 15, 2021, to May 30, 2021, to May 31, 2021.
Applicable to completion of proceedings, passing of orders, issuance of notices, and filing of appeals or documents.
Excludes compliance for specific provisions including Chapter IV and sections 10(3), 25, 27, 31, 37, 47, 50, 69, 90, 122, 129, and section 39 (except subsections 3, 4, and 5).
E-way bill compliance under section 68 is also excluded from the extension.
For actions under rule 9 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, the deadline is extended to June 15, 2021.
Extends the time limit for issuing orders on refund claims to 15 days after receiving a reply or to May 31, 2021, whichever is later.
Notification effective from April 15, 2021.

377.summary: The recent Notification No. 15/2021-Central Tax, issued on 18th May 2021, introduces several amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. Notably, Rule 23(1) now permits an extension of the time frame for cancellation of registration, which can be granted by the Additional Commissioner, Joint Commissioner, or Commissioner. Additionally, a new proviso in Rule 90(3) states that the time taken to communicate deficiencies in FORM GST RFD-03 will not count towards the two-year limit for filing refund claims. Furthermore, Rule 90(5) allows taxpayers to withdraw their refund applications using the newly introduced FORM GST RFD-01W before any refund orders are issued. The amended FORM GST RFD-07 now requires detailed reasons for withholding refunds, enhancing transparency. Lastly, Rule 138E has been updated to clarify that it pertains to the outward movement of goods, rather than just registered persons. These changes aim to streamline processes and provide more flexibility for taxpayers in managing their GST-related claims and registrations.
key_points: Notification No. 15/2021-Central Tax was issued on 18th May 2021.
The rules amend the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
Rule 23(1) now allows an extension of time for cancellation of registration by the Additional Commissioner, Joint Commissioner, or Commissioner.
A new proviso in Rule 90(3) excludes the time taken to communicate deficiencies in FORM GST RFD-03 from the two-year period for refund claims.
Rule 90(5) allows applicants to withdraw refund applications using FORM GST RFD-01W before the issuance of provisional or final refund orders.
FORM GST RFD-07 has been substituted to include detailed reasons for withholding refunds.
The term in Rule 138E has been changed to specify 'outward movement of goods' instead of just 'registered person'.
A new FORM GST RFD-01W has been introduced for the withdrawal of refund applications.

378.summary: Notification No. 16/2021-Central Tax, issued on 1st June 2021, marks the commencement of section 112 of the Finance Act, 2021. This means that the provisions outlined in this section are now officially in effect as of the same date. The Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs has confirmed this change, which is significant for businesses and individuals affected by the new regulations introduced in the Finance Act. While the specific details of section 112 are not provided in this notification, its activation indicates that stakeholders should be aware of the implications it may have on their operations. This change is part of the government's ongoing efforts to update and streamline tax regulations, and it is essential for those impacted to review the provisions of the Finance Act to understand how they may be affected. Overall, this notification signifies an important step in the implementation of new tax measures in India.
key_points: Notification No. 16/2021-Central Tax was issued on 1st June 2021.
The notification appoints 1st June 2021 as the effective date for the provisions of section 112 of the Finance Act, 2021.
Section 112 of the Finance Act, 2021 is now in force as of the appointed date.
This notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.

379.summary: Notification No. 17/2021-Central Tax, issued on June 1, 2021, amends the earlier Notification No. 83/2020-Central Tax, which was published on November 10, 2020. The key change involves the addition of 'and May, 2021' to the second proviso of the earlier notification. This amendment specifically extends the deadline for certain registered persons to file their GSTR-1 returns for the month of May 2021. This means that businesses required to submit their GSTR-1 for May now have additional time to do so. The amendment is effective immediately from the date of this notification. It's important to note that the principal notification had already been amended previously by Notification No. 12/2021-Central Tax on May 1, 2021. This latest change is aimed at providing relief to taxpayers during the ongoing challenges faced in compliance with GST requirements.
key_points: Notification No. 17/2021-Central Tax was issued on 2021-06-01.
This notification amends Notification No. 83/2020-Central Tax dated 2020-11-10.
The amendment adds the words 'and May, 2021' after 'April, 2021' in the second proviso.
The second proviso pertains to the time limit for furnishing GSTR-1 for certain registered persons.
The amendment allows for the extension of the GSTR-1 filing deadline for May 2021.
This change is effective immediately upon the notification's issuance date.
The principal notification 83/2020 was previously amended by notification No. 12/2021-Central Tax on 2021-05-01.

380.summary: Notification No. 18/2021-Central Tax, issued on June 1, 2021, brings significant amendments to Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax. The key change involves replacing the wording regarding late fees for taxpayers who are liable to pay tax but fail to do so. Additionally, the heading 'Tax period' in the Table has been updated to 'Month/Quarter'. The entries for serial numbers 4, 5, 6, and 7 have been completely revised to introduce new late fee structures based on the aggregate turnover of taxpayers. For those with an aggregate turnover exceeding 5 crores, the late fee is set at 9% for the first 15 days after the due date and 18% thereafter for the months of March, April, and May 2021. For taxpayers with an aggregate turnover up to 5 crores, the late fee varies depending on the month, with specific grace periods and percentages outlined for each month. This notification is effective from May 18, 2021, and affects all registered taxpayers under the GST regime, particularly those with varying turnover levels.
key_points: Notification No. 18/2021-Central Tax was issued on 2021-06-01.
This notification amends Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax, originally issued on 2017-06-30.
The phrase 'required to furnish the returns in FORM GSTR-3B, but fail to furnish the said return along with payment of tax' is replaced with 'liable to pay tax but fail to do so'.
In the Table, the heading 'Tax period' is changed to 'Month/Quarter'.
The entries for serial numbers 4, 5, 6, and 7 in the Table have been substituted with new provisions regarding late fees.
The new provisions specify different late fee structures for taxpayers with aggregate turnover above and below 5 crores for the months of March, April, and May 2021.
This notification is deemed to have come into force from 2021-05-18.

381.summary: Notification No. 19/2021-Central Tax, issued on June 1, 2021, introduces significant changes to the late fee structure for GSTR-3B returns. It amends the eighth proviso of Notification No. 76/2018-Central Tax, effective from May 20, 2021. The updated table specifies that taxpayers with an aggregate turnover exceeding 5 crores will have their late fees waived for 15 days from the due date for returns due in March, April, and May 2021. For those with turnover up to 5 crores, the waiver extends to 60 days for March, 45 days for April, and 30 days for May 2021. Additionally, registered persons who file their GSTR-3B returns between June 1 and August 31, 2021, for periods from July 2017 to April 2021, will have late fees waived above 500 rupees, or 250 rupees if the tax payable is nil. Furthermore, for returns from June 2021 onwards, late fees will be capped at specified amounts based on the taxpayer's turnover, providing relief to many businesses during this period.
key_points: Notification No. 19/2021-Central Tax was issued on 2021-06-01.
This notification amends the eighth proviso of Notification No. 76/2018-Central Tax, effective from 2021-05-20.
The new table substitutes the previous one for waiving late fees for GSTR-3B returns for March, April, and May 2021.
For taxpayers with turnover over 5 crores, late fees are waived for 15 days from the due date.
For taxpayers with turnover up to 5 crores, late fees are waived for 60 days for March 2021, 45 days for April 2021, and 30 days for May 2021.
Additional provisions waive late fees for GSTR-3B returns filed between 2021-06-01 and 2021-08-31 for returns due from July 2017 to April 2021.
If the total central tax payable is nil, late fees are waived above 250 rupees for the same period.
For returns from June 2021 onwards, late fees are capped at specified amounts based on turnover.

382.summary: Notification No. 20/2021-Central Tax, issued on June 1, 2021, introduces a significant amendment to the earlier Notification No. 4/2018-Central Tax. This new provision waives the late fee for registered persons who fail to submit their GSTR-1 by the due date, specifically for tax periods starting from June 2021. The waiver is structured based on the registered person's aggregate turnover from the previous financial year. For those with nil outward supplies, the late fee is capped at ₹250. If a registered person's turnover is up to ₹1.5 crores, the maximum late fee waived is ₹1,000. For those with a turnover exceeding ₹1.5 crores but not exceeding ₹5 crores, the cap is set at ₹2,500. This amendment aims to provide relief to various classes of registered persons by reducing their financial burden related to late filing of GSTR-1 during these periods.
key_points: Notification No. 20/2021-Central Tax was issued on 2021-06-01.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 4/2018-Central Tax dated 23rd January 2018.
A new proviso is added after the fourth proviso in Notification No. 4/2018.
The new proviso waives the late fee under section 47 for GSTR-1 for June 2021 onwards.
The waiver applies to registered persons based on their aggregate turnover in the preceding financial year.
For nil outward supplies, the waived late fee is capped at ₹250.
For aggregate turnover up to ₹1.5 crores, the waived late fee is capped at ₹1,000.
For aggregate turnover between ₹1.5 crores and ₹5 crores, the waived late fee is capped at ₹2,500.
