537.summary: Notification No. 56/2020-Central Tax, issued on 27th June 2020, brings important changes to the compliance deadlines set in Notification No. 46/2020-Central Tax from 9th June 2020. Specifically, the due date for a particular compliance requirement has been extended from 29th June 2020 to 30th August 2020. Additionally, another compliance deadline has been pushed back from 30th June 2020 to 31st August 2020. This extension provides businesses with additional time to meet their obligations under the GST framework. The changes are aimed at easing the compliance burden on taxpayers during this period. Businesses that were originally expected to complete their filings by the end of June now have until the end of August to do so. This amendment reflects the government's ongoing efforts to support taxpayers amid challenging circumstances.
key_points: Notification No. 56/2020-Central Tax was issued on 27th June 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 46/2020-Central Tax dated 9th June 2020.
The due date for certain compliance has been extended from 29th June 2020 to 30th August 2020.
The due date for another compliance has been extended from 30th June 2020 to 31st August 2020.

538.summary: Notification No. 57/2020-Central Tax, issued on 30th June 2020, introduces amendments to Notification No. 76/2018-Central Tax. Specifically, it adds new provisions regarding the waiver of late fees for GSTR-3B returns. Taxpayers who fail to file their returns for the specified periods but do so by 30th September 2020 will have their late fees waived, provided the fees exceed ₹250. Additionally, those with nil central tax in their returns will have the entire late fee waived. This applies particularly to taxpayers with an aggregate turnover exceeding ₹5 crores who did not file their GSTR-3B for the months of May, June, and July 2020 by the due date. The changes are effective from 25th June 2020, allowing affected taxpayers some relief from penalties during this period. This amendment aims to ease the compliance burden on businesses during challenging times.
key_points: This notification is issued on 30th June 2020.
It amends Notification No. 76/2018-Central Tax dated 31st December 2018.
A new proviso is added after the third proviso in the earlier notification.
Late fees for GSTR-3B returns filed by 30th September 2020 will be waived for certain taxpayers.
Taxpayers with nil central tax in their returns will have the entire late fee waived.
Taxpayers with an aggregate turnover over ₹5 crores who fail to file GSTR-3B for May to July 2020 will also benefit from this waiver.
The effective date of this notification is deemed to be 25th June 2020.

539.summary: Notification No. 58/2020-Central Tax, issued on 1st July 2020, introduces significant changes to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. Specifically, it amends rule 67A to allow registered persons to file Nil returns through a short messaging service (SMS). This is applicable for Nil returns in FORM GSTR-3B and FORM GSTR-1, making it easier for taxpayers to comply with their filing obligations. The SMS filing process will require verification through a One Time Password (OTP) sent to the registered mobile number, enhancing security. A Nil return is defined as one that has no entries in all tables of the respective forms. This amendment is effective immediately from 1st July 2020, following the previous amendment made by notification No. 50/2020-Central Tax on 24th June 2020. This change is particularly beneficial for those who may find electronic filing cumbersome, providing a more accessible option for submitting their returns.
key_points: Notification No. 58/2020-Central Tax was issued on 1st July 2020.
This notification amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The new rule 67A allows registered persons to file Nil returns via SMS.
The SMS filing is applicable for Nil returns in FORM GSTR-3B and FORM GSTR-1.
The SMS filing can be verified using a One Time Password sent to the registered mobile number.
A Nil return is defined as having no entries in all tables of FORM GSTR-3B or FORM GSTR-1.
This amendment comes into force on 1st July 2020.
The previous amendment was made by notification No. 50/2020-Central Tax on 24th June 2020.

540.summary: Notification No. 59/2020-Central Tax, issued on 13th July 2020, brings an important change to the earlier Notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax from 23rd April 2019. Specifically, it extends the deadline for a certain compliance requirement from the original date of 15th July 2020 to a new date of 31st August 2020. This change is made under the authority of section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The amendment aims to provide additional time for businesses to meet their obligations, reflecting the government's response to the challenges faced by taxpayers. This notification is particularly relevant for those who were affected by the previous deadline and need the extra time to . The principal notification had previously been amended by Notification No. 34/2020-Central Tax on 3rd April 2020, indicating ongoing adjustments to support taxpayers during this period.
key_points: Notification No. 59/2020-Central Tax was issued on 13th July 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax dated 23rd April 2019.
The amendment changes the deadline in the first proviso of the third paragraph.
The original deadline of '15th day of July, 2020' is now extended to '31st day of August, 2020'.
The amendment is made under the powers conferred by section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The principal notification was last amended by Notification No. 34/2020-Central Tax dated 3rd April 2020.

541.summary: Notification No. 60/2020-Central Tax, issued on July 30, 2020, introduces significant changes to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, specifically replacing FORM GST INV-01 with the new FORM GST INV-1 for e-Invoices. This amendment is effective immediately upon publication in the Official Gazette. The new form includes detailed specifications for various fields that businesses must adhere to when issuing e-Invoices. Key mandatory fields now include the Invoice Reference Number, Document Type Code, Document Number, and Document Date, ensuring that essential information is consistently captured. Optional fields such as Additional Currency Code and Reverse Charge provide flexibility for businesses. The updated schema version for this e-Invoice format is 1.1, which reflects the latest requirements for electronic invoicing. This change primarily affects businesses that are required to issue e-Invoices, ensuring they comply with the updated format and specifications.
key_points: Notification No. 60/2020-Central Tax was issued on 30th July 2020.
This notification amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
FORM GST INV-01 is replaced with FORM GST INV-1, which is the new format/schema for e-Invoice.
The new rules come into effect on the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
The new FORM GST INV-1 includes detailed specifications for various fields such as Invoice Reference Number, Document Type Code, and Supplier Information.
Mandatory fields in the new form include Invoice Reference Number, Document Type Code, Document Number, and Document Date.
Optional fields include Additional Currency Code and Reverse Charge, among others.
The schema version for the new e-Invoice format is 1.1.

542.summary: The recent amendment under Notification No. 61/2020-Central Tax, issued on 30th July 2020, modifies the earlier Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax from 21st March 2020. Specifically, it adds the phrase 'a Special Economic Zone unit and' to clarify the types of units referred to in the notification. Additionally, the financial threshold for certain provisions has been increased from 'one hundred crore rupees' to 'five hundred crore rupees'. This change is significant for businesses operating in Special Economic Zones, as it expands the scope of eligibility under the GST framework. The amendment is enacted under sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, which governs the filing of returns. Businesses should review these changes to understand how they may impact their compliance and reporting obligations under GST.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax dated 21st March 2020.
Inserts the phrase 'a Special Economic Zone unit and' before 'those referred to in sub-rules' in the first paragraph.
Changes the threshold from 'one hundred crore rupees' to 'five hundred crore rupees'.
The amendment is made under sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

543.summary: Notification No. 62/2020-Central Tax, issued on 20th August 2020, introduces significant amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. Effective from 21st August 2020, applicants who choose to authenticate their Aadhaar number must do so while submitting their application under sub-rule (4) of rule 8. The submission date will be considered as the date of Aadhaar authentication or fifteen days from the application submission in FORM GST REG-01, whichever comes first. Additionally, rule 9 has been revised to stipulate that if an applicant does not complete Aadhaar authentication, their registration will require physical verification of their business premises. However, under certain conditions, the proper officer may opt to verify documents instead. These changes aim to streamline the registration process while ensuring compliance with Aadhaar authentication requirements. Furthermore, rule 25 now includes non-authentication of Aadhaar as a reason for registration failure, thereby impacting how applications are processed. Overall, these amendments affect all applicants under the GST framework who choose to authenticate their Aadhaar.
key_points: Notification No. 62/2020-Central Tax was issued on 20th August 2020.
The rules come into effect on the date of publication, 20th August 2020.
Sub-rule (4A) of rule 8 is amended to require Aadhaar authentication for applicants opting for it, effective from 21st August 2020.
The date of application submission will be the date of Aadhaar authentication or fifteen days from submission in FORM GST REG-01, whichever is earlier.
Rule 9 is amended to include new provisions regarding Aadhaar authentication and registration processes, effective from 21st August 2020.
If Aadhaar authentication is not completed, registration will require physical verification of the business place.
The proper officer may verify documents instead of conducting physical verification under certain conditions.
Rule 25 is amended to include non-authentication of Aadhaar as a reason for failure in the registration process.

544.summary: Notification No. 63/2020-Central Tax, issued on 25th August 2020, announces that the provisions of section 100 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019 will take effect from 1st September 2020. This means that any changes or regulations outlined in section 100 will become applicable starting on that date. The notification is a formal declaration from the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs, indicating the government's intent to implement these provisions. Businesses and individuals affected by this section should prepare for the changes that will come into force, as they will need to comply with the new regulations from the specified date. This notification serves as an important update for stakeholders in the GST framework, ensuring they are aware of the upcoming changes in the law. It is crucial for those impacted to understand the implications of section 100 as they will be required to adjust their practices accordingly.
key_points: The notification is issued under Notification No. 63/2020-Central Tax.
It is dated 25th August 2020.
The provisions of section 100 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019 will come into force on 1st September 2020.
This notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.
The notification references the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019, specifically sub-section (2) of section 1.

545.summary: Notification No. 64/2020-Central Tax, issued on 31st August 2020, amends the earlier notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax from 23rd April 2019. Specifically, it extends the deadline for a certain compliance requirement from 31st August 2020 to 31st October 2020. This change is made under section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, which allows the government to make such amendments. The previous amendment to this notification was made by notification No. 59/2020-Central Tax on 13th July 2020. This extension may affect businesses that were required to meet the original deadline, providing them additional time to comply with the specified requirements. It is important for affected businesses to take note of this new deadline to ensure they are aligned with the updated compliance timeline.
key_points: The notification is issued on 31st August 2020.
It amends notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax dated 23rd April 2019.
The amendment changes the deadline from 31st August 2020 to 31st October 2020.
This change is made under the powers conferred by section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The previous amendment was made by notification No. 59/2020-Central Tax dated 13th July 2020.

546.summary: Notification No. 65/2020-Central Tax, issued on 01st September 2020, introduces an important amendment to Notification No. 35/2020-Central Tax from 03rd April 2020. Specifically, it adds a new proviso to clause (i) of the first paragraph, which extends the time limit for compliance with actions mandated under section 171 of the GST Act. This extension applies to any deadlines that fall between 20th March 2020 and 29th November 2020. As a result, businesses and authorities now have until 30th November 2020 to complete or comply with these actions. This change is particularly relevant for those who may have faced challenges in meeting their obligations during the specified period. The amendment aims to provide relief and ensure that compliance can be achieved without undue pressure, reflecting the government's response to the ongoing circumstances affecting businesses.
key_points: Notification No. 65/2020-Central Tax was issued on 01st September 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 35/2020-Central Tax dated 03rd April 2020.
A new proviso is added to clause (i) of the first paragraph of Notification No. 35/2020.
The new proviso extends the time limit for compliance of actions specified under section 171.
The extended time period applies to actions due between 20th March 2020 and 29th November 2020.
The new deadline for completion or compliance of such actions is now 30th November 2020.

547.summary: Notification No. 66/2020-Central Tax, issued on 21st September 2020, introduces an important amendment to Notification No. 35/2020-Central Tax from 3rd April 2020. Specifically, it adds a new proviso that extends the deadline for compliance related to goods sent out of India on approval for sale or return. This extension applies to any actions that were due between 20th March 2020 and 30th October 2020. As a result, businesses now have until 31st October 2020 to complete these actions without penalty. This change is particularly relevant for exporters and businesses involved in international trade, as it provides them additional time to meet their compliance obligations during a challenging period. The amendment reflects the government's ongoing efforts to support businesses affected by the pandemic and ensure smoother operations in the export sector.
key_points: Notification No. 66/2020-Central Tax was issued on 21st September 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 35/2020-Central Tax dated 3rd April 2020.
A new proviso is added after the first proviso in clause (i) of the first paragraph.
The new proviso extends the time limit for compliance of actions related to goods sent out of India on approval for sale or return.
The extended time period applies to actions that were due between 20th March 2020 and 30th October 2020.
The new deadline for compliance is now set to 31st October 2020.

548.summary: This corrigendum, issued on 22nd September 2020, amends Notification No. 67/2020-Central Tax, originally published on 21st September 2020. The changes include the removal of the figure '(ii)' from page 3, line 36, which clarifies the structure of the notification. Additionally, there is a significant correction where 'March, 2020' has been changed to 'March, 2019' on page 4, line 2, which may affect the reporting periods for businesses. Furthermore, a typographical error has been corrected from '22th' to '22nd' on page 4, line 3. These amendments ensure that the notification accurately reflects the intended provisions and timelines, which is crucial for compliance by businesses. All stakeholders relying on this notification should take note of these corrections to avoid any confusion regarding the tax periods and requirements.
key_points: The notification is a corrigendum to Notification No. 67/2020-Central Tax, issued on 21st September 2020.
It omits the figure '(ii)' from page 3, line 36 of the original notification.
It changes 'March, 2020' to 'March, 2019' on page 4, line 2.
It corrects '22th' to '22nd' on page 4, line 3.

549.summary: Notification No. 67/2020-Central Tax, issued on September 21, 2020, introduces changes to the earlier Notification No. 73/2017-Central Tax. Specifically, it adds a new proviso that waives late fees exceeding Rs. 250 for registered persons who failed to submit their GSTR-4 returns for the quarters from July 2017 to March 2020. This waiver is applicable if these returns are filed between September 22, 2020, and October 31, 2020. Additionally, if the total central tax payable in the GSTR-4 return is nil, the late fee will be fully waived. This amendment aims to provide relief to taxpayers who may have missed the deadlines for these returns over an extended period. It is particularly beneficial for those who have not filed their returns during the specified quarters, allowing them to comply without incurring significant penalties.
key_points: Notification No. 67/2020-Central Tax was issued on September 21, 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 73/2017-Central Tax dated December 29, 2017.
A new proviso is added after the second proviso in Notification No. 73/2017.
The new proviso waives the late fee exceeding Rs. 250 for GSTR-4 returns.
The waiver applies to registered persons who file GSTR-4 for the quarters from July 2017 to March 2020.
The filing period for availing the waiver is from September 22, 2020, to October 31, 2020.
The late fee will be fully waived if the total central tax payable in the return is nil.

550.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 68/2020-Central Tax, which was originally published on September 21, 2020. This corrigendum, dated September 22, 2020, corrects a typographical error found in the original notification. Specifically, the date '22th' has been amended to '22nd' in line 30 on page 4. This correction is important for clarity and accuracy in the official documentation. The original notification number is G.S.R. 573(E), and this corrigendum is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs, with the reference number F. No. CBEC-20/06/08/2019-GST. While this change may seem minor, it ensures that the official records are precise and free from errors, which is essential for compliance and reference by stakeholders in the GST framework.
key_points: The notification is a corrigendum to Notification No. 68/2020-Central Tax.
It was issued on September 22, 2020.
The correction pertains to the date mentioned in the original notification.
Specifically, the term '22th' is corrected to '22nd' in line 30 on page 4 of the original notification.
The original notification was published on September 21, 2020.
The original notification number is G.S.R. 573(E).
This corrigendum is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.
The reference number for this corrigendum is F. No. CBEC-20/06/08/2019-GST.

551.summary: Notification No. 68/2020-Central Tax, issued on September 21, 2020, introduces a significant relief for registered persons who have not submitted their GSTR-10 returns by the due date. Specifically, it waives any late fee that exceeds Rs. 250 for those who file their GSTR-10 returns between September 22, 2020, and December 31, 2020. This waiver is applicable under section 47 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, which typically imposes penalties for late filing. The government has exercised its powers under section 128 of the same Act to implement this change, responding to the needs of taxpayers during this period. This means that if you are a registered taxpayer who missed the GSTR-10 deadline, you can benefit from this waiver, reducing your financial burden related to late fees. It is an opportunity for businesses to comply without incurring excessive penalties during the specified period.
key_points: Waives late fee exceeding Rs. 250 for GSTR-10 returns.
Applicable for returns filed between September 22, 2020, and December 31, 2020.
This waiver is under section 47 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The notification is issued under the powers of section 128 of the same Act.

552.summary: Notification No. 69/2020-Central Tax, issued on 30th September 2020, brings an important change to the earlier Notification No. 41/2020-Central Tax from 5th May 2020. Specifically, it extends the due date for a certain compliance requirement from 30th September 2020 to 31st October 2020. This change is made under section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and follows the recommendations of the GST Council. The amendment is relevant to businesses that are required to comply with the provisions outlined in the original notification. Rule 80 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, is the governing rule for this amendment. Therefore, businesses should take note of this new deadline, as it provides additional time for compliance. This extension may help ease the burden on businesses that were facing challenges in meeting the original deadline.
key_points: The notification is issued on 30th September 2020.
It amends Notification No. 41/2020-Central Tax dated 5th May 2020.
The original due date of 30th September 2020 is changed to 31st October 2020.
This amendment is made under section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The amendment is based on the recommendations of the GST Council.
The relevant rule for this amendment is rule 80 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

553.summary: Notification No. 70/2020-Central Tax, issued on 30th September 2020, brings important amendments to Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax, which was originally published on 21st March 2020. The key change is the replacement of the term 'a financial year' with 'any preceding financial year from 2017-18 onwards', which broadens the scope of the notification to include multiple financial years. Additionally, the phrase 'or for exports' has been added after the mention of 'goods or services or both to a registered person', thereby clarifying that the provisions also apply to exports. This amendment is significant for businesses involved in exports and those referencing financial years back to 2017-18. The earlier notification had already been amended by Notification No. 61/2020-Central Tax on 30th July 2020, making this a continuation of updates to the GST framework. Businesses should review these changes to understand their implications better.
key_points: Notification No. 70/2020-Central Tax was issued on 30th September 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax dated 21st March 2020.
The amendment changes 'a financial year' to 'any preceding financial year from 2017-18 onwards'.
It also adds the phrase 'or for exports' after 'goods or services or both to a registered person'.
The principal notification No. 13/2020 was previously amended by Notification No. 61/2020-Central Tax on 30th July 2020.

554.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 71/2020-Central Tax on 30th September 2020, which amends the earlier Notification No. 14/2020-Central Tax from 21st March 2020. The first significant change is in the first paragraph, where the phrase 'a financial year' has been replaced with 'any preceding financial year from 2017-18 onwards'. This broadens the scope of the notification to include financial years starting from 2017-18, allowing for more flexibility in compliance. Additionally, the second paragraph has been updated to change the deadline from '1st day of October' to '1st day of December'. This extension provides additional time for businesses to meet their obligations. These amendments are particularly relevant for businesses that need to consider financial years prior to 2017-18 and those who were preparing for the October deadline, as they now have until December to comply with the requirements outlined in the original notification.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 14/2020-Central Tax dated 21st March 2020.
Changes 'a financial year' to 'any preceding financial year from 2017-18 onwards' in the first paragraph.
Changes the date from '1st day of October' to '1st day of December' in the second paragraph.
Issued on 30th September 2020 by the Ministry of Finance.

555.summary: The Government of India issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 72/2020-Central Tax, which was originally published on 30th September 2020. This corrigendum, dated 1st October 2020, makes specific corrections to the terminology used in the original notification. Specifically, the term 'Quick Reference' has been changed to 'Quick Response' in two places on page 2, specifically in line 26 and line 33. This change clarifies the intended meaning in the context of the notification. While the corrections may seem minor, they ensure that the language used is accurate and reflects the intended guidance. This update is relevant for all stakeholders who refer to this notification for compliance and operational purposes, ensuring they have the correct terminology in their records and communications.
key_points: The notification is a corrigendum to Notification No. 72/2020-Central Tax dated 30th September 2020.
The corrigendum was issued on 1st October 2020.
It corrects the term 'Quick Reference' to 'Quick Response' in two instances.
The changes are found on page 2, line 26 and line 33 of the original notification.

556.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 72/2020-Central Tax, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This notification introduces several important changes effective from the date of its publication. A new requirement has been added to rule 46, mandating that invoices issued under rule 48(4) must include a Quick Reference code that contains an embedded Invoice Reference Number (IRN). Additionally, rule 48(4) now includes a provision allowing the Commissioner to exempt certain registered persons from the obligation to issue invoices for a specified period, subject to conditions outlined in a future notification. Furthermore, rule 138A(2) has been revised to permit the electronic presentation of the Quick Reference code with the embedded IRN for verification purposes, eliminating the need for a physical copy of the tax invoice. These changes aim to streamline the invoicing process and enhance compliance for businesses operating under the GST framework.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Eleventh Amendment) Rules, 2020'.
It was issued on September 30, 2020, and comes into effect on the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
A new clause (r) is added to rule 46, requiring a Quick Reference code with an embedded Invoice Reference Number (IRN) for invoices issued under rule 48(4).
A new proviso is added to rule 48(4), allowing the Commissioner to exempt certain registered persons from issuing invoices for a specified period.
Rule 138A(2) is amended to allow electronic production of the Quick Reference (QR) code with the embedded IRN instead of a physical tax invoice for verification.

557.summary: Notification No. 73/2020-Central Tax, issued on 1st October 2020, introduces a special procedure for registered persons who have prepared tax invoices incorrectly, as per sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the CGST Rules, 2017. This notification is effective for the period from 1st October 2020 to 31st October 2020. During this time, these individuals must obtain an Invoice Reference Number (IRN) for their invoices by uploading the required details in FORM GST INV-01 on the GST portal. This upload must be completed within thirty days from the invoice date. If they fail to comply with this requirement, their invoices will not be recognized as valid. This change specifically targets those who did not follow the prescribed invoice preparation method, ensuring that they can still validate their invoices within the specified timeframe.
key_points: Notification No. 73/2020-Central Tax issued on 1st October 2020.
Applies to registered persons required to prepare tax invoices under sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the CGST Rules, 2017.
Covers the period from 1st October 2020 to 31st October 2020.
Affected persons must obtain an Invoice Reference Number (IRN) for invoices prepared incorrectly.
IRN must be obtained by uploading details in FORM GST INV-01.
The upload must occur within thirty days from the date of the invoice.
Invoices not following this procedure will not be considered valid.

558.summary: Notification No. 74/2020-Central Tax, issued on October 15, 2020, introduces a special procedure for registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to 1.5 crore rupees. These individuals are required to submit details of their outward supply of goods or services in FORM GSTR-1. The notification specifies that for the quarter from October 2020 to December 2020, the deadline for submitting FORM GSTR-1 is January 13, 2021. Additionally, for the subsequent quarter from January 2021 to March 2021, the deadline is set for April 13, 2021. It is important to note that the time limit for submitting details for the months of October 2020 to March 2021 will be announced in the Official Gazette at a later date. This change primarily affects small businesses and individuals who fall within the specified turnover limit, streamlining their reporting process under the GST framework.
key_points: Registered persons with aggregate turnover up to 1.5 crore rupees are affected.
These persons must follow a special procedure for furnishing details of outward supply.
Details must be submitted in FORM GSTR-1 under the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
For the quarter October 2020 to December 2020, the due date for FORM GSTR-1 is 13th January 2021.
For the quarter January 2021 to March 2021, the due date for FORM GSTR-1 is 13th April 2021.
The time limit for furnishing details for October 2020 to March 2021 will be notified later.

559.summary: Notification No. 75/2020-Central Tax, issued on October 15, 2020, extends the deadline for filing GSTR-1 for registered taxpayers whose aggregate turnover exceeds 1.5 crore rupees. This extension applies to the months from October 2020 through March 2021, with the new due date set for the 11th day of the month following each respective month. This change is made under the second proviso to sub-section (1) of section 37 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Additionally, the notification indicates that the time limit for furnishing returns under section 38 for these months will be communicated in a future notification. This extension is significant for businesses with higher turnover, providing them with additional time to comply with their filing obligations during this period.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-1 for registered persons with turnover over 1.5 crore rupees.
Applicable for the months from October 2020 to March 2021.
New due date is the 11th day of the month following each respective month.
This notification is issued under the second proviso to sub-section (1) of section 37 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The time limit for returns under section 38 for the same months will be notified later.

560.summary: Notification No. 76/2020-Central Tax, issued on October 15, 2020, outlines the filing deadlines for the GSTR-3B return for the months from October 2020 to March 2021. All registered taxpayers are required to submit their GSTR-3B electronically by the 20th of the month following the reporting month. However, for taxpayers with an aggregate turnover of up to five crore rupees, there are extended deadlines based on their principal place of business. Those in states like Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, and others must file by the 22nd, while taxpayers in states such as Himachal Pradesh, Punjab, and others have until the 24th. This differentiation aims to ease compliance for smaller businesses in specific regions. Additionally, taxpayers must ensure they discharge their tax liabilities by debiting their electronic ledgers by the respective deadlines. This notification is based on the powers granted under the CGST Act and aims to streamline the filing process for various states and Union territories.
key_points: The return in FORM GSTR-3B for October 2020 to March 2021 must be filed electronically by the 20th of the following month.
Taxpayers with an aggregate turnover of up to five crore rupees in specified states must file GSTR-3B by the 22nd of the following month.
The specified states for the 22nd deadline include Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Goa, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, and certain Union territories.
Taxpayers in other states must file GSTR-3B by the 24th of the following month.

The states with the 24th deadline include Himachal Pradesh, Punjab, Uttarakhand, Haryana, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Sikkim, Arunachal Pradesh, Nagaland, Manipur, Mizoram, Tripura, Meghalaya, Assam, West Bengal, Jharkhand, Odisha, and certain Union territories.
The notification is issued under section 168 of the CGST Act and sub-rule (5) of rule 61 of the CGST Rules.
Taxpayers must discharge their tax liability by debiting their electronic cash or credit ledger by the specified deadlines.

562.summary: Notification No. 78/2020-Central Tax, issued on 15th October 2020, brings significant changes to the earlier Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax. This amendment will come into effect on 1st April 2021. The revised table now states that businesses with an aggregate turnover of up to five crores will need to mention 4 digits of the Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) code on their tax invoices. For those with a turnover exceeding five crores, the requirement increases to 6 digits. Additionally, there is a provision that allows registered persons with a turnover of up to five crores to omit the HSN code when issuing invoices for supplies made to unregistered persons. This change aims to simplify compliance for smaller businesses while ensuring that larger businesses provide more detailed information on their invoices. Overall, this amendment is designed to streamline the invoicing process and reduce the burden on smaller taxpayers.
key_points: Notification No. 78/2020-Central Tax was issued on 15th October 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment will take effect from 1st April 2021.
The new table specifies that for aggregate turnover up to five crores, 4 digits of HSN code are required.
For aggregate turnover exceeding five crores, 6 digits of HSN code are required.
Registered persons with turnover up to five crores are not required to mention HSN codes for supplies made to unregistered persons.

563.summary: The Central Goods and Services Tax (Twelfth Amendment) Rules, 2020, published on October 15, 2020, introduce several important changes to the GST framework. Notably, Rule 46 now empowers the Board to specify the number of digits for Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes that certain registered persons must use. Additionally, Rule 67A allows registered persons to submit Nil returns through SMS, enhancing convenience. For businesses with an aggregate turnover exceeding five crore rupees, Rule 80 mandates an audit of accounts and submission of FORM GSTR-9C electronically for the financial years 2018-19 and 2019-20. Furthermore, an amendment to rule 138E temporarily lifts restrictions on the movement of goods for those who have not filed Nil returns for specific periods. The language in Rule 142 has been softened, allowing the proper officer more discretion. Lastly, FORM GSTR-1 has been updated to reflect 'Rate of Tax' instead of 'Total value,' ensuring clarity in reporting. These changes are significant for registered taxpayers and businesses navigating GST compliance.
key_points: The notification is titled the Central Goods and Services Tax (Twelfth Amendment) Rules, 2020.
The rules come into effect on the date of publication in the Official Gazette, which is October 15, 2020.
Rule 46 is amended to allow the Board to specify the number of digits of Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes required for certain registered persons and supplies.
Rule 67A is introduced to permit registered persons to furnish Nil returns via SMS using a registered mobile number and OTP verification.

Rule 80 is amended to require registered persons with an aggregate turnover exceeding five crore rupees to get their accounts audited and submit FORM GSTR-9C electronically for FY 2018-19 and 2019-20.
An amendment to rule 138E allows for restrictions on movement of goods to be lifted if Nil returns for specified periods are not filed.
Rule 142 is amended to change 'shall' to 'may' regarding the actions of the proper officer.
FORM GSTR-1 is updated to change the heading from 'Total value' to 'Rate of Tax' in the relevant table.

564.summary: Notification No. 80/2020-Central Tax, issued on 28th October 2020, brings an important change to the due date for filing under the earlier Notification No. 41/2020-Central Tax, which was originally set for 31st October 2020. The new due date has been extended to 31st December 2020. This amendment is made under the authority of section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, along with rule 80 of the CGST Rules, 2017. This change affects all businesses required to comply with the filing deadlines set forth in the earlier notification. The principal notification had previously been amended by Notification No. 69/2020-Central Tax on 30th September 2020, but this latest amendment specifically alters the due date, providing additional time for businesses to prepare their filings. It is crucial for affected businesses to note this extended deadline to with the updated timeline.
key_points: Notification No. 80/2020-Central Tax was issued on 28th October 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 41/2020-Central Tax dated 5th May 2020.
The amendment changes the due date from '31st October 2020' to '31st December 2020'.
The amendment is made under the powers of section 44 of the CGST Act, 2017 and rule 80 of the CGST Rules, 2017.
The principal notification No. 41/2020 was last amended by notification No. 69/2020-Central Tax on 30th September 2020.

565.summary: Notification No. 81/2020-Central Tax, issued on 10th November 2020, marks an important date for the implementation of section 97 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019. This section deals with the appointment of officers who will oversee the Goods and Services Tax (GST) framework. With this notification, the provisions of section 97 officially come into effect, which means that the government can now appoint officers to ensure proper administration and compliance of GST regulations. This change is significant for businesses and tax professionals as it establishes the authority and structure needed for effective GST governance. The Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs is responsible for this notification, indicating a formal step towards enhancing the GST framework in India. Overall, this notification sets the stage for improved oversight and management of GST-related matters.
key_points: Notification No. 81/2020-Central Tax was issued on 10th November 2020.
The notification appoints 10th November 2020 as the effective date for the provisions of section 97 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019.
Section 97 pertains to the appointment of officers for the purpose of the Goods and Services Tax.
This notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.

566.summary: On 13th November 2020, a corrigendum was issued for Notification No. 82/2020-Central Tax, which was originally published on 10th November 2020. This corrigendum specifically changes a reference in the notification text. Instead of citing 'notification No. 72/2020-Central Tax, dated 30th September 2020', it now correctly cites 'notification No. 79/2020-Central Tax, dated 15th October 2020'. This amendment is found on page 21, in lines 9, 10, and 11 of the original notification. Both the original notification and the corrigendum are published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), with the original under G.S.R. 698(E) and the new reference under G.S.R. 639(E). This change is important for those relying on the correct notification references for compliance and reporting purposes.
key_points: The notification is a corrigendum to Notification No. 82/2020-Central Tax, issued on 10th November 2020.
The corrigendum was published on 13th November 2020.
It amends the reference from 'notification No. 72/2020-Central Tax, dated 30th September 2020' to 'notification No. 79/2020-Central Tax, dated 15th October 2020'.
This change affects the text on page 21, specifically lines 9, 10, and 11 of the original notification.
The original notification was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), under G.S.R. 698(E).
The new reference is also published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), under G.S.R. 639(E).

567.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 82/2020-Central Tax, effective from January 1, 2021, which amends several rules under the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. Notably, Rule 59 now mandates that registered persons must submit details of outward supplies using FORM GSTR-1. Additionally, the Invoice Furnishing Facility (IFF) allows registered persons to report supplies up to ₹50 lakh for the first two months of a quarter. Rule 60 has been updated to ensure that details of outward supplies are accessible in FORM GSTR-2A, GSTR-4A, and GSTR-6A. Furthermore, Rule 61 alters the due dates for filing FORM GSTR-3B for small taxpayers with an annual turnover of up to ₹5 crore, requiring them to file by the 22nd or 24th day of the following month, depending on their state. This change applies to returns for the months from October 2020 to March 2021. Lastly, a new Rule 61A has been introduced, allowing registered persons to opt for quarterly returns, enhancing flexibility in compliance.
key_points: Notification No. 82/2020-Central Tax was issued on November 10, 2020.
The rules will come into effect from January 1, 2021.
Rule 59 is amended to specify that details of outward supplies must be furnished in FORM GSTR-1.
Registered persons can use the Invoice Furnishing Facility (IFF) for the first two months of a quarter for supplies up to ₹50 lakh.
Rule 60 is amended to provide that details of outward supplies will be available in FORM GSTR-2A, GSTR-4A, and GSTR-6A.
Rule 61 is amended to change the due dates for filing FORM GSTR-3B for taxpayers with turnover up to ₹5 crore.
Taxpayers in certain states must file GSTR-3B by the 22nd or 24th day of the following month for the period from October 2020 to March 2021.
A new rule 61A is introduced for opting to furnish quarterly returns.

568.summary: Notification No. 83/2020-Central Tax, issued on 10th November 2020, brings significant changes to the deadlines for filing GSTR-1, which is the return for details of outward supplies. The time limit for filing GSTR-1 for each tax period has been extended to the 11th day of the month following the tax period. Additionally, for registered persons who are required to file returns quarterly, the deadline has been further extended to the 13th day of the following month. This notification supersedes the earlier notifications, No. 74/2020 and No. 75/2020, both dated 15th October 2020, which had set different deadlines. The new rules will take effect from 1st January 2021, allowing businesses more time to comply with their filing obligations. This change is particularly relevant for businesses that need to report their outward supplies, as it provides them with additional time to prepare and submit their returns accurately.
key_points: Extends the time limit for filing GSTR-1 for each tax period until the 11th day of the following month.
For registered persons filing quarterly returns, the deadline is extended to the 13th day of the following month.
This notification supersedes Notification No. 74/2020-Central Tax and Notification No. 75/2020-Central Tax, both dated 15th October 2020.
The new provisions come into effect from 1st January 2021.
The extension applies to the details of outward supplies as per the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The notification is issued under the powers conferred by the second proviso to sub-section (1) of section 37 and section 168 of the CGST Act, 2017.

569.summary: Notification No. 84/2020-Central Tax, issued on 10th November 2020, introduces new provisions for registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to five crore rupees. Starting January 2021, these individuals can opt to file their GST returns quarterly, provided they have submitted their previous month's return on time. If their turnover exceeds five crore rupees during any quarter, they will be required to switch to monthly returns from the first month of the following quarter. The notification also includes a table that outlines deemed options for registered persons based on their turnover and whether they filed GSTR-1 on a monthly or quarterly basis for October 2020. Additionally, registered persons have the opportunity to change their return filing option electronically between 5th December 2020 and 31st January 2021. This change aims to simplify the filing process for smaller businesses while ensuring compliance with GST regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 84/2020-Central Tax was issued on 10th November 2020.
Registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to five crore rupees can opt to file returns quarterly starting January 2021.
To opt for quarterly returns, the return for the preceding month must be filed on time.
If a registered person's turnover exceeds five crore rupees in a quarter, they must switch to monthly returns from the first month of the next quarter.
The notification includes a table specifying deemed options for registered persons based on their turnover and filing history for October 2020.
Registered persons can change their return filing option electronically from 5th December 2020 to 31st January 2021.

570.summary: Notification No. 85/2020-Central Tax, issued on 10th November 2020, introduces a new procedure for registered persons who have opted to file their GST returns quarterly. Starting from 1st January 2021, these taxpayers can pay their tax liabilities in the first or second month of the quarter by depositing 35% of the tax liability from their previous quarter's return. For those filing monthly, the payment can be based on the last month's tax liability of the previous quarter. Importantly, if the balance in their electronic cash or credit ledger is sufficient to cover the tax liability, no deposit is required. However, to qualify for this special procedure, taxpayers must have filed a complete tax period return for the preceding month, which means they must have been registered for the entire duration of that tax period. This change aims to provide more flexibility in tax payments for eligible registered persons.
key_points: Notification No. 85/2020-Central Tax issued on 10th November 2020.
Effective from 1st January 2021.
Registered persons opting for quarterly returns can pay tax in the first or second month of the quarter.
Tax payment can be made by depositing 35% of the tax liability from the previous quarter's return for quarterly filers.
Monthly filers can pay based on the tax liability from the last month of the previous quarter.
No deposit is required if the electronic cash or credit ledger balance is sufficient for the tax liability.
Eligibility for this special procedure requires filing a complete tax period return prior to the month of payment.
A complete tax period is defined as being registered from the first to the last day of the tax period.

571.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 86/2020-Central Tax, which was originally published on 10th November 2020. This corrigendum was made public on 13th November 2020. The key change involves a specific wording adjustment found in line 33 on page 25 of the original notification. The term 'Central Government' has been corrected to 'Commissioner'. This change clarifies the authority responsible for certain actions or decisions outlined in the notification. It is important for businesses and stakeholders to note this amendment, as it may affect how they interpret the provisions of the original notification. By specifying 'Commissioner', the notification now directly refers to the GST Commissioner, who has the authority to act in the context mentioned. This adjustment ensures that the language used in the notification accurately reflects the intended governance structure under GST.
key_points: This is a corrigendum to Notification No. 86/2020-Central Tax.
The original notification was issued on 10th November 2020.
The corrigendum was published on 13th November 2020.
The specific change is in line 33 on page 25 of the original notification.
The phrase 'Central Government' has been replaced with 'Commissioner'.

572.summary: Notification No. 86/2020-Central Tax, issued on 10th November 2020, officially rescinds the earlier Notification No. 76/2020-Central Tax, which was dated 15th October 2020. This change is made under the authority of section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and follows recommendations from the GST Council. The rescission means that any provisions or directives from Notification 76/2020 are no longer in effect, but it does not affect any actions that were taken before this notification was issued. This is relevant for businesses and taxpayers who were operating under the guidelines of the previous notification, as they will need to adjust to the changes brought about by this rescission. The earlier notification was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, under G.S.R. 636 (E), and now, with this new notification, those provisions are no longer applicable. This change aims to streamline GST compliance and is part of the government's ongoing efforts to refine tax regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 86/2020-Central Tax was issued on 10th November 2020.
This notification rescinds Notification No. 76/2020-Central Tax dated 15th October 2020.
The rescission applies except for actions taken before the rescission.
The rescission is based on the powers conferred by section 168 of the CGST Act, 2017.
It is enacted following the recommendations of the GST Council.
The rescinded notification was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, G.S.R. 636 (E).

573.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 87/2020-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for submitting FORM GST ITC-04. This form is used for reporting goods that have been dispatched to or received from job workers. The new deadline for submission is now November 30, 2020, covering the period from July 2020 to September 2020. This change is significant for businesses that engage job workers, as it provides additional time to comply with the reporting requirements. The notification is effective from October 25, 2020, and is issued under the authority of section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This extension aims to ease compliance burdens during the specified period, allowing businesses to ensure accurate reporting without the pressure of an immediate deadline. The amendment is particularly relevant for those who may have faced challenges in meeting the original timeline.
key_points: Extends the deadline for FORM GST ITC-04 submission.
New deadline is November 30, 2020.
Covers goods dispatched to or received from job workers.
Applicable for the period from July 2020 to September 2020.
Notification comes into effect from October 25, 2020.
Issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.
Based on section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Amends the time limit as per sub-rule (3) of rule 45 of the GST Rules.

574.summary: Notification No. 88/2020-Central Tax, issued on 10th November 2020, brings a significant change to the earlier Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax, which was published on 21st March 2020. The key amendment is the reduction of the threshold limit for certain compliance requirements from 'five hundred crore rupees' to 'one hundred crore rupees'. This change will take effect from 1st January 2021. The amendment is made under sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, indicating a shift in the compliance landscape for businesses. This adjustment may impact businesses with a turnover exceeding one hundred crore rupees, as they will now be subject to different compliance obligations than those with lower turnover. The principal notification had previously been amended by Notification No. 70/2020-Central Tax on 30th September 2020, but this latest change marks a notable shift in the threshold that businesses need to be aware of moving forward.
key_points: Notification No. 88/2020-Central Tax was issued on 10th November 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax dated 21st March 2020.
The amendment changes the threshold limit from 'five hundred crore rupees' to 'one hundred crore rupees'.
The new threshold limit will be effective from 1st January 2021.
This change is made under sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The principal notification No. 13/2020 was last amended by notification No. 70/2020-Central Tax on 30th September 2020.

575.summary: Notification No. 89/2020-Central Tax, issued on 29th November 2020, introduces a waiver of penalties for registered persons who failed to comply with the provisions of notification No. 14/2020-Central Tax during the period from 1st December 2020 to 31st March 2021. This waiver specifically pertains to penalties outlined under section 125 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. However, to benefit from this waiver, affected individuals must with the provisions of notification No. 14/2020 starting from 1st April 2021. Notification No. 14/2020 was originally issued on 21st March 2020, and this new notification aims to provide relief to those who may have struggled to meet compliance requirements during the specified period. The waiver applies to all registered persons under the CGST Act, offering them a chance to rectify their compliance status without incurring penalties during the designated timeframe.
key_points: Notification No. 89/2020-Central Tax issued on 29th November 2020.
Waives penalty under section 125 of the CGST Act for non-compliance of notification No. 14/2020-Central Tax.
Non-compliance period covered is from 1st December 2020 to 31st March 2021.
Condition for waiver is compliance with notification No. 14/2020 from 1st April 2021.
Notification No. 14/2020-Central Tax was issued on 21st March 2020.
This waiver applies to all registered persons under the CGST Act.

576.summary: Notification No. 90/2020-Central Tax, issued on December 1, 2020, introduces an important amendment to Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax, which was originally published on June 28, 2017. The key change is the addition of a new proviso that mandates registered persons to mention eight digits of Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) Codes for specific chemical supplies in their tax invoices. This new requirement applies to a defined list of chemical products, each associated with its respective HSN Code, which is detailed in a newly added table. This amendment is significant for businesses engaged in the supply of these chemicals, as it standardizes the HSN Code reporting for these items. The changes take effect immediately, meaning that affected businesses must comply without delay. This amendment was made following the recommendations of the GST Council, reflecting ongoing efforts to streamline GST compliance for specific sectors.
key_points: Notification No. 90/2020-Central Tax was issued on December 1, 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax dated June 28, 2017.
A new proviso is added after the first proviso in Notification No. 12/2017.
The new proviso requires mentioning eight digits of HSN Codes for specific chemical supplies.
The specific chemical supplies and their corresponding HSN Codes are listed in a new table.
The amendment affects registered persons dealing with the specified chemical products.
The changes are effective immediately upon the notification's issuance date.
This amendment follows the recommendations of the GST Council.

577.summary: Notification No. 91/2020-Central Tax, issued on December 14, 2020, brings important amendments to the earlier notification No. 35/2020-Central Tax from April 3, 2020. Specifically, it extends the deadlines for certain compliance requirements. The original deadline of November 29, 2020, has been pushed to March 30, 2021, and the deadline of November 30, 2020, has been moved to March 31, 2021. These changes are applicable to all relevant taxpayers and are effective from December 1, 2020. This extension provides additional time for businesses to meet their obligations under the GST framework, allowing them to prepare adequately for compliance. It is crucial for affected businesses to take note of these new deadlines to ensure they align their processes accordingly. This amendment reflects the government's ongoing efforts to support businesses during challenging times.
key_points: The notification is No. 91/2020-Central Tax, issued on 14th December 2020.
It amends notification No. 35/2020-Central Tax, originally issued on 3rd April 2020.
The amendment changes the deadline from 29th November 2020 to 30th March 2021.
It also changes the deadline from 30th November 2020 to 31st March 2021.
The changes are effective from 1st December 2020.

578.summary: Notification No. 92/2020-Central Tax, issued on December 22, 2020, announces that several provisions of the Finance Act, 2020, specifically sections 119 through 124, 126, 127, and 131, will take effect starting January 1, 2021. This means that businesses and individuals will need to be aware of the changes brought about by these sections as they will now be enforceable. The notification is part of the government's efforts to implement the provisions of the Finance Act, which is designated as Act No. 12 of 2020. The Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs is responsible for this notification, indicating its importance in the context of indirect tax administration. Stakeholders in the GST framework should prepare for the implications of these sections, as they will impact compliance and operational processes from the specified date.
key_points: The notification is issued under Notification No. 92/2020-Central Tax.
It was issued on December 22, 2020.
The provisions of sections 119, 120, 121, 122, 123, 124, 126, 127, and 131 of the Finance Act, 2020 will come into force on January 1, 2021.
This notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.
The Finance Act referred to is Act No. 12 of 2020.

579.summary: Notification No. 93/2020-Central Tax, issued on 22nd December 2020, introduces an amendment to Notification No. 73/2017-Central Tax, originally published on 29th December 2017. The amendment adds a new proviso that waives the late fee for registered persons who delay filing FORM GSTR-4 for the Financial Year 2019-20. This waiver is applicable for the period from 1st November 2020 to 31st December 2020. Importantly, this benefit is exclusively available to those whose principal place of business is located in the Union Territory of Ladakh. This change aims to provide relief to businesses in Ladakh that may have faced challenges in meeting their filing deadlines during this period. As a result, affected businesses in Ladakh can now file their GSTR-4 without incurring late fees for the specified timeframe, easing their compliance burden.
key_points: Notification No. 93/2020-Central Tax was issued on 22nd December 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 73/2017-Central Tax dated 29th December 2017.
A new proviso is added after the third proviso in Notification No. 73/2017.
The new proviso waives the late fee for FORM GSTR-4 for the Financial Year 2019-20.
The waiver applies for delays from 1st November 2020 to 31st December 2020.
This waiver is specifically for registered persons whose principal place of business is in the Union Territory of Ladakh.

580.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 94/2020-Central Tax, which was originally published on 22nd December 2020. This corrigendum, dated 28th December 2020, makes two specific amendments. Firstly, it corrects the wording from 'for the proviso' to 'for the provisos' on page 8, line 31, indicating that there are multiple conditions or exceptions rather than just one. Secondly, it extends the timeframe mentioned on page 12, line 12, changing 'seven working days' to 'thirty days'. This change significantly impacts the timeline for compliance, allowing more time for the relevant parties to meet their obligations. Businesses and stakeholders who rely on this notification should take note of these changes to ensure they are aligned with the updated provisions. The amendments clarify the language and provide a more extended period for compliance, which can help in better planning and execution of GST-related activities.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 94/2020-Central Tax dated 22nd December 2020.
Changes 'for the proviso' to 'for the provisos' on page 8, line 31.
Changes 'seven working days' to 'thirty days' on page 12, line 12.
The notification was issued on 28th December 2020.

581.summary: The Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourteenth Amendment) Rules, 2020, published on December 22, 2020, introduces several significant changes to the GST rules. Notably, Rule 8(4A) now mandates biometric-based Aadhaar authentication for applications, enhancing verification processes. Additionally, the processing time for applications under Rule 9 has been extended from three to seven days, allowing more time for review. A new sub-rule (2A) under Rule 21A establishes a framework for suspending a taxpayer's registration if discrepancies are found in their returns, which could lead to cancellation if not addressed. Furthermore, Rule 36(4) reduces the input tax credit claim limit from 10% to 5%, effective January 1, 2021, impacting how businesses can claim credits. The distance requirement for certain transport regulations under Rule 138(10) has also been increased from 100 km to 200 km. Lastly, a new FORM GST REG-31 has been introduced to formally notify taxpayers of registration suspensions and potential cancellations, ensuring they are informed of any issues with their compliance. These changes are crucial for all registered taxpayers to understand as they may affect their registration status and compliance processes.
key_points: The notification is titled the Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourteenth Amendment) Rules, 2020.
Rule 8(4A) now requires biometric-based Aadhaar authentication for applications under Rule 8(4).
Rule 9(1) and (2) changes the application processing period from three days to seven days.
Rule 21A(2A) introduces a process for suspending registration based on discrepancies in returns.
Rule 36(4) changes the input tax credit claim limit from 10% to 5% effective January 1, 2021.
Rule 138(10) increases the distance for certain transport requirements from 100 km to 200 km.
New FORM GST REG-31 is introduced for notifying suspension and potential cancellation of registration.

582.summary: Notification No. 95/2020-Central Tax, issued on December 30, 2020, extends the deadline for filing the annual return, known as GSTR-9, for the financial year 2019-20. The new due date is now set for February 28, 2021. This extension is granted under the authority of section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and follows rule 80 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. The extension was made on the recommendations of the GST Council, which aims to provide relief to taxpayers during this period. This change affects all registered taxpayers who are required to file GSTR-9 for the specified financial year. By extending the deadline, the government is allowing businesses more time to prepare and submit their annual returns electronically through the common portal. This move is expected to ease the compliance burden on taxpayers, especially in light of the challenges faced during the year.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing the annual return GSTR-9 for the financial year 2019-20.
New deadline for GSTR-9 is now 28th February 2021.
This extension is based on the powers conferred by section 44 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The notification is issued under rule 80 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The decision was made on the recommendations of the GST Council.

583.summary: The recent Notification No. 1/2020-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 21st February 2020, introduces important amendments to the existing GST rate structure. Specifically, it modifies Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Notably, it omits entry S. No. 242 from Schedule II, which means that whatever goods or services were previously listed under this entry will no longer be subject to the 6% GST rate. Additionally, in Schedule IV, the previous entry S. No. 228 is replaced with a new entry that specifies 'Any chapter Lottery', indicating a change in how lotteries are categorized under the 14% GST rate. These amendments will take effect from 1st March 2020, impacting businesses involved in the sale of goods and services previously covered under the omitted entry and those dealing with lotteries. This change is significant for compliance and pricing strategies moving forward.
key_points: Notification No. 1/2020-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 21st February 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In Schedule II, entry S. No. 242 and its related entries are omitted.
In Schedule IV, entry S. No. 228 is substituted with 'Any chapter Lottery'.
The changes will come into effect on 1st March 2020.

584.summary: Notification No. 01/2020-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 21st February 2020, brings significant amendments to the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. Specifically, it removes entry S. No. 242 from Schedule II, which means that whatever goods or services were previously listed under this entry will no longer be subject to the 12% integrated tax. Additionally, in Schedule IV, the existing entry S. No. 228 is replaced with a new entry that specifies 'Any chapter Lottery', indicating a change in the tax treatment for lottery-related transactions, which will now be taxed at 28%. These amendments will take effect from 1st March 2020, impacting businesses involved in the sale of goods or services previously covered under the omitted entry and those dealing with lotteries. It is essential for affected businesses to review these changes to understand their implications on pricing and compliance.
key_points: This notification is issued on 21st February 2020.
It amends Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In Schedule II, entry S. No. 242 and its related entries are omitted.
In Schedule IV, entry S. No. 228 is substituted with 'Any chapter Lottery'.
The changes will come into effect on 1st March 2020.

585.summary: The recent Notification No. 03/2020-Central Tax (Rate) introduces several changes to the GST rates effective from 1st April 2020. Notably, it omits serial number 187 from Schedule I, which previously listed certain goods at a 2.5% tax rate. In Schedule II, a new entry, serial number 75A, has been added for 'All goods' at a 6% rate, while serial numbers 202 and 203 have been removed. Additionally, Schedule III sees the removal of serial number 73 and a change in serial number 379, where the entry in column (3) is now 'All goods' instead of the previous specification. These amendments are part of the ongoing adjustments to the GST framework, following the principal notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from June 2017, which was last updated in February 2020. Businesses affected by these changes should review their tax classifications and with the new rates.
key_points: Omission of serial number 187 and its entries from Schedule I - 2.5%.
Insertion of new serial number 75A in Schedule II - 6% for 'All goods' after serial number 75.
Omission of serial numbers 202 and 203 and their entries from Schedule II - 6%.
Omission of serial number 73 and its entries from Schedule III - 9%.
Substitution of the entry in column (3) of serial number 379 in Schedule III - 9% to 'All goods'.
This notification comes into force on 1st April 2020.
The notification amends the principal notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The last amendment to the principal notification was made by notification No. 01/2020-Central Tax (Rate) dated 21st February 2020.

586.summary: Notification No. 02/2020-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 26th March 2020, brings important changes to the existing tax structure for certain services related to aircraft. Specifically, it amends the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017 by adding a new service category under serial number 25. This new item (ia) pertains to 'Maintenance, repair or overhaul services in respect of aircrafts, aircraft engines and other aircraft components or parts' and is subject to a tax rate of 5%. Additionally, the reference in item (ii) has been updated to include this new item (ia), ensuring clarity in the tax application. These changes will take effect from 1st April 2020, impacting businesses involved in the maintenance and repair of aircraft and related components, as they will now have a specific tax rate applicable to their services.
key_points: This notification is issued as Notification No. 02/2020-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 26th March 2020.
It amends Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment adds a new item (ia) for 'Maintenance, repair or overhaul services in respect of aircrafts, aircraft engines and other aircraft components or parts' under serial number 25 in the table.
The new item (ia) is assigned a tax rate of 5%.
In item (ii) of the same table, the reference to item (i) is updated to include the new item (ia).
This notification will come into effect from 1st April 2020.

587.summary: Notification No. 02/2020-Central Tax (Rate), issued on March 26, 2020, introduces amendments to the earlier Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated June 28, 2017. The key change is the addition of a new entry under serial number 25 in the Table, which pertains to maintenance, repair, or overhaul services for aircraft, aircraft engines, and other components. This new item, labeled as (ia), will be taxed at a rate of 2.5%. Furthermore, item (ii) in the same table has been updated to include a reference to this new item (ia). These changes are significant for businesses involved in the aviation sector, as they clarify the GST treatment of specific aircraft-related services. The amendments will take effect from April 1, 2020, ensuring that the new tax rate is applied from this date onward.
key_points: This notification is issued as Notification No. 02/2020-Central Tax (Rate) on March 26, 2020.
It amends Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated June 28, 2017.
New entries are added to the Table against serial number 25, specifically for maintenance, repair, or overhaul services related to aircraft.
The new item (ia) specifies a tax rate of 2.5% for these services.
In item (ii) of the same table, the reference now includes the new item (ia).
This amendment will come into effect from April 1, 2020.

588.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 03/2020-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 25th March 2020, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Effective from 1st April 2020, several changes have been made to the tax rates listed in the schedules. Specifically, the 5% tax rate for goods listed under serial number 187 in Schedule I has been removed. Additionally, a new entry for 12% tax rate has been added in Schedule II for goods classified under HSN 3605 00 10. Furthermore, serial numbers 202 and 203 in Schedule II have been omitted. In Schedule III, the 18% tax rate for serial number 73 has also been removed, and for serial number 379, the description in column (3) has been updated to 'All goods'. These changes will affect businesses dealing with the specified goods and may require them to adjust their pricing and tax calculations accordingly.
key_points: Notification No. 03/2020-Integrated Tax (Rate) issued on 25th March 2020.
This notification amends Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In Schedule I, the 5% tax rate for serial number 187 is omitted.

In Schedule II, serial numbers 202 and 203 are omitted.
