599.summary: Notification No. 05/2020-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 16th October 2020, introduces a new entry in the existing tax framework. Specifically, it amends the earlier notification No. 12/2017, which was originally published on 28th June 2017. The new entry, numbered 19C, pertains to 'Satellite launch services supplied by Indian Space Research Organisation, Antrix Corporation Limited or New Space India Limited.' Notably, this service will be taxed at a rate of 'Nil' for both Union Territory Tax and Central Goods and Services Tax. This change is significant as it aims to promote the satellite launch sector by exempting these services from taxation. The last amendment to the principal notification was made through notification No. 04/2020 on 30th September 2020, indicating that the government is actively updating tax regulations to support specific industries. This amendment will primarily benefit the mentioned organizations involved in satellite launches, allowing them to operate without the burden of tax on these services.
key_points: This notification is No. 05/2020-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 16th October 2020.
It amends the earlier notification No. 12/2017 - Union Territory Tax (Rate), dated 28th June 2017.
A new entry, 19C, has been added to the Table in the notification.
Entry 19C specifies 'Satellite launch services supplied by Indian Space Research Organisation, Antrix Corporation Limited or New Space India Limited.'
The tax rate for entry 19C is set to 'Nil' for both Union Territory Tax and Central Goods and Services Tax.
The previous amendment to the principal notification was made by notification No. 04/2020 on 30th September 2020.

600.summary: Notification No. 01/2019-Compensation Cess (Rate), issued on June 29, 2019, introduces an exemption from the Goods and Services Tax (GST) compensation cess for certain supplies. Specifically, it exempts any supply of goods made by retail outlets located in the departure area of international airports, beyond the immigration counters, to outgoing international tourists. An 'outgoing international tourist' is defined as a person who is not normally a resident of India and is in the country for legitimate non-immigrant purposes for a stay of no more than six months. This exemption is effective from July 1, 2019, allowing these retail outlets to provide goods to international travelers without the additional burden of the compensation cess. This change is significant for retail businesses operating in international airports, as it can enhance their competitiveness and appeal to tourists by reducing costs on purchases made before departure.
key_points: Exempts supply of goods by retail outlets in the departure area of international airports from GST compensation cess.
Applicable to outgoing international tourists as defined in the notification.
The exemption is effective from July 1, 2019.
The exemption applies to goods supplied beyond immigration counters.

601.summary: Notification No. 1/2019-Compensation Cess, issued on 29th January 2019, announces that the provisions of the Goods and Services Tax (Compensation to States) Amendment Act, 2018, will come into effect on 1st February 2019. This amendment is enacted under the authority of sub-section (2) of section 1 of the aforementioned Act, which is identified as 34 of 2018. The notification is a formal declaration from the Ministry of Finance, specifically the Department of Revenue, indicating the commencement date for the new provisions. This change affects all stakeholders involved in the GST framework, particularly those dealing with compensation cess, as they will need to comply with the updated regulations starting from the specified date. The notification serves as an important update for businesses and tax practitioners to align their operations with the new legal framework established by the amendment.
key_points: The notification is issued under Notification No. 1/2019-Compensation Cess.
The date of effect for the provisions of the GST (Compensation to States) Amendment Act, 2018 is set as 1st February 2019.
This notification is issued by the Ministry of Finance, Department of Revenue.
The amendment is made under the powers conferred by sub-section (2) of section 1 of the GST (Compensation to States) Amendment Act, 2018.
The Act number referenced is 34 of 2018.
The notification was issued on 29th January 2019.

602.summary: The Notification No. 02/2019-Compensation Cess (Rate), issued on September 30, 2019, introduces several amendments to the earlier notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate). A new entry, 4A, has been added for caffeinated beverages, which will now attract a compensation cess rate of 12%. Additionally, entry 42 has been amended to clarify that it pertains to motor vehicles designed to transport no more than 13 persons, excluding specific vehicle types. Entry 46 has been revised to include certain motor vehicles for individuals with orthopedic disabilities, which must be certified by a Deputy Secretary in the Department of Heavy Industries. Furthermore, entries 50 and 51 have had their vehicle classification codes updated to reflect new codes. These changes will take effect from October 1, 2019, and are part of ongoing adjustments to the compensation cess framework, following the last amendment made in July 2018.
key_points: Inserts new entry 4A for caffeinated beverages with a compensation cess rate of 12%.
Amends entry 42 to specify motor vehicles for the transport of not more than 13 persons, excluding certain vehicles.
Revises entry 46 to include specific motor vehicles for persons with orthopedic disabilities, with certification requirements.
Changes entry 50 to substitute the vehicle classification codes to '8702, 8703 21 or 8703 22'.
Updates entry 51 to change the vehicle classification code to '8702, 8703 31'.
This notification comes into effect on October 1, 2019.
This notification amends the earlier notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) dated June 28, 2017.
The last amendment before this was made by notification No. 2/2018-Compensation Cess (Rate) dated July 26, 2018.

603.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 3/2019-Compensation Cess (Rate) on 30th September 2019, which introduces a significant change regarding the refund of unutilised input tax credit for certain goods. Specifically, this notification states that no refund will be allowed for goods classified under tariff item 24, which includes tobacco and manufactured tobacco substitutes. This restriction applies when the rate of compensation cess on inputs exceeds the rate on the output supplies of these goods. This means that businesses dealing with tobacco products will not be able to claim refunds on the excess input tax credit they accumulate due to this discrepancy in rates. The notification is based on the provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and the Goods and Services Tax (Compensation to States) Act, 2017, and aims to streamline the compensation cess framework for these specific goods.
key_points: Notification No. 3/2019-Compensation Cess (Rate) issued on 30th September 2019.
This notification specifies that no refund of unutilised input tax credit of compensation cess will be allowed for certain goods.
The goods affected fall under tariff item 24, which includes tobacco and manufactured tobacco substitutes.
The restriction applies when the input compensation cess rate is higher than the output compensation cess rate for these goods.
This notification is issued under the powers of section 54 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 and section 9 of the Goods and Services Tax (Compensation to States) Act, 2017.

604.summary: The recent amendment through Notification No. 01/2019-Central Tax, issued on January 15, 2019, modifies the earlier Notification No. 48/2017-Central Tax. A new proviso has been added to the Table against S. No. 1, which stipulates that if goods are supplied after exports where input tax credit was claimed on inputs used for those exports, a certificate from a chartered accountant must be submitted to the jurisdictional GST commissioner within six months of the supply. This requirement ensures that the goods are used in the manufacture and supply of taxable goods, excluding nil rated or fully exempted goods. However, if no input tax credit was availed, the certificate is not necessary. Additionally, the amendment removes the phrase 'on pre-import basis' from the Explanation related to serial number 1. These changes primarily affect businesses involved in the export of goods and their compliance with GST regulations regarding input tax credits.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 48/2017-Central Tax dated 18th October 2017.
Inserts a new proviso in the Table against S. No. 1 regarding goods supplied after export.
The new proviso requires a certificate from a chartered accountant if input tax credit was availed on inputs used for exports.
The certificate must be submitted to the jurisdictional GST commissioner within 6 months of supply.
No certificate is needed if input tax credit was not availed on inputs used in manufacturing export goods.
Omitted the phrase 'on pre-import basis' from the Explanation against serial number 1.

605.summary: Notification No. 01/2019-Integrated Tax, issued on January 29, 2019, announces that the provisions of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Act, 2018, which is also referred to as Act 32 of 2018, will take effect from February 1, 2019. This means that any changes or updates introduced by this amendment will be applicable starting on that date. The notification is a formal declaration by the Central Government, specifically the Ministry of Finance, and is based on the authority granted by sub-section (2) of section 1 of the same amendment act. Businesses and stakeholders in the GST framework should be aware of these changes as they will impact compliance and operational procedures from the specified date. It is essential for those affected to review the amendments to understand how they may influence their tax obligations and reporting requirements moving forward.
key_points: The notification is issued as Notification No. 01/2019-Integrated Tax.
It was published on January 29, 2019.
The provisions of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Act, 2018 (32 of 2018) will come into force on February 1, 2019.
This notification is issued under the powers of sub-section (2) of section 1 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Act, 2018.

606.summary: Notification No. 02/2019-Central Tax, issued on 29th January 2019, announces that several provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Act, 2018 will come into effect on 1st February 2019. However, it specifies that certain sections and clauses will not be implemented at this time. Specifically, clause (b) of section 8, section 17, section 18, clause (a) of section 20, and both sub-clauses (i) of clause (b) and (c) of section 28 are excluded from this enforcement. This means that while many changes under the Amendment Act will start to apply, these specific provisions will remain inactive. Businesses and stakeholders should note these exclusions as they prepare for the upcoming changes in GST regulations. The notification clarifies which parts of the Amendment Act are not yet applicable, allowing for a smoother transition for those affected by the new GST rules.
key_points: The notification is issued on 29th January 2019.
The effective date for the provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Act, 2018 is 1st February 2019.
The provisions coming into force exclude clause (b) of section 8.
Section 17 of the Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Act, 2018 is not coming into force.
Section 18 of the Act is also excluded from coming into force.
Clause (a) of section 20 is not effective from the appointed date.
Sub-clause (i) of clause (b) and sub-clause (i) of clause (c) of section 28 are excluded from the provisions coming into force.

607.summary: Notification No. 02/2019-Integrated Tax, issued on 29th January 2019, brings an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 7/2017-Integrated Tax, which was published on 14th September 2017. Specifically, this amendment changes a figure in the proviso of clause (b) from '151' to '5'. This change is significant as it alters the parameters or thresholds that may affect how integrated tax is applied. The amendment will come into effect from 1st February 2019, meaning that businesses and stakeholders should take note of this change as it may impact their tax calculations and compliance. This notification is part of the government's ongoing efforts to streamline and clarify tax regulations under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act. It is essential for businesses to understand this adjustment to ensure they are aligned with the updated tax framework.
key_points: This notification is issued as Notification No. 02/2019-Integrated Tax.
It amends Notification No. 7/2017-Integrated Tax dated 14th September 2017.
The amendment substitutes the figure '151' with '5' in the proviso of clause (b).
The effective date of this amendment is 1st February 2019.

608.summary: The Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Rules, 2019, which come into effect on February 1, 2019, introduce several significant changes to the GST framework. The heading in Chapter-II has been updated from 'Composition Rules' to 'Composition Levy', reflecting a broader scope. Notably, rule 7 now includes 'goods and services' instead of just 'goods', expanding the applicability of the rules. A new rule 11 clarifies the conditions under which businesses can obtain separate registrations for multiple locations, ensuring compliance across different places of business. Additionally, rule 21A has been introduced, allowing for the suspension of registration while cancellation proceedings are underway. This means that businesses may face a temporary halt in their registration status during such processes. Furthermore, rule 41A facilitates the transfer of unutilised input tax credit when a business obtains separate registrations, streamlining the process. Other amendments include the addition of entry 92A in rules 42 and 43, and the introduction of FORM GST ITC-02A specifically for ITC transfers. These changes are crucial for businesses operating in multiple locations and those managing their input tax credits effectively.
key_points: The amendment rules are called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Rules, 2019, effective from February 1, 2019.
The heading in Chapter-II is changed from 'Composition Rules' to 'Composition Levy'.
In rule 7, the term 'goods' is replaced with 'goods and services' in the table entry against serial number (3).
Rule 11 is substituted to clarify conditions for obtaining separate registration for multiple places of business.
New rule 21A introduces the suspension of registration pending cancellation proceedings.
Rule 41A is added for transferring unutilised input tax credit when obtaining separate registration.
Changes in rules 42 and 43 include the addition of entry 92A and the omission of certain clauses.
FORM GST ITC-02A is introduced for transferring ITC upon obtaining separate registration.

609.summary: Notification No. 03/2019-Integrated Tax, issued on 29th January 2019, brings an important amendment to Notification No. 10/2017-Integrated Tax from 13th October 2017. The key change involves substituting the existing text in the proviso concerning the Constitution's article 279A. The new wording now references the first proviso to sub-section (1) of section 22 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, which clarifies the provisions related to the Act. This amendment is set to take effect from 1st February 2019. It is important to note that this change does not apply to the State of Jammu and Kashmir. Businesses and stakeholders involved in the Integrated Goods and Services Tax framework will need to understand this amendment as it may impact their compliance and reporting obligations under the GST regime. The amendment aims to streamline the legal language and ensure clarity in the application of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act.
key_points: This notification is dated 29th January 2019.
It amends Notification No. 10/2017-Integrated Tax issued on 13th October 2017.
The amendment substitutes the existing text in the proviso regarding the Constitution's article 279A.
The new text refers to the first proviso to sub-section (1) of section 22 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act.
The amendment will come into effect from 1st February 2019.
The changes specifically affect the interpretation of the provisions related to the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act.
The amendment does not apply to the State of Jammu and Kashmir.

610.summary: Notification No. 04/2019-Central Tax, issued on 29th January 2019, introduces several amendments to the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax from 19th June 2017. Notably, it adds a new entry for the 'Joint Commissioner of Central Tax (Appeals)' after the existing serial number (k). Additionally, the phrase 'or Joint Commissioners' is now included after 'Additional Commissioners' in paragraph 2, serial number (c), broadening the scope of authority. Furthermore, paragraph 4 has been updated to replace 'Additional Commissioners of Central Tax (Appeals)' with 'any officer not below the rank of Joint Commissioner (Appeals)', clarifying the rank of officers who can handle appeals. The amendments also extend to Table I and Table III, where 'or Joint Commissioner' is added after 'Additional Commissioner'. These changes will be effective from 1st February 2019, impacting the roles and responsibilities of tax officers involved in appeals and decision-making processes.
key_points: Notification No. 04/2019-Central Tax was issued on 29th January 2019.
This notification amends Notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax dated 19th June 2017.
A new entry for 'Joint Commissioner of Central Tax (Appeals)' is added after serial number (k).
The term 'or Joint Commissioners' is added after 'Additional Commissioners' in paragraph 2, serial number (c).
In paragraph 4, 'Additional Commissioners of Central Tax (Appeals)' is replaced with 'any officer not below the rank of Joint Commissioner (Appeals)'.
The words 'or Joint Commissioner' are inserted in Table I and Table III after 'Additional Commissioner'.
The changes take effect from 1st February 2019.

611.summary: Notification No. 04/2019-Integrated Tax, issued on 30th September 2019, introduces changes regarding the place of supply for specific research and development services in the pharmaceutical sector. From 1st October 2019, the place of supply for these services will be determined by the location of the recipient, provided they are located in a non-taxable territory. This applies to services rendered under a contract between a service provider in a taxable territory and a recipient in a non-taxable territory. The services must also fulfill all conditions of export of services, except for one specific clause in the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act. The notification outlines various types of services affected, such as integrated discovery and development, evaluation of efficacy in animal models, and clinical trials. This change primarily impacts pharmaceutical companies and service providers engaged in research and development activities, ensuring clarity on tax obligations related to these services.
key_points: Notification No. 04/2019-Integrated Tax was issued on 30th September 2019.
The notification specifies that the place of supply for certain research and development services in the pharmaceutical sector will be the location of the recipient in non-taxable territory.
This applies to services provided under a contract between a service provider in taxable territory and a recipient in non-taxable territory.
The services must meet all conditions of export of services, except for sub-clause (iii) of clause (6) of Section 2 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Table A lists the services affected, including integrated discovery and development, evaluation of efficacy in animal models, and clinical trials.
The notification comes into effect on 1st October 2019.

612.summary: Notification No. 05/2019-Central Tax, issued on 29th January 2019, introduces an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Central Tax, which was published on 27th June 2017. The key change is in how the tax is calculated for certain suppliers. Previously, the tax was calculated at a fixed rate, but now it will be based on the rate specified in rule 7 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This change aims to align the tax calculation method with the existing rules, providing clarity and consistency for businesses. The new calculation method will come into effect from 1st February 2019. This amendment affects all suppliers who were previously using the fixed percentage method for tax calculation, requiring them to adjust their processes to comply with the new rule. Overall, this notification reflects the government's ongoing efforts to streamline GST regulations and ensure they are in line with the broader tax framework.
key_points: Notification No. 05/2019-Central Tax was issued on 29th January 2019.
This notification amends Notification No. 8/2017-Central Tax dated 27th June 2017.
The amendment changes the calculation of tax from a fixed percentage to a rate specified in rule 7 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The new tax calculation will be effective from 1st February 2019.

613.summary: Notification No. 06/2019-Central Tax, issued on 29th January 2019, brings an important amendment to the earlier notification No. 65/2017-Central Tax from 15th November 2017. The key change involves a substitution in the proviso concerning the applicability of certain provisions under the GST Act. Previously, the notification referred to a specific clause in the Constitution related to the State of Jammu and Kashmir, but this has now been updated to reference the first proviso to sub-section (1) of section 22 of the GST Act, along with an explanation clause. This change clarifies the legal framework under which the GST provisions apply, ensuring that the rules are aligned with the current legislative context. The amendment will take effect from 1st February 2019, impacting all stakeholders involved in GST compliance and reporting. Businesses and tax professionals should note this change to ensure their understanding and application of the GST provisions are accurate moving forward.
key_points: This notification is numbered 06/2019-Central Tax and was issued on 29th January 2019.
It amends notification No. 65/2017-Central Tax dated 15th November 2017.
The amendment substitutes the existing text in the proviso regarding the applicability of certain provisions.
The previous reference was to 'sub-clause (g) of clause (4) of article 279A of the Constitution, other than the State of Jammu and Kashmir'.
The new reference is to 'the first proviso to sub-section (1) of section 22 of the said Act, read with clause (iii) of the Explanation to the said section'.
This amendment will come into effect from 1st February 2019.

614.summary: On 31st January 2019, the Government of India issued Notification No. 07/2019-Central Tax, which amends the earlier Notification No. 66/2018-Central Tax dated 29th November 2018. The key change in this amendment is the extension of the due date for a specific compliance requirement from 31st January 2019 to 28th February 2019. This change is made under the authority of sub-section (6) of section 39 and section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The principal notification, which is now amended, was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i). This extension will affect businesses that are required to comply with the deadlines set in the earlier notification, providing them with additional time to meet their obligations. The amendment aims to ease compliance pressures for taxpayers during this period.
key_points: This notification is issued on 31st January 2019.
It amends Notification No. 66/2018-Central Tax dated 29th November 2018.
The amendment changes the due date from 31st January 2019 to 28th February 2019.
The amendment is made under sub-section (6) of section 39 and section 168 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The principal notification No. 66/2018 was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i).
The notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.

615.summary: Notification No. 08/2019-Central Tax, issued on 8th February 2019, extends the deadline for registered persons who are required to deduct tax at source under section 51 of the CGST Act. Specifically, the due date for filing GSTR-7 for the month of January 2019 has been moved to 28th February 2019. This change is significant for those businesses that fall under the category of tax deductors, as they now have additional time to complete their filing obligations. The extension is made under the authority of sub-section (6) of section 39 and section 168 of the CGST Act, ensuring compliance with the regulatory framework. This notification is part of the ongoing efforts by the government to facilitate smoother tax compliance for businesses. It is important for affected taxpayers to take note of this new deadline to ensure they meet their obligations without any issues.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-7 for January 2019.
New due date for GSTR-7 is 28th February 2019.
Applicable to registered persons required to deduct tax at source under section 51.
This notification is issued under sub-section (6) of section 39 and section 168 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The extension applies specifically to the month of January 2019.
The notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.
The original notification is dated 8th February 2019.

616.summary: Notification 09/2019-Central Tax, issued on 20th February 2019, introduces amendments to the earlier notification 34/2018-Central Tax from 10th August 2018. Specifically, it adds two new provisos regarding the filing of the GSTR-3B return for January 2019. The first proviso mandates that all registered persons must submit their GSTR-3B return electronically by 22nd February 2019. However, for those whose principal place of business is in Jammu and Kashmir, the deadline is extended to 28th February 2019. This change is significant as it provides additional time for businesses in Jammu and Kashmir to comply with their filing obligations. Overall, this amendment aims to accommodate the unique circumstances of businesses operating in that region while ensuring timely tax reporting for all registered persons across India.
key_points: The notification is titled 09/2019-Central Tax and was issued on 20th February 2019.
It amends notification 34/2018-Central Tax, originally issued on 10th August 2018.
The amendment adds two new provisos after the sixth proviso in the first paragraph of notification 34/2018.
The first new proviso states that the GSTR-3B return for January 2019 must be filed by 22nd February 2019.
The second new proviso specifies that for registered persons in Jammu and Kashmir, the GSTR-3B return for January 2019 is due by 28th February 2019.

617.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 10/2019-Central Tax, which was originally published on 7th March 2019. This corrigendum, dated 29th March 2019, makes specific corrections to the original notification. In line 17, the reference '(iii)' has been changed to '(c)', and in line 19, the reference '(iv)' has been changed to '(d)'. These changes clarify the intended references in the notification, ensuring that the document accurately reflects the correct numbering. This correction is important for businesses and stakeholders who rely on the precise language of tax notifications for compliance and reporting purposes. It is essential for those affected by this notification to review these changes to ensure they are following the correct guidelines as intended by the government.
key_points: This is a corrigendum to Notification No. 10/2019-Central Tax issued on 7th March 2019.
In line 17 of the original notification, '(iii)' is corrected to '(c)'.
In line 19 of the original notification, '(iv)' is corrected to '(d)'.
The corrigendum was issued on 29th March 2019.

618.summary: Notification No. 10/2019-Central Tax, issued on 7th March 2019, introduces an exemption from GST registration for individuals whose aggregate turnover does not exceed forty lakh rupees and who are engaged solely in the supply of goods. However, this exemption does not apply to certain categories of persons. Specifically, those required to register under section 24 of the GST Act, individuals supplying specific goods such as ice cream, pan masala, and tobacco products, and those making intra-State supplies in states like Arunachal Pradesh and Telangana are excluded from this exemption. The goods listed in the notification include ice cream (tariff item 2105), pan masala (tariff item 2106), and all tobacco products (tariff item 24). This change is significant for small businesses and suppliers in the specified states, as it clarifies who is required to register for GST. The new rules will take effect from 1st April 2019.
key_points: Notification No. 10/2019-Central Tax was issued on 7th March 2019.
The notification specifies that persons with an aggregate turnover not exceeding forty lakh rupees, engaged in exclusive supply of goods, are exempt from GST registration.
Exceptions to this exemption include persons required to register under section 24, those supplying specific goods listed in the table, and those making intra-State supplies in certain states.
The specified goods exempt from registration include ice cream (tariff item 2105), pan masala (tariff item 2106), and all tobacco products (tariff item 24).
The notification will come into effect on 1st April 2019.

619.summary: Notification No. 11/2019-Central Tax, issued on 7th March 2019, introduces a special procedure for registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to 1.5 crore rupees. These businesses are required to submit details of their outward supply of goods or services in FORM GSTR-1, as per the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. The first reporting period for this requirement covers the quarter from April to June 2019, with a submission deadline set for 31st July 2019. This notification specifically targets smaller businesses, allowing them to comply with GST requirements in a streamlined manner. Additionally, the notification mentions that the time limit for submitting details for the earlier months from July 2017 to June 2019 will be announced in the future. This is important for businesses to note, as it indicates that there will be further guidance on compliance for those earlier periods.
key_points: Notification No. 11/2019-Central Tax was issued on 7th March 2019.
Registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to 1.5 crore rupees are affected.
These registered persons must follow a special procedure for furnishing details of outward supply.
Details must be submitted in FORM GSTR-1 under the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The first quarter for which details must be furnished is April to June 2019.
The deadline for submitting FORM GSTR-1 for this quarter is 31st July 2019.
The time limit for furnishing details for the months of July 2017 to June 2019 will be notified later.

620.summary: Notification No. 12/2019-Central Tax, issued on March 7, 2019, brings an important change for registered persons with an aggregate turnover exceeding 1.5 crore rupees. It extends the deadline for filing GSTR-1, which details outward supplies, for the months of April, May, and June 2019. The new deadline for these months is now set for the 11th day of the month following each respective month. This means that businesses will have additional time to prepare and submit their GSTR-1 returns for these months. Furthermore, the notification indicates that the time limits for other returns under sections 38 and 39, covering the period from July 2017 to June 2019, will be announced in a future official gazette. This extension aims to ease compliance for businesses during this period, allowing them to manage their reporting obligations more effectively.
key_points: Extends GSTR-1 filing deadline for registered persons with turnover over 1.5 crore.
Applicable for the months from April 2019 to June 2019.
New deadline is the 11th day of the month following each respective month.
This notification is issued under the second proviso to sub-section (1) of section 37 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The time limit for returns under sections 38 and 39 for July 2017 to June 2019 will be notified later.

621.summary: Notification No. 13/2019-Central Tax, issued on March 7, 2019, outlines the requirements for filing the GSTR-3B return for the months of April, May, and June 2019. It mandates that all registered taxpayers must submit their GSTR-3B returns electronically through the common portal by the twentieth day of the month following each respective month. This means that for April 2019, the return is due by May 20, for May by June 20, and for June by July 20. Additionally, taxpayers are required to settle their tax liabilities, including any ies, by debiting their electronic cash or credit ledger before the due date. This notification is based on the recommendations of the GST Council and aligns with section 49 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. It is crucial for businesses to adhere to these timelines to with the GST regulations.
key_points: The notification is issued under Notification No. 13/2019-Central Tax on March 7, 2019.
It specifies that the return in FORM GSTR-3B must be filed electronically for the months of April 2019 to June 2019.
The due date for filing FORM GSTR-3B is the twentieth day of the month following each respective month.
Registered persons must discharge their tax liability using FORM GSTR-3B by debiting their electronic cash or credit ledger.
This requirement is in accordance with section 49 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The notification is based on the recommendations of the GST Council.
The electronic filing must be done through the common portal.

622.summary: Notification No. 14/2019-Central Tax, issued on 7th March 2019, introduces changes to the composition scheme under the GST framework. This notification replaces the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Central Tax from June 2017. It allows eligible registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to ₹1.5 crore to opt for a simplified tax payment method. However, for certain northeastern states, the threshold is lowered to ₹75 lakh, making it easier for businesses in those regions to benefit from the scheme. Importantly, manufacturers of specific goods, including ice cream, pan masala, and all tobacco products, are excluded from this option. This means that while many small businesses can take advantage of the composition scheme, those in the specified manufacturing sectors cannot. The changes will take effect from 1st April 2019, providing a clearer framework for small businesses to comply with GST regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 14/2019-Central Tax was issued on 7th March 2019.
This notification supersedes Notification No. 8/2017-Central Tax dated 27th June 2017.
Eligible registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to ₹1.5 crore can opt for a composition scheme under section 10 of the CGST Act.
For certain states (Arunachal Pradesh, Manipur, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Nagaland, Sikkim, Tripura, Uttarakhand), the turnover threshold is reduced to ₹75 lakh.
Manufacturers of specific goods listed in the notification are ineligible for the composition scheme.
The specific goods that disqualify manufacturers from the composition scheme include ice cream, pan masala, and all tobacco products.
The notification will come into effect on 1st April 2019.

623.summary: Notification No. 15/2019-Central Tax, issued on March 28, 2019, extends the deadline for submitting FORM GST ITC-04, which is used for reporting goods sent to or received from job workers. The new deadline is set for June 30, 2019, allowing businesses more time to comply with this requirement. This extension applies to transactions that occurred between July 2017 and March 2019. The notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 78/2018-Central Tax, which was issued on December 31, 2018, thus providing a fresh timeline for businesses that may have struggled to meet the previous deadline. This change is significant for businesses engaged in job work, as it ensures they can accurately report their transactions without the pressure of an immediate deadline. The notification is issued under the authority of section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and aligns with the rules governing GST compliance.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing FORM GST ITC-04.
New deadline for FORM GST ITC-04 is June 30, 2019.
Covers goods dispatched to or received from job workers.
Applicable for the period from July 2017 to March 2019.
Supersedes Notification No. 78/2018-Central Tax dated December 31, 2018.
Issued under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Also refers to sub-rule (3) of rule 45 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

624.summary: Notification No. 16/2019-Central Tax, issued on 29th March 2019, amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, effective from the date of publication. A significant change is the addition of an explanation in Rule 4(1) that clarifies the 'value of assets' includes all business assets, irrespective of whether input tax credit has been claimed. Furthermore, Rule 42 has been updated to state that the value of T4 will be zero during the construction phase for specific services, and it now mandates reporting in FORM GSTR-3B alongside FORM GSTR-2. The amendments also introduce new calculations for input tax credit for ongoing projects and those starting after 1st April 2019, ensuring clarity on how to handle projects with both exempt and non-exempt supplies. Additionally, Rule 43 has been revised to include FORM GSTR-3B for reporting input tax related to capital goods, enhancing the compliance framework for businesses involved in construction and real estate. These changes primarily affect businesses in the construction sector and those dealing with real estate projects.
key_points: Notification No. 16/2019-Central Tax was issued on 29th March 2019.
The rules come into effect from the date of publication in the official gazette.
An explanation was added to Rule 4(1) clarifying that 'value of assets' includes the entire assets of the business, regardless of input tax credit status.
Changes to Rule 42 include that from 1st April 2019, the value of T4 is zero during the construction phase for certain services.
Rule 42 now requires reporting in FORM GSTR-3B in addition to FORM GSTR-2.
New calculations for input tax credit are specified for ongoing projects and those commencing after 1st April 2019.
Rule 43 was amended to include FORM GSTR-3B for reporting input tax related to capital goods.
The amendments clarify how to calculate input tax credit for projects with both exempt and non-exempt supplies.

625.summary: Notification No. 17/2019-Central Tax, issued on April 10, 2019, introduces an important amendment to Notification No. 44/2018-Central Tax from September 10, 2018. Specifically, it adds a new proviso that mandates the filing of GSTR-1 for the month of March 2019 by April 13, 2019. This requirement applies to all businesses that need to report their outward supply of goods or services. The amendment is made under the authority of section 37(1) and section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This change is crucial for businesses as it sets a clear deadline for compliance regarding their sales reporting for March 2019. It is essential for affected businesses to take note of this new deadline to ensure they meet the filing requirements as stipulated in the amended notification.
key_points: This notification is issued on April 10, 2019.
It amends Notification No. 44/2018-Central Tax dated September 10, 2018.
A new proviso is added after the fourth proviso in the first paragraph of the earlier notification.
The new proviso states that GSTR-1 for March 2019 must be filed by April 13, 2019.
The filing is required for details of outward supply of goods or services.
This amendment is made under the powers of section 37(1) and section 168 of the CGST Act, 2017.

626.summary: Notification No. 18/2019-Central Tax, issued on April 10, 2019, extends the deadline for filing GSTR-7 for the month of March 2019. The new due date is now April 12, 2019, giving registered persons who are required to deduct tax at source an additional two days to submit their returns. This extension is made under the powers conferred by sub-section (6) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The notification specifically targets those registered persons who fall under the provisions of section 51 of the Act. By allowing this extension, the government aims to provide some relief to taxpayers in meeting their compliance obligations. The notification is officially issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs, ensuring that all affected parties are informed of this change in the filing timeline.
key_points: Extends the due date for GSTR-7 for March 2019 from April 10, 2019, to April 12, 2019.
Applicable to registered persons required to deduct tax at source under section 51 of the CGST Act.
The extension is granted under sub-section (6) of section 39 of the CGST Act.
The notification is issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.

627.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 20/2019-Central Tax on April 23, 2019, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. Key changes include new provisions in rule 23 that require taxpayers to file all returns for the period between the cancellation and revocation of their GST registration within 30 days. Additionally, rule 62 has been updated to change the terminology from 'quarterly return' to 'statement and return' for composition suppliers. A new Form GST CMP-08 has been introduced for quarterly self-assessed tax payments, which must be submitted by the 18th of the month following the quarter. Furthermore, the deadline for filing GSTR-4 has been established as the 30th of April following the end of the financial year. Taxpayers who wish to avail themselves of the benefits under notification No. 02/2019 must now indicate this in Form GST REG-01. These amendments will take effect immediately upon publication in the Official Gazette.
key_points: Notification No. 20/2019-Central Tax was issued on April 23, 2019.
The rules amend the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, specifically rule 23 and rule 62.
New provisions added to rule 23 require returns for the period between cancellation and revocation of registration to be filed within 30 days.
Rule 62 now includes changes to the submission of quarterly returns for composition suppliers, replacing 'quarterly return' with 'statement and return'.
Form GST CMP-08 is introduced for quarterly self-assessed tax payments, to be submitted by the 18th of the month following the quarter.
The deadline for filing GSTR-4 has been set to the 30th of April following the end of the financial year.
Taxpayers opting for the benefit of notification No. 02/2019 must indicate this in Form GST REG-01.
The amendments come into effect on the date of publication in the Official Gazette.

628.summary: Notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax, issued on April 23, 2019, introduces a special procedure for registered persons who pay tax under section 10 of the Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) Act. These individuals are now required to submit a quarterly statement using FORM GST CMP-08 by the 18th day of the month following each quarter. Additionally, they must file an annual return in FORM GSTR-4 by April 30 of the year following the financial year in question. This notification clarifies that if these registered persons file both FORM GST CMP-08 and FORM GSTR-4 as specified, they will be considered compliant with sections 37 and 39 of the CGST Act for the periods covered. This change primarily affects small taxpayers who opt for the composition scheme under the CGST Act, streamlining their reporting obligations and ensuring they meet their tax responsibilities effectively.
key_points: Registered persons paying tax under section 10 of the CGST Act are notified to follow a special procedure.
These persons must furnish a statement in FORM GST CMP-08 every quarter by the 18th day of the month following the quarter.
They are also required to submit a return in FORM GSTR-4 for each financial year by the 30th day of April following the end of that financial year.
Filing FORM GST CMP-08 and FORM GSTR-4 will be deemed compliance with sections 37 and 39 of the CGST Act for the relevant periods.

629.summary: Notification No. 22/2019-Central Tax, issued on April 23, 2019, establishes June 21, 2019, as the effective date for specific provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourteenth) Amendment Rules, 2018. This change specifically pertains to rule 12 of the earlier notification No. 74/2018-Central Tax, which was published on December 31, 2018. The amendment is made under the authority of section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This means that businesses and taxpayers should be aware that the rules outlined in the Fourteenth Amendment will come into effect on the specified date, impacting how they comply with GST regulations. It is important for those affected to review the changes to ensure they understand the new requirements and how they may need to adjust their processes accordingly.
key_points: The notification is dated April 23, 2019.
It appoints June 21, 2019, as the effective date for certain provisions.
The provisions relate to the Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourteenth) Amendment Rules, 2018.
Specifically, it pertains to rule 12 of notification No. 74/2018-Central Tax, dated December 31, 2018.
This notification is issued under the authority of section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

630.summary: Notification No. 23/2019-Central Tax, issued on 11th May 2019, introduces an amendment to the earlier notification No. 12/2019-Central Tax from 7th March 2019. The amendment adds a new proviso that requires registered persons in specific districts of Odisha to file their GSTR-1 for the month of April 2019 by 10th June 2019. The districts affected by this requirement include Angul, Balasore, Bhadrak, Cuttack, Dhenkanal, Ganjam, Jagatsinghpur, Jajpur, Kendrapara, Keonjhar, Khordha, Mayurbhanj, Nayagarh, and Puri. This change is significant for businesses operating in these areas, as they now have a specific deadline to submit their outward supply details electronically through the common portal. The amendment aims to streamline the filing process for these registered persons, ensuring compliance with the GST regulations in Odisha.
key_points: This notification is No. 23/2019-Central Tax, issued on 11th May 2019.
It amends notification No. 12/2019-Central Tax, dated 7th March 2019.
A new proviso is added to the first paragraph of notification No. 12/2019.
The new proviso states that GSTR-1 for April 2019 must be filed by 10th June 2019.
This filing requirement applies to registered persons in specific districts of Odisha.
The districts affected are Angul, Balasore, Bhadrak, Cuttack, Dhenkanal, Ganjam, Jagatsinghpur, Jajpur, Kendrapara, Keonjhar, Khordha, Mayurbhanj, Nayagarh, and Puri.

631.summary: Notification No. 24/2019-Central Tax introduces an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 13/2019-Central Tax. Specifically, it adds a new proviso that requires registered persons in certain districts of Odisha to file their GSTR-3B return for April 2019 electronically. The districts affected by this change include Angul, Balasore, Bhadrak, Cuttack, Dhenkanal, Ganjam, Jagatsinghpur, Jajpur, Kendrapara, Keonjhar, Khordha, Mayurbhanj, Nayagarh, and Puri. This filing must be completed by the deadline of 20th June 2019. This amendment is significant for businesses operating in these districts, as it specifies the method and timeline for compliance with GST regulations for the specified tax period. The change aims to streamline the filing process for these registered persons, ensuring they adhere to the updated requirements set forth by the government.
key_points: Inserts a new proviso in the first paragraph of Notification No. 13/2019-Central Tax.
The new proviso pertains to the filing of GSTR-3B for April 2019.
Registered persons in specific districts of Odisha must file GSTR-3B electronically.
The affected districts include Angul, Balasore, Bhadrak, Cuttack, Dhenkanal, Ganjam, Jagatsinghpur, Jajpur, Kendrapara, Keonjhar, Khordha, Mayurbhanj, Nayagarh, and Puri.
The due date for filing GSTR-3B for these districts is set for 20th June 2019.

632.summary: Notification No. 25/2019-Central Tax, issued on 21st June 2019, brings an important amendment to the earlier notification No. 22/2019-Central Tax dated 23rd April 2019. Specifically, this amendment changes the deadline from the 21st of June 2019 to the 21st of August 2019. This change is made under the authority of section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, indicating that the government is responding to public interest concerns. Businesses and taxpayers who were initially required to comply by June now have an extended deadline until August. This extension provides additional time for compliance, which may be beneficial for those who need it. It is crucial for affected parties to note this new date and adjust their plans accordingly to meet the revised deadline.
key_points: This notification is No. 25/2019-Central Tax, issued on 21st June 2019.
It amends notification No. 22/2019-Central Tax, dated 23rd April 2019.
The amendment changes the date from '21st day of June, 2019' to '21st day of August, 2019'.
The amendment is made under the powers conferred by section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

633.summary: Notification No. 26/2019-Central Tax, issued on June 28, 2019, extends the deadline for registered persons required to deduct tax at source to file their GSTR-7 returns. This extension applies specifically to the tax periods from October 2018 through July 2019, with the new deadline set for August 31, 2019. This notification supersedes three earlier notifications: 66/2018, 8/2019, and 18/2019, which had previously set different deadlines. The GSTR-7 form is specifically for those registered persons who are obligated to deduct tax at source under the provisions of section 51 of the CGST Act. By extending this deadline, the government aims to provide additional time for compliance for those affected by these requirements. This change is significant for businesses that fall under this category, ensuring they have ample time to prepare and submit their returns accurately.
key_points: Extends the time limit for filing GSTR-7 returns.
Applicable for the months from October 2018 to July 2019.
New deadline for filing is now August 31, 2019.
Supersedes earlier notifications: 66/2018, 8/2019, and 18/2019.
GSTR-7 is for registered persons required to deduct tax at source.
The extension is granted under section 39(6) of the CGST Act.
This notification was issued on June 28, 2019.

634.summary: Notification No. 27/2019-Central Tax, issued on June 28, 2019, introduces a special procedure for registered persons whose aggregate turnover is up to 1.5 crore rupees in the preceding or current financial year. These businesses are required to submit their details of outward supplies of goods or services in FORM GSTR-1. Specifically, for the quarter of July to September 2019, the deadline for submitting this information is set for October 31, 2019. Additionally, the notification mentions that the time limits for filing details for the months of July, August, and September 2019 will be announced in the Official Gazette at a later date. This change primarily affects small businesses with lower turnover, simplifying their compliance process for reporting sales during this period.
key_points: Registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to 1.5 crore rupees are notified for a special procedure.
These registered persons must furnish details of outward supply in FORM GSTR-1.
The details for the quarter July-September 2019 must be submitted by 31st October 2019.
The time limit for furnishing details for July to September 2019 will be notified later.

635.summary: Notification No. 28/2019-Central Tax, issued on June 28, 2019, extends the deadline for filing GSTR-1 for registered taxpayers whose aggregate turnover exceeds 1.5 crore rupees. The new deadline for submitting GSTR-1 for the months of July, August, and September 2019 is now set to the 11th day of the month following each respective month. This change is significant for businesses that fall under this turnover category, as it provides additional time to prepare and file their outward supply details. Furthermore, the notification indicates that the deadlines for other returns under sections 38 and 39 for the same months will be communicated in a future official announcement. This extension aims to ease compliance burdens for businesses during this period, allowing them to manage their reporting more effectively.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing GSTR-1 for registered persons with turnover over 1.5 crore rupees.
New deadline for GSTR-1 is the 11th day of the month following each month.
This extension applies to the months of July 2019, August 2019, and September 2019.
The notification is issued under the second proviso to sub-section (1) of section 37 of the CGST Act, 2017.
Details for returns under sections 38 and 39 for the same months will be notified later.

636.summary: Notification No. 29/2019-Central Tax, issued on June 28, 2019, outlines the requirements for filing the GSTR-3B return for the months of July, August, and September 2019. It mandates that all registered persons must submit their GSTR-3B returns electronically through the common portal by the twentieth day of the month following each respective month. This means that for July 2019, the return is due by August 20, for August by September 20, and for September by October 20. Additionally, registered persons are required to settle their tax liabilities using their electronic cash or credit ledger by the same due date. This notification is based on the powers granted under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and aligns with the provisions of sub-rule (5) of rule 61 of the GST Rules. This change affects all registered taxpayers who need to comply with the new filing and payment procedures during this specified period.
key_points: The notification specifies that the return in FORM GSTR-3B must be filed electronically.
The filing period for FORM GSTR-3B is from July 2019 to September 2019.
The due date for filing FORM GSTR-3B is the twentieth day of the month following the respective month.
Registered persons must discharge their tax liabilities using the electronic cash or credit ledger.
This notification is issued under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It references sub-rule (5) of rule 61 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

637.summary: Notification No. 30/2019-Central Tax, issued on June 28, 2019, introduces specific exemptions for a certain class of registered persons under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This applies to those who provide online information and database access or retrieval services from outside India to individuals in India who are not registered under GST. These registered persons will no longer be required to file the annual return in FORM GSTR-9 or the reconciliation statement in FORM GSTR-9C. This change is significant as it reduces the compliance burden for these service providers, allowing them to focus on their core business activities without the added requirement of these filings. The exemptions are based on recommendations from the GST Council and are aimed at simplifying the tax process for those supplying digital services from abroad. The relevant legal references include section 44 and rule 80 of the GST Rules, which outline the filing requirements that have now been waived for this specific group.
key_points: Notification No. 30/2019-Central Tax was issued on 28th June 2019.
This notification applies to persons registered under section 24 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
These registered persons are supplying online information and database access or retrieval services from outside India to unregistered persons in India.
Affected persons are exempt from filing the annual return in FORM GSTR-9.
Affected persons are also exempt from furnishing the reconciliation statement in FORM GSTR-9C.
The exemptions are based on recommendations from the GST Council.
The relevant sections and rules cited include section 44 and rule 80 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

638.summary: Notification No. 31/2019-Central Tax introduces several amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. A significant change is the introduction of rule 10A, which mandates that registered persons must provide their bank account details within 45 days of receiving their registration or by the due date of their first return, whichever comes first. Additionally, rule 21 has been amended to include violations of this new rule as grounds for cancellation of registration. The notification also inserts rule 32A, clarifying that the value of supply for goods and services subject to the Kerala Flood Cess will be calculated according to section 15 of the GST Act, excluding the cess itself. Furthermore, provisions have been added to allow for QR codes on tax invoices and bills of supply, with implementation dates to be announced later. Retail outlets located in international airport departure areas can now claim refunds on taxes paid for goods sold to international tourists, as per the newly introduced rule 95A. Lastly, rule 138E has been amended to replace 'returns' with 'statement in FORM GST CMP-08' and 'tax periods' with 'quarters', streamlining the compliance process for certain taxpayers.
key_points: Introduces rule 10A requiring registered persons to furnish bank account details within 45 days of registration or due return date, whichever is earlier.
Amends rule 21 to include violation of rule 10A as a reason for cancellation of registration.
Inserts rule 32A specifying that the value of supply for Kerala Flood Cess will be determined as per section 15 of the Act, excluding the cess.
Adds a new proviso in rule 46 allowing for QR codes on tax invoices, effective from a date to be notified later.
Adds a new proviso in rule 49 allowing for QR codes on bills of supply, effective from a date to be notified later.
Amends rule 67 to clarify claiming tax collected in the electronic cash ledger after validation.
Introduces rule 95A allowing retail outlets at international airports to claim refunds on taxes paid for goods supplied to outgoing international tourists.
Changes in rule 138E to substitute 'returns' with 'statement in FORM GST CMP-08' and 'tax periods' with 'quarters'.

639.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 32/2019-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for filing FORM GST ITC-04. This form is crucial for businesses that have dispatched goods to job workers or received goods from them during the period from July 2017 to June 2019. The new deadline for submitting this form is now set for 31st August 2019. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 15/2019-Central Tax, which was issued on 28th March 2019. The extension is provided under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and is based on sub-rule (3) of rule 45 of the GST Rules. This change is significant for businesses engaged in job work, as it allows them additional time to comply with the filing requirements for this specific period. It is important for affected businesses to take note of this new deadline to ensure they meet their obligations under the GST framework.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing FORM GST ITC-04.
New deadline for FORM GST ITC-04 is 31st August 2019.
Covers goods dispatched to or received from job workers.
Applicable for the period from July 2017 to June 2019.
Supersedes Notification No. 15/2019-Central Tax dated 28th March 2019.
Issued under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Based on sub-rule (3) of rule 45 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

640.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 33/2019-Central Tax, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This amendment introduces several changes effective from the date of publication. Notably, Rule 12(1A) now includes provisions for 'deduct or' and references to section 51. Additionally, Rule 46's fourth proviso has been modified to exempt suppliers of services related to admission to multiplex cinemas from certain requirements starting September 1, 2019. A new sub-rule (4A) under Rule 54 mandates that electronic tickets for multiplex cinema admissions will be considered tax invoices. Furthermore, a new Rule 83B has been added, detailing the process for GST practitioners to surrender their enrolment via FORM GST PCT-06. The amendment also extends the duration for certain actions in Rule 137 from two years to four years. Lastly, new forms have been introduced for both the cancellation of GST practitioner enrolment and for unblocking E-Way Bill generation, enhancing the procedural framework for GST compliance.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Fifth Amendment) Rules, 2019'.
It amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, effective from the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
Rule 12(1A) is amended to include the words 'deduct or' and 'section 51, or, as the case may be'.
Rule 46's fourth proviso now excludes suppliers of services related to admission to cinematograph films in multiplex screens from certain requirements, effective from September 1, 2019.
Rule 54 introduces a new sub-rule (4A) requiring electronic tickets for multiplex cinema admissions to be treated as tax invoices.
A new rule 83B is added for the surrender of GST practitioner enrolment, requiring an application in FORM GST PCT-06.
Rule 137 changes the duration for certain actions from 'two years' to 'four years'.
New forms GST PCT-06 and GST PCT-07 are introduced for GST practitioner enrolment cancellation, and FORM GST EWB-05 and EWB-06 for unblocking E-Way Bill generation.

641.summary: Notification No. 34/2019-Central Tax, issued on 18th July 2019, introduces an important amendment to the earlier notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax from 23rd April 2019. Specifically, it adds a new proviso in paragraph 2 that establishes a new due date for filing the statement of self-assessed tax. Taxpayers who need to submit FORM GST CMP-08 for the quarter from April 2019 to June 2019 now have until 31st July 2019 to do so. This change is significant for businesses that fall under the composition scheme, as it provides them with an extended timeline to fulfill their tax obligations for that quarter. The amendment aims to ease compliance for these taxpayers by allowing additional time to prepare and submit their tax details. Overall, this notification reflects the government's ongoing efforts to streamline GST processes and support businesses in meeting their tax responsibilities.
key_points: This notification is No. 34/2019-Central Tax, issued on 18th July 2019.
It amends notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax, dated 23rd April 2019.
A new proviso is added to paragraph 2 of the earlier notification.
The new due date for submitting FORM GST CMP-08 for the quarter April 2019 to June 2019 is set to 31st July 2019.

642.summary: Notification No. 35/2019-Central Tax, issued on 29th July 2019, extends the deadline for compliance from 31st July 2019 to 31st August 2019. This change amends the earlier Notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax, which was published on 23rd April 2019. The amendment is made under section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and aims to provide additional time for taxpayers to meet their obligations. This notification is particularly relevant for those who were affected by the deadlines set in the previous notifications. The principal notification No. 21/2019 was already amended by Notification No. 34/2019-Central Tax on 18th July 2019, indicating ongoing adjustments to compliance timelines. Taxpayers should take note of this new deadline to ensure they are aligned with the latest requirements. This extension is crucial for businesses to manage their GST filings effectively without the pressure of the earlier deadline.
key_points: Notification No. 35/2019-Central Tax was issued on 29th July 2019.
This notification amends Notification No. 21/2019-Central Tax dated 23rd April 2019.
The amendment changes the deadline from '31st day of July, 2019' to '31st day of August, 2019'.
The amendment is made under the powers conferred by section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The principal notification No. 21/2019 was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, G.S.R. 322(E).
Notification No. 34/2019-Central Tax dated 18th July 2019 also amended the principal notification.
The changes affect taxpayers who are required to comply with the deadlines set in the earlier notifications.
The new deadline applies specifically to the provisions outlined in the amended notification.

643.summary: On 20th August 2019, the Government of India issued Notification No. 36/2019-Central Tax, which amends the earlier Notification No. 22/2019-Central Tax dated 23rd April 2019. The key change in this amendment is the extension of the due date for a specific compliance requirement from 21st August 2019 to 21st November 2019. This decision was made under section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, following recommendations from the GST Council, indicating that the government recognizes the need for additional time for compliance. This change affects all businesses and taxpayers who were required to meet the original deadline, providing them with an extended period to fulfill their obligations. The principal notification had already been amended once before by Notification No. 25/2019-Central Tax on 21st June 2019, which shows that the government is actively managing compliance timelines in response to the needs of the business community.
key_points: The notification is issued on 20th August 2019.
It amends Notification No. 22/2019-Central Tax dated 23rd April 2019.
The original due date of 21st August 2019 is changed to 21st November 2019.
This amendment is made under section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The amendment is based on recommendations from the GST Council.
The principal notification was previously amended by Notification No. 25/2019-Central Tax dated 21st June 2019.

644.summary: Notification No. 37/2019-Central Tax, issued on 21st August 2019, introduces amendments to the earlier Notification No. 29/2019-Central Tax from 28th June 2019. It specifies that the return in FORM GSTR-3B for the month of July 2019 must be submitted electronically by 22nd August 2019. However, for registered persons in certain districts across various states, the deadline is extended to 20th September 2019. The states affected include Bihar, Gujarat, Karnataka, Kerala, Maharashtra, Odisha, and Uttarakhand, with specific districts listed for each state. Additionally, registered persons in Jammu and Kashmir also have until 20th September 2019 to file their GSTR-3B return. This notification is effective from 20th August 2019, providing clarity on the filing deadlines for different regions, ensuring that businesses in these areas are aware of their specific obligations.
key_points: This notification is issued on 21st August 2019.
It amends Notification No. 29/2019-Central Tax dated 28th June 2019.
The return in FORM GSTR-3B for July 2019 must be filed by 22nd August 2019.
For specific districts in certain states, the GSTR-3B return deadline is extended to 20th September 2019.
The states and districts affected include Bihar, Gujarat, Karnataka, Kerala, Maharashtra, Odisha, Uttarakhand, and Jammu and Kashmir.
The notification comes into effect from 20th August 2019.

646.summary: Notification No. 39/2019-Central Tax, issued on August 31, 2019, announces that the provisions of section 103 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019, will take effect from September 1, 2019. This means that any changes or requirements outlined in section 103 will now be applicable starting on that date. The notification is part of the government's efforts to implement new tax regulations and is published in the Gazette of India for official record. It is issued by the Ministry of Finance, specifically from the Department of Revenue, with Ruchi Bisht serving as the Under Secretary. This notification is significant for businesses and individuals who need to be aware of the new provisions that will impact their GST compliance and reporting. The reference number for this notification is F. No. 20/06/12/2018-GST, which may be useful for further inquiries or clarifications regarding the changes introduced.
key_points: The notification is issued under Notification No. 39/2019-Central Tax.
It was issued on August 31, 2019.
The provisions of section 103 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019, will come into force on September 1, 2019.
This notification is published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i).
The notification is issued by the Ministry of Finance, Department of Revenue.
Ruchi Bisht is the Under Secretary to the Government of India.
The notification references F. No. 20/06/12/2018-GST.

647.summary: Notification No. 40/2019-Central Tax, issued on 31st August 2019, amends the earlier Notification No. 26/2019-Central Tax from 28th June 2019. The key change is the introduction of new provisions for filing GSTR-7 for the month of July 2019. Specifically, registered persons who are required to deduct tax at source and whose principal place of business is in certain districts must file their returns electronically by 20th September 2019. The affected districts include several in Bihar, such as Araria and Kishanganj, as well as Vadodara in Gujarat, multiple districts in Karnataka, and others in Kerala, Maharashtra, Odisha, and Uttarakhand. Furthermore, registered persons in Jammu and Kashmir are also included in this requirement, ensuring they meet the same filing deadline. This amendment aims to streamline the filing process for these specific regions and with the GST regulations.
key_points: This notification is issued under Notification No. 40/2019-Central Tax on 31st August 2019.
It amends Notification No. 26/2019-Central Tax dated 28th June 2019.
The amendment introduces new provisos for filing GSTR-7 for July 2019.
Registered persons in specific districts of Bihar, Gujarat, Karnataka, Kerala, Maharashtra, Odisha, and Uttarakhand must file GSTR-7 by 20th September 2019.

The districts listed include Araria, Kishanganj, and others in Bihar; Vadodara in Gujarat; multiple districts in Karnataka; Idukki, Malappuram, and Wayanad in Kerala; several districts in Maharashtra; Balangir and others in Odisha; and Uttarkashi and Chamoli in Uttarakhand.
Additionally, registered persons in Jammu and Kashmir must also file GSTR-7 for July 2019 by the same deadline.

648.summary: Notification No. 41/2019-Central Tax, issued on August 31, 2019, waives the late fee for certain taxpayers under section 47 of the CGST Act. This waiver applies to registered persons whose principal place of business is in specific districts listed in the notification or in Jammu and Kashmir, provided they have an aggregate turnover exceeding 1.5 crore rupees in the preceding or current financial year. To qualify for the waiver, these taxpayers must submit their GSTR-1 for July 2019 by September 20, 2019. Additionally, Input Service Distributors located in the specified districts or Jammu and Kashmir can also benefit from this waiver if they file their GSTR-6 by the same deadline. The notification details specific districts in states such as Bihar, Gujarat, Karnataka, Kerala, Maharashtra, Odisha, and Uttarakhand, ensuring that eligible taxpayers in these areas can avoid late fees if they comply with the filing requirements on time.
key_points: Waives late fee under section 47 of the CGST Act for specific taxpayers.
Eligible taxpayers must have a principal place of business in specified districts or Jammu and Kashmir.
Taxpayers must have an aggregate turnover of more than 1.5 crore rupees in the preceding or current financial year.
Eligible taxpayers must file GSTR-1 for July 2019 by September 20, 2019.
Input Service Distributors in the specified districts or Jammu and Kashmir are also eligible if they file GSTR-6 by the same date.
The notification lists specific districts in Bihar, Gujarat, Karnataka, Kerala, Maharashtra, Odisha, and Uttarakhand.

649.summary: Notification No. 42/2019-Central Tax, issued on September 24, 2019, brings into effect several provisions from the Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourth Amendment) Rules, 2019. Specifically, rules 10, 11, 12, and 26 are now enforceable as of this date. This change is part of the ongoing updates to the GST framework, aimed at improving compliance and operational efficiency. The rules referenced in this notification were initially outlined in notification No. 31/2019-Central Tax, which was published on June 28, 2019. By enforcing these rules, the government is ensuring that the latest amendments to the GST regulations are implemented without delay. This notification is significant for all businesses and taxpayers who are subject to GST, as it directly impacts their compliance obligations under the updated rules. The Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs has issued this notification to clarify the effective date of these important provisions.
key_points: The notification is numbered 42/2019-Central Tax.
It was issued on September 24, 2019.
The provisions of rules 10, 11, 12, and 26 of the Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourth Amendment) Rules, 2019, will come into force on the same date.
This notification references the earlier notification No. 31/2019-Central Tax, dated June 28, 2019.
The earlier notification was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), as G.S.R 457(E).
The rules being enforced relate to the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The provisions are effective immediately from the date of this notification.
The notification was issued by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs.

650.summary: Notification No. 43/2019-Central Tax, issued on 30 September 2019, introduces an amendment to the earlier Notification No. 14/2019-Central Tax from 7 March 2019. Specifically, it adds a new entry for 'Aerated Water' under the HSN code 2202 10 10, which is now listed as Sl. No. 2A in the existing table. This change is significant for businesses dealing with aerated water products, as it clarifies the classification under the GST framework. The amendment will take effect from 1 October 2019, meaning that any transactions involving aerated water will need to adhere to this updated classification from that date onward. This change aims to streamline the GST process for this category of goods and ensure proper compliance with the tax regulations. Businesses should review their product classifications to ensure they align with this new entry.

651.summary: Notification No. 44/2019-Central Tax, issued on October 9, 2019, specifies that registered persons must file their GSTR-3B returns electronically for the months from October 2019 to March 2020. The due date for these returns is set for the twentieth day of the month following each respective month. This requirement is established under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and aligns with sub-rule (5) of rule 61 of the GST Rules. Additionally, registered persons are required to discharge their tax liabilities by debiting their electronic cash or credit ledger. This payment must be completed by the last date specified for filing the GSTR-3B return. Essentially, this notification clarifies the filing and payment process for GST returns during this period, ensuring that businesses are aware of their obligations regarding tax reporting and payment.

The due date for filing FORM GSTR-3B is the twentieth day of the month succeeding the respective month.

652.summary: Notification No. 45/2019-Central Tax, issued on October 9, 2019, introduces a special procedure for registered persons whose aggregate turnover is up to 1.5 crore rupees in the preceding or current financial year. These businesses are required to submit their details of outward supply of goods or services in FORM GSTR-1. For the quarter from October 2019 to December 2019, the deadline for submission is set for January 31, 2020. Similarly, for the subsequent quarter from January 2020 to March 2020, the deadline is April 30, 2020. Additionally, the notification mentions that the time limit for furnishing details for the months of October 2019 to March 2020 will be announced in the Official Gazette at a later date. This change specifically affects small businesses with lower turnover, streamlining their reporting process under the GST framework.

653.summary: Notification No. 46/2019-Central Tax, issued on October 9, 2019, extends the deadline for filing FORM GSTR-1 for registered persons whose aggregate turnover exceeds 1.5 crore rupees. This extension applies to the months from October 2019 through March 2020, allowing these businesses to submit their GSTR-1 by the eleventh day of the month following each respective month. This change is made under the second proviso to sub-section (1) of section 37 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Additionally, the timeline for submitting details or returns under section 38 for these months will be communicated in a future notification. This extension is significant for businesses with higher turnover, as it provides them with additional time to comply with their filing obligations during this period.
