654.summary: Notification No. 47/2019-Central Tax, issued on October 9, 2019, introduces a special procedure for certain registered persons under the GST framework. This applies specifically to those whose aggregate turnover does not exceed two crore rupees and who have not submitted their annual return for the financial years 2017-18 and 2018-19 by the due date. These individuals now have the option to furnish their annual return under section 44 of the GST Act, even if they missed the original deadline. Importantly, if they submit their return after the due date, it will still be considered as filed on time. This change aims to provide relief to smaller taxpayers who may have faced challenges in meeting their filing obligations. Overall, this notification offers a more flexible approach for compliance, allowing eligible taxpayers to rectify their filing status without facing penalties for late submission.

655.summary: The recent Notification No. 48/2019-Central Tax, issued on 9th October 2019, amends the earlier Notification No. 41/2019-Central Tax from 31st August 2019. One significant change is the adjustment of the due date for filing FORM GSTR-1, which is now set for 11th October 2019 instead of the previous date of 20th September 2019. Additionally, new provisions have been introduced specifically for registered persons in Jammu and Kashmir who have an aggregate turnover exceeding 1.5 crore rupees. These businesses must now file their FORM GSTR-1 for August 2019 by the new deadline of 11th October 2019. Furthermore, registered persons in Jammu and Kashmir are required to submit their FORM GSTR-7 for July and August 2019 by 10th October 2019. Lastly, the deadline for filing FORM GSTR-3B for July and August 2019 has been set for 20th October 2019. These changes primarily affect businesses operating in Jammu and Kashmir, ensuring they meet the revised filing requirements.

656.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 49/2019-Central Tax on 9th October 2019, which amends several rules under the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This notification introduces the Central Goods and Services Tax (Sixth Amendment) Rules, 2019, which clarify that during a suspension of registration, a registered person cannot issue tax invoices, as stated in the newly added explanation to rule 21A(3). Additionally, a new sub-rule (5) in rule 21A outlines the procedures for revoking such suspensions. Rule 36 has been amended to limit the input tax credit to 20% of the eligible credit for invoices that suppliers have not uploaded. Furthermore, rule 61 has been updated to allow for GSTR-3B filings under specific conditions when GSTR-1 or GSTR-2 deadlines are extended. The deadline for certain filings has also been extended to 31st December 2019 as per the changes in rule 117. Lastly, a new FORM GST DRC-01A has been introduced to communicate tax liabilities before issuing notices under sections 73 and 74, ensuring that taxpayers are informed of their obligations in a structured manner.
key_points: Notification No. 49/2019-Central Tax was issued on 9th October 2019.
The rules are called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Sixth Amendment) Rules, 2019.
An explanation was added to rule 21A(3) clarifying that 'shall not make any taxable supply' means no tax invoices can be issued during suspension.
A new sub-rule (5) was added to rule 21A, stating that revocation of suspension will follow specific procedures under sections 31(3) and 40.
Rule 36(4) now limits input tax credit to 20% of eligible credit for invoices not uploaded by suppliers.
Rule 61(5) was substituted to allow GSTR-3B filing under certain conditions when GSTR-1 or GSTR-2 deadlines are extended.
Rule 117(1A) changes the deadline for certain filings from 31st March 2019 to 31st December 2019.
A new FORM GST DRC-01A was introduced for communicating tax liabilities before issuing notices under sections 73 and 74.

Specifically, it adds a new proviso that sets the due date for submitting FORM GST CMP-08, which is the statement for self-assessed tax, for the quarter from July 2019 to September 2019. 663.summary: Notification No. 56/2019-Central Tax, issued on November 14, 2019, amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, particularly affecting FORM GST RFD-01 and FORM GSTR-9. The changes in FORM GST RFD-01 include new formats for Statements 1A, 2, 3, 4, 5, 5B, and 6, which detail various refund types, such as ITC accumulated due to inverted tax structure and refunds related to SEZ supplies. A new Statement 4A has also been introduced for refunds by SEZ on supplies received from DTA with payment of tax. In FORM GSTR-9, the notification clarifies entries for FY 2017-18 and FY 2018-19, allowing taxpayers to report certain details in a consolidated manner if they encounter difficulties. Additionally, it mandates the filing of FORM GSTR-1 and FORM GSTR-3B before submitting GSTR-9. These amendments are effective immediately upon publication in the Official Gazette, impacting all registered taxpayers who need to comply with the updated reporting formats and requirements.

669.summary: Notification No. 62/2019-Central Tax, issued on 26th November 2019, introduces a special procedure for businesses transitioning from the erstwhile State of Jammu and Kashmir to the Union territories of Jammu and Kashmir and Ladakh. This procedure is applicable until 31st December 2019. It defines the tax periods for October and November 2019, with October covering the 1st to 30th and November from the 31st to the 30th. Businesses must pay the applicable tax in their returns under section 39 for invoices issued from 31st October 2019 until the transition date. Additionally, businesses have the option to transfer their Input Tax Credit (ITC) from their old GSTIN to the new one, following specific procedures, including notifying tax officers and calculating ITC based on turnover ratios. Furthermore, any remaining State taxes in the electronic credit ledger for those in Ladakh will be converted to Union territory tax. Lastly, the notification exempts these businesses from certain inter-State supply regulations during this transition period.
key_points: Notification No. 62/2019-Central Tax was issued on 26th November 2019.

The special procedure is effective until 31st December 2019.
Tax periods for October 2019 are defined as 1st to 30th October, and for November 2019 as 31st October to 30th November.
Businesses must pay applicable tax in their returns under section 39 of the GST Act for invoices raised from 31st October 2019 until the transition date.
Input Tax Credit (ITC) can be transferred from the old GSTIN to the new GSTIN with specific procedures outlined.
The balance of State taxes in the electronic credit ledger for businesses in Ladakh will be transferred as Union territory tax.
Clause (i) of section 24 of the GST Act does not apply to inter-State supplies between Jammu and Kashmir and Ladakh during this period.

This amendment is retroactively effective from 30th November 2019. This change primarily affects businesses operating in Jammu and Kashmir, ensuring they meet the updated filing deadline for their GST returns during this period. It is crucial for these businesses to be aware of this new deadline to with the GST regulations.
key_points: This notification is No. 66/2019-Central Tax, issued on 12th December 2019.
It amends notification No. 29/2019-Central Tax dated 28th June 2019.
The amendment substitutes the fourth proviso in the first paragraph of the earlier notification.
Registered persons in Jammu and Kashmir must file GSTR-3B for July to September 2019 by 20th December 2019.
The amendment is effective from 30th November 2019.

674.summary: Notification No. 67/2019-Central Tax, issued on 12th December 2019, brings an important amendment to the earlier notification No. 44/2019-Central Tax from 9th October 2019. Specifically, it changes the filing deadline for the GSTR-3B return for the month of October 2019 for registered persons whose principal place of business is in Jammu and Kashmir. These taxpayers are now required to submit their GSTR-3B electronically by 20th December 2019. This amendment is effective retroactively from 30th November 2019. The change aims to provide clarity and a specific deadline for taxpayers in Jammu and Kashmir, ensuring they are aligned with the filing requirements. This update is crucial for businesses operating in that region to with the GST regulations as per the new timeline.
key_points: The notification is No. 67/2019-Central Tax, issued on 12th December 2019.
It amends notification No. 44/2019-Central Tax dated 9th October 2019.
The amendment substitutes the proviso to the first paragraph of the earlier notification.
Registered persons in Jammu and Kashmir must file GSTR-3B for October 2019 by 20th December 2019.
This amendment is effective from 30th November 2019.

675.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 68/2019-Central Tax, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, specifically rule 48. This amendment introduces new sub-rules regarding the preparation of invoices. Registered persons, as specified by the government, are now required to prepare invoices using FORM GST INV-01 after obtaining an Invoice Reference Number through the Common Goods and Services Tax Electronic Portal. If an invoice is issued in any manner other than specified in the new sub-rule (4), it will not be considered a valid invoice. Additionally, the existing provisions in sub-rules (1) and (2) will not apply to invoices prepared under this new guideline. This change is based on recommendations from the GST Council and aims to standardize the invoicing process for certain registered persons. The amendment follows the previous update made by notification No. 56/2019-Central Tax on November 14, 2019.
key_points: This notification is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Eighth Amendment) Rules, 2019.
It was issued on December 13, 2019, and comes into force on the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
Sub-rule (4) is added to rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
Sub-rule (4) states that certain registered persons must prepare invoices using FORM GST INV-01 after obtaining an Invoice Reference Number.
Sub-rule (5) specifies that invoices not prepared as per sub-rule (4) will not be considered valid invoices.
Sub-rule (6) clarifies that sub-rules (1) and (2) do not apply to invoices prepared under sub-rule (4).
This amendment follows the recommendations of the GST Council.
The previous amendment to the rules was made via notification No. 56/2019-Central Tax on November 14, 2019.

676.summary: Notification No. 69/2019-Central Tax, effective from January 1, 2020, introduces a Common Goods and Services Tax Electronic Portal for invoice preparation. This notification specifies ten websites, ranging from www.einvoice1.gst.gov.in to www.einvoice10.gst.gov.in, which will serve as the designated platforms for this purpose. These websites are managed by the Goods and Services Tax Network, ensuring a centralized approach to electronic invoicing. The notification is issued under the authority of section 146 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and aligns with sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the GST Rules. The introduction of these portals is based on the recommendations made by the GST Council, aiming to streamline the invoicing process for businesses. This change will impact all businesses required to generate GST-compliant invoices, providing them with a structured and efficient method to manage their invoicing electronically.
key_points: The notification is issued under Notification No. 69/2019-Central Tax.
It is effective from January 1, 2020.
The notification designates ten specific websites for the Common Goods and Services Tax Electronic Portal.
The websites listed are www.einvoice1.gst.gov.in to www.einvoice10.gst.gov.in.
The notification is based on section 146 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It also references sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The websites are managed by the Goods and Services Tax Network.
This notification follows the recommendations of the GST Council.

677.summary: Notification No. 70/2019-Central Tax, issued on December 13, 2019, introduces a new requirement for registered persons whose aggregate turnover exceeds 100 crore rupees. These businesses are now mandated to prepare invoices in accordance with sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This change specifically targets larger businesses, ensuring they adhere to standardized invoicing practices when supplying goods or services to other registered persons. The new invoicing requirement is set to take effect from April 1, 2020. This means that from this date onwards, affected businesses must comply with the specified invoicing rules to ensure proper documentation of their transactions. This notification aims to enhance compliance and streamline the invoicing process for high-turnover businesses in India.
key_points: Notification No. 70/2019-Central Tax issued on 13th December 2019.
This notification applies to registered persons with an aggregate turnover exceeding 100 crore rupees.
Affected registered persons must prepare invoices as per sub-rule (4) of rule 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The new invoicing requirement will come into effect from 1st April 2020.

678.summary: Notification No. 71/2019-Central Tax, issued on December 13, 2019, establishes that the provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourth Amendment) Rules, 2019, will come into effect on April 1, 2020. This amendment is based on rule 5 and was previously outlined in notification No. 31/2019-Central Tax, which was published on June 28, 2019. The earlier notification was part of the Gazette of India, specifically in the Extraordinary section. The Ministry of Finance, through the Department of Revenue, has confirmed this change, with Ruchi Bisht serving as the Under Secretary who signed off on the notification. This means that businesses and stakeholders in the GST framework should prepare for the implementation of these rules starting from the specified date in 2020. The amendment aims to streamline GST processes and compliance for taxpayers.
key_points: The notification is No. 71/2019-Central Tax.
It was issued on December 13, 2019.
The effective date for the provisions is April 1, 2020.
This notification is based on rule 5 of the Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourth Amendment) Rules, 2019.
The Fourth Amendment was made via notification No. 31/2019-Central Tax, dated June 28, 2019.
The earlier notification (31/2019) was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i).
The notification was issued by the Ministry of Finance, Department of Revenue.
Ruchi Bisht is the Under Secretary to the Government of India who signed the notification.

679.summary: Notification No. 72/2019-Central Tax introduces a new requirement for businesses with an aggregate turnover exceeding five hundred crore rupees. Starting from April 1, 2020, these businesses must include a Quick Response (QR) code on their B2C invoices issued to unregistered persons. This change aims to enhance the efficiency of transactions and improve tracking. Additionally, if a registered person provides a Dynamic Quick Response (QR) code through a digital display, this will also meet the requirement, as it allows for a cross-reference of payment. This means that businesses need to adapt their invoicing systems to comply with this new regulation, ensuring that all B2C invoices are properly formatted with the necessary QR code. The implementation of this requirement is expected to streamline the invoicing process and facilitate easier payment verification for consumers.
key_points: Introduces a requirement for B2C invoices to include a Quick Response (QR) code.
Applies to registered persons with an aggregate turnover exceeding five hundred crore rupees.
Allows for a Dynamic Quick Response (QR) code to be displayed digitally, which will also be considered compliant.
Notification comes into effect from April 1, 2020.

680.summary: On 23rd December 2019, the Government of India issued Notification No. 73/2019-Central Tax, which amends the earlier Notification No. 44/2019-Central Tax dated 9th October 2019. This amendment introduces a new proviso that requires taxpayers to file their GSTR-3B return for November 2019 electronically by 23rd December 2019. This change is significant for businesses that are required to submit their GST returns, as it sets a specific deadline for the November return. The amendment is effective from 20th December 2019, meaning that businesses should have been aware of this requirement from that date. This notification aims to streamline the filing process and ensure timely compliance with GST regulations. It is crucial for affected businesses to take note of this deadline to avoid any issues with their GST filings.
key_points: The notification is issued on 23rd December 2019.
It amends Notification No. 44/2019-Central Tax dated 9th October 2019.
A new proviso is added after the existing proviso in Notification No. 44/2019.
The new proviso states that GSTR-3B for November 2019 must be filed electronically by 23rd December 2019.
This amendment is effective from 20th December 2019.

681.summary: Notification No. 74/2019-Central Tax, issued on 26th December 2019, introduces an important amendment to the earlier notification No. 4/2018-Central Tax from 23rd January 2018. A new proviso has been added, which waives the late fee for registered persons who did not submit their GSTR-1 forms for the period from July 2017 to November 2019 by the due dates. To benefit from this waiver, these individuals must file their GSTR-1 forms between 19th December 2019 and 10th January 2020. This change is significant as it provides relief to those who may have missed the deadlines during that period, allowing them to avoid late fees if they act within the specified timeframe. The amendment is retroactively effective from 19th December 2019, meaning that the waiver applies from that date onwards. This notification aims to ease the compliance burden on taxpayers who faced challenges in filing their returns during the specified months.
key_points: The notification is No. 74/2019-Central Tax, issued on 26th December 2019.
It amends notification No. 4/2018-Central Tax dated 23rd January 2018.
A new proviso is added after the second proviso in the earlier notification.
The new proviso waives the late fee under section 47 for registered persons.
This waiver applies to those who failed to file FORM GSTR-1 from July 2017 to November 2019.
To qualify for the waiver, GSTR-1 must be filed between 19th December 2019 and 10th January 2020.
The amendment is effective from 19th December 2019.

682.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 75/2019-Central Tax, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This notification introduces several key changes effective from January 1, 2020. Notably, the percentage of eligible input tax credit under rule 36(4) is reduced from 20% to 10%. Additionally, a new rule, 86A, has been added, allowing the Commissioner to restrict the use of input tax credit in the electronic credit ledger if it is suspected to be fraudulently availed or ineligible. This restriction can last for up to one year. Furthermore, rule 138E has been amended to include a new clause that restricts individuals who have not filed their outward supply statements for two consecutive months or quarters. These changes are significant for businesses as they affect how input tax credits can be utilized and the compliance requirements for filing returns. Overall, these amendments aim to tighten the regulations surrounding GST compliance and input tax credit eligibility.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Ninth Amendment) Rules, 2019'.
It comes into effect on 1st January 2020, except as otherwise provided.
In rule 36(4) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, the percentage for eligible input tax credit is reduced from '20 per cent.' to '10 per cent.'
A new rule 86A is inserted regarding conditions for using the electronic credit ledger, effective from 1st January 2020.
Rule 86A allows the Commissioner to restrict the use of input tax credit if it is fraudulently availed or ineligible under certain conditions.
The restriction on the electronic credit ledger can last for up to one year from the date it is imposed.
In rule 138E, effective from 11th January 2020, a new clause (c) is added to restrict persons who have not filed outward supply statements for two months or quarters.
The notification amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, which were last amended by notification No. 68/2019.

683.summary: Notification No. 76/2019-Central Tax, issued on December 26, 2019, introduces an important amendment to the earlier notification No. 46/2019-Central Tax from October 9, 2019. This amendment specifically adds a new proviso for registered taxpayers whose principal place of business is in the states of Assam, Manipur, or Tripura. These taxpayers, provided they have an aggregate turnover exceeding 1.5 crore rupees in the previous or current financial year, are now allowed to submit their GSTR-1 for the month of November 2019 by December 31, 2019. This extension is beneficial for businesses in these states, giving them additional time to comply with their filing obligations. The amendment is retroactively effective from December 11, 2019. This notification follows the previous amendment made by notification No. 64/2019-Central Tax on December 12, 2019, ensuring that businesses in these regions have clarity on their filing deadlines.
key_points: This notification is No. 76/2019-Central Tax, issued on 26th December 2019.
It amends notification No. 46/2019-Central Tax, dated 9th October 2019.
A new proviso is added for registered persons in Assam, Manipur, or Tripura.
The new proviso allows these registered persons with an aggregate turnover over 1.5 crore rupees to file GSTR-1 for November 2019 by 31st December 2019.
The amendment is effective from 11th December 2019.
The principal notification was last amended by notification No. 64/2019-Central Tax on 12th December 2019.

684.summary: Notification No. 77/2019-Central Tax, issued on 26th December 2019, introduces an amendment to the earlier notification No. 44/2019-Central Tax from 9th October 2019. Specifically, it adds a new proviso that requires registered persons in Assam, Manipur, Meghalaya, and Tripura to file their GSTR-3B return for November 2019 electronically by 31st December 2019. This change is significant for businesses operating in these northeastern states, as it sets a clear deadline for compliance. The amendment is retroactively effective from 23rd December 2019, meaning that businesses should be aware of this requirement as it applies to the tax period of November 2019. This notification aims to streamline the filing process for these specific regions, ensuring that businesses adhere to the GST regulations in a timely manner.
key_points: The notification is No. 77/2019-Central Tax, issued on 26th December 2019.
It amends notification No. 44/2019-Central Tax, dated 9th October 2019.
A new proviso is added after the second proviso in the first paragraph of the earlier notification.
The new proviso states that GSTR-3B for November 2019 must be filed by certain states by 31st December 2019.
The states affected are Assam, Manipur, Meghalaya, and Tripura.
This amendment is effective from 23rd December 2019.

685.summary: Notification No. 78/2019-Central Tax, issued on 26th December 2019, introduces an amendment to the earlier notification No. 26/2019-Central Tax from 28th June 2019. The amendment adds a new proviso that requires registered persons who are obligated to deduct tax at source under section 51 to file their FORM GSTR-7 for the month of November 2019. This filing must be completed electronically by 25th December 2019. Importantly, this requirement specifically targets those whose principal place of business is located in the states of Assam, Manipur, or Tripura. The amendment is retroactively effective from 10th December 2019, meaning it applies to any relevant transactions or obligations from that date. This change is crucial for businesses in these states to with the updated filing requirements for the specified tax period.
key_points: This notification is No. 78/2019-Central Tax, issued on 26th December 2019.
It amends notification No. 26/2019-Central Tax, originally published on 28th June 2019.
A new proviso is added after the third proviso in the first paragraph of the original notification.
The new proviso states that registered persons required to deduct tax at source under section 51 must file FORM GSTR-7 for November 2019.
The deadline for filing FORM GSTR-7 is set for 25th December 2019.
This applies specifically to registered persons whose principal place of business is in Assam, Manipur, or Tripura.
The amendment is effective from 10th December 2019.

686.summary: On 29th January 2019, the Government of India issued Notification No. 01/2019-Central Tax (Rate), which rescinds the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Central Tax (Rate) that was published on 28th June 2017. This change will take effect from 1st February 2019. The earlier notification had been amended previously by Notification No. 22/2018-Central Tax (Rate) on 6th August 2018. The rescission means that the provisions outlined in Notification No. 8/2017 will no longer be in effect, but any actions taken under that notification before the rescission will still be valid. This change primarily affects businesses and entities that were operating under the guidelines of the rescinded notification, as they will need to adjust to the new regulations or guidelines that may follow this rescission. It is important for affected parties to review their compliance and operational strategies in light of this update.
key_points: Notification No. 01/2019-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 29th January 2019.
This notification rescinds the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The rescission is effective from 1st February 2019.
The rescinded notification (No. 8/2017) was last amended by Notification No. 22/2018-Central Tax (Rate) dated 6th August 2018.
The rescission applies except for actions taken before the rescission date.

687.summary: On 29th January 2019, the Government of India issued Notification No. 01/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate), which rescinds the earlier Notification No. 32/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) that was published on 13th October 2017. This change will take effect from 1st February 2019. The earlier notification had been amended previously by Notification No. 23/2018-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 6th August 2018. The rescission means that the provisions and rules outlined in Notification No. 32/2017 will no longer be applicable, but any actions taken under that notification before the rescission will remain valid. This change primarily affects businesses and entities that were following the guidelines set out in the rescinded notification, as they will need to adjust to the new regulations or guidelines that may be introduced following this rescission. It is important for affected parties to review the implications of this change on their operations and compliance requirements.
key_points: Notification No. 01/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) was issued on 29th January 2019.
This notification rescinds Notification No. 32/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 13th October 2017.
The rescission of Notification No. 32/2017 is effective from 1st February 2019.
The rescinded notification was last amended by Notification No. 23/2018-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 6th August 2018.
The rescission applies except for actions taken before the date of rescission.

688.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 2/2019-Central Tax (Rate) on 7th March 2019, which introduces a 3% central tax rate on the first supplies of goods or services made by registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to fifty lakh rupees in the preceding financial year. This rate is applicable from 1st April 2019. To qualify, businesses must not be eligible for the composition scheme, must not engage in inter-State supplies, and must not be classified as casual or non-resident taxable persons. Additionally, these registered persons are required to issue a bill of supply instead of a tax invoice and are prohibited from collecting tax from their customers. The notification also specifies certain goods, including ice cream, pan masala, and tobacco, that fall under this tax rate. Importantly, when calculating aggregate turnover for eligibility, the value of exempt services related to loans or advances will not be considered. This change is significant for small businesses looking to benefit from a lower tax rate while complying with the new regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 2/2019-Central Tax (Rate) issued on 7th March 2019.
Central tax on first supplies of goods or services up to an aggregate turnover of fifty lakh rupees is set at 3%.
This applies to registered persons whose turnover in the preceding financial year was fifty lakh rupees or below.
Conditions include not being eligible for composition scheme, not making inter-State supplies, and not being a casual or non-resident taxable person.
Registered persons must issue a bill of supply instead of a tax invoice and cannot collect tax from recipients.
The notification comes into effect on 1st April 2019.
Certain goods like ice cream, pan masala, and tobacco are specified in the annexure.
Exempt services related to loans or advances are not included in the aggregate turnover calculation.

689.summary: On 4th February 2019, the Government of India issued Notification No. 02/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate), which amends the previous Notification No. 9/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. The key change in this new notification is the omission of serial number 10D and its related entries from the Table in the earlier notification. This means that whatever provisions or tax rates were associated with serial number 10D are no longer applicable. This amendment is part of the government's ongoing efforts to streamline tax regulations and is based on recommendations from the GST Council. The principal notification No. 9/2017 had previously been amended by Notification No. 29/2018 on 31st December 2018, indicating that the government is actively revising tax rates and provisions as needed. Businesses and stakeholders should take note of this change as it may impact their tax calculations and compliance requirements.
key_points: Notification No. 02/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) was issued on 4th February 2019.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 9/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
Specifically, it omits serial number 10D and the related entries from the Table in the earlier notification.
The principal notification No. 9/2017 was last amended by Notification No. 29/2018 on 31st December 2018.

690.summary: On 25th April 2019, a corrigendum was issued to amend Notification No. 3/2019-Central Tax (Rate) from 29th March 2019. This corrigendum includes specific corrections in the published table. Firstly, on page 34, line 42, the term 'tax' has been corrected to 'central tax', clarifying the type of tax being referred to. Additionally, in line 45 of the same table, the figure 'eighteen' has been changed to 'nine', which likely affects the applicable tax rate. Furthermore, on page 51, line 26, the number '18' has been revised to '18 (9 + 9)', providing a clearer breakdown of the tax calculation. These changes are important for businesses to ensure they are referencing the correct tax rates and terms in their compliance and reporting. The amendments aim to enhance clarity in the tax structure as outlined in the original notification.
key_points: The corrigendum was issued on 25th April 2019.
It amends Notification No. 3/2019-Central Tax (Rate) dated 29th March 2019.
In the table on page 34, line 42, 'tax' is corrected to 'central tax'.
In the same table, line 45, 'eighteen' is corrected to 'nine'.
On page 51, line 26, '18' is corrected to '18 (9 + 9)'.

691.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 03/2019-Central Tax (Rate), originally published on 29th March 2019. This corrigendum, dated 30th August 2019, makes specific corrections regarding the forms referenced in the notification. Specifically, it changes the form name from 'FORM GST ITC-03' to 'FORM GST DRC-03' in two instances: once at page 42, line 31, and again at page 47, line 10. This change is important for businesses and taxpayers who need to ensure they are using the correct form for their GST compliance. The correction clarifies the documentation required for certain tax processes, ensuring that the right forms are utilized moving forward. This adjustment helps avoid confusion and ensures that all stakeholders are aligned with the correct procedural requirements under GST.
key_points: This is a corrigendum to Notification No. 03/2019-Central Tax (Rate) dated 29th March 2019.
The corrigendum was issued on 30th August 2019.
It corrects the form name from 'FORM GST ITC-03' to 'FORM GST DRC-03' at page 42, line 31.
It also corrects the form name from 'FORM GST ITC-03' to 'FORM GST DRC-03' at page 47, line 10.

692.summary: The Government of India issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 03/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 30th August 2019, which corrects specific references in the original notification dated 29th March 2019. The changes involve replacing 'FORM GST ITC-03' with 'FORM GST DRC-03' in two instances: once at page 108, line 29, and again at page 112, line 46. This amendment clarifies the correct form to be used for certain compliance requirements under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax (IGST) framework. Businesses and taxpayers who were previously referencing FORM GST ITC-03 should now use FORM GST DRC-03 as specified in the corrigendum. This change is important for ensuring that the correct documentation is utilized in compliance with GST regulations. It is crucial for affected parties to update their records and practices accordingly to align with this correction.
key_points: This is a corrigendum to Notification No. 03/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 29th March 2019.
The corrigendum was issued on 30th August 2019.
In the corrigendum, 'FORM GST ITC-03' is replaced with 'FORM GST DRC-03' at page 108, line 29.
Additionally, 'FORM GST ITC-03' is also replaced with 'FORM GST DRC-03' at page 112, line 46.

693.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 03/2019-Central Tax (Rate) on 29th March 2019, which amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Key changes include the addition of references to sub-sections (3), (4), and (148) of the CGST Act in the opening paragraph. The tax rate for construction of affordable residential apartments has been set at 0.75% for projects commencing on or after 1st April 2019. This notification also outlines specific conditions regarding input tax credit and payment methods for construction services. Registered persons are required to maintain detailed accounts of inward supplies and report any shortfall in input tax credits. Additionally, the deadline for opting to pay central tax at specified rates for ongoing projects is established as 10th May 2019. Some items in the Table have been removed, while new provisions for composite supply of works contracts have been introduced, impacting how construction services are taxed moving forward.
key_points: Notification No. 03/2019-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 29th March 2019.
This notification amends Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendments include changes to the opening paragraph, specifically adding references to sub-sections (3), (4), and (148) of the CGST Act.
In the Table, item (i) under serial number 3 is amended to specify a central tax rate of 0.75% for construction of affordable residential apartments starting on or after 1st April 2019.
The notification introduces conditions regarding input tax credit and payment methods for construction services.
It specifies that registered persons must maintain project-wise accounts for inward supplies and report any shortfall in input tax credits.
The deadline for exercising the option to pay central tax at specified rates for ongoing projects is set for 10th May 2019.
Certain items in the Table have been omitted, and new items related to composite supply of works contracts have been added.

694.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 03/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 29th March 2019, which amends the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This amendment introduces subsections (3) and (4) into the opening paragraph and expands the definition of applicable clauses to include clause (xxv). Significant changes in the Table now include new provisions for the construction of affordable residential apartments and commercial apartments, effective from 1st April 2019. Promoters are required to pay integrated tax in cash by debiting their electronic cash ledger for these projects. Additionally, the notification outlines specific conditions under which landowner-promoters can claim input tax credit. Importantly, promoters must exercise their option to pay integrated tax at the specified rates by 10th May 2019, ensuring compliance with the new provisions. This notification primarily affects real estate promoters and developers involved in residential and commercial projects.
key_points: Notification No. 03/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) was issued on 29th March 2019.
This notification amends Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
It adds subsections (3) and (4) to the opening paragraph of the earlier notification.
The definition of clauses in the earlier notification is expanded to include clause (xxv).
Changes in the Table include new entries for construction of affordable residential apartments and commercial apartments starting from 1st April 2019.
Promoters must pay integrated tax in cash by debiting the electronic cash ledger for certain projects.
The notification specifies conditions under which input tax credit can be claimed by landowner-promoters.
A deadline of 10th May 2019 is set for promoters to exercise their option to pay integrated tax at specified rates.

695.summary: Notification No. 04/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 29th March 2019, amends the earlier Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This new notification introduces entries 41A and 41B, which provide tax exemptions for services related to the transfer of development rights and Floor Space Index (FSI) for residential apartments, effective from 1st April 2019. Specifically, entry 41A addresses the tax implications for un-booked apartments, while entry 41B does the same for upfront amounts related to long-term leases. Additionally, new paragraphs 1A and 1B establish how the value of these services will be determined based on market rates. The notification also clarifies definitions for key terms such as 'apartment', 'affordable residential apartment', 'promoter', and 'project'. Overall, this amendment is significant for promoters and developers in the real estate sector, as it outlines the tax responsibilities and exemptions related to residential projects.
key_points: Notification No. 04/2019-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 29th March 2019.
This notification amends Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
It introduces new entries 41A and 41B in the Table for services related to development rights and long-term leases for residential apartments effective from 1st April 2019.
Entry 41A provides a tax exemption for transfer of development rights or Floor Space Index (FSI) for residential apartments, with conditions for tax liability on un-booked apartments.
Entry 41B offers a similar exemption for upfront amounts related to long-term leases for residential apartments, also with conditions for tax liability on un-booked apartments.
New paragraphs 1A and 1B define the value of services related to development rights and un-booked apartments based on market rates.
Definitions for terms like 'apartment', 'affordable residential apartment', 'promoter', and 'project' are clarified in the notification.
The notification comes into effect on 1st April 2019.

696.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 04/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 29th March 2019, amending the earlier Notification No. 9/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This amendment introduces new provisions for the taxation of services related to the transfer of development rights (TDR) and Floor Space Index (FSI) for residential apartment construction, effective from 1st April 2019. Specifically, two new entries (43A and 43B) have been added, detailing the tax obligations for promoters on un-booked residential apartments. The tax rates are capped at 1% for affordable apartments and 5% for others. Additionally, the notification provides definitions for key terms such as 'apartment' and 'promoter', aligning them with the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016. It also outlines how to calculate GST exemptions for un-booked apartments based on their carpet area. This change primarily affects real estate promoters involved in residential projects, ensuring clarity on their tax liabilities and obligations.
key_points: Notification No. 04/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) was issued on 29th March 2019.
This notification amends Notification No. 9/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
New entries for service by way of transfer of development rights (TDR) and Floor Space Index (FSI) have been added to the Table after serial number 43.
The new entries (43A and 43B) apply to services provided on or after 1st April 2019 for construction of residential apartments.
Promoters are liable to pay tax on un-booked residential apartments at rates not exceeding 1% for affordable apartments and 5% for others.
New definitions for terms like 'apartment', 'affordable residential apartment', and 'promoter' have been included, referencing the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016.
The notification clarifies the calculation of GST exemption for un-booked apartments based on the carpet area.
The notification comes into effect from 1st April 2019.

697.summary: Notification No. 05/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 29th March 2019, introduces significant amendments to the earlier Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax (Rate). It adds new entries in the tax rate table, specifically serial numbers 5B and 5C. Serial number 5B addresses services related to the transfer of development rights or Floor Space Index (FSI) for construction projects by promoters. Meanwhile, serial number 5C covers long-term leases of land (30 years or more) for construction, which can involve upfront payments or periodic rents. Additionally, the notification clarifies definitions for key terms such as 'apartment', 'promoter', 'project', 'Real Estate Project (REP)', 'Residential Real Estate Project (RREP)', and 'floor space index (FSI)'. These changes are effective from 1st April 2019, impacting promoters and entities involved in real estate development. This amendment aims to provide clarity and structure to the taxation of real estate transactions under GST.
key_points: Inserts new serial numbers 5B and 5C in the Table of Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax (Rate).
5B pertains to services supplied by any person through transfer of development rights or Floor Space Index (FSI) for construction by a promoter.
5C relates to long-term lease of land (30 years or more) for construction by a promoter, involving upfront amounts or periodic rent.
Defines terms such as 'apartment', 'promoter', 'project', 'Real Estate Project (REP)', 'Residential Real Estate Project (RREP)', and 'floor space index (FSI)'.
The notification comes into effect from 1st April 2019.
The principal notification No. 13/2017 was last amended by notification No. 29/2018 on 31st December 2018.

698.summary: Notification No. 05/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 29th March 2019, introduces amendments to the earlier Notification No. 10/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. Specifically, it adds new entries 6B and 6C to the existing table. Entry 6B addresses services related to the transfer of development rights or Floor Space Index (FSI) for construction projects by a promoter, while entry 6C pertains to long-term leases of land (30 years or more) for similar projects. Additionally, the notification provides definitions for several key terms, including 'apartment', 'promoter', 'project', 'Real Estate Project (REP)', 'Residential Real Estate Project (RREP)', and 'floor space index (FSI)'. These changes aim to clarify the scope of services and terms relevant to real estate projects. The amendments will take effect from 1st April 2019, impacting promoters and entities involved in real estate development and leasing.
key_points: This notification is issued on 29th March 2019.
It amends Notification No. 10/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
New entries 6B and 6C are added to the table in the notification.
Entry 6B covers services related to transfer of development rights or Floor Space Index (FSI) for construction projects by a promoter.
Entry 6C pertains to long-term lease of land (30 years or more) for construction projects by a promoter.

Definitions for terms like 'apartment', 'promoter', 'project', 'Real Estate Project (REP)', 'Residential Real Estate Project (RREP)', and 'floor space index (FSI)' are added to the explanation section.
The notification comes into effect from 1st April 2019.

699.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 06/2019-Central Tax (Rate) on 29th March 2019, which comes into effect from 1st April 2019. This notification specifically targets promoters who receive development rights or Floor Space Index (FSI) for construction projects. It establishes that the tax liability for these promoters will arise either upon the issuance of a completion certificate or when the project is first occupied, whichever occurs first. The consideration for these rights can be in various forms, including construction services or monetary payments. Additionally, it includes provisions for long-term lease payments for land used in residential projects. The notification also defines key terms such as 'apartment', 'promoter', and 'project' in accordance with the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016. Furthermore, it specifies that the tax on the services mentioned must be paid under a reverse charge mechanism as outlined in Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax (Rate). This change primarily affects real estate promoters involved in the construction of residential and commercial projects.
key_points: Notification No. 06/2019-Central Tax (Rate) issued on 29th March 2019.
Applicable from 1st April 2019.
Promoters receiving development rights or Floor Space Index (FSI) for construction projects are affected.
Tax liability arises on the issuance of the completion certificate or first occupation, whichever is earlier.
Consideration can be in the form of construction services or monetary payments.
Long-term lease payments for land also fall under this notification.
Terms like 'apartment', 'promoter', and 'project' are defined as per the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016.
Tax on services is to be paid under reverse charge as per Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax (Rate).

700.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 06/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 29th March 2019, which comes into effect on 1st April 2019. This notification specifically targets promoters who receive development rights or Floor Space Index (FSI) for construction projects after this date. It establishes that these promoters will be liable to pay integrated tax on the consideration they provide, which can include construction services or monetary payments. Additionally, promoters who enter into long-term leases for land intended for residential apartments will also be subject to this tax. The tax liability will arise either upon the issuance of a completion certificate or when the project is first occupied, whichever occurs first. Furthermore, the notification stipulates that the tax on these services must be paid under a reverse charge mechanism, as outlined in an earlier notification. Definitions for key terms such as 'apartment', 'promoter', and 'project' are aligned with the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016, ensuring clarity on the scope of this notification.
key_points: Notification No. 06/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) issued on 29th March 2019.
Effective date of the notification is 1st April 2019.
Promoters receiving development rights or Floor Space Index (FSI) after 1st April 2019 are subject to integrated tax.
Tax liability arises on the issuance of the completion certificate or first occupation of the project, whichever is earlier.
Promoters receiving long-term lease of land for residential apartments after 1st April 2019 must also pay integrated tax.
Tax on services related to development rights or FSI is to be paid under reverse charge basis as per Notification No. 10/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate).
Definitions for 'apartment', 'promoter', 'project', 'Real Estate Project', and 'Residential Real Estate Project' are provided as per the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016.
The notification clarifies that the tax applies to both monetary and non-monetary considerations for development rights or FSI.

701.summary: Notification No. 07/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 29th March 2019, introduces changes effective from 1st April 2019, requiring registered persons to pay tax on a reverse charge basis for certain supplies received from unregistered suppliers. This includes goods and services that make up the shortfall from the minimum value that promoters must purchase for construction projects, as outlined in the earlier Notification No. 11/2017. Specifically, cement classified under chapter heading 2523 and capital goods supplied to promoters for construction are now subject to this reverse charge mechanism. The notification also clarifies the definitions of key terms such as 'promoter', 'project', 'Real Estate Project', and 'Residential Real Estate Project', referencing the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016. This amendment aims to streamline tax obligations for promoters involved in real estate projects, ensuring compliance with the updated GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 07/2019-Central Tax (Rate) issued on 29th March 2019.
Effective date of the notification is 1st April 2019.
Registered persons specified in column (3) of the table must pay tax on reverse charge basis for supplies from unregistered suppliers.
The supplies include goods and services constituting shortfall from minimum value required by promoters for construction projects.
Cement under chapter heading 2523 is included in the supplies subject to reverse charge.
Capital goods supplied to promoters for construction projects are also included.
Definitions for 'promoter', 'project', 'Real Estate Project', and 'Residential Real Estate Project' are provided based on the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016.
The notification amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

702.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 07/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 29th March 2019, which will come into effect from 1st April 2019. This notification mandates that registered persons must pay tax on a reverse charge basis for certain goods and services received from unregistered suppliers. Specifically, this includes goods and services that make up the shortfall for promoters in construction projects, cement classified under chapter heading 2523, and capital goods supplied to promoters. The notification aligns the definition of 'promoter' with the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016, ensuring clarity in who is affected. Additionally, it references the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017 for specific items. The terms 'project' and 'floor space index (FSI)' are also defined to provide further context. This change primarily impacts promoters involved in real estate projects, requiring them to comply with the new tax obligations.
key_points: Notification No. 07/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) issued on 29th March 2019.
Effective date of the notification is 1st April 2019.
Registered persons must pay tax on reverse charge basis for specified goods and services received from unregistered suppliers.
Categories affected include goods and services constituting shortfall for promoters in construction projects, cement under chapter heading 2523, and capital goods supplied to promoters.
The definition of 'promoter' aligns with the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016.
The notification references earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017 for specific items.
The term 'project' refers to Real Estate Projects (REP) and Residential Real Estate Projects (RREP).
The notification clarifies the definition of 'floor space index (FSI)'.

703.summary: Notification No. 08/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 29th March 2019, introduces a new entry, 452Q, in Schedule III of the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate). This entry specifically addresses the supply of goods, excluding capital goods and cement, categorized under chapter heading 2523, made by unregistered persons to promoters for construction projects. The tax liability for this supply falls on the promoter, who is defined according to the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016. The notification clarifies that the term 'project' encompasses both Real Estate Projects (REP) and Residential Real Estate Projects (RREP). Importantly, this amendment will take effect from 1st April 2019, and it applies to all goods that meet the outlined conditions, even if they fall under different classifications in the tax schedule. This change primarily affects unregistered suppliers and promoters involved in real estate construction projects.
key_points: Inserts a new entry 452Q in Schedule III of Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate).
Entry 452Q pertains to the supply of goods other than capital goods and cement under chapter heading 2523.
This supply is made by an unregistered person to a promoter for construction projects.
The tax on this supply is payable by the promoter as the recipient of goods.
The notification comes into effect from 1st April 2019.
The term 'promoter' is defined as per the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016.
The definition of 'project' includes Real Estate Projects (REP) and Residential Real Estate Projects (RREP).
This entry applies to all goods meeting the specified conditions, regardless of other classifications.

704.summary: Notification No. 08/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 29th March 2019, introduces a new entry, 452Q, in Schedule III of the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate). This entry allows for the supply of goods, excluding capital goods and cement, categorized under chapter heading 2523, by an unregistered person to a promoter for construction projects. The tax on these supplies will be the responsibility of the promoter as the recipient. This change is effective from 1st April 2019. The notification also clarifies the definitions of 'promoter', 'Real Estate Project', and 'Residential Real Estate Project' in accordance with the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016. Importantly, this new entry applies to all relevant goods, even if they fall under different tariff classifications. This amendment is significant for unregistered suppliers and promoters involved in real estate projects, as it outlines their tax obligations and clarifies the scope of applicable goods.
key_points: Inserts a new entry 452Q in Schedule III of Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate).
Entry 452Q pertains to the supply of goods other than capital goods and cement under chapter heading 2523.
This supply is made by an unregistered person to a promoter for construction projects.
The tax on this supply is payable by the promoter as the recipient of goods.
The notification comes into effect from 1st April 2019.
The definition of 'promoter' aligns with the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016.
The terms 'Real Estate Project' and 'Residential Real Estate Project' are also defined as per the same Act.
This entry applies to all goods meeting the specified conditions, regardless of other tariff classifications.

705.summary: Notification No. 09/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 29th March 2019, introduces important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 02/2019-Central Tax (Rate) dated 7th March 2019. A new clause (8) has been added, which requires registered persons who have claimed input tax credit to pay an amount equivalent to this credit when they choose to pay tax under this notification. This payment must be made from their electronic credit or cash ledger. Importantly, any remaining input tax credit after this payment will lapse. Additionally, the notification clarifies that the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, which apply to taxpayers under section 10, will also apply to those opting for this notification, as stated in the new clause (iii) added to the Explanation. These changes will take effect from 1st April 2019, impacting registered persons who utilize input tax credit and choose to pay tax under the specified provisions.
key_points: This notification is issued on 29th March 2019.
It amends Notification No. 02/2019-Central Tax (Rate) dated 7th March 2019.

A new clause (8) is added to the Table, stating that registered persons who have availed input tax credit must pay an amount equivalent to the input tax credit on stock and capital goods when opting to pay tax under this notification.
The payment must be made via debit in the electronic credit ledger or electronic cash ledger.
The balance of input tax credit remaining after this payment will lapse.

An additional clause (iii) is added to the Explanation in paragraph 3, stating that the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, applicable to taxpayers under section 10, will also apply to those under this notification.
This notification comes into effect on 1st April 2019.

706.summary: Notification No. 9/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 10th May 2019, brings important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. Specifically, it alters the entries in the Table against serial number 3, changing the figures from '10th' to '20th' in column (5) for items (ie) and (if). This means that any references to the 10th have now been updated to the 20th, which could affect compliance dates or reporting requirements for businesses. Additionally, in Annexure IV of the same notification, the figures '10th' are also replaced with '20th' at both instances. These changes are significant for businesses that rely on the specified dates for their tax calculations and reporting, ensuring they align with the updated provisions. This amendment reflects the government's ongoing efforts to streamline GST regulations and ensure clarity in tax obligations.

This notification amends Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

707.summary: Notification No. 10/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 10th May 2019, brings specific amendments to Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. The key change involves replacing the date '10th' with '20th' in two places: first, in the Table against serial number 3 for items (ie) and (if), and second, in Annexure IV. This adjustment affects the entries in column (5) of the specified items, thereby altering the deadlines or requirements associated with them. The amendments are made under various sections of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and are intended to serve the public interest as recommended by the GST Council. This notification follows the previous amendment made by Notification No. 3/2019-Central Tax (Rate) on 29th March 2019, ensuring that businesses are aware of the updated timelines for compliance. Overall, this change is significant for businesses that need to adhere to the revised dates for their tax obligations.

708.summary: Notification No. 10/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 29th June 2019, introduces a new provision effective from 1st July 2019. It allows retail outlets located in the departure areas of international airports to claim refunds on the integrated tax paid for goods sold to outgoing international tourists. This is significant for businesses operating in these areas, as they can now recover the tax they paid on their purchases. The term 'outgoing international tourist' refers to individuals who are not residents of India and are visiting for legitimate non-immigrant purposes for a stay of no more than six months. To qualify for these refunds, the retail outlets must adhere to the conditions outlined in rule 95A of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This change aims to enhance the shopping experience for international travelers and support businesses in the airport retail sector.
key_points: Notification No. 10/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) issued on 29th June 2019.
Effective date of the notification is 1st July 2019.
Retail outlets in the departure area of international airports can claim refunds.
Refunds apply to integrated tax paid on goods supplied to outgoing international tourists.
Outgoing international tourists are defined as non-residents staying in India for up to six months.
Claims are subject to conditions specified in rule 95A of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

709.summary: Notification No. 11/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 29th June 2019, introduces a new provision effective from 1st July 2019. It allows retail outlets located in the departure areas of international airports to claim refunds on the central tax paid for goods supplied to outgoing international tourists. This is specifically aimed at retail outlets beyond immigration counters, facilitating tax-free shopping for tourists. The definition of 'outgoing international tourist' includes individuals who are not residents of India and are visiting for legitimate non-immigrant purposes for a stay of no more than six months. To claim these refunds, the retail outlets must adhere to the conditions specified in rule 95A of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This change is significant for businesses operating in international airport retail, as it enhances their ability to attract international customers by offering tax benefits.

710.summary: Notification No. 11/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) introduces an exemption from integrated tax for goods supplied by retail outlets located in the departure area of international airports. This exemption applies specifically to outgoing international tourists, who are defined as individuals not normally residing in India and staying for a maximum of six months for legitimate non-immigrant purposes. The exemption is granted under section 5 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, highlighting the government's intent to promote tourism and enhance the shopping experience for international travelers. This change is effective from July 1, 2019, allowing tourists to purchase goods without the burden of integrated tax, thereby potentially increasing sales for retailers in these airport locations. This notification is a significant step in making India a more attractive destination for international visitors by easing their shopping experience at airports.

This notification comes into effect from July 1, 2019.

711.summary: The Notification No. 12/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 31st July 2019, introduces several key changes to the GST rates applicable to electric vehicles and their charging infrastructure. Effective from 1st August 2019, a new entry 234B has been added to Schedule I, imposing a 2.5% tax rate on 'Charger or charging station for Electrically operated vehicles'. Additionally, entry 242A has been included for 'Electrically operated vehicles', which encompasses two and three-wheeled electric vehicles, also at a 2.5% tax rate. In contrast, entry 206 under Schedule II, which previously had a 6% tax rate, has been completely omitted. Furthermore, in Schedule III, the entry for inductors has been modified to clarify that it does not include chargers or charging stations for electric vehicles. These amendments aim to streamline the tax structure for electric mobility and its supporting infrastructure, reflecting the government's push towards promoting electric vehicles in India.
key_points: Inserts new entry 234B for 'Charger or charging station for Electrically operated vehicles' under Schedule I at 2.5% tax rate.
Inserts new entry 242A for 'Electrically operated vehicles, including two and three wheeled electric vehicles' under Schedule I at 2.5% tax rate.
Omitted entry 206 under Schedule II which had a 6% tax rate.
In Schedule III at 9%, modifies entry 375 to exclude 'charger or charging station for Electrically operated vehicles' from the definition of inductors.
This notification comes into force on 1st August 2019.
This notification amends the principal notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

712.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 12/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 31st July 2019, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This new notification introduces several important changes effective from 1st August 2019. Firstly, it adds new entries in Schedule I, specifically serial number 234B for 'Charger or charging station for Electrically operated vehicles' and serial number 242A for 'Electrically operated vehicles', both of which will be taxed at a rate of 5%. Additionally, entry 206 in Schedule II, which was previously taxed at 12%, has been removed entirely. Furthermore, in Schedule III, the existing entry 375 has been modified to clarify that chargers or charging stations for electrically operated vehicles are excluded from the 18% tax rate. These amendments are significant for businesses involved in the electric vehicle sector, as they clarify the tax implications for related products and services.

Amends Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

Inserts new entry 242A for 'Electrically operated vehicles, including two and three wheeled electric vehicles' at 5% tax rate.
Omitted entry 206 from Schedule II, which had a 12% tax rate.
In Schedule III, modifies entry 375 to exclude 'charger or charging station for Electrically operated vehicles' from the 18% tax rate.
The changes take effect from 1st August 2019.

713.summary: Notification No. 13/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 31st July 2019, introduces an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. Specifically, it adds a new clause (aa) under serial number 22 in the Table, which allows for a reduced tax rate on electrically operated vehicles designed to carry more than twelve passengers when supplied to local authorities. This means that local authorities can benefit from a lower tax rate when acquiring such vehicles, promoting the use of electric transportation. The definition of an 'electrically operated vehicle' is clarified to include those that operate solely on electrical energy from external sources or batteries. This amendment is effective from 1st August 2019, and it follows the previous amendment made by Notification No. 4/2019-Central Tax (Rate) on 29th March 2019. This change is significant for local authorities looking to enhance their public transport options with environmentally friendly vehicles.
key_points: This notification is issued as Notification No. 13/2019-Central Tax (Rate) on 31st July 2019.
It amends Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

The new clause (aa) specifies that an electrically operated vehicle meant to carry more than twelve passengers is taxable at a reduced rate when supplied to a local authority.
An 'electrically operated vehicle' is defined as one that runs solely on electrical energy from an external source or batteries.
This amendment comes into effect from 1st August 2019.
The principal notification was last amended by Notification No. 4/2019-Central Tax (Rate) dated 29th March 2019.

714.summary: Notification No. 13/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 31st July 2019, introduces an important amendment to the earlier notification No. 9/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. Specifically, it adds a new clause (aa) under serial number 23 in the table of the earlier notification. This new clause states that an electrically operated vehicle designed to carry more than twelve passengers will be subject to integrated tax. The definition of an 'electrically operated vehicle' is also clarified, indicating that it must run solely on electrical energy from an external source or from batteries. This change is significant for local authorities and businesses involved in the transportation sector, as it directly impacts the tax treatment of such vehicles. The amendment is effective from 1st August 2019, meaning that any transactions involving these vehicles after this date will need to comply with the new tax provisions.

715.summary: Notification No. 14/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 30th September 2019, brings several amendments to the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. Notably, S. No. 33A and its related entries have been removed from Schedule I, which lists goods taxed at 2.5%. Additionally, a new item, Marine Fuel (0.5% FO), has been introduced under S. No. 164 in the same schedule. The entry for S. No. 224 has been revised to specify '63 [other than 6305 32 00, 6305 33 00, 6309]'. Furthermore, a new entry for 'Wet grinder consisting of stone as grinder' has been added as S. No. 234C, while multiple entries from S. Nos. 235 to 242 have been omitted. In Schedule II, which covers goods taxed at 6%, new entries related to rail locomotives and their parts have been introduced, expanding the list of taxable items. These changes will affect businesses dealing with these specific goods and services, requiring them to adjust their tax calculations accordingly. The amendments take effect from 1st October 2019.

717.summary: Notification No. 15/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 30th September 2019, introduces amendments to the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. 718.summary: Notification No. 15/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 30th September 2019, introduces amendments to the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Specifically, it adds a new entry for 'Tamarind dried' under serial number 57A, which means that this product will now be recognized under the integrated tax framework. Additionally, it includes another new entry for 'Plates and cups made up of all kinds of leaves/flowers/bark' under serial number 114C. These changes are significant as they expand the list of goods subject to integrated tax, which may affect manufacturers and sellers of these items. The amendments will take effect from 1st October 2019, so businesses dealing with these products should be aware of the new classifications and ensure they comply with the updated tax regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 15/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate) was issued on 30th September 2019.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
It inserts a new entry for 'Tamarind dried' under serial number 57A in the Schedule.
It also adds a new entry for 'Plates and cups made up of all kinds of leaves/flowers/bark' under serial number 114C.
The changes will come into effect on 1st October 2019.

719.summary: Notification No. 16/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 30th September 2019, introduces amendments to the earlier Notification No. 3/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. A significant addition is made to the table against S. No. 1, which now includes petroleum operations or coal bed methane operations conducted under specific contracts as part of the Hydrocarbon Exploration Licensing Policy (HELP) or Open Acreage Licensing Policy (OALP). Additionally, a new proviso has been added to Condition No. 1, clause (e) of the ANNEXURE. This allows recipients of certain non-serviceable goods, which have been mutilated before disposal, to opt to pay tax at a reduced rate of 9%. However, this is contingent upon the recipient obtaining a certificate from the Directorate General of Hydro Carbons and presenting it to the appropriate tax authority. These changes are effective from 1st October 2019, impacting businesses involved in petroleum and coal bed methane operations, as well as those dealing with non-serviceable goods.

This notification amends Notification No. 3/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

The recipient must provide a certificate from the Directorate General of Hydro Carbons to the relevant tax authority.
This notification comes into effect on 1st October 2019.

720.summary: Notification No. 16/2019-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 30th September 2019, introduces important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 3/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Specifically, it adds a new item (6) in the TABLE against S. No. 1, which pertains to petroleum operations or coal bed methane operations conducted under the Hydrocarbon Exploration Licensing Policy (HELP) or Open Acreage Licensing Policy (OALP). Additionally, a new proviso has been included in Condition No. 1 of the ANNEXURE. This allows recipients of non-serviceable goods, which have been mutilated before disposal, to opt for an 18% tax rate on the transaction value. However, this option is contingent upon the recipient obtaining a certificate from the Directorate General of Hydro Carbons, confirming the goods' non-serviceable status. These changes will take effect from 1st October 2019, impacting businesses involved in the specified petroleum operations and those dealing with non-serviceable goods.

721.summary: Notification No. 17/2019-Central Tax (Rate) amends the earlier Notification No. 26/2018-Central Tax (Rate) issued on 31st December 2018. The key changes include the substitution of the term 'gold' with 'gold, silver or platinum' throughout the document, which broadens the scope of the notification to include these precious metals. Additionally, the reference from 'heading 7108' has been updated to 'Chapter 71', aligning it with the broader classification of precious metals. Furthermore, the definition of 'Chapter' in the Explanation has been revised to clarify that it refers to the headings specified in the First Schedule of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975. These changes are effective from 1st October 2019, impacting businesses dealing with these metals under GST regulations. This amendment aims to provide clearer guidelines for the taxation of precious metals, ensuring that all relevant parties are aware of the updated classifications and definitions.

The most significant change is the inclusion of 'silver' and 'platinum' alongside 'gold' in the context of the tax rate, which broadens the scope of the notification. Additionally, the reference to 'heading 7108' has been updated to 'Chapter 71', aligning it with the broader classification of precious metals. Furthermore, the definition of 'Chapter' in the Explanation has been revised to clarify that it refers to headings specified in the First Schedule of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975. These changes will come into effect from 1st October 2019, impacting businesses dealing with these precious metals by ensuring they are aware of the updated classifications and definitions for compliance under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act.

723.summary: Notification No. 18/2019-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 30th September 2019, introduces an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 02/2019-Central Tax (Rate) from 7th March 2019. Specifically, it adds a new entry for 'Aerated Water' under HSN code 2202 10 10 in the Annexure, following the existing Sl. No. 2. This change means that businesses dealing with aerated water will need to be aware of this classification for GST purposes. The amendment is effective from 1st October 2019, which means that any transactions involving aerated water after this date will fall under this new classification. This is a significant update for manufacturers and sellers of aerated water, as it clarifies the GST treatment for this product. Businesses should ensure they update their records and compliance measures accordingly to reflect this change.
