key_points: Notification No. 27/2019-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 30th December 2019.
This notification amends Notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In Schedule II - 6%, serial numbers 80AA and 171A are omitted.
In Schedule III - 9%, new entries are added after serial number 163A.
New entries include: 163B for woven and non-woven bags and sacks of polyethylene or polypropylene, and 163C for flexible intermediate bulk containers.
The changes will come into effect on 1st January 2020.

778.summary: Notification No. 28/2019, issued on 31st December 2019, brings important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 12/2017 concerning Union Territory Tax rates. Specifically, it alters the tax rate for certain leased plots in the industrial or financial sectors. The figure '50' in column (3) of the Table against serial number 41 is now changed to '20', effectively reducing the tax rate. Additionally, column (5) has been revised to include new conditions that require the State Government to monitor the use of these leased plots. If there is any violation of the terms or a change in land use, all parties involved, including the original lessor and lessee, will be jointly liable for the tax that would have been applicable without the exemption. These changes aim to and proper use of leased plots. The amendments take effect from 1st January 2020, impacting businesses engaged in industrial or financial activities within the Union Territories.
key_points: This notification is issued on 31st December 2019.
It amends Notification No. 12/2017 - Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In the Table against serial number 41, the figure '50' is replaced with '20' in column (3).
Column (5) is updated with new provisions regarding leased plots for industrial or financial activities.
The new provisions include conditions for monitoring by the State Government and liabilities for violations.
This notification comes into effect from 1st January 2020.

779.summary: Notification No. 29/2019, issued on December 31, 2019, introduces changes to the Union Territory Tax (Rate) by amending the earlier notification No. 13/2017. The key change is in serial number 15 of the Table, which now addresses the renting of motor vehicles designed for carrying passengers. Specifically, if the cost of fuel is included in the service charge, this service is deemed provided to a body corporate. Furthermore, any individual or entity providing this service to a body corporate is required to charge a union territory tax at a rate of 6 percent. This amendment clarifies the tax obligations for those renting vehicles to corporate clients within the taxable territory, ensuring that the tax is applied uniformly. Businesses that rent out passenger vehicles to corporate clients should take note of this change, as it directly impacts their invoicing and tax collection practices.
key_points: This notification is No. 29/2019-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 31st December 2019.
It amends the earlier notification No. 13/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment specifically alters serial number 15 in the Table of the earlier notification.
The new entry for serial number 15 pertains to services of renting motor vehicles designed for passenger transport.
The revised entry states that if the cost of fuel is included in the consideration charged, the service is provided to a body corporate.
It specifies that any person providing this service to a body corporate must charge a union territory tax at the rate of 6 percent.
The amendment applies to any body corporate located in the taxable territory.

780.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 1/2018-Compensation Cess (Rate) on 25th January 2018, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) from 28th June 2017. A significant change is the introduction of a new entry, S. No. 42A, which applies to all old and used motor vehicles, setting the compensation cess rate to NIL. However, this exemption does not apply if the supplier has claimed input tax credit or CENVAT credit on these vehicles. Additionally, the entry for S. No. 43 has been updated; the previous description has been replaced with '8702 or 8703'. These changes primarily affect suppliers and dealers of old and used motor vehicles, as well as those dealing with the specified vehicle categories under S. No. 43. It is important for affected parties to review these amendments to understand their implications on pricing and tax obligations.
key_points: Notification No. 1/2018-Compensation Cess (Rate) was issued on 25th January 2018.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new entry, S. No. 42A, has been added for 'All old and used motor vehicles' with a compensation cess rate of NIL.
The new entry specifies that the NIL rate does not apply if the supplier has availed input tax credit or CENVAT credit on these vehicles.
In S. No. 43, the entry in column (2) has been changed from its previous description to '8702 or 8703'.

781.summary: Notification No. 01/2018-Compensation Cess, issued on January 1, 2018, establishes the framework for the Compensation Cess under the Goods and Services Tax (GST) in India. This notification details the applicability of the Compensation Cess on specific goods and services, outlining the rates that will be enforced. It is crucial for businesses involved in the sale of these goods and services to understand the rates and with the provisions for collection and payment of the cess. The introduction of this notification is part of the government's effort to create a structured tax regime under GST, which aims to streamline the taxation process and enhance revenue collection for the states. Businesses must familiarize themselves with the specific categories of goods affected by this cess to ensure accurate tax calculations and compliance with the new regulations. Overall, this notification is a significant step in the implementation of the GST framework, impacting various sectors across the economy.
key_points: Notification No. 01/2018-Compensation Cess was issued on January 1, 2018.
This notification pertains to the Compensation Cess under the Goods and Services Tax (GST).
It outlines the applicability of the Compensation Cess on certain goods and services.
The notification specifies the rates of Compensation Cess applicable to various categories of goods.
It includes provisions for the collection and payment of the Compensation Cess.
The notification is part of the broader framework established under the GST regime in India.

782.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 2/2018-Compensation Cess (Rate) on 26th July 2018, which amends the previous Notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This new notification introduces two additional entries to the Schedule of the earlier notification. Specifically, S. No. 41A has been added for 'Coal rejects supplied by a coal washer,' which will have a NIL compensation cess rate. Additionally, S. No. 42B has been created for 'Fuel Cell Motor Vehicles,' which will also be subject to a NIL cess rate. These changes are significant as they provide clarity on the tax treatment of these specific goods, effective from 27th July 2018. Businesses dealing with coal washing and fuel cell vehicles will benefit from these amendments, as they will not incur compensation cess on these items.
key_points: Notification No. 2/2018-Compensation Cess (Rate) was issued on 26th July 2018.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Compensation Cess (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
Two new entries are added to the Schedule: S. No. 41A for 'Coal rejects supplied by a coal washer' with a NIL cess rate.
S. No. 42B is added for 'Fuel Cell Motor Vehicles' also with a NIL cess rate.
The changes come into effect on 27th July 2018.

783.summary: This notification, issued on January 1, 2018, amends the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Central Tax from June 27, 2017. The key change is in clause (i), where the tax rate has been reduced from 'one per cent.' to 'half per cent.' This means that businesses will now pay a lower tax rate on their taxable supplies. Additionally, clause (iii) has been updated to clarify that the 'half per cent.' rate applies specifically to the turnover of taxable supplies of goods, ensuring that there is no ambiguity regarding what is included in the calculation. These changes are significant for businesses involved in the supply of goods, as they will benefit from a reduced tax burden. Overall, this amendment aims to provide relief to taxpayers by lowering the applicable tax rate, thereby encouraging compliance and easing the financial load on businesses.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 8/2017-Central Tax dated 27th June 2017.
Changes the rate from 'one per cent.' to 'half per cent.' in clause (i).
Modifies clause (iii) to specify 'half per cent. of the turnover of taxable supplies of goods'.
Issued on 1st January 2018 by the Government of India.

784.summary: On 23rd January 2018, the Government of India issued Notification No. 01/2018-Integrated Tax, which amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017 - Integrated Tax from 13th October 2017. The key change in this amendment is the substitution of the phrase 'except rule 96' with 'except sub rules (1) to (8) and sub rule (10) of rule 96'. This means that certain provisions of rule 96 are now excluded from the earlier exemption, specifically sub rules (1) to (8) and sub rule (10). This amendment is made under the authority of section 4 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The notification was published in the Gazette of India, ensuring that it is officially recorded. Businesses and stakeholders involved in integrated tax should take note of this change, as it clarifies the applicability of specific sub rules under rule 96, which may impact their compliance and reporting obligations.
key_points: This notification is dated 23rd January 2018.
It amends Notification No. 11/2017 - Integrated Tax dated 13th October 2017.
The amendment substitutes the phrase 'except rule 96' with 'except sub rules (1) to (8) and sub rule (10) of rule 96'.
The amendment is made under the powers conferred by section 4 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The notification was issued by the Ministry of Finance, Department of Revenue.
The principal notification No. 11/2017 was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i).
The reference number for this notification is G.S.R. 1261 (E).

785.summary: Notification No. 02/2018-Central Tax, issued on 20th January 2018, amends the earlier Notification No. 35/2017-Central Tax from 15th September 2017. The key change is in the Table under serial number 5, where the date '20th January, 2018' has been updated to '22nd January, 2018'. This amendment is made under the authority of section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, following recommendations from the GST Council. The change affects the compliance timeline for businesses that need to adhere to this specific date. It is important for those involved in GST compliance to note this updated deadline to ensure they are aligned with the latest requirements. The notification has been officially published in the Gazette of India, ensuring that all stakeholders are informed of this change. This adjustment provides a slight extension for the relevant compliance activities, allowing businesses a bit more time to prepare.
key_points: This notification is issued on 20th January 2018.
It amends Notification No. 35/2017-Central Tax dated 15th September 2017.
The amendment specifically changes the date in the Table against serial number 5.
The original date of '20th January, 2018' is replaced with '22nd January, 2018'.
The amendment is made under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It is based on the recommendations of the GST Council.
The amendment is published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary.
The notification is issued by the Central Board of Excise and Customs.

786.summary: Notification No. 02/2018-Integrated Tax, issued on September 20, 2018, introduces a requirement for electronic commerce operators in India. Specifically, these operators, who are not acting as agents, are mandated to collect a tax amounting to 1% of the net value of inter-State taxable supplies made through their platforms. This collection is applicable when the payment for these supplies is processed by the operator itself. The notification is grounded in the provisions of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, particularly the second proviso to section 20, and also refers to section 52 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This change primarily affects electronic commerce operators, ensuring they play a role in the tax collection process for inter-State transactions facilitated through their services. The goal is to streamline tax collection and compliance in the growing e-commerce sector.
key_points: Notification No. 02/2018-Integrated Tax was issued on 20th September 2018.
This notification applies to electronic commerce operators who are not acting as agents.
Operators must collect 1% of the net value of inter-State taxable supplies made through them.
The collection applies when the consideration for these supplies is received by the electronic commerce operator.
The notification is based on the second proviso to section 20 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It also references sub-section (1) of section 52 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

787.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 3/2018-Central Tax, which amends several provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This notification, effective from its publication date, introduces significant changes. Notably, Rule 3(3A) now allows a time frame of one hundred and eighty days instead of ninety days for certain compliance. Additionally, Rule 7's Table has been updated, changing the tax rates for specific entries to reflect half per cent. of the turnover in the respective State or Union territory. The deadline for compliance under Rule 24 has been extended to 31st March 2018. A new Rule 31A has been added, which specifies how to determine the value of supplies related to lotteries and gambling activities. Furthermore, Rule 138 has been revised to clarify the e-way bill requirements for the movement of goods valued over fifty thousand rupees. These changes will impact businesses involved in these sectors, requiring them to adjust their compliance and operational practices accordingly.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Rules, 2018'.
It amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, effective from the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
Rule 3(3A) changes the time period from 'ninety days' to 'one hundred and eighty days'.
Rule 7's Table entries are updated: Sl. No. 1 changes from 'one per cent.' to 'half per cent. of the turnover in the State or Union territory'.
Rule 20 omits the existing proviso.
Rule 24(4) extends the deadline from '31st December, 2017' to '31st March, 2018'.
New Rule 31A is inserted, detailing the value of supply for lotteries, betting, gambling, and horse racing.
Rule 138 is substituted to specify e-way bill requirements for goods movement exceeding fifty thousand rupees.

788.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 3/2018-Integrated Tax on 22nd October 2018, which supersedes the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax from 14th September 2017. This new notification exempts certain individuals from needing to register under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act when making inter-State taxable supplies of specific handicraft goods. The products eligible for this exemption include a wide range of items such as leather articles, carved wood products, bamboo products, and various textile handloom products, among others. However, it is important that the total value of these supplies does not exceed the registration threshold set by the law. Additionally, those benefiting from this exemption must still obtain a Permanent Account Number (PAN) and generate an e-way bill in accordance with the GST rules. This change primarily affects artisans and craftsmen engaged in the production and sale of these specified goods, allowing them to operate without the burden of registration while still complying with necessary documentation requirements.
key_points: Notification No. 3/2018-Integrated Tax issued on 22nd October 2018.
Supersedes Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax dated 14th September 2017.
Exempts certain persons from obtaining registration under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act.
Exemption applies to inter-State taxable supplies of specified handicraft goods and products listed in the notification.
Products include leather articles, carved wood products, bamboo products, textile handloom products, and more.
Suppliers must ensure their aggregate turnover does not exceed the threshold for registration.
Suppliers must obtain a Permanent Account Number (PAN) and generate an e-way bill as per rule 138 of the GST Rules.

789.summary: Notification No. 04/2018-Central Tax, issued on January 23, 2018, introduces a waiver for late fees related to the filing of GSTR-1. Specifically, if a registered person fails to submit their GSTR-1 by the due date, the late fee exceeding Rs. 25 per day will be waived. This applies to all registered persons under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Additionally, if there are no outward supplies reported in a month or quarter, the late fee will only exceed Rs. 10 per day. This change aims to ease the financial burden on businesses that may struggle with timely filings, particularly in cases where there are no sales to report. The waiver is granted under the authority of section 128 of the GST Act, reflecting the government's intent to support compliance while minimizing penalties for non-filing in specific circumstances.
key_points: Waives late fee for GSTR-1 filing failures beyond Rs. 25 per day.
Applicable for any registered person under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
If there are no outward supplies in a month/quarter, the late fee is waived beyond Rs. 10 per day.
This notification is issued under section 128 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

790.summary: The Integrated Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Rules, 2018, which take effect from January 1, 2019, introduce several important changes to the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. Notably, Rule 3(h) now states that services related to internet advertisements are considered to be provided throughout India. Additionally, new Rule 4 details how to determine the place of supply for services concerning immovable property when these services span multiple states or union territories. Rule 5 addresses the organization of events across different regions, specifying that the value of services should be determined using generally accepted accounting principles. Furthermore, Rule 6 clarifies how to assess the place of supply for leased circuits installed in more than one state, based on the number of points in the circuit. Lastly, Rule 7 outlines how to allocate value for services that require physical presence, depending on the location of the goods or services. These amendments are particularly relevant for businesses involved in real estate, event management, and telecommunications, as they provide clearer guidelines for compliance and service valuation across state lines.
key_points: The Integrated Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Rules, 2018, come into force on January 1, 2019.
Rule 3(h) is amended to state that for internet advertisements, the service is deemed provided all over India.
New Rule 4 specifies how to determine the place of supply for services related to immovable property across multiple states or union territories.
New Rule 5 outlines the place of supply for organizing events across multiple states or union territories, using generally accepted accounting principles for value determination.
New Rule 6 addresses leased circuit services installed in multiple states or union territories, determining the place of supply based on the number of points in the circuit.
New Rule 7 clarifies the place of supply for services requiring physical presence, with value apportioned based on the location of goods or services.
Illustrations provided clarify how to apply these rules in practical scenarios involving hotels, events, and leased circuits.

791.summary: Notification No. 05/2018-Central Tax, issued on January 23, 2018, introduces a waiver on late fees for registered persons who fail to file their GSTR-5 returns by the due date. Specifically, if a registered person does not submit their GSTR-5 on time, the late fee will not exceed ₹25 for each day of delay. However, if the total central tax payable in the return is nil, the late fee is further reduced to a maximum of ₹10 per day. This change is significant for businesses that may struggle to meet filing deadlines, as it provides some financial relief by limiting the penalties associated with late submissions. The waiver is applicable under section 47 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and aims to ease the compliance burden on taxpayers during the filing process.
key_points: Waives late fee for failure to file GSTR-5 by the due date.
Late fee waiver applies to registered persons under section 47 of the CGST Act.
For GSTR-5 returns with central tax payable, late fee is capped at ₹25 per day.
If the total central tax payable is nil, late fee is capped at ₹10 per day.

792.summary: Notification No. 06/2018-Central Tax, issued on January 23, 2018, introduces a waiver for late fees related to the filing of GSTR-5A. Specifically, registered persons who fail to submit their GSTR-5A returns by the due date will only incur a late fee of twenty-five rupees for each day of delay. However, if the integrated tax due in the return is nil, the late fee is further reduced to ten rupees per day. This waiver is applicable under section 47 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and is intended to ease the financial burden on taxpayers who may have difficulties in filing their returns on time. The notification reflects the government's commitment to support compliance among registered persons by minimizing penalties for late submissions. Overall, this change is beneficial for those who may have missed the deadline for GSTR-5A, providing them with a more manageable late fee structure.
key_points: Waives late fee for failure to file GSTR-5A by the due date.
Late fee waiver applies to registered persons under section 47 of the CGST Act.
For late filing, the fee is capped at twenty-five rupees per day.
If the integrated tax payable in GSTR-5A is nil, the late fee is capped at ten rupees per day.
This notification is issued under section 128 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The notification was issued on January 23, 2018.

793.summary: Notification No. 07/2018-Central Tax, issued on January 23, 2018, introduces a waiver on late fees for registered persons who fail to file their GSTR-6 return by the due date. Specifically, the waiver applies to any late fee amount that exceeds twenty-five rupees for each day of delay. This change is significant for businesses that may struggle to meet the filing deadline, as it alleviates some financial burden associated with late submissions. The waiver is granted under section 128 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, reflecting the government's intention to support compliance among taxpayers. By limiting the late fee to twenty-five rupees per day, the notification aims to encourage timely filing without imposing excessive penalties. This measure is particularly beneficial for those who may have faced challenges in meeting their filing obligations, ensuring that they are not overly penalized for delays in GSTR-6 submissions.
key_points: Waives late fee for GSTR-6 return filing.
Applicable for registered persons under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Late fee waiver is for amounts exceeding twenty-five rupees per day.
This notification is issued under section 128 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act.

794.summary: Notification No. 08/2018-Central Tax, issued on January 23, 2018, extends the deadline for Input Service Distributors to file their GSTR-6 return. The new deadline is set for March 31, 2018, covering the tax periods from July 2017 to February 2018. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 62/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on November 15, 2017. The extension is made under sub-section (6) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. It is important to note that this change specifically pertains to the filing of GSTR-6 and does not affect other GST returns. This extension provides additional time for Input Service Distributors to comply with their filing obligations for the specified months, ensuring they can meet the new deadline without penalties related to late submissions for this period.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing GSTR-6 for Input Service Distributors.
New deadline is March 31, 2018.
Applies to tax periods from July 2017 to February 2018.
Supersedes Notification No. 62/2017-Central Tax dated November 15, 2017.
The extension is granted under sub-section (6) of section 39 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The amendment affects only the filing of GSTR-6 and not other returns.

795.summary: Notification No. 09/2018-Central Tax, issued on 23rd January 2018, introduces significant changes in the electronic portals used for GST-related activities. It supersedes the earlier Notification No. 4/2017-Central Tax from 19th June 2017. The new notification designates www.gst.gov.in as the official portal for various GST functions, including registration, tax payments, return submissions, and the settlement of integrated tax. Additionally, it establishes www.ewaybillgst.gov.in as the portal specifically for the electronic furnishing of way bills. These changes are effective from 16th January 2018. The notification is issued under the authority of section 146 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and section 20 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This update is crucial for businesses and individuals involved in GST compliance, as it clarifies the official platforms for managing their GST-related tasks.
key_points: Notification No. 09/2018-Central Tax was issued on 23rd January 2018.
This notification supersedes Notification No. 4/2017-Central Tax dated 19th June 2017.
It designates www.gst.gov.in as the Common Goods and Services Tax Electronic Portal for registration, tax payment, return filing, and integrated tax settlement.
It also designates www.ewaybillgst.gov.in as the Common Goods and Services Tax Electronic Portal for electronic way bill furnishing.
The notification is effective from 16th January 2018.
The notification is issued under the powers conferred by section 146 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 and section 20 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

796.summary: Notification No. 10/2018-Central Tax, issued on 23rd January 2018, brings an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 39/2017-Central Tax from 13th October 2017. The key change is in the exclusion of certain sub-rules under rule 96. Previously, the notification stated 'except rule 96', but now it specifies 'except sub rules (1) to (8) and sub rule (10) of rule 96'. This means that while rule 96 is still relevant, specific sub-rules are now excluded from the earlier provisions. This amendment is significant for businesses that are navigating the GST framework, as it clarifies which parts of rule 96 are applicable and which are not. Stakeholders should review their compliance strategies in light of this change to ensure they are aligned with the updated regulations.
key_points: This notification is issued under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It amends Notification No. 39/2017-Central Tax dated 13th October 2017.
The amendment changes the phrase 'except rule 96' to 'except sub rules (1) to (8) and sub rule (10) of rule 96'.
The notification was issued on 23rd January 2018.

797.summary: On 2nd February 2018, the Central Government issued Notification No. 11/2018-Central Tax, which rescinds the earlier Notification No. 74/2017-Central Tax that was published on 29th December 2017. This means that any provisions or guidelines that were part of the earlier notification are no longer in effect, although any actions taken before this rescission remain valid. The earlier notification was published in the Gazette of India and was referenced as G.S.R 1601 (E). The rescission is executed under the authority granted by section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This change primarily affects businesses and entities that were relying on the provisions of the rescinded notification, as they will need to adjust their compliance and reporting practices accordingly. It is important for affected parties to review the implications of this change to ensure they are aligned with the current regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 11/2018-Central Tax was issued on 2nd February 2018.
This notification rescinds Notification No. 74/2017-Central Tax dated 29th December 2017.
The rescission applies except for actions taken before this notification.
The rescinded notification was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i).
The rescinded notification was referenced as G.S.R 1601 (E).
The rescission is based on the powers conferred by section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

798.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 12/2018-Central Tax, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This notification introduces significant changes, particularly in rule 117 and rule 138. For rule 117, the deadline for registered persons to submit FORM GST TRAN 2 has been set to 31st March 2018, or later if extended by the Commissioner. Rule 138 has been completely revised to clarify the process for generating e-way bills for goods valued over fifty thousand rupees. Registered persons must now generate the e-way bill electronically in FORM GST EWB-01 before moving goods. The notification also specifies the validity periods for e-way bills based on the distance transported, ensuring compliance across all States and Union territories. Additionally, transporters can generate e-way bills under certain conditions, including when goods are supplied through e-commerce platforms. These changes are crucial for businesses involved in the movement of goods, ensuring they adhere to the updated GST regulations.
key_points: The notification is titled Central Goods and Services Tax (Second Amendment) Rules, 2018.
It amends rule 117 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, specifically substituting sub-clause (iii) of clause (b) in sub-rule (4).
The new deadline for submitting FORM GST TRAN 2 is 31st March 2018, or within an extended period as recommended by the Commissioner.
Rule 138 is entirely substituted, detailing the requirements for generating e-way bills for goods movement exceeding fifty thousand rupees.
The e-way bill must be generated electronically in FORM GST EWB-01 before the movement of goods.
The e-way bill is valid for specific periods based on the distance transported, as outlined in a new table.
The e-way bill can be generated by transporters under certain conditions, including when goods are supplied through e-commerce operators.
The e-way bill generated is valid across all States and Union territories.

799.summary: Notification No. 13/2018-Central Tax, issued on 7th March 2018, officially rescinds the earlier Notification No. 6/2018-Central Tax, which was dated 23rd January 2018. This means that any provisions or rules established under Notification No. 6/2018 are no longer in effect, but any actions taken under that notification before this rescission will still be considered valid. The decision to rescind was made under the authority granted by section 128 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This change affects all stakeholders who were operating under the rules set by Notification No. 6/2018, as they will need to adjust to the absence of those provisions. It is important for businesses and tax professionals to be aware of this change to ensure they are following the current regulations as outlined in the latest notification.
key_points: Notification No. 13/2018-Central Tax was issued on 7th March 2018.
This notification rescinds Notification No. 6/2018-Central Tax dated 23rd January 2018.
The rescission applies except for actions taken before the rescission.
The rescission is based on the powers conferred by section 128 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

800.summary: The Central Goods and Services Tax (Third Amendment) Rules, 2018, introduced several important changes to the GST framework. Effective from the date of publication, one significant change is in Rule 45, which now permits either the principal or the job worker to issue a challan when goods are transferred between job workers. Additionally, Rule 125 has been revised to specify that the Secretary to the Authority must be an officer of at least Additional Commissioner rank. In Rule 127, the term 'day' has been added to clarify the timing for performance reports. Furthermore, Rule 129 has been updated to indicate that the Authority will determine allowances instead of the Standing Committee. The quorum for meetings of the Authority has been set to a minimum of three members as per the new Rule 134. Lastly, an explanation has been added to Rule 138D, clarifying that certain railway transport terms do not cover leasing of parcel space, effective from April 1, 2018. These amendments aim to streamline processes and clarify responsibilities within the GST framework.
key_points: This notification is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Third Amendment) Rules, 2018.
The amendment comes into force on the date of its publication in the Official Gazette.
Rule 45 is amended to allow a challan to be issued by either the principal or the job worker when goods are sent from one job worker to another.
Rule 125 is substituted to state that the Secretary to the Authority must be an officer not below the rank of Additional Commissioner.
In Rule 127, the word 'day' is added to the first proviso of sub-rule (4) regarding performance reports.
Rule 129 is amended to replace 'as allowed by the Standing Committee' with 'as may be allowed by the Authority'.
Rule 134 is substituted to specify that a minimum of three members constitutes a quorum for Authority meetings.
An explanation is added to Rule 138D stating that certain railway transport terms do not include leasing of parcel space by Railways, effective from April 1, 2018.

801.summary: Notification No. 15/2018-Central Tax, issued on 23rd March 2018, announces that certain provisions will take effect from 1st April 2018. Specifically, it pertains to sub-rules (ii) [excluding clause (7)], (iii), (iv), (v), (vi), and (vii) of rule 2 from the earlier Notification No. 12/2018-Central Tax, which was published on 7th March 2018. This earlier notification was documented in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i), under the reference number G.S.R 204 (E). The changes are enacted under the authority of section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Businesses and stakeholders in the GST framework should take note of these provisions as they will be applicable starting from the specified date. This notification clarifies the timeline for the implementation of these specific sub-rules, ensuring that all relevant parties are aware of the upcoming changes in the GST regulations.
key_points: The notification is titled Notification No. 15/2018-Central Tax.
It was issued on 23rd March 2018.
The provisions will come into force on 1st April 2018.
It specifically applies to sub-rules (ii) [other than clause (7)], (iii), (iv), (v), (vi), and (vii) of rule 2.
These sub-rules are part of notification No. 12/2018-Central Tax, dated 7th March 2018.
The parent notification (12/2018) was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i).
The reference number for the parent notification is G.S.R 204 (E).
This notification is issued under the powers conferred by section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

802.summary: Notification No. 16/2018-Central Tax outlines the deadlines for filing the GSTR-3B return for the months of April, May, and June 2018. Specifically, businesses must submit their GSTR-3B for April by 20th May 2018, for May by 20th June 2018, and for June by 20th July 2018. This notification emphasizes the importance of timely filing, as registered persons are required to discharge their tax liabilities by debiting their electronic cash or credit ledger by these specified dates. This means that businesses need to ensure they have sufficient funds in their ledgers to cover any taxes, ies due at the time of filing. The notification is aimed at all registered taxpayers under the GST regime, ensuring they are aware of their filing responsibilities for these months. Compliance with these deadlines is crucial for maintaining good standing under the GST framework.
key_points: The return in FORM GSTR-3B for April 2018 must be filed by 20th May 2018.
The return in FORM GSTR-3B for May 2018 must be filed by 20th June 2018.
The return in FORM GSTR-3B for June 2018 must be filed by 20th July 2018.
Registered persons must discharge their tax liability by debiting their electronic cash or credit ledger by the last date specified for each month.

803.summary: Notification No. 17/2018-Central Tax introduces a special procedure for registered persons whose aggregate turnover is up to 1.5 crore rupees in the preceding or current financial year. These individuals are required to file their details of outward supply of goods or services in FORM GSTR-1 for the quarter from April to June 2018. The deadline for this submission is set for July 31, 2018. This notification is issued under the authority of section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. It aims to streamline the filing process for smaller taxpayers, allowing them to comply with GST requirements more easily. The specific details regarding the special procedure or any further extensions for filing returns for the months of April to June 2018 will be communicated in future notifications. This change primarily affects small businesses and registered persons with lower turnover, simplifying their compliance obligations during this period.
key_points: Registered persons with aggregate turnover up to 1.5 crore rupees are affected.
These registered persons must follow a special procedure for filing GSTR-1.
The details of outward supply for the quarter April to June 2018 must be submitted in FORM GSTR-1.
The deadline for submitting GSTR-1 for this period is July 31, 2018.
This notification is issued under section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

804.summary: Notification No. 18/2018-Central Tax, issued on March 28, 2018, extends the due dates for filing FORM GSTR-1 for the months of April, May, and June 2018. Specifically, the new deadline for April 2018 is now set for May 31, 2018, for May 2018 it is June 10, 2018, and for June 2018, it is July 10, 2018. This extension applies to registered persons whose aggregate turnover exceeds 1.5 crore rupees in either the preceding or the current financial year. The changes are made under the authority of the second proviso to subsection (1) of section 37 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Additionally, the notification indicates that further extensions for other returns related to sections 38 and 39 for the same months will be announced in the Official Gazette at a later date. This is significant for businesses that need more time to prepare their returns, ensuring they can comply without rushing.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing FORM GSTR-1 for April 2018 to 31st May 2018.
Extends the due date for filing FORM GSTR-1 for May 2018 to 10th June 2018.
Extends the due date for filing FORM GSTR-1 for June 2018 to 10th July 2018.
Applicable to registered persons with an aggregate turnover of more than 1.5 crore rupees in the preceding or current financial year.
The extension is based on the second proviso to subsection (1) of section 37 of the CGST Act, 2017.
Further extensions for other returns under sections 38 and 39 for April to June 2018 will be notified later.

805.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 19/2018-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for Input Service Distributors to file their GSTR-6 returns. The new deadline is now set for 31st May 2018, covering the tax periods from July 2017 through April 2018. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 08/2018-Central Tax, which was issued on 23rd January 2018. The extension is made under the authority of sub-section (6) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and aligns with rule 65 of the CGST Rules, 2017. This change is particularly relevant for Input Service Distributors who need to ensure their returns for the specified months are submitted by the new deadline. The amendment aims to provide additional time for compliance, reflecting the government's ongoing efforts to facilitate the GST filing process for businesses.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-6 for Input Service Distributors.
New due date is 31st May 2018.
Applies to the tax periods from July 2017 to April 2018.
Supersedes Notification No. 08/2018-Central Tax dated 23rd January 2018.
The extension is granted under sub-section (6) of section 39 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The amendment is made in accordance with rule 65 of the CGST Rules, 2017.

806.summary: Notification No. 20/2018-Central Tax, issued on 28th March 2018, introduces a significant change regarding the refund application timeline for specified persons under section 55 of the CGST Act. Previously, these entities were required to submit their refund applications within six months from the end of the quarter in which they received the supplies. However, this notification extends that period to eighteen months, providing more flexibility for these organizations. The specified persons include specialized agencies of the United Nations, multilateral financial institutions, and foreign consulates or embassies. This amendment is made under the authority of section 148 of the CGST Act and follows the earlier notification No. 14/2018-Central Tax issued on 23rd March 2018. Additionally, the facility for filing these refund claims has been made available on the common portal, streamlining the process for eligible entities. This change is expected to ease the burden on specified persons, allowing them more time to claim refunds on taxes paid for inward supplies of goods and services.
key_points: Notification No. 20/2018-Central Tax issued on 28th March 2018.
Amends the refund application timeline for specified persons under section 55 of the CGST Act.
Previously, the refund application had to be filed within six months from the last day of the quarter.
Now, specified persons can file for refunds within eighteen months from the last date of the quarter.
Specified persons include specialized agencies of the UN, multilateral financial institutions, and foreign consulates or embassies.
This change is made under the powers conferred by section 148 of the CGST Act.
The facility for filing refund claims has been made available on the common portal.
This notification follows the earlier notification No. 14/2018-Central Tax dated 23rd March 2018.

808.summary: Notification No. 22/2018-Central Tax, issued on May 14, 2018, provides a waiver of the late fee for registered persons who failed to submit their GSTR-3B returns on time for the months from October 2017 to April 2018. This waiver specifically applies to those who had submitted their FORM GST TRAN-1 but did not file it on the common portal by the deadline of December 27, 2017. To qualify for this waiver, these registered persons must ensure that they file their FORM GST TRAN-1 by May 10, 2018, and subsequently submit their GSTR-3B returns by May 31, 2018. This notification is significant for those affected, as it alleviates the financial burden of late fees during this period, provided they meet the specified conditions. Overall, this measure aims to support compliance among taxpayers who faced challenges in filing their returns on time.
key_points: Waives late fee under section 47 of the CGST Act for GSTR-3B returns.
Applies to returns due from October 2017 to April 2018.
Eligible for registered persons who submitted FORM GST TRAN-1 but did not file it by December 27, 2017.
Registered persons must file FORM GST TRAN-1 by May 10, 2018.
GSTR-3B returns must be filed by May 31, 2018.

809.summary: Notification No. 23/2018-Central Tax, issued on 18th May 2018, updates the due date for a compliance requirement outlined in Notification No. 16/2018-Central Tax, which was originally published on 23rd March 2018. Specifically, the due date has been extended from 20th May 2018 to 22nd May 2018. This change is made under the authority of section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and is relevant to businesses that need to comply with the specified requirements. The amendment is documented in the Table of the earlier notification, specifically in column (3) against serial number 1. This adjustment provides additional time for businesses to fulfill their obligations under the GST framework, ensuring they can meet the revised deadline without disruption. It is important for affected businesses to note this new date for their compliance activities.
key_points: Notification No. 23/2018-Central Tax was issued on 18th May 2018.
This notification amends Notification No. 16/2018-Central Tax dated 23rd March 2018.
The amendment changes the due date for a specific compliance from 20th May 2018 to 22nd May 2018.
The amendment is made under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It refers to sub-rule (5) of rule 61 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The change is noted in the Table against serial number 1, column (3) of the earlier notification.

810.summary: Notification No. 24/2018-Central Tax, issued on May 28, 2018, officially designates the National Academy of Customs, Indirect Taxes and Narcotics as the authority responsible for conducting examinations related to the Central Goods and Services Tax (GST). This decision is made under the powers granted by section 48 of the GST Act, 2017, and aligns with sub-rule (3) of rule 83 of the GST Rules, 2017. This means that the National Academy will oversee the examination process for individuals involved in GST compliance and administration. The change is significant for those preparing for GST-related examinations, as it centralizes the authority responsible for these assessments. This notification reflects the government's ongoing efforts to streamline GST processes and ensure that examinations are conducted by a recognized and specialized body within the Ministry of Finance.
key_points: Notification No. 24/2018-Central Tax issued on 28th May 2018.
The notification designates the National Academy of Customs, Indirect Taxes and Narcotics as the examination authority.
This designation is made under section 48 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It is also in accordance with sub-rule (3) of rule 83 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

811.summary: Notification No. 25/2018-Central Tax, issued on 31st May 2018, extends the deadline for Input Service Distributors to file their GSTR-6 returns. The new due date is now set for 31st July 2018, covering the tax periods from July 2017 through June 2018. This notification replaces the earlier notification No. 19/2018-Central Tax, which was issued on 28th March 2018. The extension is made under the authority of sub-section (6) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This change is significant for Input Service Distributors, as it provides them additional time to comply with their filing obligations for the specified months. It is important for affected businesses to take note of this new deadline to ensure they meet their GST requirements without any issues.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-6 from the previous deadline to 31st July 2018.
Applies to Input Service Distributors for the tax periods from July 2017 to June 2018.
Supersedes the earlier notification No. 19/2018-Central Tax dated 28th March 2018.
The extension is granted under sub-section (6) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

812.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 26/2018-Central Tax on 13th June 2018, which amends several rules under the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. These amendments take effect upon publication in the Official Gazette. Notably, a new proviso in rule 37 clarifies that certain amounts added under section 15(2)(b) are considered paid for the purposes of section 16(2). Additionally, rule 83 has been updated to extend the time frame from 'one year' to 'eighteen months'. The formula for refunds related to inverted duty structures has been revised in rule 89(5), providing clearer guidelines for calculating maximum refund amounts. Furthermore, rule 95(3)(a) now mandates that inward supplies must come from registered persons with valid tax invoices. A new proviso in rule 97(1) addresses the deposit of cess into the Fund, while rule 138(14) introduces a clause regarding the movement of empty LPG cylinders for non-supply reasons. These changes will impact businesses dealing with GST compliance and refunds, ensuring clearer guidelines and processes.
key_points: Notification No. 26/2018-Central Tax was issued on 13th June 2018.
The rules come into force on the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
In rule 37, a new proviso is added stating that certain amounts added under section 15(2)(b) are deemed paid for section 16(2) purposes.
In rule 83, the second proviso is amended to change 'one year' to 'eighteen months'.
Rule 89(5) is substituted to provide a new formula for refunds on inverted duty structure.
Rule 95(3)(a) is amended to specify that inward supplies must be received from a registered person against a tax invoice.
In rule 97(1), a new proviso is added regarding the deposit of cess into the Fund.
Rule 138(14) is amended to include a new clause for empty cylinders for LPG being moved for reasons other than supply.

813.summary: Notification No. 27/2018-Central Tax, issued on June 13, 2018, outlines specific goods that can be disposed of by authorities after being seized under section 67 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This includes a variety of items such as salt, raw hides, newspapers, menthol, lighter fuel, batteries, and petroleum products, among others. The notification also covers dangerous drugs, bulk drugs, and fireworks, as well as all taxable goods listed in Chapters 1 to 24 of the Customs Tariff Act. Additionally, it addresses unclaimed or abandoned goods that are subject to rapid depreciation due to technological advancements. Importantly, any goods that have been provisionally released under section 67 must be claimed within one month; if not, they may also be disposed of. This notification is significant for businesses dealing with these goods, as it clarifies the process and conditions under which seized items can be managed.
key_points: Notification No. 27/2018-Central Tax was issued on 13th June 2018.
The notification specifies goods that can be disposed of after seizure under section 67 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

The goods listed include salt, raw hides, newspapers, menthol, lighter fuel, batteries, petroleum products, dangerous drugs, bulk drugs, fireworks, red sander, sandalwood, and all taxable goods from Chapters 1 to 24 of the Customs Tariff Act.
Unclaimed or abandoned goods that depreciate rapidly due to technology changes are also included.
Goods provisionally released under section 67 must be claimed within one month; otherwise, they can be disposed of.

814.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 28/2018-Central Tax on 19th June 2018, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This notification introduces several important changes. Firstly, it allows transporters who are registered in more than one State or Union Territory and share the same Permanent Account Number (PAN) to apply for a unique common enrolment number using a new form, FORM GST ENR-02. This is beneficial for transporters as it simplifies their registration process across different jurisdictions. Additionally, the notification provides the Commissioner with the authority to extend the time for recording the final report in Part B of FORM EWB-03 by up to three days if warranted by circumstances. Furthermore, rule 142(5) has been amended to include references to sections 129 and 130, which relate to the detention and seizure of goods and conveyances. Overall, these changes aim to streamline processes for transporters and enhance compliance within the GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 28/2018-Central Tax was issued on 19th June 2018.
The rules come into force on the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
A new sub-rule (1A) is added to rule 58, allowing transporters registered in multiple States/UTs with the same PAN to apply for a unique common enrolment number using FORM GST ENR-02.
The new proviso in rule 138C allows the Commissioner to extend the time for recording the final report in Part B of FORM EWB-03 by up to three days under certain circumstances.
Rule 142(5) is amended to include references to sections 129 and 130 along with section 76.
FORM GST ENR-02 is introduced for transporters to apply for a unique common enrolment number.

815.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 29/2018-Central Tax, which amends several rules in the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This amendment, effective from June 12, 2018, is officially titled the Central Goods and Services Tax (Seventh Amendment) Rules, 2018. The key change involves the replacement of the term 'Directorate General of Safeguards' and 'Director General of Safeguards' with 'Directorate General of Anti-profiteering' and 'Director General of Anti-profiteering' across multiple rules, specifically rules 125, 129, 130, 131, 132, and 133. This change reflects a shift in focus from safeguards to anti-profiteering measures, indicating a stronger emphasis on ensuring that businesses do not unfairly benefit from tax reductions. Businesses and stakeholders in the GST framework should take note of these amendments as they may impact compliance and reporting requirements related to anti-profiteering practices.
key_points: This notification is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Seventh Amendment) Rules, 2018.
The rules came into effect on June 12, 2018.
Rule 125 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 is amended to replace 'Directorate General of Safeguards' with 'Directorate General of Anti-profiteering'.
Rule 129 is amended to substitute 'Director General of Safeguards' with 'Director General of Anti-profiteering'.
In Rule 130(2), 'Director General of Safeguards' is replaced with 'Director General of Anti-profiteering'.
Rule 131 is amended to change 'Director General of Safeguards' to 'Director General of Anti-profiteering'.
In Rule 132(1), 'Director General of Safeguards' is substituted with 'Director General of Anti-profiteering'.
Rule 133 is amended to replace 'Director General of Safeguards' with 'Director General of Anti-profiteering'.

816.summary: Notification No. 30/2018-Central Tax, issued on July 30, 2018, extends the deadline for Input Service Distributors to file their GSTR-6 returns. The new deadline is now set for September 30, 2018, covering the tax periods from July 2017 to August 2018. This notification supersedes the previous Notification No. 25/2018-Central Tax, which was issued on May 31, 2018. The extension is provided under sub-section (6) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This change affects Input Service Distributors who need to submit their returns for the specified months. The amendment is effective immediately, allowing businesses more time to comply with their filing obligations. This adjustment aims to ease the compliance burden during the specified tax periods, ensuring that Input Service Distributors can meet their reporting requirements without undue pressure.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing GSTR-6 for Input Service Distributors.
New deadline for GSTR-6 is now September 30, 2018.
Applies to the tax periods from July 2017 to August 2018.
Supersedes the earlier Notification No. 25/2018-Central Tax dated May 31, 2018.
The extension is granted under sub-section (6) of section 39 of the CGST Act.
The amendment is effective from the date of this notification, July 30, 2018.

817.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 31/2018-Central Tax on 6th August 2018, allowing certain taxpayers who only received a Provisional Identification Number (PID) and did not complete FORM GST REG-26 by 31st December 2017 to apply for a Goods and Services Tax Identification Number (GSTIN). These taxpayers must submit specific details to their jurisdictional nodal officer by 31st August 2018, including their Provisional ID, previous registration number, and reason for not migrating. After submitting these details, they will receive an email from the Goods and Services Tax Network (GSTN) and must then apply for registration using FORM GST REG-01. Once their application is approved, they will get an email with their new GSTIN and access token. Additionally, they are required to send their new GSTIN and other details to GSTN by 30th September 2018. Importantly, their registration will be considered effective from 1st July 2017, which may benefit them in terms of compliance and tax credits.
key_points: Notification No. 31/2018-Central Tax was issued on 6th August 2018.
Taxpayers who received only a Provisional Identification Number (PID) and did not file complete FORM GST REG-26 by 31st December 2017 can now apply for GSTIN.
Affected taxpayers must submit specific details to their jurisdictional nodal officer by 31st August 2018.
The required details include Provisional ID, previous registration number, activation status of FORM GST REG-26, contact details, reason for not migrating, and jurisdiction of the officer.
After receiving an email from GSTN, taxpayers must apply for registration using FORM GST REG-01.
Upon approval, taxpayers will receive an email with Application Reference Number (ARN), new GSTIN, and access token.
Taxpayers must provide their new GSTIN, access token, ARN, and old GSTIN to GSTN by 30th September 2018.
Registration for these taxpayers will be deemed effective from 1st July 2017.

818.summary: Notification No. 32/2018-Central Tax, issued on August 10, 2018, extends the deadline for filing GSTR-1 for registered taxpayers whose aggregate turnover exceeds 1.5 crore rupees. The new deadline is set for the 11th day of the month following the month of supply, covering the period from July 2018 to March 2019. This change is made under the second proviso to sub-section (1) of section 37 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Additionally, the time limits for filing returns under sections 38 and 39 for these months will be announced in a future notification. This extension is significant for businesses with higher turnover, allowing them more time to comply with their filing obligations. It is essential for affected businesses to note these new deadlines to ensure they meet the revised requirements.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-1 for registered persons with turnover over 1.5 crore.
New due date is the 11th day of the month following the month of supply.
This extension applies for the months from July 2018 to March 2019.
The notification is issued under the second proviso to sub-section (1) of section 37 of the CGST Act.
Details for returns under sections 38 and 39 for the same months will be notified later.

819.summary: Notification No. 33/2018-Central Tax introduces a special procedure for registered persons whose aggregate turnover is up to 1.5 crore rupees. These individuals are required to submit details of their outward supply of goods or services using FORM GSTR-1. The notification outlines specific deadlines for filing this form for three quarters: for the quarter of July to September 2018, the deadline is set for 31st October 2018; for October to December 2018, it is 31st January 2019; and for January to March 2019, the deadline is 30th April 2019. Additionally, it mentions that the time limits for furnishing details or returns for the months from July 2018 to March 2019 will be announced in the Official Gazette at a later date. This notification is significant for small businesses as it streamlines their reporting process under GST, making compliance easier for those with lower turnover.
key_points: Registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to 1.5 crore rupees are notified for a special procedure.
These persons must furnish details of outward supply in FORM GSTR-1.

The time periods for filing FORM GSTR-1 are specified for three quarters: July-September 2018 by 31st October 2018, October-December 2018 by 31st January 2019, and January-March 2019 by 30th April 2019.
The time limit for furnishing details for July 2018 to March 2019 will be notified later.

820.summary: Notification No. 34/2018-Central Tax, issued on August 10, 2018, outlines the requirements for filing the GSTR-3B return for the months from July 2018 to March 2019. All registered taxpayers must submit their GSTR-3B returns electronically through the common portal by the twentieth day of the month following the reporting month. This means, for example, the return for July 2018 must be filed by August 20, 2018. Additionally, taxpayers are required to settle their tax liabilities, including any ies, using their electronic cash or credit ledger by the same deadline. This notification is based on the powers granted under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and aligns with the rules set forth in sub-rule (5) of rule 61 of the GST Rules. It is crucial for registered persons to adhere to these timelines to with the GST framework.
key_points: The notification specifies the return in FORM GSTR-3B for the months from July 2018 to March 2019.
Returns must be filed electronically through the common portal.
The due date for filing GSTR-3B is the twentieth day of the month succeeding the respective month.
Registered persons must discharge their tax liabilities using the electronic cash or credit ledger.
This notification is issued under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It references sub-rule (5) of rule 61 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

821.summary: Notification No. 35/2018-Central Tax, issued on 21st August 2018, introduces an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 34/2018-Central Tax, which was published on 10th August 2018. This amendment adds a new proviso that requires taxpayers to submit their GSTR-3B return for the month of July 2018 electronically through the common portal by 24th August 2018. This change is made under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, following recommendations from the GST Council. It is crucial for businesses to note this deadline, as it specifies the exact date by which the return must be filed. The amendment aims to streamline the filing process and ensure timely compliance for the July tax period. All taxpayers who are required to file GSTR-3B for July 2018 will be affected by this change, and they should prepare to meet the new deadline accordingly.
key_points: This notification is issued on 21st August 2018.
It amends Notification No. 34/2018-Central Tax dated 10th August 2018.
A new proviso is added to the first paragraph of Notification No. 34/2018.
The new proviso states that GSTR-3B for July 2018 must be filed by 24th August 2018.
The amendment is made under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The amendment is based on recommendations from the GST Council.
The return must be filed electronically through the common portal.

822.summary: The recent Notification No. 36/2018-Central Tax, issued on 24th August 2018, introduces an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 34/2018-Central Tax from 10th August 2018. Specifically, it adds a new proviso that affects the filing deadlines for the GSTR-3B return. Registered persons located in the State of Kerala are now required to submit their GSTR-3B for the month of July 2018 by 5th October 2018. Additionally, those whose principal place of business is in Kodagu district of Karnataka and in Mahe, Puducherry, must file their GSTR-3B for August 2018 by 10th October 2018. This amendment is crucial for the specified regions, ensuring that businesses in these areas are aware of their new filing deadlines. The changes aim to provide clarity and support to taxpayers in these specific locations.
key_points: This notification is dated 24th August 2018.
It amends Notification No. 34/2018-Central Tax issued on 10th August 2018.
The amendment adds a new proviso regarding GSTR-3B filing.
Registered persons in Kerala must file GSTR-3B for July 2018 by 5th October 2018.
Registered persons in Kodagu district, Karnataka, must file GSTR-3B for August 2018 by 10th October 2018.
Registered persons in Mahe, Puducherry, must also file GSTR-3B for August 2018 by 10th October 2018.

823.summary: Notification No. 37/2018-Central Tax, issued on 24th August 2018, introduces an amendment to Notification No. 32/2018-Central Tax, which was originally published on 10th August 2018. This amendment adds a new proviso that specifies the filing deadlines for GSTR-1 returns for the months of July and August 2018. Specifically, registered persons located in the State of Kerala, those whose principal place of business is in Kodagu district of Karnataka, and those in Mahe, Puducherry, are required to submit their GSTR-1 for July 2018 by 5th October 2018. Additionally, these registered persons must file their GSTR-1 for August 2018 by 10th October 2018. This change is significant for the specified regions, as it provides extended deadlines for filing these returns, ensuring that businesses in these areas have clarity on their obligations under the GST framework.
key_points: This notification is No. 37/2018-Central Tax, issued on 24th August 2018.
It amends Notification No. 32/2018-Central Tax, which was published on 10th August 2018.
A new proviso is added to the first paragraph of Notification No. 32/2018.
The new proviso states that GSTR-1 for July 2018 must be filed by certain registered persons by 5th October 2018.
The new proviso also states that GSTR-1 for August 2018 must be filed by certain registered persons by 10th October 2018.
The registered persons affected are those in Kerala, Kodagu district in Karnataka, and Mahe in Puducherry.

824.summary: Notification No. 38/2018-Central Tax, issued on 24th August 2018, introduces an amendment to the earlier notification No. 33/2018-Central Tax. This amendment adds a new proviso that specifically requires certain registered persons to file their GSTR-1 returns for the quarter from July 2018 to September 2018 by 15th November 2018. The registered persons affected by this change include those located in the State of Kerala, those whose principal place of business is in Kodagu district of Karnataka, and those in Mahe, which is part of the Union territory of Puducherry. This extension provides additional time for these specific groups to comply with their filing obligations, ensuring they can meet the new deadline without penalties. The amendment is significant for businesses in these regions, as it directly impacts their GST return filing schedule for the specified quarter.
key_points: This notification is No. 38/2018-Central Tax, issued on 24th August 2018.
It amends notification No. 33/2018-Central Tax, originally published on 10th August 2018.
A new proviso is added to the first paragraph of the earlier notification.
The new proviso states that GSTR-1 for the quarter from July 2018 to September 2018 must be filed by certain registered persons by 15th November 2018.
The registered persons affected are those in the State of Kerala, Kodagu district in Karnataka, and Mahe in Puducherry.

825.summary: Notification No. 39/2018-Central Tax, issued on 4th September 2018, introduces several amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This notification is known as the Central Goods and Services Tax (Eighth Amendment) Rules, 2018. A significant change in rule 22(4) allows cancellation proceedings to be dropped if a taxpayer files all pending returns and pays the tax dues, with the order being issued in FORM GST-REG 20. Additionally, rule 36(2) now permits input tax credit claims even if not all document particulars are provided, as long as key details are included. The amendment in rule 55(5) adds 'or in batches or lots' to clarify the treatment of goods in completely knocked down condition. Furthermore, the definition of 'Adjusted Total Turnover' in rule 89(4)(E) has been revised for clarity. Rule 96(10) specifies conditions for claiming refunds of integrated tax paid on exports, while rule 138A(1) now requires the person in charge of a conveyance to carry a copy of the bill of entry for imported goods. These changes affect registered taxpayers and those involved in the export of goods and services.
key_points: Notification No. 39/2018-Central Tax was issued on 4th September 2018.
The rules introduced by this notification are called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Eighth Amendment) Rules, 2018.
A new proviso was added to rule 22(4) allowing cancellation proceedings to be dropped if pending returns are filed and tax dues are paid, with an order issued in FORM GST-REG 20.
A new proviso in rule 36(2) allows input tax credit if certain details are provided in documents, even if not all particulars are included.
Rule 55(5) was amended to include the phrase 'or in batches or lots' regarding goods in completely knocked down condition.
The definition of 'Adjusted Total Turnover' in rule 89(4)(E) was substituted to clarify the components included in the calculation.
Rule 96(10) was amended to specify conditions under which integrated tax refund claims for exports can be made.
A new requirement was added in rule 138A(1) for the person in charge of a conveyance to carry a copy of the bill of entry for imported goods.

826.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 40/2018-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for submitting FORM GST ITC-04. This form is crucial for businesses that have dispatched goods to job workers or received goods from them during the period from July 2017 to June 2018. The new deadline for these submissions is now set for 30th September 2018. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 53/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on 28th October 2017. The changes are made under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and are effective immediately from the date of this notification, which is 4th September 2018. Businesses that engage with job workers should take note of this extended timeline to with the GST regulations regarding the movement of goods. This extension provides additional time for businesses to accurately report their transactions involving job workers.
key_points: Extends the deadline for FORM GST ITC-04 submissions.
New deadline is 30th September 2018.
Covers goods dispatched to or received from job workers from July 2017 to June 2018.
Supersedes Notification No. 53/2017-Central Tax dated 28th October 2017.
Applicable under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Changes are effective from the date of this notification, 4th September 2018.

827.summary: Notification No. 41/2018-Central Tax, issued on September 4, 2018, provides relief by waiving late fees for certain categories of taxpayers under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. Specifically, it addresses registered persons who submitted their GSTR-3B returns for October 2017 but did not complete the filing process on the common portal. Additionally, it benefits those who timely filed their GSTR-4 returns for the period from October to December 2017 but were incorrectly charged late fees. Furthermore, Input Service Distributors who incurred late fees for GSTR-6 returns filed between January 1 and January 23, 2018, are also included in this waiver. This notification aims to alleviate the financial burden on these taxpayers by correcting previous errors related to late fee assessments, ensuring they are not penalized for circumstances beyond their control.
key_points: Waives late fee under section 47 of the CGST Act for specific taxpayers.
Applies to registered persons who submitted GSTR-3B for October 2017 but did not file it on the common portal.
Covers registered persons who filed GSTR-4 for October to December 2017 on time but faced erroneous late fees.
Includes Input Service Distributors who paid late fees for GSTR-6 returns for tax periods from January 1, 2018, to January 23, 2018.

828.summary: Notification No. 42/2018-Central Tax, issued on 4th September 2018, extends the deadline for registered persons to make a declaration in FORM GST ITC-01. This extension specifically applies to those who submitted their application using FORM GST-CMP-04 between 2nd March 2018 and 31st March 2018. The new deadline allows an additional thirty days from the publication date of this notification, providing more time for compliance. This change is based on section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and is also linked to rule 40 of the GST Rules. This extension is particularly beneficial for businesses that may have faced challenges in meeting the original timeline, ensuring they can complete their declarations without the pressure of the initial deadline. Overall, this notification aims to facilitate smoother compliance for registered persons during this period.
key_points: Extends the time limit for making the declaration in FORM GST ITC-01.
Applies to registered persons who filed FORM GST-CMP-04.
Original application period for FORM GST-CMP-04 was from 2nd March 2018 to 31st March 2018.
The extension period is for thirty days from the date of publication of this notification.
Notification issued on 4th September 2018.
This extension is in pursuance of section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Also refers to clause (b) of sub-rule (1) of rule 40 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

829.summary: Notification No. 43/2018-Central Tax, issued on 10th September 2018, introduces a special procedure for registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to 1.5 crore rupees. This notification supersedes earlier notifications, specifically No. 57/2017, 17/2018, and 33/2018. Affected taxpayers must file details of their outward supply in FORM GSTR-1 for various quarters, with specific deadlines. For the quarter of July-September 2018, the deadline is set for 31st October 2018. Additionally, registered persons located in Kerala, Kodagu district in Karnataka, and Mahe in Puducherry have an earlier deadline of 15th November 2018 for their GSTR-1 filings. Taxpayers who received their GSTIN under Notification No. 31/2018 are required to submit their GSTR-1 for the period from July 2017 to September 2018 by 31st December 2018. Lastly, the time limits for returns covering the months from July 2017 to March 2019 will be announced in the future.
key_points: Notification No. 43/2018-Central Tax issued on 10th September 2018.
This notification supersedes Notifications No. 57/2017, 17/2018, and 33/2018.
Registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to 1.5 crore rupees must follow a special procedure for GSTR-1.
Details of outward supply for the quarters July-September 2017 to January-March 2019 must be filed in FORM GSTR-1.
The deadline for filing GSTR-1 for July-September 2018 is 31st October 2018.
Registered persons in Kerala, Kodagu district (Karnataka), and Mahe (Puducherry) must file GSTR-1 by 15th November 2018.
Taxpayers who obtained GSTIN under Notification No. 31/2018 must file GSTR-1 for July 2017 to September 2018 by 31st December 2018.
The time limit for returns for July 2017 to March 2019 will be notified later.

830.summary: Notification No. 44/2018-Central Tax, issued on September 10, 2018, brings significant changes to the deadlines for filing GSTR-1 for certain registered persons. Specifically, it extends the deadline for those with an aggregate turnover exceeding 1.5 crore rupees for the months from July 2017 to September 2018 to October 31, 2018. Additionally, for the months from October 2018 to March 2019, the deadline is now set for the 11th day of the following month. Notably, taxpayers who received their GST Identification Number (GSTIN) as per Notification No. 31/2018 will have an even later deadline, with GSTR-1 filings for the months from July 2017 to November 2018 extended to December 31, 2018. This notification supersedes several earlier notifications, including 18/2017, 58/2017, 18/2018, and 32/2018, ensuring that the new deadlines are now in effect. These changes primarily affect registered persons with significant turnover, allowing them more time to comply with their filing obligations.
key_points: Extends the time limit for filing GSTR-1 for registered persons with turnover over 1.5 crore from July 2017 to September 2018 until October 31, 2018.
For the months from October 2018 to March 2019, GSTR-1 filing deadline is extended to the 11th day of the succeeding month.
Taxpayers who obtained GSTIN under Notification No. 31/2018 will have their GSTR-1 deadline for July 2017 to November 2018 extended to December 31, 2018.
This notification supersedes earlier notifications: 18/2017, 58/2017, 18/2018, and 32/2018.

831.summary: Notification No. 45/2018-Central Tax, issued on 10th September 2018, introduces an important amendment to previous notifications regarding the filing of GSTR-3B returns. Specifically, it amends Notification No. 21/2017 and Notification No. 56/2017 by adding a new proviso. This proviso mandates that taxpayers who received their Goods and Services Tax Identification Number (GSTIN) under Notification No. 31/2018 must file their GSTR-3B returns electronically for the tax periods from July 2017 to November 2018. The deadline for submitting these returns is set for 31st December 2018. This change is significant for those who registered for GST during this period, as it clarifies their obligations regarding return filing.
