It ensures that all relevant taxpayers are aware of the electronic filing requirement and the specific deadline they must meet.
key_points: This notification is issued under Notification No. 45/2018-Central Tax dated 10th September 2018.
It amends Notification No. 21/2017-Central Tax dated 8th August 2017 and Notification No. 56/2017-Central Tax dated 15th November 2017.
A new proviso is added to the first paragraph of both notifications.
The new proviso states that GSTR-3B returns for the period from July 2017 to November 2018 must be filed electronically.
The deadline for filing these GSTR-3B returns is set for 31st December 2018.
This requirement applies to taxpayers who obtained their GST Identification Number (GSTIN) as per Notification No. 31/2018-Central Tax dated 6th August 2018.

832.summary: Notification No. 46/2018-Central Tax, issued on 10th September 2018, introduces amendments to two earlier notifications: 35/2017 and 16/2018. The key change is the addition of a new proviso that requires taxpayers who have obtained their Goods and Services Tax Identification Number (GSTIN) under notification 31/2018 to file their GSTR-3B returns for the period from July 2017 to November 2018. This filing must be done electronically through the common portal, and the deadline for submission is set for 31st December 2018. This amendment affects those taxpayers who registered under the specified notification and ensures they comply with the new filing requirements within the stipulated timeframe. The changes aim to streamline the return filing process for the specified tax periods, providing clarity on deadlines for affected taxpayers.
key_points: This notification is issued on 10th September 2018.
It amends notification number 35/2017-Central Tax dated 15th September 2017.
It also amends notification number 16/2018-Central Tax dated 23rd March 2018.
A new proviso is added to both notifications regarding GSTR-3B returns.
Taxpayers with GSTIN obtained under notification 31/2018-Central Tax must file GSTR-3B for July 2017 to November 2018.
The deadline for filing GSTR-3B is set for 31st December 2018.

833.summary: Notification No. 47/2018-Central Tax, issued on 10th September 2018, introduces an important amendment to the earlier notification 34/2018-Central Tax from 10th August 2018. Specifically, it adds a new proviso that mandates taxpayers who received their Goods and Services Tax Identification Number (GSTIN) under notification 31/2018-Central Tax, dated 6th August 2018, to file their GSTR-3B returns electronically. This requirement covers the tax periods from July 2017 to November 2018. The deadline for submitting these returns is set for 31st December 2018. This change is significant for those taxpayers as it formalizes the electronic filing process for a specific period, ensuring compliance with the updated GST regulations. Taxpayers should be aware of this new deadline and the requirement to file electronically, as it directly impacts their reporting obligations under the GST framework.
key_points: This notification is issued on 10th September 2018.
It amends notification number 34/2018-Central Tax dated 10th August 2018.
A new proviso is added after the second proviso in the first paragraph of notification 34/2018.
The new proviso states that GSTR-3B returns for July 2017 to November 2018 must be filed electronically.
The deadline for filing these GSTR-3B returns is set for 31st December 2018.

834.summary: The Central Government has issued the Central Goods and Services Tax (Ninth Amendment) Rules, 2018, which came into effect upon publication in the Official Gazette. A significant change is the introduction of a new sub-rule (1A) in Rule 117, allowing the Commissioner to extend the deadline for submitting FORM GST TRAN-1 to 31st March 2019. This extension is specifically for registered persons who were unable to submit the form by the original due date due to technical issues on the common portal, as recommended by the GST Council. Additionally, those who file FORM GST TRAN-1 under this new provision can also submit their FORM GST TRAN-2 by 30th April 2019. Furthermore, Rule 142 has been amended to include section 125 alongside section 76 in sub-rule (5). These changes aim to provide relief to taxpayers facing technical challenges and clarify the rules surrounding GST compliance.
key_points: This notification is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Ninth Amendment) Rules, 2018.
The rules come into force on the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
Rule 117 is amended to include a new sub-rule (1A) allowing the Commissioner to extend the deadline for FORM GST TRAN-1 submission to 31st March 2019 for those facing technical difficulties.
Registered persons can submit FORM GST TRAN-2 by 30th April 2019 if they file FORM GST TRAN-1 under the new sub-rule (1A).
Rule 142 is amended to include section 125 in sub-rule (5) alongside section 76.

835.summary: Notification No. 49/2018-Central Tax, issued on September 13, 2018, amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 by introducing FORM GSTR-9C. This new form serves as a reconciliation statement that taxpayers must file for the financial year 2017-18, specifically covering the period from July 2017 to March 2018. It requires taxpayers to provide detailed information about their turnover, taxable turnover, tax paid, and input tax credit. Importantly, businesses with multiple GSTINs under the same PAN are required to derive and declare their turnover separately for each GSTIN. The rules take effect immediately upon publication in the Official Gazette. Additionally, FORM GSTR-9C mandates taxpayers to specify reasons for any discrepancies found in their reported turnover and tax amounts, ensuring greater transparency and accuracy in GST compliance. This change primarily affects businesses that are required to reconcile their financial statements with their GST returns, enhancing the overall accountability in the GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 49/2018-Central Tax was issued on September 13, 2018.
This notification amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
It introduces FORM GSTR-9C, which is a reconciliation statement to be filed by taxpayers.
FORM GSTR-9C must be filed for the financial year 2017-18, covering the period from July 2017 to March 2018.
The reconciliation statement includes details like turnover, taxable turnover, tax paid, and input tax credit.
The rules come into effect on the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
Taxpayers with multiple GSTINs under the same PAN must derive and declare GSTIN-wise turnover in the statement.
The form requires reasons for any discrepancies in turnover and tax amounts.

836.summary: Notification No. 50/2018-Central Tax, issued on 13th September 2018, introduces important changes regarding the implementation of section 51 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 33/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on 15th September 2017. The key change is that the provisions of section 51 will come into effect from 1st October 2018. This section specifically applies to certain entities, including authorities or boards established by an Act of Parliament or State Legislature that have more than 51% government participation. Additionally, it covers societies formed by the Central or State Government or Local Authorities under the Societies Registration Act, 1860, as well as public sector undertakings. These changes will affect how these entities comply with GST regulations, ensuring they are aligned with the provisions set forth in the new notification.
key_points: Notification No. 50/2018-Central Tax was issued on 13th September 2018.
This notification supersedes Notification No. 33/2017-Central Tax dated 15th September 2017.
The provisions of section 51 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 will come into force on 1st October 2018.
Section 51 applies to authorities or boards set up by an Act of Parliament or State Legislature, with over 51% government participation.
It also applies to societies established by the Central or State Government or Local Authorities under the Societies Registration Act, 1860.
Public sector undertakings are also included under the provisions of section 51.

837.summary: Notification No. 51/2018-Central Tax, issued on September 13, 2018, announces the enforcement of Section 52 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This section will come into effect on October 1, 2018. Section 52 primarily deals with the collection of tax at source (TCS) by e-commerce operators on the supplies made through their platforms. This means that from the specified date, e-commerce operators will be required to deduct a certain percentage of tax from the payments made to suppliers using their platforms and remit it to the government. This change affects e-commerce businesses and their suppliers, as they will need to adjust their accounting and tax practices to comply with the new requirements. The notification signifies a step towards enhancing tax compliance in the digital economy, ensuring that the government can collect taxes more effectively from online transactions.
key_points: The notification is issued under Notification No. 51/2018-Central Tax.
The date of issuance is September 13, 2018.
Section 52 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 is being enforced.
The provisions of Section 52 will come into force on October 1, 2018.

838.summary: Notification No. 52/2018-Central Tax, issued on September 20, 2018, introduces a new tax obligation for electronic commerce operators who are not acting as agents. These operators are now required to collect a tax at a rate of 0.5% on the net value of intra-State taxable supplies made through their platform by other suppliers. This means that if a supplier sells goods or services through an e-commerce platform, the platform operator must collect this tax when they receive payment for those supplies. The tax is specifically applicable to intra-State transactions, which are sales occurring within the same state. This change is mandated under section 52(1) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and aims to streamline tax collection in the e-commerce sector. Operators need to ensure they are compliant with this requirement to facilitate proper tax administration.
key_points: Notification No. 52/2018-Central Tax issued on 20th September 2018.
This notification applies to electronic commerce operators who are not acting as agents.
Operators must collect a tax at a rate of 0.5% on the net value of intra-State taxable supplies.
The tax is applicable only for supplies made through the operator by other suppliers.
The consideration for these supplies must be collected by the electronic commerce operator.
This notification is issued under the authority of section 52(1) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

839.summary: The Central Government has issued the Central Goods and Services Tax (Eleventh Amendment) Rules, 2018, which will take effect from 23rd October 2017. This amendment specifically alters Rule 96 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. The previous sub-rule (10) has been replaced with a new provision that outlines the conditions under which exporters can claim refunds of integrated tax paid on their exports. Importantly, to be eligible for these refunds, exporters must not have received supplies on which the supplier has availed benefits from certain earlier notifications. These notifications include No. 48/2017-Central Tax, No. 40/2017-Central Tax (Rate), No. 41/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate), and two Customs notifications issued on 13th October 2017. This change primarily affects exporters who are looking to claim refunds and ensures that they are aware of the conditions tied to their refund claims.
key_points: This notification is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Eleventh Amendment) Rules, 2018.
The rules come into effect from 23rd October 2017.
Rule 96 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 is amended.
Sub-rule (10) of Rule 96 is substituted with a new provision.
The new sub-rule (10) specifies conditions for claiming refunds of integrated tax on exports.
Refund claimants must not have received supplies benefiting from specific earlier notifications.
The notifications referenced include No. 48/2017-Central Tax, No. 40/2017-Central Tax (Rate), No. 41/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate), and two Customs notifications dated 13th October 2017.

840.summary: Notification No. 54/2018-Central Tax, issued on 9th October 2018, amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, specifically altering rules related to refunds for unutilised input tax credit and integrated tax on exports. The amendment to sub-rule (4B) of rule 89 clarifies that refunds for unutilised input tax credit on zero-rated supplies will only be granted if the claimant has not availed benefits under certain specified notifications. Similarly, sub-rule (10) of rule 96 has been revised to restrict refunds of integrated tax paid on exports if the claimant has availed benefits under specific notifications related to capital goods and zero-rated supplies. These changes are effective immediately upon publication in the Official Gazette. This notification is particularly relevant for businesses engaged in exports and those claiming refunds on input tax credits, ensuring they are aware of the new conditions that may affect their refund claims.
key_points: Notification No. 54/2018-Central Tax was issued on 9th October 2018.
This notification amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
Sub-rule (4B) of rule 89 is substituted to clarify conditions for claiming refund of unutilised input tax credit on zero-rated supplies.
The new sub-rule (4B) specifies that refunds will be granted only if certain notifications related to input supplies are not availed.
Sub-rule (10) of rule 96 is also substituted to outline conditions for claiming refund of integrated tax paid on exports.
The amended sub-rule (10) restricts refunds if the claimant has availed benefits under specific notifications related to capital goods and zero-rated supplies.
The changes are effective from the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
The principal rules were last amended by notification No. 53/2018-Central Tax on the same date.

841.summary: Notification No. 55/2018-Central Tax, issued on 21st October 2018, introduces an important amendment to Notification No. 34/2018-Central Tax, which was originally published on 10th August 2018. The amendment adds a new proviso that specifies the deadline for submitting the GSTR-3B return for the month of September 2018. Taxpayers are now required to file this return electronically through the common portal by 25th October 2018. This change is significant for businesses that are registered under GST and need to comply with the filing requirements for that specific month. The amendment aims to clarify the submission timeline and ensure that all relevant parties are aware of the updated deadline. This is particularly important for those who may have been uncertain about the due date for the September return. Overall, this notification helps streamline the filing process for businesses and ensures compliance with the GST regulations.
key_points: This notification is issued under Notification No. 55/2018-Central Tax on 21st October 2018.
It amends Notification No. 34/2018-Central Tax dated 10th August 2018.
A new proviso is added after the third proviso in the first paragraph of Notification No. 34/2018.
The new proviso states that the return in FORM GSTR-3B for September 2018 must be submitted electronically by 25th October 2018.
amended_refs: 34/2018-was published in the Gazette of India
47/2018-was published i n the Gazette of India vide number G

842.summary: Notification No. 56/2018-Central Tax, issued on 23rd October 2018, introduces important changes for casual taxable persons engaged in inter-State supplies of specific handicraft goods. This notification replaces the earlier Notification No. 32/2017-Central Tax from 15th September 2017. It outlines categories of products that are exempt from GST registration, including leather articles, various carved wood products, bamboo items, and textile handloom products, among others. To qualify for this exemption, these suppliers must ensure their aggregate turnover does not exceed the registration threshold set out in section 22 of the GST Act. Additionally, they are required to obtain a Permanent Account Number (PAN) and generate an e-way bill in compliance with rule 138 of the GST Rules. The notification also indicates that these suppliers can benefit from Notification No. 03/2018-Integrated Tax, issued on 22nd October 2018, which further supports their operations under the GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 56/2018-Central Tax was issued on 23rd October 2018.
This notification supersedes Notification No. 32/2017-Central Tax dated 15th September 2017.
It specifies categories of casual taxable persons exempt from obtaining GST registration for inter-State supplies of certain handicraft goods.
The exempted products include leather articles, carved wood products, bamboo products, textile handloom products, and more, as listed in the provided table.
These persons must have a Permanent Account Number (PAN) and generate an e-way bill as per rule 138 of the GST Rules.
The exemption applies only if the aggregate turnover does not exceed the threshold for registration as per section 22 of the GST Act.
The notification also references the benefit of Notification No. 03/2018-Integrated Tax dated 22nd October 2018.

843.summary: Notification No. 57/2018-Central Tax, issued on 23rd October 2018, introduces an amendment to Notification No. 50/2018-Central Tax from 13th September 2018. This amendment adds a proviso that exempts certain authorities under the Ministry of Defence from the provisions of the earlier notification, effective from 1st October 2018. Specifically, this exemption applies to the authorities listed under clause (a) of sub-section (1) of section 51 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. The notification also includes an Annexure that details the code numbers assigned to various Controllers and Offices of Defence Accounts, totaling 31 entries. This change primarily affects the Ministry of Defence authorities, clarifying which offices are subject to the provisions of the GST Act and which are not. Business owners and clients dealing with these specific authorities should take note of this amendment to understand their compliance obligations better.
key_points: Notification No. 57/2018-Central Tax was issued on 23rd October 2018.
This notification amends Notification No. 50/2018-Central Tax dated 13th September 2018.
A new proviso is added stating that the notification does not apply to certain authorities under the Ministry of Defence.
The exemption for the Ministry of Defence authorities is effective from 1st October 2018.
The authorities exempted are specified under clause (a) of sub-section (1) of section 51 of the Act.
The notification includes an Annexure listing the code numbers for various Controllers/Offices of Defence Accounts.
The Annexure contains 31 entries detailing the designations and corresponding code numbers for Defence Accounts Controllers.

844.summary: Notification No. 58/2018-Central Tax, issued on 26th October 2018, mandates that individuals whose GST registration was cancelled by the proper officer on or before 30th September 2018 must file their final return using FORM GSTR-10. The deadline for submitting this form is 31st December 2018. This requirement is established under section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, which allows the government to specify conditions for filing returns. This notification specifically targets those whose registrations have been cancelled, ensuring they complete their tax obligations by the end of December. It is crucial for these individuals to be aware of this requirement to avoid any complications related to their GST compliance. The notification reflects the government's ongoing efforts to streamline the GST process and ensure that all taxpayers fulfill their responsibilities, particularly those who have had their registrations revoked.
key_points: Persons whose GST registration was cancelled on or before 30th September 2018 are affected.
These individuals must file their final return using FORM GSTR-10.
The deadline for submitting FORM GSTR-10 is set for 31st December 2018.
This notification is issued under section 148 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

845.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 59/2018-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for submitting FORM GST ITC-04. This form is crucial for businesses that deal with job workers, as it pertains to the declaration of goods sent to or received from job workers between July 2017 and September 2018. The new deadline for filing this form is now set for December 31, 2018. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 40/2018-Central Tax, which was issued on September 4, 2018. The extension is provided under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and relates specifically to sub-rule (3) of rule 45 of the GST Rules. This change is significant for businesses that have engaged job workers during the specified period, allowing them additional time to comply with the reporting requirements. It is essential for affected businesses to take note of this new deadline to ensure proper filing.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing FORM GST ITC-04.
New deadline is December 31, 2018.
Covers goods dispatched to or received from job workers from July 2017 to September 2018.
Supersedes Notification No. 40/2018-Central Tax dated September 4, 2018.
Issued under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Relates to sub-rule (3) of rule 45 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

846.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 60/2018-Central Tax, which was originally published on 30th October 2018. This corrigendum, released on 12th November 2018, corrects a specific phrase in the original notification. The phrase 'furnish an application to the effect' has been amended to 'furnish an undertaking to the effect'. This change is found in line 13 of the original document. The notification is part of the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, and is referenced as G.S.R. 1075 (E). This amendment clarifies the requirement for taxpayers regarding the submission process, ensuring that they understand they need to provide an undertaking instead of an application. This change is relevant for businesses and individuals who are required to comply with the provisions outlined in the original notification, as it directly impacts how they should proceed with their submissions.
key_points: This is a corrigendum to Notification No. 60/2018-Central Tax issued on 30th October 2018.
The corrigendum was published on 12th November 2018.
In the original notification, the phrase 'furnish an application to the effect' has been changed.
The corrected phrase now reads 'furnish an undertaking to the effect'.
This change is noted in line 13 of the original notification.
The notification was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i).
The reference number for the original notification is G.S.R. 1075 (E).
The corrigendum is issued by the Central Board of Excise and Customs.

847.summary: The Notification No. 60/2018-Central Tax, issued on 30th October 2018, introduces significant amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. A key change is the insertion of Rule 83A, which mandates that Goods and Services Tax Practitioners must pass an examination conducted by the National Academy of Customs, Indirect Taxes and Narcotics (NACIN). This examination will be held twice a year, and practitioners must register online and pay a fee as specified by NACIN. They are required to pass the exam within two years of their enrollment, with an additional year granted to those who enrolled before 1st July 2018. Additionally, Rule 109A has been updated to specify that appeals must be handled by officers not below the rank of Joint Commissioner (Appeals). The new Rule 142A outlines the procedure for recovering dues under existing laws, allowing for electronic submissions via FORM GST DRC-07A. Furthermore, various forms such as FORM GST REG-16 and FORM GSTR-4 have been modified to align with these new requirements, ensuring that practitioners and businesses are aware of the updated procedures and compliance measures.
key_points: Notification No. 60/2018-Central Tax is issued on 30th October 2018.
Introduces the Central Goods and Services Tax (Thirteenth Amendment) Rules, 2018.
Inserts Rule 83A regarding the examination of Goods and Services Tax Practitioners, to be conducted by NACIN.
Examinations will occur twice a year, with registration and fee payment managed online through NACIN's specified website.
Practitioners must pass the examination within two years of enrollment, with an extension for those enrolled before 1st July 2018.
Updates Rule 109A to require any officer not below the rank of Joint Commissioner (Appeals) for certain appeals.
Introduces Rule 142A for the recovery of dues under existing laws, allowing for electronic uploads in FORM GST DRC-07A.
Modifies various forms including FORM GST REG-16, FORM GSTR-4, and FORM GST PMT-01 to reflect new procedures and requirements.

848.summary: Notification No. 61/2018-Central Tax, issued on 5th November 2018, introduces an important amendment to Notification No. 50/2018-Central Tax, which was originally published on 13th September 2018. The key change is the addition of a new proviso that exempts the supply of goods or services between public sector undertakings from the provisions of the earlier notification. This exemption is effective from 1st October 2018. The amendment is made under the authority of section 1(3) and section 51 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This change clarifies that transactions between public sector entities, regardless of whether they are distinct persons, will not be subject to the rules outlined in the previous notification. This amendment follows another update made by Notification No. 57/2018-Central Tax on 23rd October 2018, ensuring that public sector undertakings have clear guidelines regarding their inter-supply transactions. The notification is officially recorded under F. No. CBEC/20/06/16/2018-GST.
key_points: This notification is issued as Notification No. 61/2018-Central Tax on 5th November 2018.
It amends Notification No. 50/2018-Central Tax dated 13th September 2018.
A new proviso is added stating that the notification does not apply to the supply of goods or services between public sector undertakings.
The exemption for public sector undertakings is effective from 1st October 2018.
The amendment is made under the powers conferred by section 1(3) and section 51 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The principal notification was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i) as G.S.R 868(E).
This notification follows the earlier amendment made by Notification No. 57/2018-Central Tax dated 23rd October 2018.
The amendment is noted under F. No. CBEC/20/06/16/2018-GST.

849.summary: Notification No. 62/2018-Central Tax, issued on 29th November 2018, introduces amendments to Notification No. 34/2018-Central Tax from 10th August 2018. Specifically, it adds two new provisos regarding the filing of GSTR-3B returns. The first proviso allows registered persons in Srikakulam district, Andhra Pradesh, to file their GSTR-3B for September and October 2018 by 30th November 2018. The second proviso applies to registered persons in certain districts of Tamil Nadu, including Cuddalore and Thiruvarur, allowing them to file their GSTR-3B for October 2018 by 20th December 2018. This amendment is significant for businesses in these regions as it provides them with extended deadlines for compliance with GST return filing, reflecting the government's responsiveness to the needs of taxpayers in specific areas.
key_points: Notification No. 62/2018-Central Tax was issued on 29th November 2018.
This notification amends Notification No. 34/2018-Central Tax, which was issued on 10th August 2018.
The amendment adds two new provisos after the fourth proviso in Notification No. 34/2018.
The first new proviso states that GSTR-3B for September and October 2018 must be filed by 30th November 2018 for businesses in Srikakulam district, Andhra Pradesh.

The second new proviso states that GSTR-3B for October 2018 must be filed by 20th December 2018 for businesses in specific districts of Tamil Nadu: Cuddalore, Thiruvarur, Puddukottai, Dindigul, Nagapatinam, Theni, Thanjavur, Sivagangai, Tiruchirappalli, Karur, and Ramanathapuram.
amended_refs: 34/2018-was published in the Gazette of India

850.summary: The recent amendment in Notification No. 63/2018-Central Tax introduces specific filing deadlines for GSTR-1 forms for certain districts. For registered persons whose principal place of business is in Srikakulam district, Andhra Pradesh, they are required to submit their GSTR-1 for September 2018 and October 2018 by 30th November 2018. Additionally, registered persons in several districts of Tamil Nadu, including Cuddalore, Thiruvarur, and others, must file their GSTR-1 for October 2018 by 20th December 2018. This amendment is significant as it provides clarity on the filing timelines for these specific regions, ensuring that businesses in these areas are aware of their obligations. The changes aim to streamline the filing process and with the GST regulations for the affected districts. Overall, this notification helps businesses in Srikakulam and the listed Tamil Nadu districts to meet their GST filing requirements within the specified deadlines.
key_points: Inserts new provisos in Notification No. 44/2018-Central Tax dated 10th September 2018.
Registered persons in Srikakulam district, Andhra Pradesh must file GSTR-1 for September 2018 by 30th November 2018.
Registered persons in Srikakulam district, Andhra Pradesh must file GSTR-1 for October 2018 by 30th November 2018.

Registered persons in Cuddalore, Thiruvarur, Puddukottai, Dindigul, Nagapatinam, Theni, Thanjavur, Sivagangai, Tiruchirappalli, Karur, and Ramanathapuram in Tamil Nadu must file GSTR-1 for October 2018 by 20th December 2018.

851.summary: Notification No. 64/2018-Central Tax, issued on 29th November 2018, introduces an amendment to the earlier notification No. 43/2018-Central Tax from 10th September 2018. Specifically, it adds a new proviso that mandates registered persons whose principal place of business is in Srikakulam district, Andhra Pradesh, to submit their GSTR-1 for the quarter from July to September 2018. This filing must be completed electronically through the common portal by the deadline of 30th November 2018. This change is significant for businesses operating in Srikakulam, as it clarifies their obligation to report their outward supplies for the specified period. The amendment aims to and streamline the filing process for these registered persons in the district. It is essential for affected businesses to be aware of this requirement and to act accordingly to meet the new deadline.
key_points: This notification is No. 64/2018-Central Tax, issued on 29th November 2018.
It amends notification No. 43/2018-Central Tax, dated 10th September 2018.
A new proviso is added after the second proviso in paragraph 2 of the earlier notification.
The new proviso requires registered persons in Srikakulam district, Andhra Pradesh, to file GSTR-1 for July to September 2018.
The deadline for filing GSTR-1 is set for 30th November 2018.
The filing must be done electronically through the common portal.

852.summary: Notification No. 65/2018-Central Tax, issued on 29th November 2018, extends the deadline for filing GSTR-4 for the quarter from July to September 2018. The new deadline is now set for 30th November 2018. This extension specifically applies to registered taxpayers who are paying tax under section 10 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act and are based in the Srikakulam district of Andhra Pradesh. The extension is granted under the provisions of sub-section (6) of section 39 of the CGST Act, which allows the Commissioner to modify timelines for filing returns. This change is particularly relevant for businesses in the specified district, providing them additional time to comply with their tax filing obligations for that quarter. The notification aims to ease compliance for affected taxpayers during this period.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-4 for the quarter July to September 2018.

Applicable to registered persons paying tax under section 10 of the CGST Act.

853.summary: Notification No. 66/2018-Central Tax, issued on November 29, 2018, extends the deadline for registered persons who are required to deduct tax at source under section 51 of the CGST Act. Specifically, the time limit for filing GSTR-7 for the months of October, November, and December 2018 has been pushed back to January 31, 2019. This change is significant for those businesses that need to comply with TDS requirements, as it provides additional time to prepare and submit their returns. The extension is granted under the authority of sub-section (6) of section 39 of the CGST Act, ensuring that affected taxpayers can meet their obligations without the pressure of the original deadlines. This notification is crucial for maintaining compliance and ensuring that businesses can manage their tax responsibilities effectively during this period.
key_points: Extends the time limit for filing GSTR-7 for TDS by registered persons.
Applies to the tax periods of October 2018, November 2018, and December 2018.
New deadline for filing GSTR-7 is now January 31, 2019.

The notification is effective as of November 29, 2018.
The extension is applicable to registered persons required to deduct tax at source under section 51 of the CGST Act.

854.summary: Notification No. 67/2018-Central Tax, issued on 31st December 2018, brings important changes to the earlier Notification No. 31/2018-Central Tax from 6th August 2018. Specifically, it alters the deadlines for certain compliance requirements. The due date in clause (i) has been extended from 31st August 2018 to 31st January 2019, providing additional time for businesses to meet their obligations. Similarly, the deadline in clause (iv) has been pushed back from 30th September 2018 to 28th February 2019. These changes are significant for businesses that were struggling to meet the original deadlines, as they now have more time to prepare and file necessary documents. This amendment reflects the government's ongoing efforts to facilitate compliance and ease the burden on taxpayers during the GST implementation phase. Businesses affected by these changes should take note of the new dates to ensure they are aligned with the revised timelines.
key_points: Notification No. 67/2018-Central Tax was issued on 31st December 2018.
This notification amends Notification No. 31/2018-Central Tax dated 6th August 2018.
In clause (i) of paragraph 2, the due date is changed from 31st August 2018 to 31st January 2019.
In clause (iv) of paragraph 2, the due date is changed from 30th September 2018 to 28th February 2019.

855.summary: Notification No. 68/2018-Central Tax, issued on December 31, 2018, brings important amendments to two earlier notifications: No. 21/2017 and No. 56/2017. Specifically, it extends the compliance period for taxpayers from 'July, 2017 to November, 2018' to 'July, 2017 to February, 2019'. Consequently, the deadline for compliance has also been pushed back from December 31, 2018, to March 31, 2019. This change is significant for businesses that need to align their GST filings and payments within this new timeframe. The amendments were made under the authority of section 168 of the CGST Act, 2017, and were recommended by the GST Council. These updates provide additional time for businesses to meet their GST obligations, reflecting the government's ongoing efforts to facilitate compliance. The principal notifications were previously amended by Notification No. 45/2018 on September 10, 2018, indicating a continuous evolution of GST regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 68/2018-Central Tax was issued on December 31, 2018.
This notification amends Notification No. 21/2017-Central Tax dated August 8, 2017.
It also amends Notification No. 56/2017-Central Tax dated November 15, 2017.
The amendment changes the period from 'July, 2017 to November, 2018' to 'July, 2017 to February, 2019'.
The deadline for compliance is extended from 'December 31, 2018' to 'March 31, 2019'.
These changes are made under the powers conferred by section 168 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The amendments were made on the recommendations of the GST Council.
The principal notifications were last amended by Notification No. 45/2018 on September 10, 2018.

856.summary: Notification No. 69/2018-Central Tax, issued on December 31, 2018, brings important amendments to previous notifications regarding GST compliance periods. Specifically, it modifies notification No. 35/2017 and notification No. 16/2018. The key change is the extension of the compliance period from 'July, 2017 to November, 2018' to 'July, 2017 to February, 2019'. Additionally, the deadline for compliance has been pushed back from December 31, 2018, to March 31, 2019. This amendment is made under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, following recommendations from the GST Council. The last amendment to these notifications was made by notification No. 46/2018-Central Tax on September 10, 2018. This extension will benefit businesses that need more time to comply with the GST requirements for the specified periods.
key_points: This notification is issued on December 31, 2018.
It amends notification No. 35/2017-Central Tax dated September 15, 2017.
It also amends notification No. 16/2018-Central Tax dated March 23, 2018.
The amendment changes the period from 'July, 2017 to November, 2018' to 'July, 2017 to February, 2019'.
The deadline for compliance is extended from 'December 31, 2018' to 'March 31, 2019'.
The amendments are made under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
