The sections that will come into force include sections 4 to 13, 16 to 19, 21, and 23 to 25. This means that from this date, the rules and regulations outlined in these sections will be applicable for businesses and taxpayers. The notification is a crucial step in implementing the GST framework, as it lays down the foundational provisions that govern the integrated tax system in India. Businesses should be aware that these sections will dictate how integrated tax is applied, impacting their compliance and reporting obligations under the new GST regime. This notification is part of the government's broader effort to streamline taxation and improve the ease of doing business in India.
key_points: The notification is dated 28th June 2017.
It appoints 1st July 2017 as the effective date for certain provisions.
Sections 4 to 13, 16 to 19, 21, and 23 to 25 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 will come into force.
This notification is issued under the authority of sub-section (3) of section 1 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

996.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 14/2017-Central Tax, which was originally published on 01 July 2017. This corrigendum, dated 29 July 2019, corrects a specific phrase in the English version of the notification. The phrase 'the Central Board of Excise and Customs' has been amended to read 'the Government'. This change appears on page 2, line 3 of the original notification. The correction clarifies the authority responsible for the provisions outlined in the original notification. While this may seem like a minor change, it is important for stakeholders to note the correct reference to ensure accurate understanding and compliance with the GST regulations as intended by the government. This adjustment does not alter any substantive provisions of the original notification but ensures that the language accurately reflects the government's role.
key_points: This is a corrigendum to Notification No. 14/2017-Central Tax dated 01 July 2017.
The corrigendum was issued on 29 July 2019.
In the English version of the original notification, the phrase 'the Central Board of Excise and Customs' is corrected to 'the Government'.
This change is found on page 2, line 3 of the original notification.

997.summary: Notification No. 4/2017-Central Tax, issued on 19th June 2017, establishes www.gst.gov.in as the official Common Goods and Services Tax Electronic Portal. This portal is crucial for various GST-related activities, including registration, payment of taxes, filing returns, computing integrated tax, and managing electronic way bills. The notification is grounded in the powers granted by section 146 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and section 20 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. It is important for businesses and individuals involved in GST compliance, as they will use this portal for essential functions related to their tax obligations. The portal will officially be operational starting from 22nd June 2017, providing a centralized platform for all GST-related processes. This change aims to streamline and simplify the GST compliance process for taxpayers across India.
key_points: Notification No. 4/2017-Central Tax was issued on 19th June 2017.
The notification designates www.gst.gov.in as the Common Goods and Services Tax Electronic Portal.
This portal facilitates registration, tax payment, return filing, integrated tax computation, and electronic way bill management.
The notification is issued under section 146 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 and section 20 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The notification will come into effect on 22nd June 2017.

998.summary: Notification No. 4/2017-Integrated Tax, issued on 28th June 2017, establishes the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. These rules are significant as they are retroactively effective from 22nd June 2017. The notification clarifies that the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 will also apply to integrated tax, ensuring consistency in the application of tax regulations. This means that businesses dealing with integrated tax must adhere to the same rules that govern central tax, as outlined in section 20 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This alignment is intended to streamline compliance and reduce confusion among taxpayers. Overall, this notification is crucial for businesses engaged in interstate trade, as it provides a clear framework for the application of integrated tax rules alongside existing central tax regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 4/2017-Integrated Tax was issued on 28th June 2017.
The rules are called the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
These rules are deemed to have come into force on 22nd June 2017.
The rules apply the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 to integrated tax.
The application is in relation to section 20 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

999.summary: Notification No. 5/2017-Central Tax, issued on 19th June 2017, introduces an important exemption for certain suppliers under the GST framework. Specifically, it identifies individuals or entities that are engaged solely in supplying taxable goods or services, where the tax is to be paid on a reverse charge basis by the recipient. These suppliers are now exempt from the requirement to obtain GST registration, which simplifies compliance for them. This exemption is grounded in sub-section (3) of section 9 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and is effective from 22nd June 2017. The notification aims to ease the burden on small suppliers who may not need to register for GST if their supplies fall under this category. This change is significant for those businesses that primarily operate under a reverse charge mechanism, allowing them to focus on their operations without the additional administrative requirement of registration.
key_points: Notification No. 5/2017-Central Tax issued on 19th June 2017.
This notification specifies persons engaged in making supplies of taxable goods or services liable to reverse charge.
These specified persons are exempt from obtaining GST registration.
The notification comes into force on 22nd June 2017.
The exemption applies under sub-section (3) of section 9 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The exemption is based on the total tax being paid on reverse charge basis by the recipient.
This notification is issued under the powers conferred by sub-section (2) of section 23 of the GST Act.

1000.summary: Notification No. 5/2017-Integrated Tax, issued on 28th June 2017, introduces specific requirements for mentioning Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes on tax invoices based on the annual turnover of businesses. Effective from 1st July 2017, businesses with an annual turnover of up to ₹1.5 crore are exempt from mentioning HSN codes on their invoices. For those with an annual turnover exceeding ₹1.5 crore but not exceeding ₹5 crore, they are required to include 2 digits of the HSN code. Furthermore, businesses with an annual turnover surpassing ₹5 crore must include 4 digits of the HSN code. This change aims to standardize the invoicing process and enhance clarity in tax reporting, impacting how businesses prepare their invoices based on their turnover thresholds.
key_points: Notification No. 5/2017-Integrated Tax was issued on 28th June 2017.
This notification specifies HSN code requirements for tax invoices based on annual turnover.
Businesses with an annual turnover of up to ₹1.5 crore do not need to mention HSN codes.
Businesses with an annual turnover of more than ₹1.5 crore and up to ₹5 crore must mention 2 digits of HSN codes.
Businesses with an annual turnover of more than ₹5 crore must mention 4 digits of HSN codes.
The notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1001.summary: Notification No. 6/2017-Central Tax, issued on June 19, 2017, introduces new verification methods for GST compliance. Specifically, it allows for Aadhaar based Electronic Verification Code (EVC) and Bank account based One Time Password (OTP) as valid modes of verification under rule 26 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This means that businesses can now authenticate their documents using these electronic methods, which is expected to streamline the verification process. Importantly, any verification done through these methods must be completed within two days of submitting the relevant documents. This notification will take effect from June 22, 2017, making it essential for businesses to be aware of these new procedures as they prepare their GST filings. Overall, this change aims to enhance the efficiency and security of the verification process in the GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 6/2017-Central Tax was issued on June 19, 2017.
The notification introduces two modes of verification under rule 26 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The modes of verification are Aadhaar based Electronic Verification Code (EVC) and Bank account based One Time Password (OTP).
Verification using these modes must be completed within two days of submitting the documents.
This notification will come into effect on June 22, 2017.

1002.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 6/2017-Integrated Tax on 28th June 2017, which establishes specific interest rates applicable under various sections of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax (IGST) Act, 2017. The interest rate is set at 18% per annum for Section 20 of the IGST Act when read with subsection (1) of Section 50 of the Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) Act. For subsection (3) of Section 50, the interest rate increases to 24%. Additionally, both subsection (12) of Section 54 and Section 56 of the CGST Act will have an interest rate of 6%. A slightly higher rate of 9% applies when Section 20 of the IGST Act is read with the proviso to Section 56 of the CGST Act. This notification is significant for businesses as it clarifies the interest rates that will apply for various scenarios under the GST framework, and it will take effect from 1st July 2017.
key_points: Notification No. 6/2017-Integrated Tax issued on 28th June 2017.
The notification sets interest rates for various sections of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Interest rate of 18% per annum for Section 20 of the IGST Act read with subsection (1) of Section 50 of the CGST Act.
Interest rate of 24% per annum for Section 20 of the IGST Act read with subsection (3) of Section 50 of the CGST Act.
Interest rate of 6% per annum for Section 20 of the IGST Act read with subsection (12) of Section 54 of the CGST Act.
Interest rate of 6% per annum for Section 20 of the IGST Act read with Section 56 of the CGST Act.
Interest rate of 9% per annum for Section 20 of the IGST Act read with the proviso to Section 56 of the CGST Act.
This notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1003.summary: The Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Rules, 2017, which came into effect on June 22, 2017, introduce several important changes to the GST rules. Notably, rule 10(4) now allows for documents to be 'duly signed or verified through electronic verification code' instead of just being 'digitally signed'. Additionally, rule 19(1) has been updated to clarify references to sub-rule (2) of rule 8. The amendments also strengthen compliance by modifying rule 21 to include violations related to issuing invoices without actual supply of goods or services. Furthermore, rule 24 has been revised to remove the second proviso and add a new sub-rule (3A), which states that if a registration certificate is not provided within 15 days, it will be deemed granted. Changes in Form GST CMP-04 clarify the categories of registered persons eligible for composition levy, while Form GST REG-12 extends the timeframe for certain actions from 30 days to 90 days. These amendments aim to streamline processes and enhance clarity for businesses operating under the GST framework.
key_points: The amendment is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Amendment) Rules, 2017.
The rules came into effect on June 22, 2017.
In rule 10(4), 'digitally signed' is replaced with 'duly signed or verified through electronic verification code'.
In rule 19(1), the second proviso now refers to 'sub-rule (2) of rule 8'.
In rule 21, clause (b) is amended to include violations related to issuing invoices without supply of goods or services.
In rule 24(1), the second proviso is omitted, and a new sub-rule (3A) is added regarding registration certificates.
In Form GST CMP-04, serial number 5 is updated to clarify categories of registered persons eligible for composition levy.
In Form GST REG-12, the timeframe for certain actions is extended from 30 days to 90 days.

1004.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 07/2017-Integrated Tax, which provides an important update regarding job workers involved in inter-State supply of services. Specifically, job workers who supply services to registered persons are now exempt from the requirement to obtain registration under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This exemption is based on section 20 of the Act. However, there are specific exclusions to this exemption. Job workers who are required to register under section 22(1) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, or those who choose to register voluntarily under section 25(3), will not benefit from this exemption. Additionally, job workers providing services related to certain goods specified in rule 138 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules are also excluded. This notification aims to simplify compliance for many job workers while ensuring that those who meet specific criteria still adhere to registration requirements.
key_points: Job workers engaged in making inter-State supply of services to a registered person are exempted from obtaining registration under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
This exemption is provided under section 20 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The exemption does not apply to job workers who are liable to be registered under section 22(1) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Job workers who voluntarily opt for registration under section 25(3) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, are also excluded from this exemption.

The exemption does not apply to job workers involved in supplying services related to goods listed against serial number 151 in the Annexure to rule 138 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

1005.summary: Notification No. 08/2017-Central Tax, issued on June 27, 2017, introduces a composition scheme for eligible registered persons whose aggregate turnover in the previous financial year does not exceed seventy-five lakh rupees. Under this scheme, manufacturers can pay one percent of their turnover in the State as central tax, while those engaged in specific supplies can pay two and a half percent, and other suppliers can pay half percent. Notably, for certain states like Arunachal Pradesh and Assam, the turnover threshold is lowered to fifty lakh rupees. However, manufacturers of specific goods such as ice cream, pan masala, and all tobacco products are not eligible for this composition scheme. This notification is issued under section 10 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and aims to simplify tax compliance for small businesses while ensuring that certain regulated products remain under standard tax provisions.
key_points: Eligible registered persons with aggregate turnover not exceeding seventy five lakh rupees can opt for a composition scheme.
Manufacturers can pay one percent of their turnover in State as central tax under this scheme.
Persons making specific supplies can pay two and a half percent of their turnover in State.
Other suppliers can pay half percent of their turnover in State.
For certain states, the aggregate turnover threshold is reduced to fifty lakh rupees.
Manufacturers of specific goods listed in the table are ineligible for the composition scheme.
The specified goods include ice cream, pan masala, and all tobacco products.
The notification is issued under the powers of section 10 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

1006.summary: Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax, issued on September 14, 2017, specifies that individuals making inter-State taxable supplies of handicraft goods are exempt from obtaining registration under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, provided their total supplies do not exceed twenty lakh rupees in a financial year. For suppliers in Special Category States, this limit is reduced to ten lakh rupees. While registration is not required, these suppliers must still obtain a Permanent Account Number (PAN) and generate an e-way bill according to rule 138 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. The notification defines handicraft goods as those predominantly made by hand, even if some machinery is involved, and includes a comprehensive table listing specific products along with their Harmonized System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes. This change primarily benefits small-scale artisans and craftsmen engaged in the handicraft sector, allowing them to operate without the burden of registration while still adhering to necessary compliance measures.
key_points: Persons making inter-State taxable supplies of handicraft goods are exempt from obtaining registration under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The exemption applies if the aggregate value of such supplies does not exceed twenty lakh rupees in a financial year.
For Special Category States, the exemption limit is set at ten lakh rupees.
Suppliers must obtain a Permanent Account Number (PAN) and generate an e-way bill as per rule 138 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
Handicraft goods are defined as products made predominantly by hand, even if some machinery is used.
A detailed table lists specific handicraft products and their corresponding Harmonized System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes.
The notification was issued on September 14, 2017, under Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax.

1007.summary: Notification No. 09/2017-Central Tax, issued on 28th June 2017, establishes that 1st July 2017 will be the effective date for several provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This includes the implementation of sections 6 to 9, 11 to 21, and 31 to 41, among others. Notably, sections 42 and 43 will come into force, but the provisos to sub-sections (9) of both sections are excluded from this notification. This means that businesses and individuals involved in taxable activities will need to comply with these provisions starting from the appointed date. The notification is a significant step in the rollout of GST in India, as it marks the beginning of the operational framework for various aspects of the tax system. The Ministry of Finance, through the Central Board of Excise and Customs, is responsible for this announcement, which is crucial for ensuring that stakeholders are aware of the new regulations that will govern their tax obligations.
key_points: The notification is dated 28th June 2017.
It appoints 1st July 2017 as the date when specific provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 come into force.
Sections 6 to 9, 11 to 21, 31 to 41, 42 (except the proviso to sub-section (9)), 43 (except the proviso to sub-section (9)), 44 to 50, 53 to 138, 140 to 145, and 147 to 163, 165 to 174 are included.
The notification is issued by the Ministry of Finance, Department of Revenue, Central Board of Excise and Customs.

1008.summary: Notification No. 09/2017-Integrated Tax, issued on 13th October 2017, brings important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax from 14th September 2017. Specifically, it updates the description for serial number 9 in the Table, now including 'Textile (handloom products), Handmade shawls, stoles and scarves' along with specific chapters 50, 58, 61, 62, 63. Additionally, the notification introduces new entries after serial number 28, which cover various handmade products such as 'Chain stitch', 'Crewel, namda, gabba', 'Wicker willow products', 'Toran', and 'Articles made of shola', all categorized under 'Any chapter'. These changes are significant for businesses dealing in these specific textile and handmade goods, as they clarify the classifications and potentially impact tax treatment. Business owners in the textile sector should review these updates to ensure they align their product classifications accordingly.
key_points: This notification is Notification No. 09/2017-Integrated Tax, issued on 13th October 2017.
It amends Notification No. 8/2017-Integrated Tax, dated 14th September 2017.
In the Table of Notification No. 8/2017, serial number 9 is updated to include 'Textile (handloom products), Handmade shawls, stoles and scarves Including 50, 58, 61, 62, 63'.

New entries are added after serial number 28, including: '29 Chain stitch Any chapter', '30 Crewel, namda, gabba Any chapter', '31 Wicker willow products Any chapter', '32 Toran Any chapter', and '33 Articles made of shola Any chapter'.

1009.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 10/2017-Central Tax on June 28, 2017, which introduces significant amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, effective from July 1, 2017. This notification adds new rules, specifically rules 27 to 35, which provide detailed guidelines on how to determine the value of supply for goods and services. Rule 27 outlines the valuation process when the consideration is not entirely in money, emphasizing the use of open market value or alternative methods if that is unavailable. Rule 28 focuses on transactions between distinct or related persons, allowing for valuation based on open market value or similar goods. Rule 29 deals with the relationship between a principal and an agent, offering options for valuation based on market prices or a percentage of the agent's charges. Additionally, rules 30 and 31 introduce methods for determining value based on cost and a residual method. Finally, rule 35 clarifies how to calculate the tax amount when the value of supply includes integrated tax, central tax, state tax, or union territory tax. These changes will affect businesses engaged in the supply of goods and services, particularly those dealing with complex transactions involving non-monetary considerations.
key_points: Notification No. 10/2017-Central Tax was issued on June 28, 2017.
The rules come into effect from July 1, 2017.
New rules 27 to 35 have been added to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
Rule 27 defines the value of supply when consideration is not wholly in money, detailing methods for valuation.
Rule 28 specifies the value of supply between distinct or related persons, with options for valuation based on open market value or similar goods.
Rule 29 addresses the value of supply between a principal and an agent, allowing for open market value or a percentage of the price charged by the agent.
Rules 30 and 31 provide methods for determining value based on cost and a residual method, respectively.
Rule 35 outlines how to determine the tax amount when the value of supply includes various taxes.

1010.summary: Notification No. 10/2017-Integrated Tax introduces specific exemptions for individuals making inter-State supplies of taxable services. It states that those with an aggregate turnover of up to twenty lakh rupees in a financial year are exempt from obtaining GST registration. This is significant for small service providers who may not have the resources to manage GST compliance. Additionally, for special category states, which are defined in the Constitution, the threshold for exemption is lowered to ten lakh rupees. This means that businesses in these states can benefit from reduced compliance burdens. However, Jammu and Kashmir is excluded from this special category consideration. Overall, this notification aims to ease the regulatory requirements for small service providers, allowing them to operate without the complexities of GST registration, thereby promoting business growth in these sectors.
key_points: Specifies persons making inter-State supplies of taxable services.
Exemption applies to those with an aggregate turnover not exceeding twenty lakh rupees in a financial year.
For special category states, the turnover limit is reduced to ten lakh rupees.
Special category states are defined in sub-clause (g) of clause (4) of article 279A of the Constitution, excluding Jammu and Kashmir.

1011.summary: Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax, issued on 28th June 2017, brings an important amendment to Notification No. 6/2017-Central Tax, which was originally published on 19th June 2017. The key change is in the electronic verification process for GST filings. Specifically, clause (ii) has been updated to allow the electronic verification code to be generated through net banking login on the common portal, enhancing the convenience for users. Furthermore, a new clause (iii) has been added, which allows for the generation of the electronic verification code directly on the common portal. This amendment is effective from 22nd June 2017, meaning that businesses and taxpayers who file GST returns will now have more flexible options for generating their electronic verification codes. This change aims to streamline the filing process and improve user experience on the GST portal.
key_points: This notification is issued as Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax.
It amends Notification No. 6/2017-Central Tax dated 19th June 2017.
The amendment substitutes clause (ii) of the earlier notification.
The new clause (ii) specifies that the electronic verification code can be generated through net banking login on the common portal.
Additionally, the new clause (iii) states that an electronic verification code can also be generated directly on the common portal.
This amendment is effective from 22nd June 2017.

1012.summary: Notification No. 11/2017-Integrated Tax, issued on 13th October 2017, outlines the authority of officers appointed under the State and Union Territory GST Acts to act as proper officers for sanctioning refunds. Specifically, these officers can handle refund applications under section 20 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax (IGST) Act, in conjunction with sections 54 and 55 of the Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) Act. However, it is important to note that rule 96 of the CGST Rules is excluded from this provision. This change directly affects registered persons who are located within the territorial jurisdiction of these officers, allowing them to apply for refunds more efficiently. By designating these officers as proper authorities, the government aims to streamline the refund process, ensuring that taxpayers can receive their refunds in a timely manner. This notification is part of the broader framework of the GST regime, which seeks to simplify tax administration and enhance compliance for businesses.
key_points: Notification No. 11/2017-Integrated Tax was issued on 13th October 2017.
This notification specifies that officers appointed under the State and Union Territory GST Acts can act as proper officers for refund sanctions.
The relevant sections for refund sanctions are section 20 of the IGST Act and sections 54 and 55 of the CGST Act.
The notification excludes rule 96 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 from this provision.
This applies to registered persons located within the territorial jurisdiction of the specified officers.

1013.summary: Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax, issued on 28th June 2017, introduces specific requirements for mentioning Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes on tax invoices based on the annual turnover of businesses. For businesses with an annual turnover of up to ₹1.5 crore, there is no requirement to mention HSN codes. However, those with an annual turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore are required to include 2 digits of HSN codes in their invoices. For businesses exceeding an annual turnover of ₹5 crore, the requirement increases to 4 digits of HSN codes. This notification aims to standardize the invoicing process and enhance compliance among registered persons. The new rules will take effect from 1st July 2017, impacting how businesses prepare their tax invoices moving forward. This change is significant for businesses as it aligns with the GST framework and helps in better classification of goods and services.
key_points: Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax issued on 28th June 2017.
This notification specifies HSN code requirements for tax invoices based on annual turnover.
Businesses with an annual turnover up to ₹1.5 crore do not need to mention HSN codes.
Businesses with an annual turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore must mention 2 digits of HSN codes.
Businesses with an annual turnover exceeding ₹5 crore must mention 4 digits of HSN codes.
The notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1014.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 12/2017-Integrated Tax on 15th November 2017, which amends the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. These rules are effective from 1st July 2017. A significant change is the introduction of a new rule that specifies how to determine the value of advertisement services provided to government entities when there is no contract in place. For instance, the value for newspaper advertisements is based on the payment for specific editions published in various states. Similarly, for printed materials like pamphlets, the value is determined by the distribution ratio across states. The rules also clarify that for hoardings, the value is based on their location, while for advertisements on trains, it is calculated according to the length of railway tracks in each state. Additionally, television advertisement values will now be based on viewership figures published by the Broadcast Audience Research Council. This amendment impacts businesses providing advertisement services to government bodies, requiring them to issue separate invoices based on state-wise values.
key_points: Notification No. 12/2017-Integrated Tax was issued on 15th November 2017.
The rules are deemed to have come into force on 1st July 2017.
A new rule has been inserted after rule 2 regarding the proportion of value for advertisement services supplied to government entities.
The value of advertisement services for newspapers is based on the amount payable for publishing in specific editions.
For printed materials, the value is determined by the distribution ratio across states.
Hoarding values are based on the location of the hoardings in different states.
For advertisements on trains, the value is calculated based on the length of railway tracks in each state.
Television advertisement values are based on viewership figures from the Broadcast Audience Research Council.

1015.summary: Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax, issued on 28th June 2017, establishes specific interest rates applicable under various sections of the GST Act. The interest rate for sub-section (1) of section 50 is fixed at 18%, while sub-section (3) of the same section has a higher rate of 24%. For sub-section (12) of section 54 and section 56, the interest rate is set at 6%. Additionally, the proviso to section 56 has an interest rate of 9%. These rates are crucial for businesses and individuals who may be liable for interest under these sections. The notification will take effect from 1st July 2017, meaning that any applicable interest calculations will need to adhere to these new rates from that date. This change is significant for those involved in GST compliance and financial planning, as it directly impacts the cost of delayed payments or refunds under the GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax issued on 28th June 2017.
The notification fixes interest rates for specific sections of the GST Act.
Interest rate for sub-section (1) of section 50 is set at 18%.
Interest rate for sub-section (3) of section 50 is set at 24%.
Interest rate for sub-section (12) of section 54 is set at 6%.
Interest rate for section 56 is set at 6%.
Interest rate for the proviso to section 56 is set at 9%.
This notification comes into force on 1st July 2017.

1016.summary: Notification No. 14/2017-Central Tax, issued on 1st July 2017, establishes the appointment of various officers from the Directorate General of Goods and Services Tax Intelligence, Directorate General of Goods and Services Tax, and Directorate General of Audit as central tax officers. This means that these officers are now empowered to exercise all the necessary powers under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 and the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The notification includes a detailed table that outlines the ranks of these officers and the corresponding powers they hold. This change is significant as it formalizes the authority of these officers across India, ensuring they can effectively carry out their duties related to tax administration. The notification is effective from the same date it was issued, which is 1st July 2017, marking an important step in the governance of GST in India.
key_points: Notification No. 14/2017-Central Tax was issued on 1st July 2017.

The notification appoints officers in the Directorate General of Goods and Services Tax Intelligence, Directorate General of Goods and Services Tax, and Directorate General of Audit as central tax officers.
These officers are granted powers under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 and the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The notification specifies a table listing the ranks of officers and their corresponding powers.
The notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1017.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 15/2017-Central Tax on 1st July 2017, which amends several rules under the Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) framework. This notification introduces the Central Goods and Services Tax (Third Amendment) Rules, 2017, which come into effect immediately. Notably, Rule 44(2) now specifies that 'central tax, State tax, Union territory tax and integrated tax' must be considered, broadening the scope of tax types involved. Additionally, Rule 96(1)(b) has been updated to allow the use of FORM GSTR-3B alongside FORM GSTR-3 for certain filings. A significant addition is Rule 96A, which outlines the process for obtaining refunds of integrated tax paid on exports under a bond or Letter of Undertaking, requiring the submission of FORM GST RFD-11. Furthermore, Rule 117(1) clarifies the definition of 'eligible duties and taxes' related to input tax credit, while Rule 119 replaces 'agent' with 'job-worker' to better reflect the roles involved. Lastly, a new Chapter XVII has been introduced to govern inspection, search, and seizure procedures, establishing new forms such as FORM GST INS-01 and FORM GST INS-02 for these processes.
key_points: Notification No. 15/2017-Central Tax was issued on 1st July 2017.
The rules are called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Third Amendment) Rules, 2017.
Rule 44(2) is amended to include 'central tax, State tax, Union territory tax and integrated tax' instead of just 'integrated tax and central tax'.
Rule 96(1)(b) is amended to include 'or FORM GSTR-3B' after 'FORM GSTR-3'.
A new Rule 96A is added for refund of integrated tax paid on export under bond or Letter of Undertaking, requiring FORM GST RFD-11.
Rule 117(1) is amended to specify 'eligible duties and taxes' in relation to input tax credit.
Rule 119 changes the term 'agent' to 'job-worker'.
New Chapter XVII introduces rules for inspection, search, and seizure, including FORM GST INS-01 and FORM GST INS-02.

1018.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 16/2017-Central Tax, which was originally published on June 7, 2017. This corrigendum, dated July 10, 2017, corrects a specific line in the original notification. Instead of referring to 'paragraph 5', it now correctly refers to 'paragraphs 3.20 and 3.21'. This change is important for clarity and ensures that the correct paragraphs are referenced in the context of the notification. The original notification was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, and the corrigendum is part of the ongoing efforts by the Ministry of Finance to maintain accuracy in tax regulations. The reference number for this corrigendum is F.No 349/74/2017 - GST(Pt), and it is issued by the Department of Revenue. This correction does not introduce new requirements but clarifies existing references, which is essential for compliance by businesses affected by this notification.
key_points: The notification is a corrigendum to Notification No. 16/2017-Central Tax.
The corrigendum was issued on July 10, 2017.
It corrects line 6 of the original notification dated June 7, 2017.
The correction changes 'paragraph 5' to 'paragraphs 3.20 and 3.21'.
The original notification was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i).
The original notification was published under G.S.R. 848(E) on July 7, 2017.
The corrigendum is issued by the Ministry of Finance, Department of Revenue.
The reference number for the corrigendum is F.No 349/74/2017 - GST(Pt).

1019.summary: Notification No. 16/2017-Central Tax, issued on July 7, 2017, outlines new conditions for registered persons wishing to export goods or services without paying integrated tax. Eligible individuals include status holders defined in the Foreign Trade Policy 2015-2020 and those who have received foreign inward remittances of at least 10% of their export turnover, with a minimum of one crore rupees in the previous financial year. Additionally, these individuals must not have faced prosecution for GST-related offences involving tax evasion exceeding 250 lakh rupees. To facilitate this process, a Letter of Undertaking (LUT) must be submitted in duplicate for each financial year, attached to FORM GST RFD-11 as specified in rule 96A of the GST Rules. The LUT must be signed by authorized personnel such as the Managing Director, Company Secretary, or other designated individuals. This notification aims to simplify the export process for eligible businesses by allowing them to use an LUT instead of a bond.
key_points: Introduces conditions for registered persons to supply goods/services for export without paying integrated tax.
Eligible persons include status holders as per the Foreign Trade Policy 2015-2020.
Also eligible are those who received foreign inward remittances of at least 10% of export turnover, minimum one crore rupees, in the previous financial year.
Eligible persons must not have been prosecuted for GST-related offences where tax evasion exceeds 250 lakh rupees.
A Letter of Undertaking (LUT) must be submitted in duplicate for a financial year.
The LUT is to be annexed to FORM GST RFD-11 as per rule 96A of the GST Rules.
The LUT must be executed by specific authorized individuals such as the Managing Director or Company Secretary.
This notification is issued under the powers of sub-rule (5) of rule 96A of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

1020.summary: The Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourth Amendment) Rules, 2017, published on 27th July 2017, introduce several important changes to the GST framework. Notably, rule 24(4) now sets a new deadline of 30th September 2017 for certain actions, replacing the previous thirty-day timeframe. Rule 34 has been revised to clarify how the rate of exchange for taxable goods and services should be determined, aligning it with the Customs Act and accounting principles. Additionally, rule 44 has been updated to ensure that input tax credits are calculated separately for different tax types and allows for estimation based on market prices if invoices are missing. The amendment to rule 46 mandates specific endorsements on export invoices, enhancing clarity for exporters. Furthermore, rule 61(5) now gives the Commissioner the authority to require electronic filing of GSTR-3B under certain conditions. Minor terminology changes have been made in rules 83 and 89, and FORM GST TRAN-1 and TRAN-2 have been updated to reflect 'HSN as applicable' in their headings. These amendments will impact businesses involved in GST compliance, particularly those dealing with exports and input tax credits.
key_points: The notification is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Fourth Amendment) Rules, 2017.
The rules come into force on the date of publication in the Official Gazette, which is 27th July 2017.
In rule 24(4), the deadline for certain actions is changed to 'on or before 30th September 2017' from 'within a period of thirty days from the appointed day'.
Rule 34 is substituted to clarify the rate of exchange for taxable goods and services based on the Customs Act and generally accepted accounting principles.
Rule 44(2) and (3) are substituted to specify separate determination of input tax credit for different types of taxes and to allow estimation based on market price if tax invoices are unavailable.
In rule 46, the third proviso is amended to require specific endorsements on invoices for exports and SEZ supplies.
Rule 61(5) is amended to allow the Commissioner to specify the filing of GSTR-3B electronically under certain circumstances.
Changes are made to rules 83 and 89 regarding terminology, and FORM GST TRAN-1 and TRAN-2 are updated to replace column headings with 'HSN as applicable'.

1021.summary: Notification No. 18/2017-Central Tax, issued on August 8, 2017, provides an extension for filing details of outward supplies in FORM GSTR-1. Specifically, the time frame for July 2017 has been changed to allow businesses to file from September 1 to September 5, 2017. Similarly, for August 2017, the new filing window is set from September 16 to September 20, 2017. This extension is granted under the authority of the second proviso to sub-section (1) of section 37 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This change affects all registered taxpayers who need to submit their GSTR-1 for the specified months, providing them with additional time to ensure accurate reporting of their outward supplies. The notification is effective immediately from the date of issuance.
key_points: Extends the time limit for filing details of outward supplies in FORM GSTR-1.
For July 2017, the new filing period is from 1st to 5th September 2017.
For August 2017, the new filing period is from 16th to 20th September 2017.
This notification is issued under the second proviso to sub-section (1) of section 37 of the CGST Act, 2017.

1022.summary: Notification No. 19/2017-Central Tax, issued on 8th August 2017, extends the deadlines for filing details of inward supplies in FORM GSTR-2. Specifically, for the month of July 2017, businesses now have until 10th September 2017 to submit their details, while for August 2017, the deadline is extended to 25th September 2017. This change is significant for businesses that need more time to compile their inward supply data for these months. The notification is effective immediately from the date of issuance, allowing businesses to adjust their filing schedules accordingly. This extension aims to ease the compliance burden on taxpayers during the initial phases of GST implementation. All businesses required to file GSTR-2 for these months should take note of the new deadlines to ensure they meet the revised timelines.
key_points: Extends the time limit for filing details of inward supplies in FORM GSTR-2.
For July 2017, the new filing period is from 6th to 10th September 2017.
For August 2017, the new filing period is from 21st to 25th September 2017.
This notification is effective from 8th August 2017.

1023.summary: Notification No. 20/2017-Central Tax, issued on 8th August 2017, provides an extension for filing the GSTR-3 return for the months of July and August 2017. Specifically, businesses now have until 15th September 2017 to file their GSTR-3 for July, and until 30th September 2017 for August. This change is made under the authority of section 39(6) and section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Act, 2017, reflecting the government's response to the needs of taxpayers during this period. The extension aims to ease the compliance burden on businesses as they adapt to the new GST framework. All registered taxpayers who are required to file GSTR-3 for these months will benefit from this additional time, allowing them to ensure accurate reporting of their tax liabilities.
key_points: Extends the time limit for filing GSTR-3 for July 2017 to 11th to 15th September 2017.
Extends the time limit for filing GSTR-3 for August 2017 to 26th to 30th September 2017.
This notification is issued under section 39(6) and section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Act, 2017.
The notification is effective from 8th August 2017.

1024.summary: Notification No. 21/2017-Central Tax, issued on 8th August 2017, outlines the due dates for filing the GSTR-3B return for the months of July and August 2017. Specifically, businesses must file their GSTR-3B for July by 20th August 2017 and for August by 20th September 2017. This notification is based on the recommendations from the GST Council and is issued under the authority of sub-rule (5) of rule 61 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. The effective date of this notification is the same as the issuance date, meaning that businesses should take note of these deadlines immediately. This change primarily affects all registered taxpayers who are required to file GSTR-3B returns, ensuring they are aware of the specific filing dates for these two months. Compliance with these deadlines is essential for maintaining good standing under the GST framework.
key_points: The notification specifies the due dates for filing GSTR-3B for July and August 2017.
GSTR-3B for July 2017 is due on 20th August 2017.
GSTR-3B for August 2017 is due on 20th September 2017.
This notification is issued under sub-rule (5) of rule 61 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
It is based on the recommendations of the GST Council.
The notification comes into effect from 8th August 2017.

1025.summary: The Central Government has issued the 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Fifth Amendment) Rules, 2017' to amend several existing rules under the GST framework. Notably, Rule 3(4) has been updated to extend the time period for certain actions from sixty days to ninety days. Additionally, Rule 17(2) now allows for recommendations from the Ministry of External Affairs, which may affect how certain entities operate under GST. Rule 40(1)(b) requires registered persons to declare their eligibility for input tax credit within thirty days using FORM GST ITC-01. A new Rule 44A has been introduced to address the reversal of credit related to Additional duty of Customs on gold dore bars. Furthermore, Rule 61(5) has been revised to clarify the manner and conditions for declarations. The validity of FORM GST PMT-06 challans has been set to fifteen days under Rule 87(2), and Rule 103 now mandates that members of the Authority for Advance Ruling must be at least Joint Commissioners. These changes will impact various stakeholders, including registered persons and government departments, by clarifying processes and extending timelines.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Fifth Amendment) Rules, 2017'.
Rule 3(4) is amended to change the time period from 'sixty days' to 'ninety days'.
Rule 17(2) now includes a provision for recommendations from the Ministry of External Affairs.
Rule 40(1)(b) specifies a thirty-day period for declaring eligibility for input tax credit via FORM GST ITC-01.
A new Rule 44A is added regarding the reversal of credit for Additional duty of Customs on gold dore bars.
Rule 61(5) is amended to specify the manner and conditions for certain declarations.
Rule 87(2) introduces a fifteen-day validity for FORM GST PMT-06 challans.
Rule 103 is updated to state that officers appointed as members of the Authority for Advance Ruling must be at least Joint Commissioners.

1026.summary: Notification No. 23/2017-Central Tax, issued on 17th August 2017, outlines the conditions for registered persons to file their GSTR-3B returns for July 2017. It builds upon the earlier Notification No. 21/2017-Central Tax. Registered persons who are entitled to input tax credit but choose not to file FORM GST TRAN-1 must submit their GSTR-3B by 20th August 2017. Conversely, those opting to file FORM GST TRAN-1 must complete their GSTR-3B by 28th August 2017. For those filing by the latter date, it is required to compute and deposit the tax payable by 20th August. If the tax calculated in GSTR-3B exceeds the amount deposited, the excess must be paid by 28th August along with any applicable interest. This notification is effective immediately upon publication in the Official Gazette, impacting all registered persons required to file GSTR-3B for the specified month.
key_points: This notification is issued under Notification No. 21/2017-Central Tax dated 08th August 2017.
It specifies the conditions for filing GSTR-3B for July 2017.
Registered persons not filing FORM GST TRAN-1 must file GSTR-3B by 20th August 2017.
Registered persons opting to file FORM GST TRAN-1 must file GSTR-3B by 28th August 2017.
Those filing GSTR-3B by 28th August must compute and deposit tax payable by 20th August.
Excess tax payable must be deposited by 28th August along with applicable interest.
The notification comes into effect from the date of publication in the Official Gazette.

1027.summary: Notification No. 24/2017-Central Tax, issued on 21st August 2017, amends the earlier Notification No. 23/2017-Central Tax dated 17th August 2017. The key changes involve extending several deadlines. Specifically, in Sl. No. 1, column (3), the date has been moved from 20th August 2017 to 25th August 2017. Similarly, in Sl. No. 2, column (4), item (i) also sees a date change from 20th August 2017 to 25th August 2017, while item (iii) changes from 21st August 2017 to 26th August 2017. Additionally, in Sl. No. 3, column (3), the date is again extended from 20th August 2017 to 25th August 2017. Furthermore, a new phrase 'on or before 25th August 2017' has been added to paragraph 2 regarding the electronic credit ledger. These amendments provide additional time for compliance related to the electronic credit ledger and are effective immediately upon publication in the Official Gazette.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 23/2017-Central Tax dated 17th August 2017.
In Sl. No. 1, column (3), the date is changed from '20th August 2017' to '25th August 2017'.
In Sl. No. 2, column (4), item (i), the date is changed from '20th August 2017' to '25th August 2017'.
In Sl. No. 2, column (4), item (iii), the date is changed from '21st August 2017' to '26th August 2017'.
In Sl. No. 3, column (3), the date is changed from '20th August 2017' to '25th August 2017'.
In paragraph 2, the phrase 'on or before 25th August 2017' is added before the Explanation regarding the electronic credit ledger.
This notification is effective from the date of publication in the Official Gazette.

1028.summary: Notification No. 25/2017-Central Tax, issued on August 28, 2017, extends the deadline for filing GST returns for the month of July 2017. This extension specifically targets businesses that provide online information and database access services from outside India to non-taxable online recipients. The new deadline for these businesses to submit their returns is now set for September 15, 2017. This change is made under the authority of section 39(6) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The notification aims to provide relief to affected service providers by allowing them additional time to comply with the filing requirements. It is important for these businesses to note the new deadline and ensure that they file their returns accordingly. The notification will take effect from the date it is published in the Official Gazette.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing GST returns for July 2017.
Applies specifically to suppliers of online information and database access services from outside India.
New deadline for filing is now 15th September 2017.
This extension is granted under section 39(6) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

1029.summary: Notification No. 26/2017-Central Tax, issued on 28th August 2017, extends the deadlines for Input Service Distributors to file their returns in FORM GSTR-6. Specifically, the deadline for the month of July 2017 has been moved to 8th September 2017, while the deadline for August 2017 has been set to 23rd September 2017. This change is made under the authority of section 39(6) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, which allows for such extensions. The notification aims to provide relief to Input Service Distributors by giving them additional time to comply with their filing obligations. It is important for affected businesses to take note of these new deadlines to ensure they file their returns on time. The notification will take effect from the date it is published in the Official Gazette.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing GSTR-6 for Input Service Distributors.
For July 2017, the new deadline is 8th September 2017.
For August 2017, the new deadline is 23rd September 2017.
This extension is granted under section 39(6) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

1030.summary: Notification No. 27/2017-Central Tax, issued on 30th August 2017, introduces significant amendments to Rule 138 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. The key change mandates that an e-way bill must be generated for the movement of goods valued over fifty thousand rupees, which can be done electronically using FORM GST EWB-01 on the common portal. This rule also requires transporters to update the details of conveyance when transferring goods between vehicles. The validity of the e-way bill is set at one day for distances up to 100 km, with an additional day granted for every subsequent 100 km. Additionally, the notification specifies certain goods that are exempt from the e-way bill requirement, as detailed in the Annexure. Importantly, the e-way bill generated under this rule is valid across all States and Union territories, ensuring a streamlined process for businesses involved in the transportation of goods.
key_points: Notification No. 27/2017-Central Tax was issued on 30th August 2017.
This notification amends Rule 138 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The e-way bill must be generated for goods with a consignment value exceeding fifty thousand rupees.
The e-way bill can be generated in FORM GST EWB-01 electronically on the common portal.
Transporters must update details of conveyance in the e-way bill if transferring goods between conveyances.
The validity period of the e-way bill is one day for distances up to 100 km, with one additional day for every 100 km thereafter.
Certain goods are exempt from e-way bill requirements as specified in the Annexure.
The e-way bill generated is valid across all States and Union territories.

1031.summary: Notification No. 28/2017-Central Tax, issued on September 1, 2017, provides a significant relief for registered persons who failed to file their GSTR-3B returns for July 2017 by the due date. Specifically, the notification waives the late fee that would typically be charged under section 47 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. This waiver applies to all registered taxpayers who missed the deadline for submitting their returns for that month. The decision was made by the Central Government based on recommendations from the GST Council, highlighting a responsive approach to the challenges faced by businesses during that period. By alleviating the financial burden of late fees, the government aims to encourage compliance and support taxpayers in fulfilling their obligations without the added stress of penalties. This move is particularly beneficial for those who may have encountered difficulties in meeting the filing requirements during July 2017.
key_points: Waives late fee under section 47 of the CGST Act for GSTR-3B returns.
Applies to all registered persons who did not file GSTR-3B for July 2017.
The due date for filing GSTR-3B for July 2017 was not met by some taxpayers.
This notification is issued under the powers of section 128 of the CGST Act.

1032.summary: Notification No. 29/2017-Central Tax, issued on 5th September 2017, extends the deadlines for filing various GST returns for the months of July and August 2017. Specifically, the due date for FORM GSTR-1 for July is now 10th September 2017, while FORM GSTR-2 can be filed between 11th and 25th September 2017, and FORM GSTR-3 is due by 30th September 2017. For August 2017, the due date for FORM GSTR-1 has been extended to 5th October 2017, FORM GSTR-2 can be filed from 6th to 10th October 2017, and FORM GSTR-3 is now due by 15th October 2017. This notification supersedes the earlier notifications No. 18/2017, 19/2017, and 20/2017, which were issued on 8th August 2017. This extension affects all registered taxpayers who need to file these specific returns, providing them additional time to comply with the GST requirements for the specified months.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing FORM GSTR-1 for July 2017 to 10th September 2017.
Extends the due date for filing FORM GSTR-2 for July 2017 to 11th-25th September 2017.
Extends the due date for filing FORM GSTR-3 for July 2017 to 30th September 2017.
Extends the due date for filing FORM GSTR-1 for August 2017 to 5th October 2017.
Extends the due date for filing FORM GSTR-2 for August 2017 to 6th-10th October 2017.
Extends the due date for filing FORM GSTR-3 for August 2017 to 15th October 2017.
Supersedes earlier notifications No. 18/2017, 19/2017, and 20/2017 issued on 8th August 2017.

1033.summary: Notification No. 31/2017-Central Tax, issued on 11th September 2017, brings an important update for Input Service Distributors (ISDs) regarding their return filing deadlines. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 26/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on 28th August 2017. The key change is the extension of the deadline for ISDs to file their returns for July 2017, now due by 13th October 2017. This applies specifically to the returns required under sub-section (4) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, as outlined in rule 65 of the GST Rules. Additionally, the notification indicates that the deadline for filing returns for August 2017 will be communicated in a future Official Gazette announcement. This extension provides ISDs with additional time to ensure their compliance for the specified month, allowing them to manage their filing obligations more effectively.
key_points: Notification No. 31/2017-Central Tax was issued on 11th September 2017.
This notification supersedes Notification No. 26/2017-Central Tax dated 28th August 2017.
The time limit for Input Service Distributors to file returns for July 2017 is extended to 13th October 2017.
This extension applies specifically to the return under sub-section (4) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act.
The relevant rule for this return is rule 65 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The extension for the month of August 2017 will be notified later in the Official Gazette.

1034.summary: Notification No. 32/2017-Central Tax, issued on September 15, 2017, provides an exemption from GST registration for casual taxable persons who supply handicraft goods. This exemption is available if their total taxable supplies do not exceed ₹20 lakh in a financial year across India, or ₹10 lakh for Special Category States, excluding Jammu and Kashmir. To qualify, these individuals must obtain a Permanent Account Number (PAN) and generate an e-way bill as required by rule 138 of the GST Rules. The exemption specifically applies to inter-State supplies of handicraft goods, which are defined in the notification with a detailed list of products and their corresponding HSN codes. This change is significant for artisans and small businesses engaged in the handicraft sector, as it reduces the compliance burden and encourages them to operate without the need for formal registration, provided they meet the specified thresholds.
key_points: Casual taxable persons making taxable supplies of handicraft goods are exempt from obtaining GST registration.
The exemption applies if the aggregate value of supplies does not exceed ₹20 lakh in a financial year for all India.
For Special Category States (excluding Jammu and Kashmir), the threshold is ₹10 lakh.
These persons must obtain a Permanent Account Number (PAN) and generate an e-way bill as per rule 138 of the GST Rules.
The exemption is applicable for inter-State taxable supplies of handicraft goods.
Handicraft goods are defined by specific products listed in a table with corresponding HSN codes.
The notification is issued under the powers of section 23(2) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

1035.summary: Notification No. 33/2017-Central Tax, issued on 15th September 2017, establishes that certain provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 will come into effect on 18th September 2017. This specifically pertains to section 51, which outlines the tax deduction responsibilities of certain entities. The notification identifies that authorities or boards created by an Act of Parliament or State Legislature, public sector undertakings, and societies formed by the Central or State Government or Local Authorities under the Societies Registration Act, 1860 are included. These entities will be required to deduct tax from payments made to suppliers of taxable goods or services. However, the exact date when this tax deduction obligation will commence will be announced later by the Central Government. This change is significant for the specified entities as it formalizes their role in the GST framework, ensuring compliance with tax deduction requirements.
key_points: The notification is dated 15th September 2017.
It appoints 18th September 2017 as the date when certain provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 come into force.
Specifically, it relates to sub-section (1) of section 51 of the Act.
The provisions apply to authorities or boards set up by an Act of Parliament or State Legislature, and public sector undertakings.
It includes societies established by the Central or State Government or Local Authorities under the Societies Registration Act, 1860.
These entities will be required to deduct tax from payments made to suppliers of taxable goods or services.
The exact date for when the tax deduction obligation will start will be notified later by the Central Government.
This notification is issued under the powers conferred by sub-section (3) of section 1 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

1036.summary: The Notification No. 34/2017-Central Tax introduces several amendments to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017, effective from various dates. Notably, it allows individuals with provisional registration to opt for tax payment under section 10 starting October 1, 2017, by submitting FORM GST CMP-02. Additionally, registered persons can now revise their FORM GST TRAN-1 declaration once, as per the newly added rule 120A. The amendments also stipulate that Commissioners of State tax or central tax must have served for at least one year. Changes in rule 124 clarify the salary structure for Technical Members, including provisions for retired officers. Furthermore, rule 127 now mandates that a performance report be submitted to the Council quarterly. The notification also introduces new rules regarding e-way bill generation for goods sent to job-workers and handicraft items. Lastly, FORM GST TRAN-1 has been updated to include new sections for tax credits, and FORM GSTR-4 has changed the terminology from 'Intra-State Supplies' to 'Inter-State Supplies'. These changes affect various stakeholders, including registered taxpayers and officials involved in GST compliance and administration.
key_points: Introduces sub-rule (3A) in rule 3 allowing provisional registrants to opt for tax payment under section 10 from October 1, 2017, by filing FORM GST CMP-02.
Inserts rule 120A allowing registered persons to revise their FORM GST TRAN-1 declaration once within specified timeframes.
Amends rule 122 to require Commissioners of State tax or central tax to have served for at least one year.
Substitutes sub-rule (3) in rule 124 regarding the salary of the Technical Member, including provisions for retired officers.
Adds new clauses in rule 127 requiring a performance report to be submitted to the Council quarterly.
Inserts new provisos in rule 138 regarding e-way bill generation for goods sent to job-workers and handicraft goods.
Amends FORM GST TRAN-1 to include additional sections and instructions for availing tax credits.
Changes entry 8B(2) in FORM GSTR-4 from 'Intra-State Supplies' to 'Inter-State Supplies'.

1037.summary: Notification No. 35/2017-Central Tax, issued on September 15, 2017, establishes the requirement for registered persons to file their GSTR-3B returns electronically for the months of August through December 2017. The last dates for these filings are set as follows: August 2017 must be filed by September 20, 2017; September 2017 by October 20, 2017; October 2017 by November 20, 2017; November 2017 by December 20, 2017; and December 2017 by January 20, 2018. Additionally, the notification mandates that taxpayers must settle their tax liabilities using their electronic cash or credit ledgers by these deadlines. This requirement is grounded in section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, along with rule 61(5) of the GST Rules. This notification is crucial for ensuring compliance with the filing of returns and payment of taxes during this period.
key_points: Introduces the requirement to file GSTR-3B for the months of August to December 2017.

Specifies the last dates for filing GSTR-3B: August 2017 by 20th September 2017, September 2017 by 20th October 2017, October 2017 by 20th November 2017, November 2017 by 20th December 2017, and December 2017 by 20th January 2018.
Mandates that registered persons must discharge their tax liabilities via electronic cash or credit ledger by the last date specified for each month.
Cites section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 and rule 61(5) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 as the legal basis for this notification.

1038.summary: The Central Government has issued the 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Eighth Amendment) Rules, 2017', which introduces several important changes to the GST rules. Notably, the deadline for a specific compliance requirement has been extended from 30th September to 31st October, providing additional time for businesses. Additionally, several rules (118, 119, and 120) have been amended to clarify that the periods previously referred to as 'ninety days of the appointed day' will now be determined by rule 117 or extended by the Commissioner. This change aims to provide more flexibility in compliance timelines. Furthermore, a new heading has been added to Rule 120A regarding the revision of declarations in FORM GST TRAN - 1. In FORM GST REG - 29, the title has been updated to reflect the cancellation of registration for migrated taxpayers instead of provisional registration, and the term 'Provisional ID' has been replaced with 'GSTIN'. These amendments are significant for businesses navigating the GST framework, ensuring they are aware of the updated timelines and terminologies.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Eighth Amendment) Rules, 2017'.
Rule 24(4) is amended to change the deadline from '30th September' to '31st October'.
Rule 118 is amended to replace 'a period of ninety days of the appointed day' with 'the period specified in rule 117 or such further period as extended by the Commissioner'.
Rule 119 is similarly amended to reflect the same change regarding the period specified in rule 117.
Rule 120 is also amended to replace 'ninety days of the appointed day' with 'the period specified in rule 117 or such further period as extended by the Commissioner'.
A new marginal heading 'Revision of declaration in FORM GST TRAN - 1' is inserted in Rule 120A.
In FORM GST REG - 29, the heading is changed from 'APPLICATION FOR CANCELATION OF PROVISIONAL REGISTRATION' to 'APPLICATION FOR CANCELATION OF REGISTRATION OF MIGRATED TAXPAYERS'.
In FORM GST REG - 29, 'Provisional ID' is replaced with 'GSTIN' under PART - A, item (i).

1039.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 37/2017-Central Tax on 4th October 2017, which replaces the earlier Notification No. 16/2017-Central Tax from 7th July 2017. This new notification allows registered persons to use a Letter of Undertaking instead of a bond when exporting goods or services without paying integrated tax. However, this option is not available to those who have faced prosecution for significant tax evasion exceeding ₹2.5 crore. The Letter of Undertaking must be submitted in duplicate on the registered person's letterhead, following the format specified in FORM GST RFD-11, and must be signed by authorized individuals such as the Managing Director or a working partner. Additionally, if the registered person does not pay the required tax within the specified timeframe, the facility to export without paying integrated tax will be revoked. Importantly, these provisions also extend to zero-rated supplies made to Special Economic Zone developers or units, ensuring they can benefit from this streamlined process.
key_points: Notification No. 37/2017-Central Tax was issued on 4th October 2017.
This notification supersedes Notification No. 16/2017-Central Tax dated 7th July 2017.
Registered persons can now furnish a Letter of Undertaking instead of a bond for exporting goods/services without paying integrated tax.
Eligibility for the Letter of Undertaking excludes those prosecuted for GST-related offences where tax evasion exceeds ₹2.5 crore.
The Letter of Undertaking must be on the registered person's letterhead and submitted in duplicate as per FORM GST RFD-11.
The Letter must be executed by specific authorized individuals such as the Managing Director or a working partner.
If the registered person fails to pay the due tax within the specified period, the export facility without payment of integrated tax will be withdrawn.
The provisions also apply to zero-rated supplies made to Special Economic Zone developers or units.

1040.summary: Notification No. 38/2017-Central Tax, issued on 13th October 2017, brings important amendments to Notification No. 32/2017-Central Tax, which was originally published on 15th September 2017. The key change is in the Table of the earlier notification, where serial number 9 now specifies 'Textile (handloom products), Handmade shawls, stoles and scarves', along with the inclusion of chapters 50, 58, 61, 62, and 63. Additionally, new entries have been introduced after serial number 28, which include various handmade products such as 'Chain stitch', 'Crewel, namda, gabba', 'Wicker willow products', 'Toran', and 'Articles made of shola', all classified under 'Any chapter'. These changes affect manufacturers and sellers of these specific textile and handmade products, ensuring they are correctly classified under the GST framework. This amendment aims to clarify the tax treatment of these goods, facilitating better compliance and understanding for businesses involved in these sectors.
key_points: Notification No. 38/2017-Central Tax was issued on 13th October 2017.
This notification amends Notification No. 32/2017-Central Tax dated 15th September 2017.
In the Table of Notification No. 32/2017, serial number 9 is updated to include 'Textile (handloom products), Handmade shawls, stoles and scarves' with specific chapters 50, 58, 61, 62, 63.

New entries are added after serial number 28 in the Table, including 'Chain stitch', 'Crewel, namda, gabba', 'Wicker willow products', 'Toran', and 'Articles made of shola', all categorized under 'Any chapter'.

1041.summary: Notification No. 39/2017-Central Tax, issued on 13th October 2017, introduces important changes regarding the sanction of refunds under the Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) Act. It allows officers appointed under the respective State Goods and Services Tax (SGST) or Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (UTGST) Acts to act as proper officers for refund applications under sections 54 and 55 of the CGST Act. This means that registered persons seeking refunds can now approach these officers within their territorial jurisdiction for assistance. However, it's important to note that rule 96 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 is not included in this provision, indicating that certain refund processes may still follow different guidelines. This change streamlines the refund process and clarifies the roles of various officers involved, making it easier for businesses to navigate their refund applications under the GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 39/2017-Central Tax was issued on 13th October 2017.
This notification specifies that officers appointed under the State or Union Territory GST Acts can act as proper officers for refund sanctions under the CGST Act.
The relevant sections for refund are section 54 and section 55 of the CGST Act.
These officers will handle refund applications from registered persons within their territorial jurisdiction.
The notification excludes rule 96 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 from this provision.
This change is made under the powers conferred by section 6(1) of the CGST Act.

1042.summary: Notification No. 40/2017-Central Tax, issued on 13th October 2017, outlines the tax obligations for certain registered persons under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. Specifically, it targets those whose aggregate turnover in the previous financial year did not exceed ₹1.5 crore and who have not opted for the composition scheme under section 10. These individuals are required to pay central tax on their outward supply of goods at the time of supply, as detailed in section 12(2)(a) of the Act. Additionally, they must comply with the provisions of section 14, which relates to the time of supply. The notification mandates that these registered persons furnish necessary details and returns as outlined in Chapter IX of the Act. The timeframe for tax payment is also specified within the Act. This notification is crucial for small businesses to understand their tax responsibilities and with the GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 40/2017-Central Tax was issued on 13th October 2017.
It applies to registered persons with an aggregate turnover not exceeding ₹1.5 crore in the preceding financial year.
Registered persons who did not opt for the composition levy under section 10 of the CGST Act are included.
These registered persons must pay central tax on outward supply of goods at the time of supply as per section 12(2)(a) of the CGST Act.
The notification also mentions compliance with provisions of section 14 of the CGST Act.
Affected persons must furnish details and returns as specified in Chapter IX of the CGST Act.
The prescribed period for tax payment is as specified in the CGST Act.

1043.summary: Notification No. 41/2017-Central Tax, issued on 13th October 2017, extends the deadline for composition suppliers to file their GSTR-4 return for the quarter of July to September 2017. The new due date for this filing is now set for 15th November 2017. This change is made under the authority of sub-section (6) of section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and is in accordance with rule 62 of the CGST Rules, 2017. This extension is particularly relevant for businesses that are registered under the composition scheme, allowing them additional time to comply with their tax obligations for the specified quarter. The decision aims to ease the compliance burden on these suppliers, ensuring they have adequate time to prepare their returns accurately. This notification is part of the government's ongoing efforts to facilitate smoother tax processes for small businesses operating under the GST framework.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-4 for composition suppliers.
New due date is 15th November 2017.
Original tax period covered is July to September 2017.
This extension is based on sub-section (6) of section 39 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The notification is issued under rule 62 of the CGST Rules, 2017.

1044.summary: Notification No. 42/2017-Central Tax, issued on October 13, 2017, extends the deadline for filing GSTR-5A for the months of July, August, and September 2017. The new deadline is set for November 20, 2017. This extension specifically applies to businesses providing online information and database access or retrieval services from outside India to non-taxable online recipients. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 25/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on August 28, 2017. The changes are effective retroactively from September 15, 2017. This means that businesses affected by this notification now have additional time to comply with their filing obligations for the specified months. It is important for those in the online service sector to take note of this extension to ensure they meet the new deadline.
key_points: Extends the time limit for furnishing GSTR-5A for July, August, and September 2017.
New deadline for GSTR-5A is now the 20th of November, 2017.
Applicable to persons supplying online information and database access or retrieval services from outside India.
This notification supersedes Notification No. 25/2017-Central Tax dated 28th August 2017.
The notification is effective from the 15th of September, 2017.

1045.summary: Notification No. 43/2017-Central Tax, issued on 13th October 2017, extends the deadline for Input Service Distributors to file their GSTR-6 returns. The new due date is set for 15th November 2017, which applies to the tax periods of July, August, and September 2017. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 26/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on 28th August 2017. The extension is aimed at providing additional time for businesses to comply with their filing obligations for these specific months. Input Service Distributors who were struggling to meet the previous deadlines will benefit from this extension, allowing them to ensure their returns are accurate and complete. This change reflects the government's ongoing efforts to facilitate smoother compliance with the GST framework.
key_points: Extends the due date for filing GSTR-6 for Input Service Distributors.
New due date is 15th November 2017.
Applies to the tax periods of July 2017, August 2017, and September 2017.
Supersedes Notification No. 26/2017-Central Tax dated 28th August 2017.

1046.summary: Notification No. 44/2017-Central Tax, issued on October 13, 2017, extends the deadline for registered persons to submit their declaration in FORM GST ITC - 01.
