This applies specifically to those who became eligible for input tax credit during the months of July, August, and September 2017. The new deadline for making this declaration is now set for October 31, 2017. This change is made under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, which allows for such extensions. This extension provides additional time for businesses to ensure they can claim their eligible input tax credits, which can be crucial for cash flow and tax compliance. It is important for affected businesses to take note of this new deadline to ensure they submit their declarations on time.
key_points: Extends the time limit for making a declaration in FORM GST ITC - 01.
Applies to registered persons who became eligible for input tax credit during July, August, and September 2017.
New deadline for the declaration is now the 31st day of October 2017.
This extension is in pursuance of section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

1047.summary: The Central Government has issued the 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Ninth Amendment) Rules, 2017', effective from October 13, 2017. This amendment introduces several key changes to the GST rules. Notably, it allows individuals with provisional registration to opt for tax payment under section 10 starting from the first day of the month following their filing of FORM GST CMP-02, with a deadline of March 31, 2018. Additionally, a new rule permits the issuance of a single 'invoice-cum-bill of supply' for both taxable and exempt goods or services provided to unregistered persons. Changes to rule 54 now require a 'consolidated tax invoice' for monthly service supplies. Furthermore, a new proviso in rule 62 mandates that those opting for section 10 mid-quarter must file FORM GSTR-4 for that quarter. Updates to FORM GST CMP-02 and revisions in the tables of FORM GSTR-1 and FORM GSTR-1A , particularly in reporting zero-rated supplies and deemed exports. These amendments aim to streamline compliance for businesses under the GST framework.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Central Goods and Services Tax (Ninth Amendment) Rules, 2017'.
It comes into effect on the date of its publication in the Official Gazette, which is October 13, 2017.
Rule 3(3A) is amended to allow provisional registrants to opt for tax payment under section 10 starting from the first day of the month after filing FORM GST CMP-02 by March 31, 2018.
A new rule 46A is added, allowing a single 'invoice-cum-bill of supply' for taxable and exempt supplies to unregistered persons.
Rule 54(2) is amended to replace 'tax invoice' with 'consolidated tax invoice' and adds a provision for monthly service supply invoices.
A new proviso is added to rule 62(1) for registered persons opting for section 10 tax payment mid-quarter, requiring FORM GSTR-4 for that quarter.
FORM GST CMP-02 is updated to reference rule 3(3) and 3(3A).
Tables in FORM GSTR-1 and FORM GSTR-1A are revised to reflect changes in reporting zero-rated supplies and deemed exports.

1048.summary: The recent Notification No. 46/2017-Central Tax, issued on 13th October 2017, brings significant changes to the composition scheme limits under GST. Specifically, it amends the earlier Notification No. 8/2017-Central Tax, which was published on 27th June 2017. The threshold limit for the composition scheme has been increased from seventy-five lakh rupees to one crore rupees, allowing more businesses to benefit from this simplified tax regime. Additionally, the previous threshold limit of fifty lakh rupees has been raised to seventy-five lakh rupees. This means that businesses with a turnover up to one crore rupees can now opt for the composition scheme, which simplifies compliance and reduces tax burdens. These changes are aimed at easing the financial pressure on small and medium enterprises, making it easier for them to operate under the GST framework. Overall, this amendment is a positive step for many businesses looking to streamline their tax processes.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 8/2017-Central Tax dated 27th June 2017.
Changes the threshold limit for composition scheme from 'seventy-five lakh rupees' to 'one crore rupees'.
Changes the threshold limit for the previous composition scheme from 'fifty lakh rupees' to 'seventy-five lakh rupees'.
Issued on 13th October 2017 by the Ministry of Finance.

1049.summary: The Central Government has issued the Central Goods and Services Tax (Tenth Amendment) Rules, 2017, which will take effect upon publication in the Official Gazette. A significant change is in Rule 89(1), where the process for filing refund applications for deemed exports has been clarified. Now, either the recipient or the supplier can file for refunds, provided the recipient does not claim input tax credit and gives an undertaking. Additionally, Rule 96A(1)(a) has been modified to allow the Commissioner to extend the three-month period for filing claims if necessary. Furthermore, FORM GST RFD-01 has been updated; 'Statement - 2' now includes a new format for refunds related to exports of services with tax payment, and 'Statement - 4' has been revised for refunds on supplies to SEZ units or developers. These changes aim to streamline the refund process for businesses engaged in deemed exports and those supplying to SEZs.
key_points: This notification is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Tenth Amendment) Rules, 2017.
The rules come into force on the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
Rule 89(1) is amended to allow the recipient or supplier of deemed export supplies to file for refunds under certain conditions.
Rule 96A(1)(a) is amended to allow for an extension of the three-month period for filing claims as permitted by the Commissioner.
FORM GST RFD-01 is amended, replacing 'Statement - 2' with a new format for refunds related to exports of services with payment of tax.
FORM GST RFD-01 also replaces 'Statement - 4' with a new format for refunds on supplies made to SEZ units or developers.

1050.summary: Notification No. 48/2017-Central Tax, issued on 18th October 2017, designates certain supplies of goods as deemed exports, which can benefit registered persons involved in these transactions. The notification specifies that supplies made against Advance Authorisation, Export Promotion Capital Goods Authorisation, and to Export Oriented Units are considered deemed exports. Additionally, it includes the supply of gold by banks or public sector undertakings as deemed exports when made against Advance Authorisation. Advance Authorisation allows for the import or domestic procurement of inputs specifically for physical exports, while Export Promotion Capital Goods Authorisation facilitates the import of capital goods for the same purpose. The notification also clarifies what constitutes an Export Oriented Unit, including various technology parks recognized under the Foreign Trade Policy 2015-20. This change is significant for businesses engaged in export activities, as it provides clarity on the treatment of these supplies under GST.
key_points: Notification No. 48/2017-Central Tax was issued on 18th October 2017.
The notification identifies specific supplies of goods as deemed exports.
Deemed exports include supplies against Advance Authorisation, Export Promotion Capital Goods Authorisation, to Export Oriented Units, and gold supplied by specified banks or PSUs.
Advance Authorisation allows import or domestic procurement of inputs for physical exports.
Export Promotion Capital Goods Authorisation permits import of capital goods for physical exports.
Export Oriented Units include various technology parks approved under the Foreign Trade Policy 2015-20.

1051.summary: Notification No. 49/2017-Central Tax, issued on 18th October 2017, outlines the documentation necessary for suppliers of deemed export supplies to successfully claim refunds. Specifically, suppliers must provide an acknowledgment from the relevant Tax officer or a signed tax invoice from the recipient Export Oriented Unit confirming receipt of the supplies. Additionally, the recipient must submit two undertakings: one stating that they have not availed any input tax credit on the deemed export supplies, and another confirming that they will not claim a refund for these supplies, thereby permitting the supplier to do so. This notification is crucial for suppliers engaged in deemed exports, as it clarifies the requirements for refund claims, ensuring that both parties understand their responsibilities and the necessary documentation to facilitate the process.
key_points: Notification No. 49/2017-Central Tax was issued on 18th October 2017.
This notification specifies the evidence required for suppliers of deemed export supplies to claim refunds.
The evidence includes acknowledgment from the jurisdictional Tax officer or a signed tax invoice from the recipient Export Oriented Unit.
An undertaking from the recipient stating that no input tax credit has been availed on the deemed export supplies is required.
Another undertaking from the recipient is needed, confirming that they will not claim a refund for these supplies, allowing the supplier to claim it instead.

1052.summary: Notification No. 50/2017-Central Tax, issued on 24th October 2017, provides a waiver of the late fee for registered persons who did not submit their GSTR-3B returns for August and September 2017 by the due date. This waiver applies specifically under section 47 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. As a result, all registered taxpayers who missed the deadline for these two months will not have to pay the late fees that would typically apply. This decision was made following recommendations from the GST Council, aiming to ease the compliance burden on businesses during that period. It is important for affected taxpayers to take note of this waiver, as it provides relief from penalties associated with late filing for the specified months. This notification reflects the government's ongoing efforts to support businesses in managing their GST obligations effectively.
key_points: Waives late fee under section 47 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Applicable to all registered persons.
Covers GSTR-3B returns for the months of August and September 2017.
This notification was issued on 24th October 2017.

1053.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 51/2017-Central Tax, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This amendment, effective from the date of publication, introduces several important changes. Firstly, the deadline for certain filings under Rule 24(4) has been extended from 31st October 2017 to 31st December 2017, providing additional time for compliance. Additionally, Rule 45(3) now allows the Commissioner to extend the time limit for filing returns through a notification, offering more flexibility to taxpayers. New provisions have also been added to Rules 96(2) and 96A(2) that require suppliers to submit export details in FORM GSTR-1 only after they have filed FORM GSTR-3B. This ensures that the export information is accurately captured and transmitted electronically to the Customs system. Furthermore, the information provided in Table 6A of FORM GSTR-1 will be auto-drafted based on the details submitted, streamlining the process for taxpayers. These changes are particularly relevant for businesses involved in exports and those needing to manage their filing timelines effectively.
key_points: The notification is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Eleventh Amendment) Rules, 2017.
It comes into force on the date of its publication in the Official Gazette.
Rule 24(4) is amended to extend the deadline for certain filings from 31st October 2017 to 31st December 2017.
Rule 45(3) is amended to allow the Commissioner to extend the time limit for filing returns by notification.
New provisos are added to Rule 96(2) regarding the submission of export details in FORM GSTR-1 after filing FORM GSTR-3B.
Similar new provisos are added to Rule 96A(2) concerning the submission of export details in FORM GSTR-1 after filing FORM GSTR-3B.
The information in Table 6A of FORM GSTR-1 will be auto-drafted based on the details provided under the new provisions.

1054.summary: Notification No. 52/2017-Central Tax, issued on 28th October 2017, amends the earlier Notification No. 44/2017-Central Tax from 13th October 2017. The key change is the extension of a compliance deadline from 31st October 2017 to 30th November 2017. This adjustment is significant for taxpayers who are required to adhere to the timelines set under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. The amendment is made under section 168 of the Act and specifically alters clause (b) of sub-rule (1) of rule 40 of the GST Rules. As a result, businesses will now have an additional month to meet their obligations for the specified tax period. This change is effective immediately upon publication, allowing affected parties to adjust their compliance strategies accordingly. Overall, this extension provides some relief to taxpayers who may have been facing challenges in meeting the original deadline.
key_points: Notification No. 52/2017-Central Tax was issued on 28th October 2017.
This notification amends Notification No. 44/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on 13th October 2017.
The amendment changes the deadline from '31st October 2017' to '30th November 2017'.
This change affects the compliance timeline for taxpayers under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act.
The amendment is made under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The relevant rule being amended is clause (b) of sub-rule (1) of rule 40 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The new deadline applies to the same tax period as previously stated in Notification No. 44/2017.
This notification will come into effect immediately upon publication.

1055.summary: Notification No. 53/2017-Central Tax, issued on October 28, 2017, extends the deadline for submitting FORM GST ITC-04, which is used for reporting goods sent to or received from job workers. The new deadline is set for November 30, 2017, allowing businesses more time to comply with this requirement. This extension specifically applies to transactions that occurred during the quarter from July to September 2017. The notification is issued under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and is also based on sub-rule (3) of rule 45 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This change is significant for businesses that engage job workers, as it provides additional time to ensure accurate reporting of goods involved in job work activities. Companies should take note of this extended deadline to manage their compliance effectively.
key_points: Extends the deadline for FORM GST ITC-04 submissions.
New deadline is November 30, 2017.
Applies to goods dispatched to or received from job workers.
Covers the quarter from July to September 2017.
Issued under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Also based on sub-rule (3) of rule 45 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

1056.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 54/2017-Central Tax, which amends the earlier Notification No. 30/2017-Central Tax dated 11th September 2017. This amendment specifically extends the due dates for certain compliance requirements. For Sl. No. 2, the deadline has been moved from 31st October 2017 to 30th November 2017, providing additional time for businesses to meet their obligations. Similarly, for Sl. No. 3, the due date has been extended from 10th November 2017 to 11th December 2017. These changes are significant for businesses that need to file their returns or comply with GST regulations, as they now have more time to prepare and submit the necessary documentation. This notification was issued on 30th October 2017 by the Central Board of Excise and Customs, reflecting the government's ongoing efforts to facilitate compliance under the GST framework.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 30/2017-Central Tax dated 11th September 2017.
Extends the due date for Sl. No. 2 from 'Upto 31st October 2017' to 'Upto 30th November 2017'.
Extends the due date for Sl. No. 3 from 'Upto 10th November 2017' to 'Upto 11th December 2017'.
Issued on 30th October 2017 by the Central Board of Excise and Customs.

1057.summary: The Central Government has issued Notification No. 55/2017-Central Tax on 15th November 2017, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This amendment introduces the Central Goods and Services Tax (Twelfth Amendment) Rules, 2017, which come into effect upon publication. A key change is the addition of an explanation to rule 43, clarifying that the aggregate value of exempt supplies does not include certain services outlined in notification No. 42/2017-Integrated Tax. Additionally, rule 54(2) now states that a supplier 'may issue' rather than 'shall issue' certain documents. New rules 97A and 107A allow for manual filing and processing of applications, which provides flexibility for taxpayers. Rule 109A outlines the appeal process for individuals dissatisfied with decisions made under the GST Act. Furthermore, amendments to rule 124 enable the Central Government to terminate the appointments of the Chairman and Technical Member with the necessary approvals. Lastly, new forms GST-RFD-01 A and GST-RFD-01 B have been introduced for manual refund applications, catering to various types of taxpayers, including casual and non-resident taxable persons.
key_points: Notification No. 55/2017-Central Tax was issued on 15th November 2017.
The rules are called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Twelfth Amendment) Rules, 2017.
An explanation was added to rule 43, clarifying that the aggregate value of exempt supplies excludes certain services as specified in notification No. 42/2017-Integrated Tax.
In rule 54(2), 'supplier shall issue' was changed to 'supplier may issue'.
New rules 97A and 107A were inserted, allowing for manual filing and processing alongside electronic filing.
Rule 109A was added, detailing the appointment of Appellate Authority and the appeal process for aggrieved persons.
Changes were made to rule 124, allowing the Central Government to terminate the appointment of the Chairman and Technical Member with approval.
New forms GST-RFD-01 A and GST-RFD-01 B were introduced for manual refund applications.

1058.summary: Notification No. 56/2017-Central Tax, issued on November 15, 2017, outlines the filing deadlines for the GSTR-3B return for the first three months of 2018. Specifically, registered persons must file their GSTR-3B returns electronically by the 20th of the following month: January 2018 returns are due by February 20, February 2018 returns by March 20, and March 2018 returns by April 20. This notification emphasizes the requirement for taxpayers to discharge their tax liabilities by utilizing their electronic cash or credit ledger by these deadlines. This means that businesses must ensure they have sufficient balance in their ledgers to cover any tax, ies owed by the specified dates. The notification is significant for all registered taxpayers under the GST regime, as it sets clear timelines for compliance in the early months of 2018.
key_points: The return in FORM GSTR-3B must be filed electronically for specified months.
For January 2018, the last date for filing is 20th February 2018.
For February 2018, the last date for filing is 20th March 2018.
For March 2018, the last date for filing is 20th April 2018.
Tax liabilities must be discharged by debiting the electronic cash or credit ledger by the last date specified.

1059.summary: Notification No. 57/2017-Central Tax, issued on November 15, 2017, introduces a special procedure for registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to 1.5 crore rupees. This applies to those in the preceding or current financial year. These registered persons are required to file their details of outward supply of goods or services in FORM GSTR-1 for specific quarters. The deadlines for these filings are set as follows: for the quarter of July to September 2017, the due date is December 31, 2017; for October to December 2017, it is February 15, 2018; and for January to March 2018, it is April 30, 2018. Additionally, the notification mentions that further details regarding the special procedure for filing returns for the months from July 2017 to March 2018 will be announced later in the Official Gazette. This change is significant for small businesses as it outlines their filing obligations under the GST framework.
key_points: Registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to 1.5 crore rupees are notified to follow a special procedure for filing GSTR-1.
The notification applies to the financial year preceding or the current financial year.
Details of outward supply must be furnished in FORM GSTR-1 for specific quarters.
The deadlines for filing GSTR-1 are: July-September 2017 by 31st December 2017, October-December 2017 by 15th February 2018, and January-March 2018 by 30th April 2018.
The special procedure for filing returns for July 2017 to March 2018 will be notified later in the Official Gazette.

1060.summary: Notification No. 58/2017-Central Tax, issued on November 15, 2017, extends the deadlines for registered persons with an aggregate turnover exceeding 1.5 crore rupees to furnish details of outward supplies in FORM GSTR-1. This applies to the months from July 2017 to March 2018. Specifically, the deadline for submitting FORM GSTR-1 for July to October 2017 has been extended to December 31, 2017. For November 2017, the new deadline is January 10, 2018; for December 2017, it is February 10, 2018; for January 2018, it is March 10, 2018; for February 2018, it is April 10, 2018; and for March 2018, it is May 10, 2018. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 30/2017-Central Tax issued on September 11, 2017, ensuring that the new deadlines are now in effect for the specified months.
key_points: Extends the time limit for furnishing details of outward supplies in FORM GSTR-1.
Applicable for registered persons with aggregate turnover exceeding 1.5 crore rupees.
New deadlines for FORM GSTR-1 are set for July to March 2018.
Deadline for July to October 2017 is now 31st December 2017.
Deadline for November 2017 is now 10th January 2018.
Deadline for December 2017 is now 10th February 2018.
Deadline for January 2018 is now 10th March 2018.
Deadline for February 2018 is now 10th April 2018.
Deadline for March 2018 is now 10th May 2018.
Supersedes Notification No. 30/2017-Central Tax dated 11th September 2017.

1061.summary: Notification No. 59/2017-Central Tax, issued on 15th November 2017, amends the earlier Notification No. 41/2017-Central Tax, which was published on 13th October 2017. The key change in this amendment is the extension of the due date for a specific compliance requirement from 15th November 2017 to 24th December 2017. This adjustment is made under the authority of sub-section (6) of section 39 and section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The principal notification, which is being amended, was published in the Gazette of India, and is identified by the number G.S.R. 1255(E). This change primarily affects businesses that are required to comply with the deadlines set forth in the earlier notification, providing them with additional time to meet their obligations. It is important for affected businesses to take note of this new deadline to ensure they can complete their filings accordingly.
key_points: This notification is issued on 15th November 2017.
It amends Notification No. 41/2017-Central Tax dated 13th October 2017.
The amendment changes the due date from 15th November 2017 to 24th December 2017.
The amendment is made under sub-section (6) of section 39 and section 168 of the CGST Act, 2017.
The principal notification No. 41/2017 was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i).
The notification number for the principal notification is G.S.R. 1255(E).

1062.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 60/2017-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for non-resident taxable persons to file their GSTR-5 returns. Originally, the due dates for the months of July, August, September, and October 2017 were set earlier, but this notification pushes the new deadline to December 11, 2017. This change specifically affects non-resident taxable persons who are required to submit their returns under section 39 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. The extension is granted under the authority of section 39(6) and section 168 of the Act, allowing these taxpayers additional time to comply with their filing obligations for the specified months. This move aims to provide relief to non-resident taxpayers, ensuring they have adequate time to prepare and submit their returns accurately.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing GSTR-5 for non-resident taxable persons.
Original due date for GSTR-5 for July, August, September, and October 2017 was earlier than December 11, 2017.
New deadline for GSTR-5 is now December 11, 2017.
Applicable to non-resident taxable persons as defined under section 39 of the CGST Act.
This extension applies specifically to the months of July, August, September, and October 2017.
The notification is issued under the powers of section 39(6) and section 168 of the CGST Act.

1063.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 61/2017-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for filing GSTR-5A for the months of July, August, September, and October 2017. The new deadline is set for the 15th of December 2017. This extension specifically applies to businesses that provide online information and database access or retrieval services from outside India to non-taxable online recipients, as defined under section 14 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 42/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on 13th October 2017. Therefore, businesses affected by this change now have additional time to comply with their filing obligations for the specified months. This extension aims to ease the compliance burden for service providers operating from outside India, ensuring they can meet their reporting requirements without undue pressure.
key_points: Extends the time limit for furnishing GSTR-5A for July, August, September, and October 2017.
New deadline for GSTR-5A is now the 15th of December 2017.
Applicable to persons supplying online information and database access or retrieval services from outside India.
Targets non-taxable online recipients as defined in section 14 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Supersedes Notification No. 42/2017-Central Tax dated 13th October 2017.

1064.summary: Notification No. 62/2017-Central Tax, issued on November 15, 2017, extends the deadline for Input Service Distributors to file their GSTR-6 return for July 2017 until December 31, 2017. This change supersedes the earlier Notification No. 43/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on October 13, 2017. The extension specifically affects Input Service Distributors, allowing them additional time to comply with their filing obligations for that month. It is important to note that the deadlines for filing returns for August, September, and October 2017 will be communicated in future notifications. This extension is made under the authority of section 39(6) and section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The new deadline is solely for the month of July 2017, providing relief to those who may have faced challenges in meeting the original deadline.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing GSTR-6 for July 2017 to December 31, 2017.
This notification supersedes Notification No. 43/2017-Central Tax dated October 13, 2017.
The extension applies specifically to Input Service Distributors.
The return filing for August, September, and October 2017 will be notified later.
The extension is based on the powers conferred by section 39(6) and section 168 of the CGST Act.
The notification was issued on November 15, 2017.
The new deadline is applicable for the month of July 2017 only.

1065.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 63/2017-Central Tax on 15th November 2017, which amends the earlier Notification No. 53/2017-Central Tax dated 28th October 2017. The key change is the extension of the due date for a specific compliance requirement from 30th November 2017 to 31st December 2017. This amendment is made under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and also references sub-rule (3) of rule 45 of the GST Rules. This change affects businesses that are required to comply with the deadlines set in the earlier notification, providing them with additional time to meet their obligations. It is important for affected businesses to take note of this new deadline to ensure they are aligned with the updated requirements. The extension aims to facilitate smoother compliance for taxpayers during this period.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 53/2017-Central Tax dated 28th October 2017.
Changes the due date from 30th November 2017 to 31st December 2017.
Issued under section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Also refers to sub-rule (3) of rule 45 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.

1066.summary: Notification No. 64/2017-Central Tax, issued on November 15, 2017, introduces a waiver on late fees for registered persons who fail to file their GSTR-3B returns for the month of October 2017 and onwards by the due date. Specifically, the late fee for such delays is capped at Rs. 25 per day. However, if the total central tax payable in the return is nil, the late fee is further reduced to Rs. 10 per day. This change is significant as it provides relief to taxpayers who may face challenges in timely filing, ensuring that they are not excessively penalized for late submissions. The waiver applies to all registered persons under the GST framework, making it a broad relief measure. This notification is issued under section 128 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, reflecting the government's intent to ease compliance burdens during this period.
key_points: Waives late fee for GSTR-3B returns for October 2017 onwards.
Late fee is capped at Rs. 25 per day for delays beyond the due date.
If the total central tax payable is nil, the late fee is capped at Rs. 10 per day.
This waiver applies to all registered persons under the GST Act.
The notification is issued under section 128 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

1067.summary: Notification No. 65/2017-Central Tax, issued on November 15, 2017, outlines specific exemptions for certain service providers under the GST framework. It states that individuals or entities making supplies of services through an electronic commerce operator, who is responsible for collecting tax at source as per section 52 of the CGST Act, are exempt from obtaining GST registration if their total turnover does not exceed twenty lakh rupees in a financial year. Additionally, for those operating in 'special category States', the threshold is lowered to ten lakh rupees. This means that smaller service providers using e-commerce platforms can operate without the burden of registration, provided they stay within these turnover limits. This notification is significant for small businesses and service providers, as it simplifies compliance requirements and encourages participation in the digital economy without the need for extensive regulatory obligations.
key_points: Specifies persons making supplies of services through an electronic commerce operator required to collect tax at source under section 52 of the CGST Act.
Exempts these persons from obtaining GST registration if their aggregate turnover does not exceed twenty lakh rupees in a financial year.
For 'special category States', the aggregate value of supplies must not exceed ten lakh rupees to qualify for exemption.
The notification is issued under the powers conferred by sub-section (2) of section 23 of the CGST Act.

1068.summary: Notification No. 66/2017-Central Tax, issued on 15th November 2017, replaces the earlier Notification No. 40/2017-Central Tax from 13th October 2017. This new notification specifically targets registered persons who have not opted for the composition levy under section 10 of the Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) Act. These individuals are now required to pay central tax on the outward supply of goods at the time of supply, as outlined in clause (a) of sub-section (2) of section 12 of the CGST Act. Additionally, it includes provisions related to section 14 of the CGST Act, which deals with the time of supply. The notification also mandates that these registered persons must furnish the necessary details and returns in accordance with Chapter IX of the CGST Act. The payment period for this group will follow the timelines specified in the CGST Act. Overall, this change clarifies the tax obligations for registered persons not opting for the composition scheme, ensuring they comply with the specified payment and reporting requirements.
key_points: Notification No. 66/2017-Central Tax issued on 15th November 2017.
Supersedes Notification No. 40/2017-Central Tax dated 13th October 2017.
Applies to registered persons who did not opt for the composition levy under section 10 of the CGST Act.
Specifies that these registered persons must pay central tax on outward supply of goods at the time of supply.
Refers to clause (a) of sub-section (2) of section 12 of the CGST Act for payment timing.
Includes provisions attracting section 14 of the CGST Act.
Mandates furnishing of details and returns as per Chapter IX of the CGST Act.
Payment period for this class of registered persons is as specified in the CGST Act.

1069.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 67/2017-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for registered persons to make a declaration in FORM GST ITC-01. This extension allows those who became eligible for input tax credit between July and November 2017 to submit their declarations until January 31, 2018. This change is significant as it provides additional time for businesses to claim their input tax credits, which can help improve cash flow and reduce tax liabilities. The notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 44/2017-Central Tax, which had set a different deadline. Therefore, businesses that missed the previous deadline now have a new opportunity to comply and benefit from the input tax credit provisions under the GST Act. This extension is particularly relevant for those who may have faced challenges in meeting the earlier timeline.
key_points: Extends the time limit for making a declaration in FORM GST ITC-01.
New deadline for the declaration is January 31, 2018.
This applies to registered persons who became eligible for input tax credit during July to November 2017.
Supersedes Notification No. 44/2017-Central Tax dated October 13, 2017.

1070.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 68/2017-Central Tax, which extends the deadline for non-resident taxable persons to file their GSTR-5 returns. The new deadline is set for January 31, 2018, covering the tax periods from July 2017 to December 2017. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 60/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on November 15, 2017. The extension is granted under the provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, specifically under sub-section (6) of section 39. This change is significant for non-resident taxable persons who may have faced challenges in meeting the previous deadlines. The amendment takes effect immediately from the date of this notification, December 21, 2017, allowing affected parties additional time to comply with their filing obligations.
key_points: Extends the deadline for filing GSTR-5 for non-resident taxable persons.
New deadline is January 31, 2018.
Applies to tax periods: July 2017, August 2017, September 2017, October 2017, November 2017, and December 2017.
Supersedes Notification No. 60/2017-Central Tax dated November 15, 2017.
The extension is granted under sub-section (6) of section 39 of the CGST Act.
The amendment is effective from the date of this notification, December 21, 2017.

1071.summary: Notification No. 69/2017-Central Tax, issued on December 21, 2017, extends the deadline for filing FORM GSTR-5A, which is specifically for those providing online information and database access or retrieval services from outside India to non-taxable online recipients. The new deadline is set for January 31, 2018, covering the tax periods from July 2017 through December 2017. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 61/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on November 15, 2017. The extension is significant for businesses engaged in cross-border online services, ensuring they have additional time to comply with GST filing requirements. The relevant sections cited in this notification include sub-section (6) of section 39 and section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, along with section 20 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This change aims to facilitate smoother compliance for affected service providers during the initial implementation phase of GST.
key_points: Extends the time limit for filing FORM GSTR-5A.
Applies to returns for the months of July 2017 to December 2017.
New deadline for filing is January 31, 2018.
Affects persons supplying online information and database access or retrieval services from outside India.
Supersedes Notification No. 61/2017-Central Tax dated November 15, 2017.
Covers non-taxable online recipients as per section 14 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Cited sections include sub-section (6) of section 39 and section 168 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

1072.summary: The Central Government has issued the Central Goods and Services Tax (Thirteenth Amendment) Rules, 2017, which will take effect upon publication in the Official Gazette. This amendment introduces significant changes to various GST forms. In FORM GSTR-1, Table 6 has been updated to include specific details for zero-rated supplies and deemed exports, enhancing clarity for reporting these transactions. Additionally, FORM GST RFD-01 has been revised; clause (h) of Table 7 now refers to both 'Recipient of deemed export supplies' and 'Supplier of deemed export supplies', broadening the scope of who can claim refunds. New Statements 1A and 5B have been added to FORM GST RFD-01 to facilitate refunds related to ITC accumulated due to inverted tax structure and deemed exports, respectively. Similarly, FORM GST RFD-01A has undergone changes, including the same clause (g) update and the introduction of a new Declaration for recipients and suppliers of deemed exports. These changes aim to streamline the refund process and clarify the requirements for claiming refunds in the context of deemed exports.
key_points: This notification is called the Central Goods and Services Tax (Thirteenth Amendment) Rules, 2017.
The rules come into force on the date of publication in the Official Gazette.
In FORM GSTR-1, Table 6 is substituted to include details for zero-rated supplies and deemed exports.
In FORM GST RFD-01, clause (h) of Table 7 changes 'Recipient of deemed export' to 'Recipient of deemed export supplies/ Supplier of deemed export supplies'.
A new Statement 1A is added in FORM GST RFD-01 for refunds related to ITC accumulated due to inverted tax structure.
A new Statement 5B is added in FORM GST RFD-01 for refunds on account of deemed exports.
In FORM GST RFD-01A, clause (g) of Table 7 also changes 'Recipient of deemed export' to 'Recipient of deemed export supplies/ Supplier of deemed export supplies'.
A new Declaration is added in FORM GST RFD-01A for recipients/suppliers of deemed exports.

1073.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 71/2017-Central Tax on 29th December 2017, which replaces the earlier Notification No. 57/2017-Central Tax from 15th November 2017. This new notification allows registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to 1.5 crore rupees to follow a special procedure for filing their GST returns. Specifically, these businesses must submit their details of outward supply in FORM GSTR-1 for three quarters: for July to September 2017 by 10th January 2018, for October to December 2017 by 15th February 2018, and for January to March 2018 by 30th April 2018. Additionally, the notification mentions that further details regarding the special procedure for the months from July 2017 to March 2018 will be provided in future official announcements. This change is significant for small businesses as it simplifies their filing process during this period.
key_points: Notification No. 71/2017-Central Tax was issued on 29th December 2017.
This notification supersedes Notification No. 57/2017-Central Tax dated 15th November 2017.
Registered persons with an aggregate turnover of up to 1.5 crore rupees can follow a special procedure for filing.
Details of outward supply must be furnished in FORM GSTR-1 for specific quarters.
The time periods for filing FORM GSTR-1 are: July-September 2017 by 10th January 2018, October-December 2017 by 15th February 2018, and January-March 2018 by 30th April 2018.
The special procedure for the months of July 2017 to March 2018 will be notified later in the Official Gazette.

1074.summary: Notification No. 72/2017-Central Tax, issued on December 29, 2017, extends the deadlines for registered persons with an aggregate turnover exceeding 1.5 crore rupees to furnish details of outward supplies in FORM GSTR-1. This applies to the months from July 2017 to March 2018. The new deadlines are set as follows: for the months of July to November 2017, the deadline is now January 10, 2018; for December 2017, it is February 10, 2018; for January 2018, it is March 10, 2018; for February 2018, it is April 10, 2018; and for March 2018, it is May 10, 2018. This notification supersedes the earlier Notification No. 58/2017-Central Tax, which was issued on November 15, 2017. The extension aims to provide additional time for compliance with the GST requirements during this period.
key_points: Extends the time limit for furnishing details of outward supplies in FORM GSTR-1.
Applicable for registered persons with aggregate turnover exceeding 1.5 crore rupees.
New deadlines for FORM GSTR-1 are set for the months from July 2017 to March 2018.
Deadline for July-November 2017 is now 10th January 2018.
Deadline for December 2017 is now 10th February 2018.
Deadline for January 2018 is now 10th March 2018.
Deadline for February 2018 is now 10th April 2018.
Deadline for March 2018 is now 10th May 2018.
Supersedes Notification No. 58/2017-Central Tax dated 15th November 2017.

1075.summary: Notification No. 73/2017-Central Tax, issued on December 29, 2017, introduces a waiver for late fees associated with the late filing of GSTR-4. This waiver applies to all registered persons who do not submit their GSTR-4 returns by the due date. Specifically, if a registered person files late, the late fee will not exceed twenty-five rupees for each day of delay. However, if the total central tax due in the GSTR-4 return is nil, the late fee is further reduced to a maximum of ten rupees per day. This change is significant as it alleviates the financial burden on taxpayers who may miss the deadline, especially those with no tax liability. The notification aims to encourage compliance by making it less costly for businesses to file their returns late, thereby supporting them in maintaining their GST obligations.
key_points: Waives late fee for late filing of GSTR-4 under section 47 of the CGST Act.
Late fee waiver applies to registered persons who fail to file GSTR-4 by the due date.
For late filing, the late fee is capped at twenty-five rupees per day.
If the total central tax payable in GSTR-4 is nil, the late fee is capped at ten rupees per day.

1076.summary: Notification No. 74/2017-Central Tax, issued on December 29, 2017, establishes February 1, 2018, as the effective date for certain provisions outlined in serial numbers 2(i) and 2(ii) of the earlier Notification No. 27/2017-Central Tax, which was published on August 30, 2017. This change is made under the authority of section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The provisions that are coming into force relate to specific entries that were previously detailed in the earlier notification. This notification will be officially published in the Gazette of India, ensuring that it is accessible to all stakeholders. The Central Board of Excise and Customs is responsible for this notification, with Ruchi Bisht serving as the Under Secretary overseeing its issuance. Businesses and taxpayers should take note of this effective date to with the new provisions as they come into effect.
key_points: The effective date for the provisions of serial numbers 2(i) and 2(ii) of notification No. 27/2017-Central Tax is set to be February 1, 2018.
This notification is issued under the authority of section 164 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Notification No. 74/2017-Central Tax was published on December 29, 2017.
The earlier notification being amended is No. 27/2017-Central Tax, dated August 30, 2017.
The provisions being enforced are related to specific entries listed in the earlier notification.
The notification will be published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Part II, Section 3, Sub-section (i).
The notification is issued by the Central Board of Excise and Customs.
Ruchi Bisht is the Under Secretary to the Government of India responsible for this notification.

1077.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 75/2017-Central Tax, which amends the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This notification, effective from December 29, 2017, introduces several important changes. Rule 17 now specifies that the Unique Identity Number (UIN) is applicable across India. Additionally, Rule 19 clarifies that any amendments to registration applications cannot take effect before the application submission date unless specifically ordered by the Commissioner. A significant change in Rule 89 provides a new formula for calculating refunds on zero-rated supplies, effective from October 23, 2017. Furthermore, Rule 95 mandates that refund applications must be submitted quarterly using FORM GST RFD-10, along with a statement in FORM GSTR-11. Rule 96 has been updated to include services in the refund process for integrated tax paid on exports. Lastly, several forms, including FORM GST REG-10, FORM GSTR-11, and FORM GST RFD-10, have been revised to reflect these amendments, impacting businesses involved in GST compliance and refund claims.
key_points: Notification No. 75/2017-Central Tax is the Fourteenth Amendment to the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
The amendment comes into force on the date of its publication in the Official Gazette, which is December 29, 2017.
Rule 17 is amended to clarify that the Unique Identity Number (UIN) granted is applicable to the territory of India.
Rule 19 is updated to state that amendments to registration applications cannot take effect before the application submission date unless ordered by the Commissioner.
Rule 89 substitutes sub-rule (4) to provide a new formula for calculating refunds on zero-rated supplies effective from October 23, 2017.
Rule 95 is amended to require refund applications to be made in FORM GST RFD-10 once every quarter, along with a statement in FORM GSTR-11.
Rule 96 is updated to include services in the heading and introduces a new sub-rule regarding refund claims for integrated tax paid on exports.
Several forms, including FORM GST REG-10, FORM GSTR-11, and FORM GST RFD-10, have been substituted with new versions reflecting these changes.

1078.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017, making several important amendments. Firstly, the description of coffee has been revised from 'Coffee, whether or not roasted or decaffeinated' to 'Coffee roasted, whether or not decaffeinated' on page 69. Additionally, a new entry for Bran and other residues from cereals or leguminous plants has been added on page 72. The notification also corrects the code for a specific product from '2710 19 00' to '2711 19 00' on page 74. Furthermore, the classification of certain goods has been expanded to include '84, 85 or 94' on page 77. A new entry for dried citrus fruits has been introduced on page 87, while several beverage codes have been updated on page 88. Lastly, a new entry for road tractors with engine capacity exceeding 1800 cc has been added on page 127. These changes will affect businesses dealing with these specific goods and require them to update their records accordingly.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
Changes 'Coffee, whether or not roasted or decaffeinated' to 'Coffee roasted, whether or not decaffeinated' on page 69, line 28.
Inserts a new entry for Bran, sharps and other residues derived from cereals or leguminous plants on page 72, after line 14.
Changes '2710 19 00' to '2711 19 00' on page 74, line 42.
Changes '84 or 85' to '84, 85 or 94' on page 77, line 28.
Inserts a new entry for dried citrus fruits on page 87, after line 17.
Updates several codes related to beverages on page 88, lines 34-37.
Inserts a new entry for road tractors with engine capacity more than 1800 cc on page 127, after line 49.

1079.summary: On 27th July 2017, a corrigendum was issued to amend Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This corrigendum includes several important changes across different schedules. For instance, in Schedule I, the HSN codes for items in S. No. 59 and 61 have been updated from '1106 10 90' to '0713'. Additionally, S. No. 92 now includes a broader description that adds 'mishri, batasha, bura' to 'Palmyra sugar'. In Schedule II, S. No. 14 has been modified to include 'desiccated coconuts' alongside 'shelled or peeled'. Schedule III has also seen changes, particularly in S. No. 55, which now specifies that it excludes 'Fountain pen ink and Ball pen ink'. Furthermore, Schedule IV has been corrected in S. No. 158 to clarify the description of optical fibres and related items. Lastly, S. No. 215 in Schedule IV has been expanded to encompass various gaming machines and their accessories. These changes affect businesses dealing with these goods and services, ensuring clarity in tax classifications.
key_points: The corrigendum was issued on 27th July 2017, amending Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In Schedule I, S. No. 59 and 61, the HSN code '1106 10 90' is replaced with '0713'.
In Schedule I, S. No. 92, the description is updated to include 'Palmyra sugar, mishri, batasha, bura' instead of just 'Palmyra sugar'.
In Schedule II, S. No. 14, 'desiccated coconuts' is added after 'shelled or peeled'.
In Schedule III, S. No. 55, the description is changed to exclude 'Fountain pen ink and Ball pen ink'.
In Schedule IV, S. No. 158, the term 'optical fibres optical fibres, bundles or cables' is corrected to 'optical fibres, optical fibre bundles or cables'.
In Schedule IV, S. No. 215, the description is expanded to include various gaming machines and accessories.

1080.summary: The corrigendum issued on 30th June 2017 modifies certain entries in the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017. Specifically, it changes the wording at page 75, line 20, where '30' is now stated as '30 or any Chapter', broadening the scope of the classification. Additionally, at page 99, line 33, the phrase 'other than those' has been removed, which may clarify the applicability of the tax rate. Furthermore, at page 117, line 5, the terms 'goggles and the like, corrective, protective or other' have been omitted, potentially simplifying the description of taxable goods. These changes are intended to enhance clarity and precision in the tax regulations, affecting businesses that deal with the specified goods and services under the integrated tax framework.
key_points: This is a corrigendum to Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In the corrigendum, at page 75, line 20, '30' is replaced with '30 or any Chapter'.
At page 99, line 33, the phrase 'other than those' is omitted.
At page 117, line 5, the phrase 'goggles and the like, corrective, protective or other' is omitted.

1081.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017, making several important amendments to the GST rates and descriptions in various schedules. In Schedule I, for S. No. 59 and 61, the code '1106 10 90' has been replaced with '0713'. Additionally, S. No. 92 now includes '1702 or 1704' and expands the description to include 'mishri, batasha, bura' alongside 'Palmyra sugar'. S. No. 185 has been updated to include 'lobhan' with 'Agarbatti', and S. No. 220 has revised entries for 'saima', 'gotasitara', and 'glzal'. A new entry for 'Photo voltaic cells' has been added in S. No. 234. In Schedule II, S. No. 14 now includes 'and desiccated coconuts' in its description. Furthermore, in Schedule III, S. No. 55 has been modified to clarify that it excludes 'Fountain pen ink and Ball pen ink'. These changes affect businesses dealing with these specific goods and services, ensuring that the GST rates and descriptions are accurate and up-to-date.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In Schedule I, S. No. 59 and 61, replaces '1106 10 90' with '0713'.
In Schedule I, S. No. 92, changes column (2) from '1702' to '1702 or 1704' and updates column (3) to include 'mishri, batasha, bura' with 'Palmyra sugar'.
In Schedule I, S. No. 185, updates column (3) from 'Agarbatti' to 'Agarbatti, lobhan'.
In Schedule I, S. No. 220, modifies column (3) entries for 'saima', 'gotasitara', and 'glzal'.
In Schedule I, S. No. 234, adds 'Photo voltaic cells' after entry (g).
In Schedule II, S. No. 14, adds 'and desiccated coconuts' after 'shelled or peeled'.
In Schedule III, S. No. 55, changes description to exclude 'Fountain pen ink and Ball pen ink'.

1082.summary: The corrigendum issued on 30th June 2017 makes specific amendments to Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) that was published on 28th June 2017. Firstly, it clarifies the text by changing '30' to '30 or any Chapter' on page 247, which broadens the applicability of the provision. Additionally, on page 272, the removal of the phrase 'other than those' simplifies the language and potentially expands the scope of the notification. Lastly, on page 290, the deletion of the phrase 'goggles and the like, corrective, protective or other' removes specific examples that may have limited the interpretation of the goods covered. These changes are important for businesses as they may affect how certain goods are classified under GST, impacting tax rates and compliance requirements. It is essential for affected businesses to review these amendments to understand their implications on their operations.
key_points: This corrigendum is related to Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) issued on 28th June 2017.
In the corrigendum, the phrase '30' is replaced with '30 or any Chapter' on page 247, line 7.
The words 'other than those' are omitted from page 272, line 10.
The phrase 'goggles and the like, corrective, protective or other' is removed from page 290, line 11.

1083.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017, making several important amendments. Firstly, the description of coffee has been updated from 'Coffee, whether or not roasted or decaffeinated' to 'Coffee roasted, whether or not decaffeinated' on page 241. Additionally, a new entry for bran and residues has been added on page 243, specifically '103A 2302 Bran, sharps and other residues...'. There are also changes in the classification codes, such as updating '2710 19 00' to '2711 19 00' on page 246 and modifying the range from '84 or 85' to '84, 85 or 94' on page 249. Furthermore, a new entry for citrus fruits has been introduced on page 259, and several item codes have been revised on page 260. Lastly, a new entry for road tractors has been added on page 301. These changes affect businesses dealing with these specific goods, ensuring they are correctly classified under the GST framework.

1084.summary: The Government of India issued Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) on June 28, 2017, establishing the integrated tax rates for various goods under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The notification specifies that goods listed in Schedule I will attract a 5% tax, while those in Schedule II will be taxed at 12%, Schedule III at 18%, Schedule IV at 28%, Schedule V at 3%, and Schedule VI at 0.25%. This means that businesses involved in the inter-State supply of these goods must apply the respective tax rates as outlined. For instance, Schedule I includes items such as frozen fish, UHT milk, and various nuts and oils, which are now subject to the specified tax rates. This notification is crucial for businesses to understand their tax obligations when supplying goods across state lines, ensuring compliance with the new tax structure introduced by the GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 1/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) issued on 28th June 2017.
Integrated tax rates specified: 5% for Schedule I goods, 12% for Schedule II, 18% for Schedule III, 28% for Schedule IV, 3% for Schedule V, and 0.25% for Schedule VI.
Schedule I includes goods like frozen fish, UHT milk, and various types of nuts and oils.
The notification is issued under the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The rates apply to inter-State supplies of goods as per the specified schedules.
The notification outlines detailed descriptions of goods under each tax rate category.

1085.summary: Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 28th June 2017, establishes the central tax rates applicable to various goods under the GST framework. The notification outlines specific tax rates for different schedules: 2.5% for Schedule I, which includes items like frozen fish and UHT milk; 6% for Schedule II; 9% for Schedule III; 14% for Schedule IV; 1.5% for Schedule V; and 0.125% for Schedule VI. While the notification details the items under Schedule I, it does not specify the goods included in the other schedules. This means that businesses dealing with the specified goods in Schedule I will need to apply the 2.5% tax rate, while those dealing with goods in the other schedules will need to refer to the respective rates. This notification is crucial for businesses to understand their tax obligations under the GST regime, particularly for those involved in the supply of food products and other specified goods.
key_points: The notification is issued under Notification No. 1/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
It specifies the central tax rates for various goods: 2.5% for Schedule I, 6% for Schedule II, 9% for Schedule III, 14% for Schedule IV, 1.5% for Schedule V, and 0.125% for Schedule VI.
Schedule I includes goods like frozen fish, UHT milk, and various types of edible products with a tax rate of 2.5%.
Schedule II includes goods with a tax rate of 6%, but specific items are not detailed in the provided text.
Schedule III has a tax rate of 9%, with specific items not mentioned in the provided text.
Schedule IV has a tax rate of 14%, with specific items not mentioned in the provided text.
Schedule V has a tax rate of 1.5%, with specific items not mentioned in the provided text.
Schedule VI has a tax rate of 0.125%, with specific items not mentioned in the provided text.

1086.summary: This corrigendum issued on July 12, 2017, modifies the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) from June 28, 2017. Specifically, it updates the description of dried leguminous vegetables by clarifying that the exemption applies only to those not packaged in unit containers and not bearing a registered brand name. This change aims to provide clearer guidelines on which products are subject to the integrated tax. Additionally, the notification removes the phrase '[proposed GST Nil]' from the document, indicating that there is no longer a proposal for a nil GST rate on certain items. These amendments are crucial for businesses dealing with dried leguminous vegetables, as they need to be aware of the specific conditions under which these products are taxed. Overall, the changes enhance clarity and ensure that businesses can comply with the updated tax regulations effectively.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 2/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

1087.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 2/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate), which was originally published on 28th June 2017. This corrigendum, dated 27th July 2017, makes specific amendments to the tax rate schedule. For S.No. 59, the entry in column (2) has been changed from '9' to '7, 9 or 10', allowing for a broader range of applicable tax rates. Additionally, for S.No. 102, the entry in column (2) has been updated from '2302' to '2301, 2302', which clarifies the classification of goods under the integrated tax regime. These changes are important for businesses that deal with the specified goods, as they may affect the applicable tax rates and compliance requirements. It is essential for affected businesses to review these amendments to ensure they are aligned with the updated tax classifications and rates.

1088.summary: The recent corrigendum to Notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 12th July 2017, brings important changes to the classification of dried leguminous vegetables. Specifically, the description of 'Dried leguminous vegetables, shelled, whether or not skinned or split' has been amended to clarify that it does not include those that are packaged in unit containers and carry a registered brand name. This change aims to provide clearer guidance on the tax treatment of these products. Additionally, the notification has removed the phrase '[proposed GST Nil]', which may have previously indicated a tax exemption. These amendments are significant for businesses dealing with dried leguminous vegetables, as they will need to adjust their understanding of the applicable GST rates based on this updated classification. Overall, this corrigendum helps streamline the tax framework for these products, ensuring that businesses are aware of the specific conditions under which GST applies.

1089.summary: The Government of India has issued a corrigendum to Notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017, which was published in the Gazette of India. This corrigendum, dated 27th July 2017, makes specific amendments to the tax rates listed in the original notification. In the schedule, for S.No. 59, the entry in column (2) has been updated from '9' to '7, 9 or 10', allowing for a broader range of applicable rates. Additionally, for S.No. 102, the entry in column (2) has been modified from '2302' to '2301, 2302', which clarifies the classification of goods under the GST framework. These changes are important for businesses and taxpayers as they directly affect the tax rates applicable to certain goods. It is essential for those involved in the supply of these goods to be aware of these updates to ensure accurate compliance with the GST regulations.

1090.summary: Notification No. 02/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on June 28, 2017, provides an exemption from the integrated tax for inter-State supplies of a wide range of goods. This exemption is applicable under section 5 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. The notification includes a detailed Schedule listing various categories of goods that are exempt, such as live animals (including asses, bovine animals, and poultry), different types of meat (fresh, chilled, or frozen), fish, dairy products like milk and curd, and a variety of fruits and vegetables. Additionally, it covers grains and certain other items, ensuring that these goods can be supplied across state lines without the burden of integrated tax. This change is significant for businesses involved in the trade of these goods, as it reduces their tax liability and encourages inter-State commerce in essential products.
key_points: Notification No. 02/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) was issued on June 28, 2017.
This notification exempts inter-State supplies of specific goods from the integrated tax under section 5 of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The exemption applies to various categories of goods listed in the Schedule, including live animals, meat, fish, dairy products, fruits, vegetables, grains, and certain other items.
The Schedule includes detailed entries specifying the chapter, heading, sub-heading, and tariff item for each exempted good.

1091.summary: Notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax, issued on June 28, 2017, introduces a significant exemption from central tax for intra-State supplies of a wide range of goods. This exemption covers various live animals, including poultry and livestock, as well as different types of meat, fish, dairy products, fruits, vegetables, and grains. The detailed Schedule lists 137 specific entries that outline the chapters, headings, and descriptions of the goods eligible for this exemption. Notably, the exemption applies to goods that are not packaged in unit containers and do not carry a registered brand name. This move aims to support the agricultural and food sectors by reducing tax burdens on essential goods, thereby benefiting farmers and consumers alike. The exemption is granted based on the recommendations of the GST Council, emphasizing its importance in promoting public interest. Businesses involved in the supply of these goods will find relief from the central tax, which can enhance their competitiveness in the market.
key_points: Notification No. 2/2017-Central Tax was issued on June 28, 2017.
This notification exempts intra-State supplies of specific goods from the whole of the central tax under section 9 of the CGST Act.
The goods exempted include live animals, various types of meat, fish, dairy products, fruits, vegetables, grains, and certain other items as listed in the Schedule.
The Schedule specifies 137 entries detailing the chapters, headings, and descriptions of the exempted goods.
Examples of exempted goods include live poultry, fresh milk, fresh fruits like bananas and apples, and various types of seeds.
The exemption applies to goods that are not put up in unit containers and do not bear a registered brand name.
This exemption is granted in the public interest based on recommendations from the GST Council.

1092.summary: Notification No. 3/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on June 28, 2017, introduces an exemption from integrated tax for certain inter-State supplies of goods related to petroleum and coal bed methane operations. Specifically, these goods are exempt from tax beyond a rate of 5%. The exemption is applicable to supplies made to licensees, contractors, and their subcontractors, provided they meet specific conditions. A key requirement is the production of certificates from the Directorate General of Hydro Carbons, confirming that the goods are necessary for the specified operations. The notification details a range of goods eligible for this exemption, including drilling rigs, seismic survey equipment, and various oil field chemicals. This change is significant for businesses involved in petroleum operations, as it reduces their tax burden on essential supplies. Compliance with the outlined conditions is crucial to benefit from this exemption, ensuring that all parties involved in the supply chain adhere to the requirements set forth in the notification.
key_points: Notification No. 3/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) was issued on June 28, 2017.
This notification exempts inter-State supplies of specified goods from integrated tax beyond a certain rate.
The specified goods include those required for petroleum operations and coal bed methane operations.
The applicable tax rate for these goods is set at 5%.
Conditions for exemption include the requirement of certificates from the Directorate General of Hydro Carbons.
The notification outlines specific goods such as drilling rigs, seismic survey equipment, and oil field chemicals.
The exemption applies to supplies made to licensees, contractors, and their subcontractors involved in petroleum operations.
The notification includes detailed conditions for compliance to maintain the exemption.

1093.summary: Notification No. 3/2017-Central Tax, issued on June 28, 2017, introduces an exemption from excess central tax for specific intra-State supplies of goods related to petroleum operations, setting the applicable tax rate at 2.5%. This exemption is aimed at supplies made to entities such as the Oil and Natural Gas Corporation and contractors engaged in petroleum activities. To qualify for this exemption, suppliers must adhere to certain conditions, including obtaining certificates from the Directorate General of Hydro Carbons, which confirm that the goods are necessary for the specified petroleum operations. The notification outlines a detailed list of exempt goods, which includes various types of drilling rigs, seismic survey equipment, and other materials essential for petroleum exploration and production. It is crucial for suppliers to ensure that all documentation is in order at the time of supply to benefit from this exemption. This notification is part of the broader framework established under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, which aims to streamline tax processes in India.
key_points: Notification No. 3/2017-Central Tax was issued on June 28, 2017.
This notification exempts certain intra-State supplies of goods related to petroleum operations from excess central tax.
The applicable rate for these goods is set at 2.5%.
Goods must be supplied to specific entities like Oil and Natural Gas Corporation or contractors involved in petroleum operations.
Conditions for exemption include the requirement of certificates from the Directorate General of Hydro Carbons.
The list of exempt goods includes various equipment and materials necessary for petroleum operations, such as drilling rigs and seismic survey equipment.
The notification specifies that the exemption applies only if certain conditions are met, including documentation at the time of supply.
The notification is part of the implementation of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

1095.summary: Notification No. 4/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 28th June 2017, introduces specific goods that are subject to integrated tax on a reverse charge basis, effective from 1st July 2017. This means that for certain goods, the recipient, who must be a registered person, is responsible for paying the tax instead of the supplier. The goods listed include cashew nuts, bidi wrapper leaves, tobacco leaves, and silk yarn, with suppliers typically being agriculturists or silk yarn manufacturers. Additionally, lottery supplies are included, where the supplier can be a State Government, Union Territory, or local authority. This notification aligns with the provisions of the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 and refers to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 for the classification of these goods. Businesses dealing with these specified goods should be aware of their responsibilities under this notification.
key_points: Notification No. 4/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) was issued on 28th June 2017.
This notification specifies goods subject to integrated tax on a reverse charge basis.
The effective date for this notification is 1st July 2017.
Goods listed include cashew nuts, bidi wrapper leaves, tobacco leaves, and silk yarn.
The supplier for these goods is typically an agriculturist or a manufacturer of silk yarn.
The recipient of the supply must be a registered person.
Lottery supplies are also included, with the supplier being a State Government, Union Territory, or local authority.
The notification refers to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 for tariff classifications.

1096.summary: Notification No. 4/2017-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 28th June 2017, introduces a reverse charge mechanism for certain goods under GST, effective from 1st July 2017. This means that the recipient of the goods, who must be a registered person, will be responsible for paying the central tax instead of the supplier. The goods affected include cashew nuts, bidi wrapper leaves, tobacco leaves, and silk yarn, with suppliers primarily being agriculturists or silk yarn manufacturers. Additionally, lottery supplies are included, where the supplier is a State Government, Union Territory, or local authority, and the recipient is a lottery distributor or selling agent. The notification also clarifies that the definitions and classifications of goods will follow the Customs Tariff Act, 1975. This change impacts registered persons who deal in these specified goods, shifting the tax liability to them under the reverse charge mechanism.
key_points: Notification No. 4/2017-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 28th June 2017.
This notification specifies goods subject to reverse charge mechanism under GST.
Effective date for this notification is 1st July 2017.
Goods listed include cashew nuts, bidi wrapper leaves, tobacco leaves, and silk yarn.
The supplier for these goods is primarily an agriculturist or a manufacturer of silk yarn.
The recipient of these goods must be a registered person.
Lottery supplies are also included, with the supplier being a State Government or local authority.
The notification references the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 for tariff classifications.

1097.summary: Notification No. 5/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate), issued on 28th June 2017, outlines specific goods for which businesses will not be able to claim a refund of unutilised input tax credit. This applies when the input tax rate exceeds the output tax rate for these goods. The goods affected include various woven fabrics such as silk, wool, cotton, and manmade materials, as well as several railway-related items like locomotives and railway coaches. This notification is significant for manufacturers and suppliers of these goods, as they will need to adjust their accounting practices accordingly. The rules cited in the notification reference the Central Goods and Services Tax Act and the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, ensuring that businesses understand the legal framework governing these changes. Importantly, this notification takes effect from 1st July 2017, meaning that businesses should be aware of these stipulations in their operations starting from that date.
key_points: Notification No. 5/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) issued on 28th June 2017.
The notification specifies goods for which no refund of unutilised input tax credit will be allowed.
Goods listed include various woven fabrics (silk, wool, cotton, manmade materials) and railway-related items.
The notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.
The relevant sections cited are clause (ii) of the proviso to sub-section (3) of section 54 of the CGST Act and section 20 of the IGST Act.

1098.summary: Notification No. 5/2017-Central Tax (Rate), issued on 28th June 2017, outlines specific goods for which businesses will not be able to claim a refund of unutilised input tax credit. This applies when the tax rate on inputs is higher than that on the output supplies of these goods. The notification includes a list of 15 tariff items, covering various woven fabrics such as silk, wool, cotton, and manmade materials, as well as several railway-related products like locomotives and coaches. Importantly, this notification will take effect from 1st July 2017. Businesses dealing with these specified goods should be aware that they cannot claim refunds for accumulated input tax credit related to these items. The interpretation of these tariff items will follow the guidelines set out in the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975, ensuring clarity in application.
key_points: Notification No. 5/2017-Central Tax (Rate) was issued on 28th June 2017.
This notification specifies goods for which no refund of unutilised input tax credit will be allowed.
The affected goods include various woven fabrics (silk, wool, cotton, manmade materials) and railway-related items.
The notification lists 15 specific tariff items and their corresponding descriptions.
This notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.
The rules for interpreting the tariff items are based on the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975.

1099.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 6/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) on 28th June 2017, which specifies that the Canteen Stores Department (CSD) under the Ministry of Defence is eligible to claim a refund. Specifically, the CSD can claim a refund of fifty percent of the integrated tax paid on all inward supplies of goods. This refund is applicable for goods that the CSD receives for the purpose of supplying them to Unit Run Canteens or to authorized customers. The notification is set to take effect from 1st July 2017. This change is significant for the CSD as it allows them to recover a portion of the tax paid, thereby potentially reducing their overall tax burden and improving their financial operations. Businesses involved in supplying goods to the CSD or those operating within the framework of the CSD's Unit Run Canteens should take note of this provision.
key_points: Notification No. 6/2017-Integrated Tax (Rate) issued on 28th June 2017.
Canteen Stores Department (CSD) is specified as eligible for a refund.
CSD can claim a refund of 50% of the integrated tax paid on inward supplies.
The refund applies to goods received for subsequent supply to Unit Run Canteens or authorized customers.
This notification is effective from 1st July 2017.

1100.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 6/2017-Central Tax (Rate) on 28th June 2017, which specifies that the Canteen Stores Department (CSD) under the Ministry of Defence is eligible to claim a refund of 50% of the central tax paid on inward supplies of goods. This refund applies specifically to goods that the CSD receives for the purpose of supplying them to Unit Run Canteens or to authorized customers. The notification is effective from 1st July 2017 and is issued under section 55 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This change is significant for the CSD as it allows them to recover a portion of the tax paid, thereby potentially reducing their overall tax burden. Businesses involved in supplying goods to the CSD or those operating within the framework of the Unit Run Canteens should take note of this refund provision, as it may impact their pricing and supply strategies.
key_points: Notification No. 6/2017-Central Tax (Rate) issued on 28th June 2017.
Canteen Stores Department (CSD) specified as eligible for a refund.
