These changes primarily affect businesses operating in Daman and Diu, ensuring they comply with the new local GST regulations.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (Daman and Diu) Rules, 2017'.
These rules come into effect from July 1, 2017.
The Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 are adapted for Daman and Diu with specific modifications.
Rule 1 substitutes 'Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017' with 'Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (Daman and Diu) Rules, 2017'.
Rule 90(4) is amended to state that deficiencies communicated in FORM GST RFD-03 under the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules are also deemed communicated under these rules.
In Rule 117(1), the second proviso is replaced to require specific details for claims under section 140 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act.
Clauses (a) and (b) of Rule 117(4) are omitted.
Rule 119 is substituted to require a declaration of stock in FORM GST TRAN-1 within ninety days of the appointed day.

1211.summary: Notification No. 09/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on June 28, 2017, introduces an exemption for intra-State supplies of goods or services received by a deductor under section 51 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. This exemption applies specifically when the supplier is not registered for GST. Additionally, the deductor must not be liable to register under any clause other than sub-clause (vi) of section 24 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act. This means that certain businesses or individuals who act as deductors can receive supplies without incurring Union Territory tax, provided they meet the specified conditions. The exemption is designed to ease the tax burden on deductors and is effective from July 1, 2017. This change is particularly relevant for businesses operating in Union Territories that engage with unregistered suppliers.
key_points: Exempts intra-State supplies of goods or services received by a deductor under section 51 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act from Union Territory tax.
The exemption applies only if the supplier is not registered for GST.
The deductor must not be liable to be registered under any clause other than sub-clause (vi) of section 24 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act.
This notification is effective from July 1, 2017.

1212.summary: The 'Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (Lakshadweep) Rules, 2017' have been introduced, effective from July 1, 2017. This notification adapts the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 specifically for the Union Territory of Lakshadweep, incorporating several modifications. Notably, Rule 1 now refers to the new Lakshadweep rules instead of the Central rules. Additionally, Rule 90(4) has been amended to clarify that any deficiencies noted in FORM GST RFD-03 under the Central GST Rules will also be considered communicated under the new rules. In Rule 117(1), the second proviso has been updated to require applicants to specify claims under the Central Sales Tax Act in detail. Furthermore, clauses (a) and (b) of Rule 117(4) have been removed, simplifying the requirements. Lastly, Rule 119 has been replaced to mandate that individuals subject to certain provisions must declare their stock in FORM GST TRAN-1 within ninety days of the appointed day. These changes primarily affect businesses operating in Lakshadweep, ensuring they comply with the new local GST framework.
key_points: The notification is titled 'Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (Lakshadweep) Rules, 2017'.
These rules come into effect from July 1, 2017.
The Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 are adapted for Lakshadweep with specific modifications.
Rule 1 substitutes 'Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017' with 'Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (Lakshadweep) Rules, 2017'.
Rule 90(4) is amended to state that deficiencies communicated in FORM GST RFD-03 under the Central GST Rules are also deemed communicated under these rules.
In Rule 117(1), the second proviso is replaced to require specific claims under the Central Sales Tax Act to be detailed in applications.
Clauses (a) and (b) of Rule 117(4) are omitted.
Rule 119 is replaced to require a declaration of stock held by a principal and agent in FORM GST TRAN-1 within ninety days of the appointed day.

1213.summary: Notification No. 10/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on June 28, 2017, introduces an exemption for intra-State supplies of second hand goods from Union territory tax. This exemption specifically applies to registered persons who are involved in the buying and selling of second hand goods. The key condition is that the goods must be received from suppliers who are not registered. The exemption is grounded in sub-section (4) of section 7 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and the valuation for tax purposes is determined according to sub-rule (5) of rule 32 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017. This change is significant for businesses dealing in second hand goods, as it reduces their tax burden when sourcing from unregistered suppliers. The notification is effective from July 1, 2017, allowing affected businesses to benefit from this exemption immediately.
key_points: Exempts intra-State supplies of second hand goods from Union territory tax.
Applies to registered persons dealing in buying and selling second hand goods.
Exemption is for goods received from suppliers who are not registered.
The exemption is applicable under sub-section (4) of section 7 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The valuation for tax purposes is determined under sub-rule (5) of rule 32 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017.
This notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1214.summary: Notification No. 10/2017-Central Tax, issued on June 30, 2017, establishes specific interest rates applicable under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (UTGST) Act and the Central Goods and Services Tax (CGST) Act. The interest rate for Section 21 of the UTGST Act, when read with sub-section (1) of Section 50 of the CGST Act, is set at 18%. For sub-section (3) of Section 50, the rate is 24%. Additionally, when Section 21 of the UTGST Act is considered with sub-section (12) of Section 54 and Section 56 of the CGST Act, the interest rate is fixed at 6%. Furthermore, a 9% interest rate applies when Section 21 of the UTGST Act is read with the Proviso to Section 56 of the CGST Act. These rates are crucial for businesses operating in Union Territories as they determine the interest payable on delayed payments or refunds. The notification takes effect from July 1, 2017, impacting all relevant taxpayers under the UTGST framework.
key_points: This notification is issued under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The notification fixes interest rates for various sections of the Union Territory and Central Goods and Services Tax Acts.
Interest rate of 18% is set for Section 21 of the UTGST Act read with sub-section (1) of Section 50 of the CGST Act.
Interest rate of 24% is set for Section 21 of the UTGST Act read with sub-section (3) of Section 50 of the CGST Act.
Interest rate of 6% is set for Section 21 of the UTGST Act read with sub-section (12) of Section 54 of the CGST Act.
Interest rate of 6% is also set for Section 21 of the UTGST Act read with Section 56 of the CGST Act.
Interest rate of 9% is set for Section 21 of the UTGST Act read with the Proviso to Section 56 of the CGST Act.
This notification comes into effect from July 1, 2017.

1215.summary: On 30th June 2017, a corrigendum was issued for Notification No. 11/2017 - Union Territory Tax (Rate), which was originally published on 28th June 2017. The change specifically addresses a phrase in line 26 of the original notification. It corrects 'scheme of classification of services' to 'scheme of classification of services annexed to notification No. 11/2017 - Central Tax (Rate)'. This amendment clarifies the reference to the classification scheme related to the Central Tax notification, ensuring that it accurately points to the correct document. The original Central Tax notification was also published on the same date, 28th June 2017, under G.S.R. 690(E). This correction is important for stakeholders who rely on the accurate classification of services for compliance and reporting under the GST framework. The amendment does not change any tax rates or compliance requirements but ensures clarity in the documentation.
key_points: This is a corrigendum to Notification No. 11/2017 - Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The corrigendum was issued on 30th June 2017.
In line 26 of the original notification, the phrase 'scheme of classification of services' is corrected.
The corrected phrase now reads 'scheme of classification of services annexed to notification No. 11/2017 - Central Tax (Rate)'.
The reference for the Central Tax notification is also updated to include its publication details.
The original Central Tax notification was published on 28th June 2017, under G.S.R. 690(E).

1216.summary: The Notification No. 11/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on June 28, 2017, establishes the tax rates for various intra-State services under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act. It specifies that all services under Chapter 99 are taxed at 2%, while construction services are taxed at 9%. The notification details various service categories, including distributive trade, accommodation, food and beverage, and transport services, with tax rates ranging from 2.5% to 9%. For instance, accommodation services in hotels with a declared tariff between ₹1,000 and ₹2,500 are taxed at 6%, while those exceeding ₹7,500 are taxed at 14%. Additionally, passenger transport services have specific rates, such as air travel in economy class being taxed at 2.5%. Each service category comes with specific conditions that affect the applicable tax rate, ensuring clarity for service providers and consumers alike. This notification is crucial for businesses operating in these sectors to understand their tax obligations.
key_points: Notification No. 11/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 28th June 2017.
Union territory tax is levied on intra-State supply of services as specified in the notification.
All services under Chapter 99 are taxed at 2% and construction services at 9%.
Various service categories including distributive trade, accommodation, food and beverage, and transport services are taxed at rates ranging from 2.5% to 9%.
Specific conditions apply for services like construction, accommodation, and transport, affecting the applicable tax rate.
For example, accommodation services in hotels with a declared tariff of ₹1,000 to ₹2,500 are taxed at 6%, while those above ₹7,500 are taxed at 14%.
Transport services have varying rates, with passenger transport by air in economy class taxed at 2.5%.
The notification outlines detailed descriptions and conditions for each service category.

1217.summary: Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax, issued on 30th June 2017, introduces specific requirements for mentioning Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes in tax invoices for registered persons in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax. Businesses with an annual turnover of less than ₹1.5 crore are exempt from including HSN codes in their invoices. However, those with a turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore must include 2 digits of HSN codes, while businesses with a turnover of ₹5 crore and above are required to include 4 digits. This change aims to standardize the invoicing process and enhance compliance among different categories of taxpayers. The new requirements will be effective from 1st July 2017, meaning businesses need to adjust their invoicing practices accordingly to align with these regulations.
key_points: Notification No. 11/2017-Central Tax issued on 30th June 2017.
This notification applies to the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (Andaman and Nicobar Islands).
Registered persons with an annual turnover of less than ₹1.5 crore are not required to mention Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes in tax invoices.
Those with an annual turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore must include 2 digits of HSN codes in their tax invoices.
Businesses with an annual turnover of ₹5 crore and above are required to include 4 digits of HSN codes in their tax invoices.
The notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1218.summary: The Notification No. 12/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on June 28, 2017, introduces exemptions for specific intra-State supply of services from Union Territory Tax. This includes services provided by charitable entities registered under section 12AA of the Income-tax Act, which are exempt from tax. Additionally, services related to the transfer of a going concern and pure services provided to government entities are also exempt. Transportation services for passengers and goods are included under this exemption, provided they meet certain conditions. Furthermore, services offered by the Central Government, State Government, and local authorities to business entities with an aggregate turnover of up to twenty lakh rupees (or ten lakh for special category states) are exempt, especially when the consideration does not exceed five thousand rupees in certain cases. The notification outlines various headings and descriptions of services that qualify for these exemptions, making it crucial for businesses and service providers to understand their eligibility under this framework.
key_points: Notification No. 12/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 28th June 2017.
The notification exempts certain intra-State supply of services from Union Territory Tax as per the specified conditions.
Services by charitable entities registered under section 12AA of the Income-tax Act are exempt from tax.
Services related to the transfer of a going concern and pure services to government entities are also exempt.
Transportation services for passengers and goods under specific conditions are exempt from tax.
Certain services provided by the Central Government, State Government, and local authorities to business entities with turnover limits are exempt.
The exemption applies to services with consideration not exceeding five thousand rupees in specific cases.
The notification includes various headings and descriptions of services eligible for exemption.

1219.summary: Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax, issued on 30th June 2017, outlines the requirements for businesses regarding the inclusion of Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes in their tax invoices. Effective from 1st July 2017, the notification specifies that businesses with an annual turnover of less than ₹1.5 crore are exempt from mentioning HSN codes in their invoices. For those with a turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore, they are required to include 2 digits of the HSN code. Meanwhile, businesses with an annual turnover of ₹5 crore or more must include 4 digits of the HSN code. This change aims to standardize the invoicing process and enhance compliance among different categories of businesses based on their turnover. It is essential for businesses to understand these requirements to ensure proper invoicing practices moving forward.
key_points: Notification No. 12/2017-Central Tax issued on 30th June 2017.
The notification specifies requirements for mentioning Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes in tax invoices.
Businesses with an annual turnover of less than ₹1.5 crore are not required to mention HSN codes.
Businesses with an annual turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore must mention 2 digits of HSN codes.
Businesses with an annual turnover of ₹5 crore and above must mention 4 digits of HSN codes.
This notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1220.summary: On 25th September 2017, a corrigendum was issued to amend Notification No. 13/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This change specifically alters the description of services provided by advocates. Previously, the notification detailed that services supplied by individual advocates, including senior advocates, to business entities were taxable. The new wording simplifies this to state that services provided by individual advocates or firms of advocates are taxable as legal services. Additionally, the definition of 'legal service' has been clarified to encompass any advice, consultancy, or assistance in any branch of law, explicitly including representational services before courts, tribunals, or authorities. This amendment aims to streamline the understanding of legal services under GST, making it clearer for both advocates and their clients regarding the scope of taxable services.
key_points: This corrigendum was issued on 25th September 2017.
It amends Notification No. 13/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment affects the description of services provided by advocates in the tax table.
The previous description included specific mention of services to business entities and contracts through other advocates.
The new description simplifies this to 'services provided by an individual advocate or firm of advocates by way of legal services.'
The definition of 'legal service' is clarified to include advice, consultancy, or assistance in any branch of law.
The explanation now explicitly states that it includes representational services before any court, tribunal, or authority.

1221.summary: Notification No. 13/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 28th June 2017, introduces a reverse charge mechanism for specific services under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act. Effective from 1st July 2017, this notification outlines that services provided by goods transport agencies, advocates, arbitral tribunals, and others will have the tax liability shifted to the recipient. This means that entities like factories, societies, and various business organizations will be responsible for paying the tax instead of the service providers. Additionally, it specifies that the person who pays for freight transportation is deemed the recipient of the service. Definitions for terms like 'Body Corporate' are included to ensure clarity. However, certain services, such as renting of immovable property, are excluded from this reverse charge mechanism. This change primarily affects businesses and organizations operating in the Union Territories that engage in these specified services.
key_points: Notification No. 13/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 28th June 2017.
This notification specifies that certain services will be taxed on a reverse charge basis.
The services affected include those provided by goods transport agencies, advocates, arbitral tribunals, and others as listed in the table.
The reverse charge applies to recipients such as factories, societies, business entities, and various other organizations.
The notification comes into effect on 1st July 2017.
It clarifies that the person liable to pay freight for transportation is considered the recipient of the service.
Definitions for terms like 'Body Corporate' and 'business entity' are provided as per existing laws.
The notification excludes certain services from the reverse charge mechanism, such as renting of immovable property.

1222.summary: Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax, issued on 30th June 2017, introduces new requirements for tax invoices in the Union Territory of Dadra and Nagar Haveli based on annual turnover. Specifically, businesses with an annual turnover of less than ₹1.5 crore are exempt from mentioning Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes in their tax invoices. For those with a turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore, they are required to include 2 digits of HSN codes. Businesses with a turnover of ₹5 crore or more must include 4 digits of HSN codes in their invoices. This change aims to standardize the invoicing process and enhance compliance with GST regulations. The new rules will take effect from 1st July 2017, impacting how registered persons issue their tax invoices based on their turnover brackets.
key_points: Notification No. 13/2017-Central Tax issued on 30th June 2017.
The notification applies to the Union Territory of Dadra and Nagar Haveli.
Registered persons with annual turnover less than ₹1.5 crore are not required to mention Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes in tax invoices.
Registered persons with annual turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore must mention 2 digits of HSN codes in tax invoices.
Registered persons with annual turnover of ₹5 crore and above must mention 4 digits of HSN codes in tax invoices.
The notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1223.summary: Notification No. 14/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 28th June 2017, clarifies that certain activities performed by the Central Government, State Government, or local authorities will not be classified as a supply of goods or services under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act. Specifically, this exemption applies to services related to functions assigned to a Panchayat as per article 243G of the Constitution. This means that when these authorities engage in activities tied to their responsibilities under this constitutional provision, they will not incur Union Territory GST liabilities for those activities. This notification is effective from 1st July 2017, impacting how local governance functions are treated under GST. It is essential for local authorities and government bodies to understand this exemption to ensure proper compliance and reporting under the GST framework.
key_points: Notification No. 14/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 28th June 2017.
This notification specifies that certain activities by the Central Government, State Government, or local authorities are not considered a supply of goods or services.
The specific activities exempted are those related to functions entrusted to a Panchayat under article 243G of the Constitution.
The notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1224.summary: Notification No. 14/2017-Central Tax, issued on 30th June 2017, introduces specific requirements for mentioning Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes in tax invoices under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (Daman and Diu) Rules, 2017. Businesses with an annual turnover of less than ₹1.5 crore are exempt from mentioning HSN codes. For those with a turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore, they must include 2 digits of the HSN code in their invoices. Companies with a turnover of ₹5 crore or more are required to include 4 digits of the HSN code. This change aims to standardize the invoicing process and enhance compliance. The new requirements take effect from 1st July 2017, impacting how businesses in Daman and Diu prepare their tax invoices based on their annual turnover. This notification is part of the government's ongoing efforts to streamline GST regulations in Union Territories.
key_points: Notification No. 14/2017-Central Tax was issued on 30th June 2017.
This notification applies to the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (Daman and Diu) Rules, 2017.
It specifies that registered persons with an annual turnover of less than ₹1.5 crore need not mention Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes in tax invoices.
For those with an annual turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore, 2 digits of HSN codes must be mentioned.
Businesses with an annual turnover of ₹5 crore and above are required to mention 4 digits of HSN codes.
The notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1225.summary: Notification No. 15/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 28th June 2017, introduces a significant change regarding the refund of unutilised input tax credit. Effective from 1st July 2017, this notification states that no refund will be allowed for unutilised input tax credit in relation to certain services. Specifically, the services affected are those listed under sub-item (b) of item 5 of Schedule II of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This decision is made under the authority of clause (xiv) of section 21 of the Union Territory Goods and Service Tax, 2017, and is also tied to sub-section (3) of section 54 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. Businesses providing these specific services will need to be aware that they cannot claim refunds for any unutilised input tax credit, which may impact their cash flow and financial planning. This change is crucial for service providers operating in the Union Territories, as it directly affects their tax recovery options.
key_points: Notification No. 15/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 28th June 2017.
Effective date of the notification is 1st July 2017.
No refund of unutilised input tax credit is allowed for specific services.
The services affected are specified in sub-item (b) of item 5 of Schedule II of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
This notification is issued under clause (xiv) of section 21 of the Union Territory Goods and Service Tax, 2017.
It is also related to sub-section (3) of section 54 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.

1226.summary: Notification No. 15/2017-Central Tax, issued on 30th June 2017, introduces specific requirements for mentioning Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes in tax invoices under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (Lakshadweep) Rules, 2017. This change affects registered persons based on their annual turnover. If a business has an annual turnover of less than ₹1.5 crore, they are not required to include any HSN code in their invoices. For those with a turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore, they must include 2 digits of the HSN code. Businesses with a turnover of ₹5 crore or more are required to mention 4 digits of the HSN code. This notification is effective from 1st July 2017, meaning businesses need to comply with these requirements starting from that date. This structured approach aims to standardize the invoicing process and enhance clarity in tax reporting for different turnover brackets.
key_points: Notification No. 15/2017-Central Tax issued on 30th June 2017.
This notification pertains to the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax (Lakshadweep) Rules, 2017.
It specifies that registered persons must mention Harmonised System of Nomenclature (HSN) codes in tax invoices based on their annual turnover.
For turnover less than ₹1.5 crore, no HSN code is required.
For turnover between ₹1.5 crore and ₹5 crore, 2 digits of HSN code must be mentioned.
For turnover of ₹5 crore and above, 4 digits of HSN code must be mentioned.
The notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1227.summary: Notification No. 16/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 28th June 2017, outlines the conditions under which the United Nations and specified international organisations can claim refunds of union territory tax on goods and services used for official purposes. Additionally, foreign diplomatic missions and consular posts in India are also eligible for refunds, but they must meet specific requirements. These include obtaining a certificate from the Ministry of External Affairs and providing an undertaking for services received. For goods, the mission must certify that the items are for their use and will not be disposed of within three years. If the Ministry later withdraws the certificate, the refund will no longer be available from that date. This notification is effective from 1st July 2017, impacting international organisations and foreign diplomatic entities operating in India.
key_points: Notification No. 16/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 28th June 2017.
This notification specifies that the United Nations and specified international organisations can claim refunds of union territory tax paid on goods and services for official use.
Foreign diplomatic missions and consular posts in India can also claim refunds, subject to specific conditions outlined in the notification.
Refunds for foreign diplomatic missions require a certificate from the Ministry of External Affairs and an undertaking for services received.
For goods, a certificate must confirm that they are for the mission's use and not disposed of within three years.
If the Ministry of External Affairs withdraws the certificate, the refund will no longer be available from that date.
The notification comes into effect from 1st July 2017.

1228.summary: Notification No. 16/2017-Central Tax, issued on 13th October 2017, brings an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 2/2017-Union Territory Tax, which was published on 27th June 2017. The key change in this amendment is the increase of the threshold limit for registration under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax from seventy-five lakh rupees to one crore rupees. This adjustment is made under the authority of section 10 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, and also refers to section 21 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. As a result, businesses with a turnover below one crore rupees will not be required to register under the Union Territory GST, which could ease the compliance burden for smaller businesses. This change is significant for those operating in Union Territories, as it raises the bar for mandatory registration, potentially allowing more businesses to operate without the complexities of GST registration.
key_points: Notification No. 16/2017-Central Tax was issued on 13th October 2017.
This notification amends Notification No. 2/2017-Union Territory Tax dated 27th June 2017.
The amendment changes the threshold limit from 'seventy-five lakh rupees' to 'one crore rupees'.
This change is made under the powers of section 10 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It also references section 21 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The amendment affects the threshold for registration under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax.

1229.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 17/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 28th June 2017, which will take effect from 1st July 2017. This notification mandates that the electronic commerce operator must pay the tax on intra-State supplies for certain services. Specifically, this includes transportation services provided by radio-taxis, motorcabs, maxicabs, and motorcycles. Additionally, it covers accommodation services offered in hotels, inns, guest houses, clubs, campsites, and other commercial lodging facilities. However, if the service provider is registered under clause (vi) of section 21 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, they are exempt from this requirement. The notification also clarifies the definitions of 'radio taxi', 'maxicab', 'motorcab', and 'motorcycle' according to the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988. This change primarily affects electronic commerce operators and service providers in the transportation and accommodation sectors within Union Territories.
key_points: Notification No. 17/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 28th June 2017.
Effective from 1st July 2017.
Tax on intra-State supplies for specific services is to be paid by the electronic commerce operator.
Services affected include transportation of passengers by radio-taxi, motorcab, maxicab, and motorcycle.
Also includes accommodation services in hotels, inns, guest houses, clubs, campsites, or other commercial lodging places.
Exemption applies if the service provider is liable for registration under clause (vi) of section 21 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Definitions provided for 'radio taxi', 'maxicab', 'motorcab', and 'motorcycle' as per the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988.

1230.summary: Notification No. 17/2017-Central Tax, issued on 24th October 2017, addresses the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. It clarifies that any notifications previously issued under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 will also apply to the Union Territory Act. This means that the rules and provisions that were established for the Central GST will automatically extend to the Union Territory GST, ensuring consistency in tax regulations across different jurisdictions. Importantly, this notification is effective retroactively from 22nd June 2017, which means that any actions or compliance requirements from that date will be governed by this notification. This change is significant for businesses operating in Union Territories, as they will now have a clearer understanding of how existing GST notifications apply to them. Overall, this notification aims to streamline the tax framework for Union Territories by aligning it with the Central GST provisions.
key_points: Notification No. 17/2017-Central Tax was issued on 24th October 2017.
This notification relates to the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It states that notifications under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 are automatically extended to the Union Territory Act.
The provisions of this notification are effective from 22nd June 2017.

1231.summary: Notification No. 18/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 30th June 2017, introduces significant amendments to the earlier Notification No. 1/2017. Specifically, it adds new entries under the 2.5% tax rate for various types of mineral or chemical fertilizers, including nitrogenous, phosphatic, and potassic fertilizers, as well as mixed fertilizers in small packages. These new entries are numbered 182A to 182D and clarify that they are not intended for use as fertilizers. Additionally, the notification removes entries 66 to 69 from Schedule II, which were previously listed under the 6% tax rate. These changes will take effect from 1st July 2017. Businesses dealing with fertilizers will need to take note of these new classifications and the removal of the previous entries to with the updated tax structure.
key_points: Notification No. 18/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 30th June 2017.
This notification amends Notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
New entries for 2.5% tax rate are added for mineral or chemical fertilizers under serial numbers 182A to 182D.
The new entries include nitrogenous, phosphatic, potassic fertilizers, and mixed fertilizers in specific packaging.
Entries 66 to 69 under the 6% tax rate in Schedule II are omitted.
The changes come into effect from 1st July 2017.

1232.summary: Notification No. 19/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 18th August 2017, introduces amendments to the earlier Notification No. 1/2017, which was published on 28th June 2017. This amendment adds new entries to Schedule III, all subject to a 9% tax rate. The newly included items are essential parts for tractors, such as tyres, tubes, hydraulic pumps, and gear boxes, with specific serial numbers ranging from 452A to 452O. For instance, 452A refers to tyres for tractors, while 452B covers tubes for those tyres. Other entries include agricultural diesel engines with a cylinder capacity exceeding 250 cc, bumpers, brakes, and steering wheels. This change is significant for manufacturers and suppliers of tractor parts, as it clarifies the tax obligations for these specific items under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act.
key_points: Notification No. 19/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 18th August 2017.
This notification amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
New entries have been added to Schedule III at a tax rate of 9%.
The new entries include various tractor parts such as tyres, tubes, hydraulic pumps, and gear boxes.
Specific serial numbers from 452A to 452O have been assigned to these new entries.
The new entries cover items like agricultural diesel engines, bumpers, brakes, and steering wheels for tractors.

1233.summary: Notification No. 20/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 22nd August 2017, amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This amendment introduces new tax entries for composite supply of works contracts, specifically for projects involving historical monuments, irrigation works, and housing schemes like the Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana. It sets a tax rate of 6% for services such as passenger transport by motorcab and goods transport agency services, with conditions that input tax credits cannot be claimed. The definition of a goods transport agency is clarified, requiring the issuance of a consignment note. Additionally, the notification updates tax entries related to printing services and textiles, specifying applicable rates. A new cultural service entry includes 'planetarium' alongside drama. These changes primarily impact contractors, transport service providers, and businesses in the printing and textile sectors, ensuring they are aware of the updated tax obligations and definitions.
key_points: This notification is issued on 22nd August 2017 as Notification No. 20/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate).
It amends Notification No. 11/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
Changes include new entries for composite supply of works contracts related to historical monuments, irrigation works, and housing projects under specific government schemes.
The tax rate for certain services, such as transport of passengers by motorcab and services of goods transport agencies, is set at 6% with specific conditions.
The definition of goods transport agency is clarified, stating it must issue a consignment note.
The notification also updates entries related to printing services and textiles, specifying tax rates and conditions.
New provisions include the addition of 'planetarium' to the list of cultural services.
The changes affect contractors, transport service providers, and businesses involved in printing and textiles.

1234.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 21/2017, amending the earlier Notification No. 12/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax rates. This new notification introduces several important changes. Firstly, it adds a new entry (9A) for services provided by FIFA and its subsidiaries related to the FIFA U-17 World Cup 2017, which will be taxed at a nil rate, contingent upon certification from the Director of Sports. Additionally, two new entries (11A and 11B) have been added for services rendered by Fair Price Shops to both the Central and State Governments under the Public Distribution System, also at a nil tax rate. Furthermore, the notification updates entry 35 by replacing the existing insurance scheme names with the 'Restructured Weather Based Crop Insurance Scheme (RWCIS)' and 'Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY)'. Lastly, it clarifies that a Limited Liability Partnership, as defined under the Limited Liability Partnership Act, will be recognized as a partnership firm. These changes primarily affect entities involved in these specific services and insurance schemes within the Union Territories.
key_points: Inserts new entry 9A for services related to FIFA U-17 World Cup 2017, with a nil tax rate, requiring certification from the Director (Sports).
Inserts new entries 11A and 11B for services provided by Fair Price Shops to Central and State Governments under the Public Distribution System (PDS), both with a nil tax rate.
Amends entry 35 by substituting 'Weather Based Crop Insurance Scheme' with 'Restructured Weather Based Crop Insurance Scheme (RWCIS)'.
Amends entry 35 by substituting 'National Agricultural Insurance Scheme' with 'Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY)'.
Inserts a new clause in the Explanation of paragraph 3, stating that a Limited Liability Partnership is considered a partnership firm.

1235.summary: The recent Notification No. 22/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 22nd August 2017, introduces important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 13/2017, which was published on 28th June 2017. One significant change is the addition of a clause in the Table that specifies that goods transport agencies (GTA) who have not paid the Union Territory tax at the rate of 6% are now included in the tax framework. This means that GTAs that fall under this category will need to be aware of their tax obligations. Additionally, the notification clarifies the status of Limited Liability Partnerships (LLPs) by stating that they will be considered as partnership firms under the Limited Liability Partnership Act, 2008. This clarification is crucial for LLPs as it aligns their treatment with that of traditional partnership firms in the context of Union Territory tax. Overall, these amendments aim to provide clearer guidelines for businesses operating in the Union Territories.
key_points: Notification No. 22/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 22nd August 2017.
Amends Notification No. 13/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
Inserts a new clause in the Table against serial number 1, stating that goods transport agencies (GTA) who have not paid Union Territory tax at the rate of 6% are included.
Adds a new clause (e) in the Explanation, defining a Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) as a partnership firm under the Limited Liability Partnership Act, 2008.

1236.summary: Notification No. 23/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 22nd August 2017, introduces an important amendment to the earlier notification No. 17/2017, which was published on 28th June 2017. The amendment adds a new clause (iii) that specifically addresses services related to housekeeping, such as plumbing and carpentering. This means that these services are now included under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax framework, but with a notable exception. If the service provider is required to register under clause (vi) of section 21 of the UTGST Act, they will not benefit from this exemption. This change is significant for service providers in the housekeeping sector, as it clarifies their tax obligations. The amendment was made following recommendations from the GST Council, ensuring that the tax structure remains aligned with current service industry practices. The principal notification was originally published as G.S.R. 708 (E) in the Gazette of India, which is important for record-keeping and compliance.
key_points: This notification is No. 23/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 22nd August 2017.
It amends the earlier notification No. 17/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new clause (iii) is added to the first paragraph of the earlier notification.
The new clause (iii) covers services by way of housekeeping, including plumbing and carpentering.
The exemption applies unless the service provider is liable for registration under clause (vi) of section 21 of the UTGST Act.
This amendment is based on the recommendations of the GST Council.
The principal notification was published in the Gazette of India, Extraordinary, as G.S.R. 708 (E).

1237.summary: Notification No. 24/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 21st September 2017, brings significant changes to the earlier notification No. 11/2017. Specifically, it revises the entries in the Table against serial number 3, particularly item (vi). The new item (vi) now details the types of construction and maintenance services provided to the Central Government, State Government, Union Territory, local authorities, or governmental authorities. These services pertain to structures meant for educational, clinical, cultural, or residential purposes, primarily for self-use or for employees. This replaces the previous item (vi) entirely. Additionally, a new item (vii) has been introduced to cover construction services that do not fall under the earlier categories. This amendment is crucial for businesses involved in providing such services, as it clarifies the scope and nature of taxable activities under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax framework.
key_points: This notification is No. 24/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 21st September 2017.
It amends notification No. 11/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment specifically alters the entries in the Table against serial number 3, item (vi).
The new item (vi) describes services provided to various government entities related to construction and maintenance of specific structures.
These structures include those for educational, clinical, cultural, or residential purposes predominantly for self-use or employees.
The previous item (vi) has been replaced entirely with this new definition.
The amendment also introduces a new item (vii) for construction services not covered by the previous items.
The notification references the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 for context.

1238.summary: Notification No. 25/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 21st September 2017, introduces an amendment to the previous notification No. 12/2017, which was published on 28th June 2017. This amendment adds a new entry to the tax rate table, specifically after serial number 81. The new entry pertains to Chapter 9996, which covers services related to the right of admission to events organized under the FIFA U-17 World Cup 2017. Importantly, the tax rate for these services has been set to 'Nil', meaning that no Union Territory tax will be levied on these admissions. This change is significant for event organizers and attendees of the FIFA U-17 World Cup, as it effectively reduces the cost of admission by eliminating the tax burden. This amendment reflects the government's intention to promote sports events and enhance public interest in such activities.
key_points: This notification is No. 25/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 21st September 2017.
It amends the earlier notification No. 12/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new entry has been added to the Table after serial number 81.
The new entry is for Chapter 9996, concerning services related to admission rights for events organized under the FIFA U-17 World Cup 2017.
The tax rate for this service is set to 'Nil'.

1239.summary: Notification No. 26/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on September 21, 2017, provides a significant tax exemption for specific goods. It exempts the intra-state supply of heavy water and nuclear fuels, which are categorized under Chapter 28 of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975. This exemption is specifically applicable when these goods are supplied by the Department of Atomic Energy to the Nuclear Power Corporation of India Ltd. The exemption covers the entire Union Territory tax that would typically be levied under section 7 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. This decision was made in the public interest and follows recommendations from the GST Council. As a result, the Nuclear Power Corporation of India Ltd will benefit from this exemption, reducing their tax burden on these essential supplies. This notification reflects the government's commitment to supporting the nuclear energy sector in India.
key_points: Exemption applies to intra-state supply of heavy water and nuclear fuels.
These goods fall under Chapter 28 of the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975.
The exemption is granted by the Department of Atomic Energy.
The recipient of the exempted goods is the Nuclear Power Corporation of India Ltd.
The exemption is from the whole of the Union Territory tax under section 7 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
This notification is issued under the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 8 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
The notification is dated 21st September 2017.
It was issued following recommendations from the GST Council.

1240.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 27/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 22nd September 2017, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017. This notification introduces several changes across different schedules. In Schedule I, new entries for walnuts, dried tamarind, and roasted gram have been added, while existing entries have been modified to clarify brand name definitions. Schedule II has removed walnuts from S. No. 15 and excluded tamarind from dried fruits in S. No. 17. Additionally, S. No. 49 now provides a broader definition of brand names. Schedule III includes updates to diabetic foods and introduces rice rubber rolls for paddy de-husking machines. In Schedule IV, medical-grade gloves and specifications for computer monitors have been updated. Lastly, the definitions of 'brand name' and 'registered brand name' have been clarified, ensuring that businesses understand the implications of these terms. These changes affect various sectors, particularly those dealing with food products and consumer goods.
key_points: Notification No. 27/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 22nd September 2017.
Amends Notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In Schedule I, changes include new entries for walnuts (0802), dried tamarind (0813), and roasted gram (2106).
In Schedule II, walnuts are removed from S. No. 15, and tamarind is excluded from dried fruits in S. No. 17.
S. No. 49 in Schedule II now includes a broader definition of brand names.
Schedule III now includes changes to diabetic foods and adds rice rubber rolls for paddy de-husking machines.
Schedule IV updates include medical-grade gloves and changes to computer monitor specifications.
The definition of 'brand name' and 'registered brand name' has been clarified in the Explanation.

1241.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 28/2017 on 22nd September 2017, which amends the earlier Notification No. 2/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax rates. This amendment clarifies the conditions under which certain goods can be exempt from tax based on their branding. Specifically, for items listed under serial numbers 27, 29, 45, and 65-108, the wording has been revised to specify that goods must not only bear a registered brand name but also clarify the conditions under which an actionable claim or enforceable right can be foregone. Additionally, new entries have been added for Cotton seed oil cake, Khadi fabric, and clay idols, expanding the list of exempt goods. The definitions of 'brand name' and 'registered brand name' have also been updated to provide clearer guidance. Furthermore, two new annexures have been introduced, outlining the process for voluntarily foregoing rights on brand names and listing various indigenous handmade musical instruments. These changes will primarily affect manufacturers and sellers of the specified goods in the Union Territories.
key_points: Notification No. 28/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 22nd September 2017.
This notification amends Notification No. 2/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
Changes include revised wording for items at serial numbers 27, 29, 45, 65-108, clarifying conditions for registered brand names.
New entries added for serial numbers 102A (Cotton seed oil cake), 130A (Khadi fabric), and 135A (Idols made of clay).
The definition of 'brand name' and 'registered brand name' has been updated in the Explanation section.
Annexures I and II have been added, detailing conditions for foregoing rights on brand names and listing indigenous handmade musical instruments.

1242.summary: Notification No. 29/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 22nd September 2017, introduces an amendment to the earlier Notification No. 5/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Specifically, it adds a new entry for 'Corduroy fabrics' in the tax rate table, positioned after serial number 6. This means that businesses dealing with corduroy fabrics will now have a specific classification under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax framework. The inclusion of this entry is significant for manufacturers, wholesalers, and retailers of corduroy fabrics, as it clarifies the tax treatment applicable to these goods. This amendment reflects the government's ongoing efforts to refine and update the tax structure to ensure clarity and compliance within the textile sector. Stakeholders in the textile industry should take note of this change to ensure they apply the correct tax rates moving forward.
key_points: Notification No. 29/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 22nd September 2017.
This notification amends Notification No. 5/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment adds a new entry in the Table after S. No. 6.
The new entry is S. No. 6A for 'Corduroy fabrics' under the relevant tax rate.

1243.summary: On 29th September 2017, the Government of India issued Notification No. 30/2017 to amend the earlier Notification No. 12/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. This amendment introduces a new entry, numbered 9B, in the existing Table of the notification. The new entry specifically addresses the supply of services related to transit cargo destined for Nepal and Bhutan, which are classified as landlocked countries. Importantly, the tax rate for these services has been set to Nil, meaning that no Union Territory tax will be levied on such transactions. This change is significant for businesses involved in logistics and trade with these neighboring countries, as it reduces their tax burden on transit services. The last amendment prior to this was made by Notification No. 25/2017 on 21st September 2017, indicating ongoing adjustments to the tax framework for Union Territories. Overall, this amendment aims to facilitate smoother trade operations with Nepal and Bhutan by eliminating tax on specific services.
key_points: This notification is issued on 29th September 2017.
It amends Notification No. 12/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new entry is added to the Table after serial number 9A.
The new entry is 9B, which pertains to Chapter 99.
The new entry 9B covers the supply of services associated with transit cargo to Nepal and Bhutan.
The tax rate for this new entry is set to Nil.
The previous amendment to this notification was made by Notification No. 25/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 21st September 2017.

1244.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 31/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 13th October 2017, which amends the earlier Notification No. 11/2017 dated 28th June 2017. Key changes include the expansion of terms in the table for serial number 3, where 'Government, a local authority or a Governmental authority' is replaced with 'Central Government, State Government, Union territory, a local authority, a Governmental Authority or a Government Entity'. Additionally, for items (iii) and (vi), a new proviso specifies that services provided to a Government Entity must be related to work entrusted by the Central or local authorities. The amendment also clarifies the conditions for composite works contracts involving earthwork under item (vii). Furthermore, item (vi) under serial number 8 now specifies conditions for input tax credit related to passenger transport services. Changes to item (v) under serial number 9 clarify the conditions for the transportation of natural gas. Lastly, new sub-items under serial number 26 introduce job work services related to printing and various food products, expanding the scope of taxable services.
key_points: Notification No. 31/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 13th October 2017.
It amends Notification No. 11/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.

Changes include substituting terms in the table for serial number 3, items (iii) and (vi) to include 'Central Government, State Government, Union territory, a local authority, a Governmental Authority or a Government Entity'.
For items (iii) and (vi), a new proviso was added stating that services supplied to a Government Entity must relate to a work entrusted by the Central Government or local authorities.
Item (vii) was amended to specify a composite supply of works contract involving predominantly earth work, with a new proviso regarding procurement by Government Entities.
Changes were made to item (vi) under serial number 8 regarding transport of passengers, specifying conditions for input tax credit.
Item (v) under serial number 9 was amended to clarify conditions for transportation of natural gas through pipelines.
New sub-items were added under serial number 26, including job work services related to printing and various food products.

1245.summary: The recent amendment through Notification No. 32/2017 modifies the earlier Notification No. 12/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax (Rate). It updates the definition of 'governmental authority' in serial number 5 to include various government levels and local authorities. A new entry, 9C, has been added to exempt services provided by a Government Entity to different government bodies against grants from tax. Additionally, entry 21A has been introduced to exempt services from goods transport agencies to unregistered persons, with specific exclusions. Another new entry, 23A, relates to access to roads or bridges for which payment is made as an annuity, also exempt from tax. The amendment clarifies the upfront amounts for long-term leases of industrial plots in serial number 41, specifying the entities involved. Lastly, the definitions of 'Governmental Authority' and 'Government Entity' have been revised to provide clearer criteria for these terms. This notification impacts various government entities and service providers in the Union Territories.
key_points: Amends Notification No. 12/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
Substitutes 'governmental authority' with 'Central Government, State Government, Union territory, local authority or Governmental Authority' in serial number 5.
Inserts new entry 9C for services supplied by a Government Entity to various authorities against grants, with a tax rate of Nil.
Inserts new entry 21A for services provided by a goods transport agency to unregistered persons, with a tax rate of Nil, excluding certain recipients.
Inserts new entry 23A for service by way of access to a road or bridge on payment of annuity, with a tax rate of Nil.
Substitutes entry in serial number 41 regarding upfront amounts for long-term leases of industrial plots, clarifying the entities involved.
Revises clause (zf) to define 'Governmental Authority' and adds a new definition for 'Government Entity' in paragraph 2.

1246.summary: Notification No. 33/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 13th October 2017, introduces an amendment to the previous notification No. 13/2017, which was published on 28th June 2017. The key change is the addition of a new entry in the Table of the earlier notification. This new entry, now listed as serial number 10, specifically addresses the supply of services provided by members of the Overseeing Committee to the Reserve Bank of India. The members of this committee are appointed by the Reserve Bank itself. This amendment clarifies the tax treatment of these specific services under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax framework. Businesses and entities involved in providing these services should take note of this change, as it may impact their compliance and reporting obligations under the Union Territory GST regime.
key_points: This notification is No. 33/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 13th October 2017.
It amends the earlier notification No. 13/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new entry has been added to the Table in the notification, specifically after serial number 9.
The new entry (serial number 10) pertains to the supply of services by members of the Overseeing Committee to the Reserve Bank of India.
The members of the Overseeing Committee are constituted by the Reserve Bank of India.

1247.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 34/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 13th October 2017, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017 dated 28th June 2017. Key changes include the revision of entries in various schedules, such as Schedule I, where S. No. 29 is updated to '0802, 0813', and new entries for sliced dried mangoes and khakhra are introduced. In Schedule II, the entry for dried fruits now excludes mangoes, and S. No. 111 is completely omitted. Schedule III also sees changes, particularly in S. No. 16, which now specifies that khakhra and plain chapatti are excluded from certain categories. Additionally, a new proviso in the ANNEXURE clarifies the responsibilities of brand owners and packers regarding actionable claims on brand names. These amendments affect businesses dealing with the specified goods and require them to adjust their GST compliance accordingly.
key_points: Notification No. 34/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 13th October 2017.
Amends Notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In Schedule I, S. No. 29's entry is changed to '0802, 0813'.
New entries added: '30A 0804 Mangoes sliced, dried' and '99A 1905 or 2106 Khakhra, plain chapatti or roti'.
In Schedule II, S. No. 16's entry is revised to exclude 'mangoes' from dried fruits.
S. No. 111 and related entries are omitted in Schedule II.
In Schedule III, S. No. 16 now excludes 'khakhra, plain chapatti or roti'.
A new proviso is added in the ANNEXURE regarding brand name claims and packing responsibilities.

1248.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 35/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 13th October 2017, which amends the earlier Notification No. 2/2017. This new notification introduces a new entry, 122A, for 'Duty Credit Scrips' in the tax rate schedule, which will be applicable after the existing entry 122. Additionally, it adds entry 150, which pertains to the supply of goods by a Government entity to various government bodies against grants. The definition of 'Government Entity' has also been clarified, specifying that it includes any authority or body with at least 90% government ownership. Furthermore, a new proviso has been added in ANNEXURE I, which outlines the requirements for brand owners and packagers when they are different entities. Specifically, if a brand owner is not the same as the packager, they must file an affidavit confirming they are voluntarily giving up their rights to the brand name. This notification affects businesses dealing with government entities and those involved in branding and packaging goods.
key_points: Notification No. 35/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 13th October 2017.
Inserts new entry 122A for 'Duty Credit Scrips' after S. No. 122 in the Schedule.
Inserts new entry 150 for 'Supply of goods by a Government entity' after S. No. 149 in the Schedule.
Defines 'Government Entity' in the Explanation, including authorities or bodies with 90% or more government participation.
Adds a proviso in ANNEXURE I regarding brand name claims and packing responsibilities.
Requires an affidavit from the brand owner if they are different from the packager, stating they are voluntarily foregoing rights on the brand name.

1249.summary: Notification No. 36/2017, issued on October 13, 2017, introduces an important amendment to the earlier Notification No. 4/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax rates. Specifically, it adds a new entry to the existing table of goods subject to tax. This new entry, listed as S. No. 6, includes used vehicles, seized and confiscated goods, old and used goods, as well as waste and scrap. The suppliers of these goods can be various government entities, including the Central Government, State Government, Union territory, or local authorities. Importantly, the recipient of these supplies must be a registered person under the GST framework. This amendment clarifies the tax treatment of these specific goods, ensuring that registered persons engaging in transactions involving these items are aware of their obligations under the Union Territory GST Act.
key_points: Notification No. 36/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 13th October 2017.
This notification amends Notification No. 4/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new entry is added after S. No. 5 in the table of the earlier notification.
The new entry (S. No. 6) covers used vehicles, seized and confiscated goods, old and used goods, waste, and scrap.
The supplier of these goods can be the Central Government, State Government, Union territory, or a local authority.
The recipient of the supply must be a registered person.

1250.summary: Notification No. 37/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 13th October 2017, establishes the tax rate for intra-State supplies of motor vehicles at 65% of the union territory tax as per Notification No. 1/2017. This rate applies specifically to motor vehicles and is subject to certain conditions. Notably, the provisions of this notification will cease to apply after 1st July 2020. For the tax to be applicable, the motor vehicle must have been purchased by the lesser before 1st July 2017 and supplied on lease before that date. Additionally, the supplier must be a registered person and should not have claimed input tax credit on the vehicle. This notification is crucial for businesses dealing in motor vehicles within union territories, as it outlines the specific tax obligations and conditions that must be met for compliance.
key_points: Notification No. 37/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 13th October 2017.
This notification specifies the union territory tax rate on intra-State supplies of motor vehicles.
The tax rate for motor vehicles is set at 65% of the union territory tax applicable under Notification No. 1/2017.
The provisions of this notification will not apply on or after 1st July 2020.
Conditions for applicability include that the motor vehicle must have been purchased by the lesser before 1st July 2017 and supplied on lease before the same date.
The supplier must be a registered person and must not have availed input tax credit on the motor vehicle.

1251.summary: Notification No. 38/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 13th October 2017, brings an important amendment to the earlier notification No. 8/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Specifically, this new notification removes the proviso under Paragraph 1 of the previous notification. As a result of this change, the exemption that was previously outlined will now be applicable to all registered persons until 31st March 2018. This means that businesses registered under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act will benefit from this exemption without the conditions that were previously stipulated. The amendment aims to simplify the tax structure and provide relief to businesses operating in the Union Territories during this period. Overall, this change is significant for registered taxpayers as it clarifies the exemption timeline and conditions, ensuring they can plan their tax obligations accordingly.
key_points: The notification is No. 38/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 13th October 2017.
It amends the earlier notification No. 8/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment involves the omission of the proviso under Paragraph 1 of the earlier notification.
The exemption provided in the earlier notification will now apply to all registered persons until 31st March 2018.

1252.summary: Notification No. 39/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on October 18, 2017, introduces a 2.5% tax rate on intra-State supplies of specific food preparations. These preparations must be packaged in unit containers and are intended for free distribution to economically weaker sections of society under government-approved programs. To qualify for this tax rate, suppliers must obtain a certificate from a Deputy Secretary or an equivalent officer, confirming that the food has been distributed as intended. This distribution must occur within five months from the supply date, although the jurisdictional commissioner can allow extensions. The notification also clarifies that the definitions of tariff items and classifications are based on the First Schedule of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975, and the interpretation rules from this Act will apply. This change primarily affects suppliers of these food preparations, ensuring they comply with the new tax rate and conditions for distribution.
key_points: The notification is issued under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It establishes a Union Territory tax rate of 2.5% on intra-State supplies of specified goods.
The goods affected are food preparations in unit containers for free distribution to economically weaker sections.
These goods must be distributed under a program approved by the Central or State Government.
A certificate from a Deputy Secretary or equivalent is required to confirm the distribution of these goods.
The distribution must occur within five months from the date of supply, with possible extensions by the jurisdictional commissioner.
The notification references the First Schedule to the Customs Tariff Act, 1975 for tariff classifications.
Interpretation rules from the Customs Tariff Act apply to this notification.

1253.summary: Notification No. 40/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on October 23, 2017, introduces an exemption for the intra-State supply of taxable goods by registered suppliers to registered recipients for export. This exemption reduces the applicable Union territory tax to 0.05% under section 7 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017. To qualify for this exemption, several conditions must be met. The registered supplier must issue a tax invoice, and the registered recipient must export the goods within 90 days of this invoice. Additionally, the recipient must include the supplier's GSTIN and invoice number in the shipping bill and must be registered with an Export Promotion Council or a recognized Commodity Board. Goods must be transported directly to the export point or a registered warehouse. If the recipient aggregates supplies from multiple suppliers, the goods must first go to a registered warehouse before being exported. Importantly, if the recipient fails to export the goods within the stipulated 90 days, the supplier will not be eligible for the tax exemption.
key_points: Exemption applies to intra-State supply of taxable goods by a registered supplier to a registered recipient for export.
The exemption reduces the Union territory tax to 0.05% under section 7 of the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Conditions for exemption include the requirement for a tax invoice and export within 90 days of the invoice date.
The registered recipient must indicate the supplier's GSTIN and invoice number in the shipping bill.
The recipient must be registered with an Export Promotion Council or Commodity Board recognized by the Department of Commerce.
Goods must be moved directly to the export point or a registered warehouse before export.
If aggregating supplies from multiple suppliers, goods must first go to a registered warehouse.
Failure to export within 90 days disqualifies the registered supplier from the exemption.

1254.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 41/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 14th November 2017, which amends the earlier Notification No. 1/2017 dated 28th June 2017. This amendment primarily affects the GST rates and classifications of various goods listed in Schedules I and II. Notably, entries for S. No. 1 and S. No. 2 in Schedule I have been substituted to specify that goods must be put up in unit containers and bear registered brand names or names with enforceable rights. Additionally, S. Nos. 3 to 6 have been removed from Schedule I. In Schedule II, several entries have been omitted or replaced, including S. Nos. 2 to 10 and S. No. 44, while new entries such as 'Fly Ash' and 'Medical grade oxygen' have been introduced. The changes clarify conditions regarding brand names and actionable claims, which will impact manufacturers and sellers of these goods, ensuring they are aware of the updated classifications and tax implications.
key_points: Notification No. 41/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 14th November 2017.
This notification amends Notification No. 1/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
In Schedule I, S. No. 1 and S. No. 2 entries have been substituted to include specific goods with registered brand names.
S. Nos. 3, 4, 5, and 6 have been omitted from Schedule I.
Several entries in Schedule II have been omitted or substituted, including S. Nos. 2 to 10 and S. No. 44.
New entries have been added in both schedules, including items like 'Fly Ash' and 'Medical grade oxygen'.
Specific conditions regarding brand names and actionable claims have been clarified in multiple entries.
The notification includes detailed changes to various goods, affecting their GST rates and classifications.

1255.summary: Notification No. 42/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 14th November 2017, brings significant changes to the earlier Notification No. 2/2017. It replaces S. Nos. 8 and 9 with new entries that specify fresh or chilled goods and other goods subject to certain conditions. Additionally, S. Nos. 10 to 17 have been removed entirely from the Schedule. The notification also updates S. Nos. 21 and 22, introducing new entries for fresh or chilled goods and other goods with specific conditions. New entries have been added after S. No. 30 and S. No. 43, covering frozen vegetables and certain roots. Furthermore, S. No. 46 has been amended to include 'dried' in the description of goods. Lastly, the definition of 'registered brand name' has been revised to encompass brands registered after 15th May 2017 under various legal frameworks. These changes will affect businesses dealing with the specified goods in the Union Territories, ensuring they comply with the updated tax structure.
key_points: This notification is issued as Notification No. 42/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 14th November 2017.
It amends Notification No. 2/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
S. Nos. 8 and 9 in the Schedule are replaced with new entries for fresh or chilled goods and other goods with specific conditions.
S. Nos. 10 to 17 are omitted from the Schedule.
S. Nos. 21 and 22 are replaced with new entries for fresh or chilled goods and other goods with specific conditions.
New entries are added after S. No. 30 and S. No. 43 for specific goods, including frozen vegetables and certain roots.
S. No. 46 is amended to include 'dried' in the description of goods.
The definition of 'registered brand name' is updated to include brands registered after 15th May 2017 under various laws.

1256.summary: Notification No. 43/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 14th November 2017 to amend the earlier Notification No. 4/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. The key change is the addition of a new entry in the tax rate table, specifically Sl. No. 4A, which includes '5201 Raw cotton' and applies to 'Any registered person'. This means that registered persons dealing with raw cotton will now have a specific entry under the Union Territory tax rates. The amendment takes effect from 15th November 2017, ensuring that businesses involved in the trade of raw cotton are aware of this new classification. This change is significant for those in the agricultural sector, particularly for agriculturists and traders who are registered under the GST framework, as it clarifies the tax treatment for raw cotton transactions.
key_points: This notification is issued as Notification No. 43/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) on 14th November 2017.
It amends Notification No. 4/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
A new entry is added in the table after Sl. No. 4, specifically Sl. No. 4A.
The new entry 4A pertains to '5201 Raw cotton' and is applicable to 'Any registered person'.
This amendment comes into effect from 15th November 2017.

1257.summary: Notification No. 44/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 14th November 2017, brings important amendments to the earlier Notification No. 5/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) from 28th June 2017. Specifically, it updates the entries for Sl. No. 6A, 6B, and 6C in the table of the previous notification. The new entry for Sl. No. 6A now includes '5608 Knotted netting of twine, cordage or rope; made up fishing nets and other made up nets, of textile materials'. For Sl. No. 6B, the entry has been changed to '5801 Corduroy fabrics', while Sl. No. 6C now reads '5806 Narrow woven fabrics, other than goods of heading 5807; narrow fabrics consisting of warp without weft assembled by means of an adhesive (bolducs)'. These changes will be effective from 15th November 2017, impacting businesses dealing with these specific textile products in the Union Territories.
key_points: Notification No. 44/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) was issued on 14th November 2017.
This notification amends Notification No. 5/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment substitutes entries for Sl. No. 6A, 6B, and 6C in the table of the earlier notification.
New entry for Sl. No. 6A is '5608 Knotted netting of twine, cordage or rope; made up fishing nets and other made up nets, of textile materials'.
New entry for Sl. No. 6B is '5801 Corduroy fabrics'.
New entry for Sl. No. 6C is '5806 Narrow woven fabrics, other than goods of heading 5807; narrow fabrics consisting of warp without weft assembled by means of an adhesive (bolducs)'.
The changes will take effect from 15th November 2017.

1258.summary: Notification No. 45/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate), issued on 14th November 2017, introduces a tax exemption for specific goods supplied to designated institutions, limiting the union territory tax to 2.5%. This exemption applies to scientific instruments, computer software, and prototypes valued at fifty thousand rupees or less. The institutions eligible for this exemption include public funded research institutions, research institutions, departments of both Central and State Governments, and Regional Cancer Centres. To benefit from this exemption, these institutions must provide certificates from appropriate authorities at the time of supply, confirming that the goods are intended for research purposes. This notification is effective from 15th November 2017, allowing eligible institutions to take advantage of the reduced tax rate on the specified goods. This change aims to support research and development activities by easing the financial burden associated with the procurement of essential research materials.
key_points: Notification No. 45/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) issued on 14th November 2017.
Exempts certain goods from union territory tax, limiting it to 2.5% for specified institutions.
Goods include scientific instruments, computer software, and prototypes valued under fifty thousand rupees.
Eligible institutions include public funded research institutions, research institutions, departments of Central and State Governments, and Regional Cancer Centres.
Conditions for exemption include producing certificates from relevant authorities at the time of supply.
The notification comes into effect from 15th November 2017.

1259.summary: Notification No. 46/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) introduces several amendments to the earlier Notification No. 11/2017, effective from 15th November 2017. One significant change is the replacement of 'Services provided' with 'Composite supply of works contract' in the context of works contracts. Additionally, the definition of food supply by restaurants has been refined, specifying that it applies to establishments with a declared tariff of ₹7,500 and above per unit per day. This means that restaurants located in hotels or similar establishments will have different tax implications. Item (iv) under serial number 7 has been removed, simplifying the tax structure. Furthermore, the notification adds 'manufacture of handicraft goods' under serial number 26, expanding the scope of taxable activities. The term 'declared tariff' is also clarified to encompass all amenities included in accommodation charges. These changes will impact businesses in the hospitality and food service sectors, particularly those operating in union territories.
key_points: This notification is issued under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It amends Notification No. 11/2017-Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The changes will take effect from 15th November 2017.

For serial number 3, item (vi), 'Services provided' is replaced with 'Composite supply of works contract as defined in clause (119) of section 2 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, provided'.
For serial number 7, item (i), the definition of food supply by restaurants has been updated to include specific conditions regarding declared tariffs.
Item (iv) under serial number 7 has been omitted.
For serial number 26, item (i), 'manufacture of handicraft goods' has been added.
The term 'declared tariff' is clarified to include all amenities provided in accommodation units.

1260.summary: The Government of India has issued Notification No. 47/2017 to amend the earlier Notification No. 12/2017 regarding Union Territory Tax rates. This amendment, effective from 15th November 2017, makes specific changes to the tax treatment of certain services. Notably, the entry for serial number 11A has been updated to clarify that Fair Price Shops providing services to the Central or State Governments through the sale of food grains and other essentials under the Public Distribution System will be taxed differently. Additionally, the previous entry at serial number 11B has been completely removed. A new entry, serial number 79A, has been introduced, which exempts services related to admission to protected monuments from tax. This means that visitors to these sites will not incur any tax charges, promoting access to cultural heritage. These changes will affect Fair Price Shops and operators of protected monuments, ensuring clarity in tax obligations for these services.
key_points: This notification is issued under the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
It amends Notification No. 12/2017 - Union Territory Tax (Rate) dated 28th June 2017.
The amendment substitutes the entry for serial number 11A to specify services provided by Fair Price Shops to governments under the Public Distribution System.
Serial number 11B and its related entries are omitted entirely.
A new entry is added as serial number 79A for services by way of admission to protected monuments under the Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains Act, 1958.
This new entry (79A) is exempt from tax, indicated by 'Nil' in the tax rate columns.
The changes take effect from 15th November 2017.
